CLL (C) and Waldenström (E) cause chronic/primary cold agglutinin disease in older adults via a clonal lymphoproliferative process, suggested when no infection precedes; here a clear infectious trigger and acute course favor secondary post-infectious disease. CMV (D) is a rarer trigger and does not match the Mycoplasma serology.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management of his cold agglutinin disease?
options: Cold avoidance and supportive care; treat the underlying infection and transfuse warmed blood if needed
stages.explanation: Cold agglutinin disease responds poorly to steroids and splenectomy because hemolysis is complement-mediated and hepatic, so the cornerstone is thermal protection (keep warm, avoid cold exposure) with supportive care; secondary post-infectious cases often resolve as the infection clears, and any transfused blood should be warmed (B). High-dose steroids (A) are first-line for warm AIHA, not cold agglutinin disease. Splenectomy (C) is ineffective because clearance is hepatic, not splenic. Plasma exchange (D) can transiently remove IgM in severe acute crises but is not definitive. Hydroxychloroquine (E) treats lupus, not cold agglutinin disease. For severe primary/chronic disease, rituximab is the preferred targeted therapy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During cold weather he develops worsening acrocyanosis and a hemoglobin drop. Besides warming him, which intervention best addresses severe, refractory cold-agglutinin hemolysis driven by complement?
options: Rituximab (anti-CD20), with complement inhibition (e.g., sutimlimab) considered for severe disease
stages.explanation: For severe or chronic cold agglutinin disease, rituximab targets the clonal B cells producing IgM, and complement (classical pathway/C1s) inhibition with sutimlimab rapidly halts complement-mediated hemolysis, making (B) the best targeted approach when warming alone is insufficient. Corticosteroids (A) are largely ineffective in cold agglutinin disease. Iron (C) and folate (D) are adjuncts supporting erythropoiesis but do not treat the underlying complement-driven destruction. Therapeutic phlebotomy (E) has no role and would worsen anemia. Recognizing that the pathology is complement-driven and IgM-producing-clone-driven explains why B-cell depletion and complement blockade, not steroids/splenectomy, are the effective levers.

cases.diagnosis: Cold agglutinin disease (secondary cold agglutinin syndrome after Mycoplasma pneumoniae)
pearls: Cold agglutinin disease is IgM-mediated; the DAT is positive for C3 (complement) and negative for IgG.
Suspect it when RBCs clump at room temperature, the analyzer reports a spuriously high MCV, and there is cold-induced acrocyanosis.
Mycoplasma pneumoniae (anti-I) and EBV (anti-i) are classic infectious triggers of secondary cold agglutinin syndrome.
Treat with cold avoidance and supportive care; steroids/splenectomy are ineffective, while rituximab and complement inhibitors (sutimlimab) help severe disease.

cases.title: Dark Morning Urine, Low Counts, and Abdominal Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent dark urine in the mornings plus new severe abdominal pain and swelling.
cases.history: A 37-year-old man is admitted with two days of severe right-upper-quadrant and epigastric pain, abdominal distension, and worsening fatigue. For over a year he has noticed episodic dark, cola-colored urine, most striking on first morning void, along with easy fatigue and occasional shortness of breath. He has had recurrent oral bleeding after brushing and a few episodes of nosebleeds. No fevers, night sweats, or weight loss. No alcohol excess, no hepatitis risk factors, no anabolic steroids, no recent travel. PMH: an episode of "low blood counts" two years ago, never fully worked up. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: software developer, minimal alcohol, never-smoker, no illicit drug use. Family history: noncontributory. On review he reports leg swelling and new ascites-like abdominal fullness. He denies hematochezia and melena. He has had two prior superficial leg vein clots attributed to "sitting too much." He looks more jaundiced to his partner over the last few days, and his urine this morning was "almost black."
cases.exam: Pale, mildly jaundiced young man, uncomfortable from abdominal pain. Sclerae icteric. Cardiovascular: tachycardic, regular, soft flow murmur. Lungs clear. Abdomen distended with tender hepatomegaly, a fluid wave suggesting ascites, and RUQ tenderness without rebound. Spleen not clearly enlarged. Scattered petechiae on the shins and a resolving gum bleed are noted. Mild bilateral lower-extremity edema. No lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam normal. There is no asterixis. Skin shows no telangiectasias or spider angiomata.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His CBC shows pancytopenia, labs show intravascular hemolysis (high LDH, low haptoglobin) with a NEGATIVE direct antiglobulin test, and he now has hepatic venous congestion. Which unifying diagnosis best fits?
stages.explanation: The combination of Coombs-negative intravascular hemolysis (dark morning urine, high LDH, low haptoglobin), pancytopenia, recurrent unusual thromboses, and now Budd-Chiari (hepatic vein thrombosis) is the classic triad of paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (B). Warm AIHA (A) is DAT-positive and not thrombosis-prone or pancytopenic. Hereditary spherocytosis (C) causes extravascular hemolysis with spherocytes and is DAT-negative but does not cause pancytopenia or thrombosis. TTP (D) causes microangiopathic hemolysis with schistocytes and neurologic/renal findings, not the chronic hemoglobinuria or marrow failure. ITP (E) causes isolated thrombocytopenia without hemolysis. PNH uniquely links complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis, cytopenias, and atypical thrombosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hb 8.0 g/dL
WBC 2.9 ×10³/µL
Platelets 70 ×10³/µL
Reticulocytes 7.5%
LDH 1180 U/L
Haptoglobin <10 mg/dL
Total bilirubin 3.2 mg/dL (indirect predominant)
Direct antiglobulin test: negative
Urinalysis: hemoglobinuria, hemosiderinuria
Doppler ultrasound: absent flow in the hepatic veins with hepatomegaly and ascites, consistent with Budd-Chiari syndrome

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most specific confirmatory test for paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria?
options: Flow cytometry showing deficiency of GPI-anchored proteins (CD55, CD59) and FLAER
stages.explanation: PNH results from a PIGA mutation that prevents synthesis of the GPI anchor, so GPI-anchored complement regulators CD55 (DAF) and CD59 (MIRL) are deficient; flow cytometry quantifying loss of CD55/CD59 on RBCs and granulocytes, plus FLAER binding on white cells, is the gold standard (C). The sucrose (A) and Ham (B) tests are historical, insensitive screens now replaced by flow cytometry. Osmotic fragility (D) evaluates hereditary spherocytosis. Marrow karyotype (E) assesses MDS/leukemia and may be obtained to evaluate associated marrow failure but does not diagnose PNH. Loss of CD55/CD59 leaves RBCs vulnerable to complement-mediated intravascular lysis, the hallmark of PNH.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why are patients with PNH prone to life-threatening thrombosis such as Budd-Chiari syndrome?
options: Antithrombin deficiency from hepatic synthetic failure
Complement-mediated platelet activation, free hemoglobin scavenging nitric oxide, and impaired fibrinolysis
Factor V Leiden mutation present in all PNH patients
stages.explanation: PNH thrombophilia is multifactorial: complement attack activates platelets and releases prothrombotic microparticles, free plasma hemoglobin from intravascular lysis scavenges nitric oxide (promoting vasoconstriction and platelet aggregation), and there is impaired fibrinolysis, predisposing to thrombosis in unusual sites like the hepatic (Budd-Chiari), portal, and cerebral veins, so (B) is correct. Antithrombin deficiency (A) is not the mechanism; his liver failure is a consequence, not cause. Factor V Leiden (C) is not universal in PNH. Hyperviscosity from paraprotein (D) describes Waldenström/myeloma. HIT antibodies (E) require heparin exposure and cause a different syndrome. This thrombophilia drives much of PNH mortality.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond anticoagulating his acute Budd-Chiari, what is the most appropriate disease-directed therapy for his PNH?
options: A terminal complement inhibitor (eculizumab or ravulizumab), with meningococcal vaccination/prophylaxis
stages.explanation: Terminal complement inhibition with eculizumab or ravulizumab (anti-C5) blocks the membrane attack complex, dramatically reducing intravascular hemolysis, transfusion need, and thrombotic risk; patients must receive meningococcal vaccination (and often antibiotic prophylaxis) because C5 blockade increases susceptibility to Neisseria, making (B) correct. He also needs anticoagulation for the acute thrombosis. Corticosteroids (A) have no durable role. Splenectomy (D, phlebotomy aside) is ineffective because hemolysis is intravascular/complement-mediated, and surgery raises thrombosis risk; phlebotomy (D) is irrelevant. Hydroxyurea (E) treats myeloproliferative disease, not PNH. Allogeneic stem cell transplant remains the only cure and is considered for severe marrow failure.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before starting his complement inhibitor, which prophylactic measure is most critical to prevent a fatal complication?
options: Meningococcal (Neisseria meningitidis) vaccination, ideally 2 weeks before therapy, with antibiotic coverage if started urgently
stages.explanation: C5 inhibitors block formation of the membrane attack complex needed to kill Neisseria, so meningococcal disease can be rapidly fatal; patients must be vaccinated against N. meningitidis (serogroups ABCWY) ideally ≥2 weeks before starting, with bridging antibiotic prophylaxis if therapy cannot wait, making (B) correct. Pneumococcal (A) and influenza (C) vaccines are reasonable general measures but do not address the specific Neisseria risk created by terminal complement blockade. Acyclovir (D) and antifungal prophylaxis (E) target viral and fungal infections, not the meningococcal vulnerability unique to complement inhibition. This vaccination requirement is a board-favorite safety pearl.

cases.diagnosis: Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria with Budd-Chiari syndrome
pearls: PNH triad: Coombs-negative intravascular hemolysis (dark morning urine), cytopenias/marrow failure, and thrombosis in unusual sites.
Confirm with flow cytometry showing loss of GPI-anchored CD55 and CD59 (and FLAER), caused by an acquired PIGA mutation.
Thrombosis (e.g., Budd-Chiari) is a leading cause of death; free hemoglobin scavenges nitric oxide and complement activates platelets.
Treat with terminal complement inhibitors (eculizumab/ravulizumab) plus meningococcal vaccination; transplant is the only cure.

cases.title: Bruising, Fevers, and Exhaustion in a Young Painter
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of fatigue, easy bruising, mouth sores, and fevers.
cases.history: A 25-year-old man is admitted with three weeks of profound fatigue, easy bruising, gum bleeding, and recurrent low-grade fevers with mouth ulcers. He also reports a sore throat and a small skin abscess. He works refinishing furniture in a poorly ventilated home workshop using industrial solvents and adhesives and recently spent two weeks applying a benzene-containing stripper without a respirator. Two months ago, a walk-in clinic abroad prescribed him oral chloramphenicol for a prolonged "infection." No prior chemotherapy, radiation, or known toxin exposure otherwise. No travel to malaria zones. PMH: none. Medications: none currently; recently completed the chloramphenicol course. Allergies: none. Social: heavy hobbyist solvent use, social alcohol, never-smoker, no IV drugs. Family history: no blood disorders, no early cancers. Review of systems: dyspnea on exertion, dizziness, and several nosebleeds this week. He denies bone pain, lymph node swelling, and night sweats, and has not noticed weight loss. He has had no recent viral rash or hepatitis symptoms.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, pale young man, febrile and tachycardic. Conjunctivae pale; sclerae anicteric. Oral exam shows aphthous ulcers and a small amount of gingival oozing. Scattered petechiae over the lower legs and several ecchymoses on the arms. A 1-cm fluctuant abscess on the forearm with surrounding erythema. Cardiovascular: tachycardic with a soft flow murmur. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft and non-tender; crucially NO hepatomegaly, NO splenomegaly, and NO lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam normal. No bone tenderness over the sternum or long bones.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His CBC shows pancytopenia with a very low reticulocyte count. Given fever, neutropenia, and bleeding, what is the single most urgent immediate action?
options: Schedule an outpatient bone marrow biopsy next week
Begin a workup but defer treatment until biopsy results return
Obtain cultures and start empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for febrile neutropenia
Transfuse platelets to a goal of 150 ×10³/µL
stages.explanation: Fever in a neutropenic patient is a medical emergency; after blood and site cultures, empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., an antipseudomonal beta-lactam) must start immediately because untreated neutropenic sepsis is rapidly fatal, making (C) correct. Deferring the marrow biopsy or treatment (A, B) ignores the acute infection risk. Platelet transfusion (D) may be indicated for active bleeding or very low counts, but a goal of 150k is excessive and does not address the febrile neutropenia priority. Empiric steroids (E) are inappropriate before a diagnosis and could worsen infection. Stabilize and treat the life-threatening neutropenic fever first, then pursue the marrow diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & MARROW
stages.content:
Hb 7.2 g/dL
WBC 1.6 ×10³/µL (absolute neutrophils 0.3 ×10³/µL)
Platelets 14 ×10³/µL
Reticulocytes 0.2% (absolute very low)
MCV 104 fL
Peripheral smear: pancytopenia, NO blasts, NO schistocytes, NO dysplastic forms
Bone marrow biopsy: markedly hypocellular (<10% cellularity) with fatty replacement, no fibrosis, no infiltrating blasts
Cytogenetics: normal karyotype
Vitamin B12, folate, HIV, hepatitis panel: pending/negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The marrow is markedly hypocellular with no blasts, fibrosis, or dysplasia, and cytogenetics are normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Pancytopenia with a markedly hypocellular, fatty marrow lacking blasts, fibrosis, and dysplasia, plus a very low reticulocyte count, defines aplastic anemia (C), here plausibly triggered by benzene and chloramphenicol. AML (A) would show ≥20% blasts. MDS (B) shows a normo/hypercellular marrow with dysplasia and often abnormal cytogenetics. Myelofibrosis (D) shows marrow fibrosis with teardrop cells and a leukoerythroblastic smear, often with splenomegaly. Megaloblastic anemia (E) raises MCV but marrow is hypercellular with megaloblastic changes and is corrected by replacing B12/folate; his marrow is empty, not megaloblastic. The empty marrow with normal cytogenetics is the key discriminator.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: Which exposures in his history are the most classic causes of acquired aplastic anemia?
stages.explanation: Benzene (a solvent that suppresses hematopoietic stem cells) and chloramphenicol (which causes both dose-related reversible marrow suppression and a rare idiosyncratic irreversible aplasia) are textbook causes of acquired aplastic anemia, making (B) correct and consistent with his solvent work and recent antibiotic course. Acetaminophen/caffeine (A), penicillin/ibuprofen (C), and aspirin/ethanol (D) are not classic aplastic anemia triggers, though many drugs can rarely cause marrow injury. Iron and vitamin C (E) do not cause marrow aplasia. Other recognized causes include radiation, certain chemotherapies, viral hepatitis (seronegative), parvovirus (pure red cell aplasia), and idiopathic autoimmune aplasia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is 25 with severe aplastic anemia. What is the preferred definitive treatment for a young patient with a matched sibling donor?
options: Long-term red cell and platelet transfusions only
Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation from an HLA-matched sibling
stages.explanation: For a young patient (<40-50 years) with severe aplastic anemia and an HLA-matched sibling donor, allogeneic stem cell transplant is the treatment of choice and is potentially curative (B). Removing the offending exposures and supportive care (transfusions, antibiotics) are essential but transfusions alone (A) do not restore marrow and risk alloimmunization and iron overload. Splenectomy (C) plays no role in aplastic anemia. Erythropoietin (D) is ineffective because the marrow lacks progenitors to respond. Hydroxyurea (E) suppresses marrow and is contraindicated. For older patients or those without a matched sibling donor, immunosuppressive therapy with antithymocyte globulin plus cyclosporine (often with eltrombopag) is first-line.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: While awaiting transplant, which supportive measure is most appropriate to reduce transplant complications and infection risk?
options: Transfuse liberally with non-irradiated products from family members
Use leukoreduced, irradiated blood products and minimize transfusions, avoiding directed donations from potential donors
Give prophylactic granulocyte transfusions to all patients
Start broad immunosuppression with high-dose steroids
stages.explanation: Blood products should be leukoreduced (to reduce alloimmunization and CMV transmission) and irradiated (to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease in this immunocompromised patient), transfusions minimized to limit alloimmunization, and directed/family donations avoided because they can sensitize against a future related donor, making (B) correct. Non-irradiated family-donor products (A) risk TA-GVHD and sensitization. Routine prophylactic granulocyte transfusions (C) are not standard. High-dose steroids (D) increase infection risk without treating aplasia. Iron supplementation (E) is harmful given transfusional iron loading. These transfusion-stewardship principles directly protect future transplant success and reduce infectious and immune complications.

cases.diagnosis: Acquired severe aplastic anemia (benzene/chloramphenicol exposure)
pearls: Aplastic anemia is pancytopenia with a hypocellular, fatty marrow and very low reticulocytes, but NO blasts, fibrosis, or dysplasia.
Classic toxic causes include benzene and chloramphenicol; also radiation, hepatitis (seronegative), and idiosyncratic drug reactions.
Allogeneic stem cell transplant from a matched sibling is preferred for young patients; ATG plus cyclosporine for others.
Use leukoreduced, irradiated blood products, minimize transfusions, and avoid family-donor units to protect a future transplant.

cases.title: Slowly Worsening Anemia in a Woman with a Chest Mass
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of progressive fatigue and breathlessness on exertion.
cases.history: A 54-year-old woman presents with three months of steadily worsening fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and pallor noticed by her family. She denies bleeding, dark urine, jaundice, or weight loss, and has no fevers or night sweats. She had a recent chest x-ray for a lingering cough that incidentally showed an anterior mediastinal opacity. She has noticed mild bilateral eyelid heaviness by the end of the day and occasional double vision, though she attributed these to fatigue. No new medications, no recent viral illness, no sick contacts with rash. PMH: hypothyroidism. Medications: levothyroxine 88 mcg daily. Allergies: none. Social: schoolteacher, never-smoker, rare alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems notable for the eyelid drooping and dyspnea but no joint pains, rashes, or oral ulcers. She required no transfusions previously and has never been anemic before. She reports the fatigue is profound and not relieved by rest, and stairs now leave her winded.
cases.exam: Pale, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Conjunctivae markedly pale; sclerae anicteric with NO jaundice. Mild bilateral ptosis worsening with sustained upgaze; extraocular movements show subtle fatigable diplopia. Cardiovascular: regular tachycardia with a soft systolic flow murmur. Lungs clear; no dullness. Abdomen soft, non-tender, with NO hepatosplenomegaly. No lymphadenopathy, petechiae, or ecchymoses. No glossitis. Neurologic exam otherwise normal with normal proximal strength on first testing. Skin without rash.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her labs show isolated normocytic anemia with normal WBC and platelets and a strikingly LOW reticulocyte count. There are no signs of hemolysis or bleeding. What is the best next step to characterize the anemia?
options: Calculate the reticulocyte response and obtain a bone marrow examination
stages.explanation: Isolated anemia with an inappropriately low reticulocyte count and no hemolysis or blood loss indicates a production problem; quantifying the reticulocyte deficit and examining the marrow identifies the cause, making (B) correct. Empiric iron (A) is unjustified without evidence of deficiency and her normocytic indices. Transfuse-and-discharge (C) ignores the diagnostic question and the mediastinal mass. Erythropoietin (D) is premature before knowing the marrow has progenitors capable of responding. Electrophoresis (E) evaluates hemoglobinopathies, irrelevant to an acquired hypoproliferative normocytic anemia. The clue of a near-absent reticulocyte response with normal other lineages points toward a selective red-cell production defect.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hb 7.6 g/dL
MCV 90 fL
WBC 6.8 ×10³/µL (normal differential)
Platelets 230 ×10³/µL
Reticulocytes 0.2% (absolute 8 ×10⁹/L)
LDH normal; haptoglobin normal; bilirubin normal
Iron studies, B12, folate: normal
Bone marrow: normal cellularity with marked reduction in erythroid precursors; myeloid and megakaryocytic lines normal
CT chest: 4.5 cm well-circumscribed anterior mediastinal mass consistent with thymoma
Acetylcholine receptor antibody: positive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The marrow shows selective absence of erythroid precursors with preserved myeloid and megakaryocytic lines. What is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Selective loss of erythroid precursors with normal granulocytic and megakaryocytic lines, isolated anemia, and near-absent reticulocytes defines pure red cell aplasia (B). Aplastic anemia (A) causes pancytopenia with a globally hypocellular marrow, not isolated erythroid loss. MDS (C) shows dysplasia across lineages and frequent cytopenias with abnormal cytogenetics. Anemia of chronic disease (D) is mild, with low-normal reticulocytes and altered iron studies, not a near-absent erythroid line. Megaloblastic anemia (E) shows macro-ovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils, and a hypercellular marrow with megaloblastic precursors, corrected by B12/folate, which are normal here. The single-lineage erythroid arrest is the defining feature.

stages.label: ASSOCIATION
stages.question: Given the anterior mediastinal mass, positive acetylcholine receptor antibody, ptosis, and fatigable diplopia, what is the most likely associated condition driving her PRCA?
stages.explanation: An anterior mediastinal mass consistent with thymoma, plus fatigable ptosis/diplopia and positive AChR antibodies (myasthenia gravis), points to thymoma-associated pure red cell aplasia (B); thymoma is a classic cause of acquired PRCA and frequently co-occurs with myasthenia gravis. Parvovirus B19 (A) causes PRCA particularly in immunocompromised or hemolytic-anemia patients via persistent infection, but would not explain the thymoma and AChR antibodies. CLL (C) and LGL leukemia (D) are other PRCA associations but would show characteristic lymphocyte abnormalities, absent here. Diamond-Blackfan anemia (E) is a congenital PRCA presenting in infancy with congenital anomalies, not new-onset disease at 54.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of her thymoma-associated pure red cell aplasia?
options: Observation alone since PRCA always resolves spontaneously
Surgical thymectomy, with immunosuppression (e.g., cyclosporine or steroids) for persistent PRCA
stages.explanation: Thymoma-associated PRCA is treated by removing the thymoma (thymectomy), and because PRCA may persist or relapse after resection, immunosuppression such as cyclosporine or corticosteroids is added for ongoing disease, making (B) correct; thymectomy also helps the coexisting myasthenia gravis. Observation alone (A) is wrong because thymoma-associated PRCA does not reliably remit. Splenectomy (C) has no role. High-dose iron (D) is inappropriate and risks overload given likely transfusions. Plasma exchange (E) treats antibody-mediated crises like TTP, not PRCA. If parvovirus B19 were the cause, IVIG would be used; here the thymoma drives the disease, so surgery plus immunosuppression is appropriate.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: In a different patient, suppose PRCA were instead driven by chronic parvovirus B19 infection (positive B19 PCR, immunocompromised host) rather than thymoma. What is the most appropriate treatment?
stages.explanation: Persistent parvovirus B19 causes PRCA when the host cannot mount neutralizing antibody (e.g., immunocompromised states), and IVIG supplies neutralizing antibody that clears the virus and restores erythropoiesis, making (C) correct. Thymectomy (A) treats thymoma-associated PRCA, irrelevant when the cause is viral. Cyclosporine (B) and corticosteroids (D) target immune-mediated PRCA but would not eradicate active parvovirus and could worsen viral persistence by further immunosuppression. Eculizumab (E) is a complement inhibitor for PNH/aHUS, with no role in parvovirus-induced PRCA. Matching therapy to etiology (IVIG for parvovirus, thymectomy for thymoma, immunosuppression for idiopathic/autoimmune) is the key teaching point.

cases.diagnosis: Pure red cell aplasia associated with thymoma (and myasthenia gravis)
pearls: Pure red cell aplasia is isolated anemia with near-absent reticulocytes and selective loss of marrow erythroid precursors; other lineages are normal.
Key associations: thymoma (often with myasthenia gravis), parvovirus B19 (especially if immunocompromised), CLL, LGL leukemia, and drugs.
An anterior mediastinal mass plus fatigable ptosis and positive AChR antibodies points to thymoma with myasthenia gravis.
Treat thymoma-associated PRCA with thymectomy plus immunosuppression; treat parvovirus-driven PRCA with IVIG.

cases.title: Sudden Pallor and Collapse in a Girl Who Bruises Easily
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of extreme tiredness and pallor with near-fainting at school.
cases.history: A 12-year-old girl is brought to the ED for two days of worsening pallor, profound fatigue, and a near-syncopal episode at school. One week ago she had fevers, headache, and a bright red "slapped-cheek" facial rash followed by a lacy rash on her arms; several classmates had a similar illness. She is known to have hereditary spherocytosis, diagnosed in early childhood, with baseline mild jaundice and an enlarged spleen, managed with daily folic acid. Her usual hemoglobin runs around 10-11 g/dL with chronic reticulocytosis. Over the last two days she has become much paler than her usual baseline and is short of breath climbing stairs. No black or bloody stools, no heavy menses (premenarchal), no recent medications other than folic acid. PMH: hereditary spherocytosis. Medications: folic acid 1 mg daily. Allergies: none. Family history: father and paternal grandmother have "a spleen condition" requiring splenectomy. She denies abdominal pain out of proportion and denies dark urine beyond her usual mild tinge.
cases.exam: Strikingly pale, tachycardic girl, alert but tired-appearing. Sclerae mildly icteric (her baseline). Conjunctivae very pale. Cardiovascular: tachycardic with a soft flow murmur and bounding pulses. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft; spleen palpable 4 cm below the costal margin (her chronic baseline), non-tender. No new petechiae, ecchymoses, or active bleeding. Faded lacy reticular rash on the upper arms. No lymphadenopathy. Capillary refill slightly delayed. Neurologic exam normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has known hereditary spherocytosis and now severe anemia after a febrile illness with a slapped-cheek rash. Which lab finding would most specifically distinguish an aplastic crisis from a hemolytic crisis?
stages.explanation: In chronic hemolysis the marrow compensates with high reticulocytes; an aplastic crisis is defined by the marrow shutting down red cell production, so the reticulocyte count drops inappropriately low despite severe anemia, making (B) the key discriminator. Elevated indirect bilirubin (A), spherocytes (C), and low haptoglobin (D) are present chronically in hereditary spherocytosis and during hemolytic crises, so they do not distinguish the two. Splenomegaly (E) is her baseline. A hemolytic crisis would show even higher reticulocytes, whereas the hallmark of parvovirus-induced aplastic crisis is reticulocytopenia because the virus halts erythropoiesis. The low retic count is the single most specific clue.

stages.label: LABS & SMEAR
stages.content:
Hb 4.6 g/dL (baseline ~10.5)
MCV 84 fL
WBC 6.2 ×10³/µL
Platelets 240 ×10³/µL
Reticulocytes 0.1% (markedly low)
Total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL (her usual mild baseline)
LDH mildly elevated
Peripheral smear: spherocytes, NO polychromasia/reticulocytes, no schistocytes
Parvovirus B19 IgM: positive; PCR positive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The reticulocyte count is near zero and parvovirus B19 serology is positive. What is the diagnosis and the responsible mechanism?
options: Hemolytic crisis from increased splenic destruction
Aplastic crisis: parvovirus B19 infects and destroys erythroid progenitors, halting red cell production
Iron deficiency anemia from poor diet
stages.explanation: Parvovirus B19 uses the P antigen (globoside) to infect erythroid progenitors and transiently arrests erythropoiesis; in a patient with hereditary spherocytosis whose shortened red cell lifespan depends on constant marrow output, this causes a sudden, severe aplastic crisis with reticulocytopenia, making (B) correct. A hemolytic crisis (A) would raise, not abolish, reticulocytes. Iron deficiency (D as AIHA, C as iron) does not fit the acute post-viral reticulocytopenia and positive parvovirus serology; AIHA would be Coombs-positive with reticulocytosis. Splenic sequestration (E) causes a rapid hemoglobin drop with an enlarging spleen and is more typical of sickle cell disease, with preserved or high reticulocytes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her hemoglobin is 4.6 g/dL with tachycardia and near-syncope. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Red blood cell transfusion with supportive care
stages.explanation: An aplastic crisis is self-limited (marrow recovers in about 1-2 weeks), but the anemia can be life-threateningly severe; with Hb 4.6, tachycardia, and presyncope she needs red cell transfusion as a bridge until her own erythropoiesis resumes, making (B) correct. Oral iron with outpatient follow-up (A) is dangerously inadequate for symptomatic profound anemia and she is not iron deficient. Corticosteroids (C) treat autoimmune hemolysis, not parvovirus aplastic crisis. Emergent splenectomy (D) is not indicated for a transient, self-resolving crisis. Plasma exchange (E) is for TTP. Supportive transfusion bridges the gap; recovery is heralded by a brisk reticulocytosis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding infection control and counseling, which statement is most appropriate for this parvovirus B19 aplastic crisis?
options: She poses no transmission risk and needs no precautions
Use droplet precautions while viremic and counsel that pregnant contacts should consult their physician due to fetal hydrops risk
Antiviral therapy with acyclovir is curative
She should avoid all future vaccinations
stages.explanation: During an aplastic crisis patients are highly viremic and contagious (unlike immunocompetent children with simple fifth disease, who are no longer infectious once the rash appears), so droplet/contact precautions are warranted; importantly, pregnant contacts should be counseled because parvovirus B19 can cause fetal anemia and hydrops fetalis, making (B) correct. "No risk" (A) is wrong given high viremia. Lifelong isolation (C) is excessive; the crisis is transient. Acyclovir (D) does not treat parvovirus (there is no specific antiviral; IVIG is used for persistent infection in the immunocompromised). Avoiding vaccinations (E) is unfounded. The fetal-hydrops counseling point is high-yield.

cases.diagnosis: Transient aplastic crisis from parvovirus B19 in hereditary spherocytosis
pearls: Aplastic crisis = sudden severe anemia with a LOW reticulocyte count; in chronic hemolysis the marrow can no longer compensate.
Parvovirus B19 infects erythroid progenitors (via P antigen), transiently halting erythropoiesis for ~1-2 weeks.
Treat with supportive RBC transfusion; recovery is signaled by a brisk reticulocytosis.
Patients in aplastic crisis are highly contagious; counsel pregnant contacts about fetal hydrops, and use IVIG for persistent infection in the immunocompromised.

cases.title: Confusion, Bruising, and Fever in a Previously Well Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute confusion with widespread bruising and dark urine.
cases.history: A 41-year-old woman is transferred to the ICU after presenting with two days of headache, fluctuating confusion, and a fall, with family noting transient slurred speech and bruising over her limbs. She has felt fatigued with intermittent fevers and noticed dark urine. No recent diarrhea, no obvious bleeding source, no new medications, no recent infections, no anticoagulants. She had one prior uneventful pregnancy. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: none; no aspirin, no NSAIDs, no clopidogrel. Allergies: none. Social: marketing manager, social alcohol, never-smoker, no drug use. Family history: noncontributory. In the ED she had a brief generalized seizure. Review of systems notable for fatigue, easy bruising, dark urine, and waxing-waning mental status. She denies melena and hematemesis. There is no history of cancer, HIV, or autoimmune disease, and no recent quinine or ticlopidine exposure. Her mentation fluctuates between lucid and disoriented during evaluation.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing woman, intermittently confused and not oriented to date. Scattered petechiae and ecchymoses over the arms and legs; mild scleral icterus. No focal motor deficit between episodes; transient dysarthria observed. Cardiovascular: regular tachycardia. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft, non-tender, with NO hepatosplenomegaly. No nuchal rigidity. Skin without purpuric rash beyond bruising. No oliguria reported but urine is tea-colored. Neurologic exam fluctuates, with waxing and waning encephalopathy and no fixed lateralizing signs.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Labs show anemia and severe thrombocytopenia with schistocytes, high LDH, low haptoglobin, and a NEGATIVE Coombs test. Coagulation studies (PT/PTT/fibrinogen) are normal. What category of disorder is this?
stages.explanation: Coombs-negative hemolysis with schistocytes plus thrombocytopenia and normal coagulation studies defines a microangiopathic hemolytic anemia / thrombotic microangiopathy, making (B) correct; mechanical RBC shearing across platelet-rich microthrombi produces schistocytes. DIC (A) would prolong PT/PTT and consume fibrinogen, which are normal here, arguing against it. ITP (C) causes isolated thrombocytopenia without hemolysis or schistocytes. Warm AIHA (D) is Coombs-positive with spherocytes, not schistocytes. Vitamin K deficiency (E) prolongs PT without hemolysis or thrombocytopenia. The combination of MAHA plus thrombocytopenia with normal coagulation and neurologic findings should immediately raise concern for TTP.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Hb 8.4 g/dL
Platelets 12 ×10³/µL
Reticulocytes elevated
LDH 1320 U/L
Haptoglobin <10 mg/dL
Total bilirubin 2.8 mg/dL (indirect)
Direct antiglobulin test: negative
PT 12.5 s / PTT 30 s / fibrinogen 320 mg/dL (all normal)
Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
Peripheral smear: numerous schistocytes, marked thrombocytopenia
ADAMTS13 activity: <5% with inhibitor present

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: ADAMTS13 activity is <5% with an inhibitor. Which pentad and mechanism define this diagnosis of thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura?
options: Deficiency of factor VIII causing bleeding
Severe ADAMTS13 deficiency leaving ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers that drive platelet microthrombi; pentad of MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neurologic changes, fever, renal involvement
Complement dysregulation as the primary defect
stages.explanation: TTP results from severely deficient ADAMTS13 (here autoantibody-mediated), the protease that cleaves von Willebrand factor; uncleaved ultra-large vWF multimers spontaneously bind platelets, forming microthrombi that shear RBCs, producing the classic pentad of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, neurologic symptoms, fever, and renal dysfunction, making (B) correct. Factor VIII deficiency (A) is hemophilia, a bleeding disorder. Shiga toxin (C) defines typical HUS, where renal failure dominates and ADAMTS13 is normal. Antiphospholipid antibodies (D) cause large-vessel and obstetric thrombosis, not MAHA with ADAMTS13 deficiency. Complement dysregulation (E) is the mechanism of atypical HUS, not classic TTP. The full pentad is often incomplete; MAHA plus thrombocytopenia alone should prompt treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most urgent, life-saving treatment for her TTP?
options: Platelet transfusion to correct the thrombocytopenia
Urgent therapeutic plasma exchange (plasmapheresis), with corticosteroids and caplacizumab
stages.explanation: TTP is a hematologic emergency; urgent plasma exchange removes the ADAMTS13 inhibitor and ultra-large vWF multimers while replenishing functional ADAMTS13, dramatically reducing mortality, and is paired with corticosteroids (immunosuppression) and caplacizumab (anti-vWF) in immune TTP, making (B) correct. Platelet transfusion (A) is generally avoided unless life-threatening bleeding because it can "fuel" microthrombi and worsen the disease. Antibiotics alone (C) do not treat TTP. Splenectomy (D) is a salvage option for refractory/relapsing disease, not the acute lifesaver. Heparin (E) does not address the platelet-vWF microthrombi and adds bleeding risk in a thrombocytopenic patient. Start plasma exchange immediately, even before ADAMTS13 results return.

stages.label: PRINCIPLE
stages.question: Why should platelet transfusion be avoided in TTP unless there is catastrophic bleeding?
options: Platelets are ineffective because they are immediately destroyed by the spleen
Added platelets can incorporate into vWF-driven microthrombi, potentially worsening organ ischemia
Platelet transfusion causes anaphylaxis in all TTP patients
It raises the risk of cold agglutinin formation
stages.explanation: In TTP the core problem is platelet-rich microthrombi forming on ultra-large vWF multimers; transfusing platelets can supply more substrate for thrombus formation, potentially precipitating or worsening ischemic events such as stroke, so platelets are withheld except for life-threatening hemorrhage, making (B) correct. Splenic destruction (A) is not the rationale, and platelets are not uniformly ineffective. Universal anaphylaxis (C) is false. Transfused platelets do not neutralize plasma exchange (D). Cold agglutinin formation (E) is unrelated. This "don't add fuel to the fire" principle is a frequently tested distinction between TTP (avoid platelets) and primary bleeding/consumptive states where platelets may be indicated.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She improves with plasma exchange but relapses weeks later with falling platelets and recurrent schistocytes. What is the most appropriate next step for relapsing immune TTP?
options: Resume plasma exchange and add rituximab (anti-CD20) to suppress the ADAMTS13 autoantibody
stages.explanation: Relapsing immune (acquired) TTP is driven by persistent anti-ADAMTS13 autoantibodies; restarting plasma exchange controls the acute relapse, and adding rituximab depletes the antibody-producing B cells to achieve durable remission, making (B) correct. Stopping therapy (A) risks fatal progression. Lifelong platelet transfusions (C) are contraindicated and do not treat the underlying autoantibody. Iron therapy (D) does not address the autoimmune microangiopathy. Warfarin (E) is ineffective for vWF-platelet microthrombi and adds bleeding risk in thrombocytopenia. Caplacizumab and immunosuppression also have roles. Recognizing that the relapse is autoantibody-mediated explains why B-cell depletion is the key therapeutic lever.

cases.diagnosis: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (acquired, ADAMTS13 deficiency with inhibitor)
pearls: TTP pentad: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, neurologic changes, fever, and renal dysfunction (often incomplete).
Mechanism: severe ADAMTS13 deficiency leaves ultra-large vWF multimers that form platelet microthrombi; coagulation studies (PT/PTT/fibrinogen) are normal.
Urgent plasma exchange is life-saving; add steroids and caplacizumab, and consider rituximab for immune/relapsing disease.
Avoid platelet transfusion unless catastrophic bleeding, as it can worsen microthrombi.

cases.title: A Toddler with Bloody Diarrhea Who Stops Making Urine
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of bloody diarrhea followed by pallor, swelling, and decreased urination.
cases.history: A 4-year-old girl is admitted after five days of crampy abdominal pain and diarrhea that became grossly bloody, now with new pallor, puffy eyes, and markedly decreased wet diapers over the last day. The diarrheal illness began about a week after a family barbecue where she ate an undercooked hamburger; two other children at the event also had diarrhea. She has become lethargic and irritable. No prior kidney problems, no recent medications other than as-needed acetaminophen, no antibiotics given for the diarrhea. PMH: healthy, immunizations up to date. Medications: none routine. Allergies: none. Social: attends preschool; lives with parents and a sibling. Family history: noncontributory. Parents report her urine looks dark and scant and her ankles and face look swollen. She has had low-grade fevers. They deny rash, joint swelling, and prior bruising. She has been drinking poorly and vomited twice today.
cases.exam: Pale, tired-appearing young child with periorbital and pedal edema. Sclerae anicteric to mildly icteric. Mucous membranes slightly tacky. Cardiovascular: tachycardic, regular, soft flow murmur. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft with mild diffuse tenderness and active bowel sounds; no rebound. NO hepatosplenomegaly. Scattered petechiae on the lower legs. Mild generalized edema. Decreased skin turgor is not prominent given edema. Neurologic exam: irritable but no focal deficit or seizure at present. Blood pressure is elevated for age.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After bloody diarrhea she now has pallor, edema, hypertension, and reduced urine output. Which initial lab pattern would most strongly suggest hemolytic uremic syndrome?
options: Isolated leukocytosis with normal hemoglobin and platelets
Anemia with schistocytes, thrombocytopenia, and a rising creatinine
stages.explanation: The classic triad of hemolytic uremic syndrome is microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (anemia with schistocytes), thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury (rising creatinine), making (B) correct and fitting her post-diarrheal pallor, petechiae, edema, and oliguria. Isolated leukocytosis with normal counts (A) does not capture the hemolysis and renal failure. Microcytic anemia with low ferritin (C) is iron deficiency, unrelated to this acute triad. A normal CBC with high lipase (D) suggests pancreatitis, not HUS. Eosinophilia with high IgE (E) suggests allergy/parasites. The temporal link to bloody diarrhea and the MAHA-thrombocytopenia-AKI triad is the hallmark of typical (Shiga-toxin) HUS.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Hb 7.5 g/dL
Platelets 45 ×10³/µL
WBC 16 ×10³/µL
Reticulocytes elevated
LDH 1450 U/L; haptoglobin <10 mg/dL
Total bilirubin 1.9 mg/dL (indirect)
Direct antiglobulin test: negative
BUN 64 mg/dL; creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (baseline ~0.4)
PT/PTT/fibrinogen: normal
Peripheral smear: schistocytes, thrombocytopenia
Stool culture/PCR: Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7 positive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Stool studies confirm Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7. How does the toxin cause this syndrome?
options: It directly lyses red cells in the bloodstream
Shiga toxin damages endothelial cells (especially in the kidney), triggering microthrombi that shear RBCs and consume platelets
It depletes ADAMTS13 causing ultra-large vWF multimers
It activates the coagulation cascade consuming fibrinogen
It causes immune complex deposition in glomeruli
stages.explanation: Shiga toxin binds the Gb3 receptor abundant on renal microvascular endothelium, injuring endothelial cells and provoking platelet-rich microthrombi; RBCs are sheared as they pass these thrombi (schistocytes), platelets are consumed (thrombocytopenia), and glomerular microvascular occlusion causes AKI, making (B) correct. Direct RBC lysis (A) is not the mechanism. ADAMTS13 deficiency (C) defines TTP, and in typical HUS ADAMTS13 is normal. Consumptive coagulopathy with fibrinogen depletion (D) describes DIC, but her PT/PTT/fibrinogen are normal. Immune-complex glomerulonephritis (E) is a different renal process (e.g., post-streptococcal). The endothelial-injury/microthrombi mechanism explains the renal-predominant thrombotic microangiopathy of HUS.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of management for typical (Shiga-toxin) HUS in this child?
options: Empiric antibiotics to eradicate the E. coli
Meticulous supportive care: fluid/electrolyte management, transfusion as needed, and dialysis for severe AKI
Routine plasma exchange for all cases
Antimotility agents to control the diarrhea
stages.explanation: Typical HUS is managed supportively: careful fluid and electrolyte management, control of hypertension, red cell transfusion for symptomatic anemia, and dialysis for severe AKI or refractory metabolic derangements, making (B) correct; most children recover renal function with supportive care. Antibiotics (A) are generally avoided in STEC infection because they may increase toxin release and the risk of HUS. Routine plasma exchange (C) is the mainstay of TTP, not typical childhood HUS (it may be considered in atypical/complement-mediated HUS, where eculizumab is used). Antimotility agents (D) are contraindicated as they can worsen STEC disease and HUS risk. Steroids (E) have no established role.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which intervention would have most directly reduced this child's risk of developing Shiga-toxin HUS during her diarrheal illness?
options: Avoiding antibiotics and antimotility agents while providing supportive hydration
Immediate plasma exchange at diarrhea onset
stages.explanation: During STEC gastroenteritis, both antibiotics and antimotility agents (e.g., loperamide) are associated with increased risk of progression to HUS, so avoiding them while maintaining supportive hydration is the prudent course, making (B) correct. Early antibiotics plus loperamide (A) is exactly what to avoid. Prophylactic platelet transfusion (C) does not prevent HUS and is unnecessary without bleeding. Daily NSAIDs (D) are nephrotoxic and could worsen renal injury. Plasma exchange at diarrhea onset (E) is not indicated for typical pediatric HUS and would not prevent it. The board-favorite teaching point is that antibiotics and antimotility drugs can precipitate HUS in STEC infection.

cases.diagnosis: Hemolytic uremic syndrome (Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157:H7)
pearls: Typical HUS triad: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (schistocytes), thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury, classically after bloody diarrhea.
Shiga toxin injures renal endothelium (Gb3 receptor), forming microthrombi; ADAMTS13 is normal and coagulation studies are normal (unlike DIC).
Management is supportive: fluids/electrolytes, transfusion as needed, and dialysis for severe AKI.
Avoid antibiotics and antimotility agents in STEC infection, as they increase the risk of progressing to HUS.

cases.title: Oozing and Mottled Limbs in a Septic ICU Patient
cases.chiefComplaint: Bleeding from IV sites and dusky extremities in a critically ill patient with sepsis.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man is in the ICU with severe sepsis from gram-negative bacteremia following a perforated diverticulitis and emergent laparotomy. Over the past 12 hours nurses note persistent oozing from venipuncture and central line sites, blood in the nasogastric aspirate, and new mottling with dusky discoloration of the fingers and toes. He has been on broad-spectrum antibiotics and vasopressors. No personal or family history of bleeding or clotting disorders. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes. Home medications: amlodipine, metformin (held), no anticoagulants. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker. Family history: noncontributory. He is intubated and sedated. Despite resuscitation he remains hypotensive on norepinephrine, with worsening renal function and rising lactate. The team notes both bleeding (oozing, GI) and evidence of microvascular thrombosis (acral ischemia). Hemoglobin has been trending down and platelets are falling rapidly compared with admission values.
cases.exam: Intubated, sedated man appearing critically ill. Diffuse oozing from line and venipuncture sites; bloody NG output. Skin shows dusky, mottled fingers and toes with early acral necrosis and scattered ecchymoses and petechiae over the trunk. Sclerae mildly icteric. Cardiovascular: tachycardic on vasopressors. Lungs with ventilator-delivered breath sounds, bibasilar crackles. Abdomen with a fresh surgical dressing, distended, hypoactive bowel sounds. Extremities cool with delayed capillary refill. Neurologic exam limited by sedation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has simultaneous bleeding and microvascular thrombosis in the setting of sepsis. Which laboratory pattern would confirm disseminated intravascular coagulation?
options: Normal PT/PTT, normal fibrinogen, normal D-dimer
Prolonged PT and PTT, LOW fibrinogen, HIGH D-dimer, falling platelets, with schistocytes
Isolated prolonged PTT that corrects with mixing
Isolated thrombocytopenia with normal coagulation studies
stages.explanation: DIC is a consumptive coagulopathy: widespread activation of coagulation consumes platelets and clotting factors (prolonging PT and PTT) and fibrinogen, while accelerated fibrinolysis raises D-dimer, and microthrombi shear RBCs producing schistocytes, making (B) correct and explaining his combined bleeding and thrombosis. Entirely normal studies (A) and high fibrinogen with low D-dimer (E) exclude active consumption. An isolated prolonged PTT correcting on mixing (C) suggests a factor deficiency, not DIC. Isolated thrombocytopenia with normal coagulation (D) suggests ITP or a thrombotic microangiopathy like TTP/HUS, which spare fibrinogen and coagulation times. The hallmark of DIC is the full consumptive picture with high D-dimer and low fibrinogen.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Hb 8.1 g/dL (down from 11.0)
Platelets 38 ×10³/µL (down from 180)
PT 22 s (prolonged); INR 2.0
PTT 55 s (prolonged)
Fibrinogen 90 mg/dL (low)
D-dimer markedly elevated (>20 µg/mL)
LDH elevated; haptoglobin low
Peripheral smear: schistocytes, thrombocytopenia
Lactate 5.2 mmol/L; creatinine rising
Blood cultures: gram-negative rods

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His PT/PTT are prolonged, fibrinogen is low, D-dimer is very high, and platelets are falling. What single mechanism best explains this picture?
options: Isolated platelet sequestration in the spleen
Systemic activation of coagulation (e.g., by sepsis-induced tissue factor) consuming platelets and factors, with secondary fibrinolysis
stages.explanation: In sepsis, inflammatory mediators induce widespread tissue factor expression, triggering systemic thrombin generation and microvascular fibrin thrombi that consume platelets and clotting factors (prolonging PT/PTT, lowering fibrinogen); secondary fibrinolysis then generates high D-dimer, and the depletion produces bleeding while microthrombi cause ischemia, making (B) correct. Splenic sequestration (A) cannot explain the coagulation abnormalities. Pure hyperfibrinolysis without thrombin (C) would not consume platelets or cause microthrombotic acral ischemia. Vitamin K deficiency (D) prolongs PT (and later PTT) but spares platelets and fibrinogen and does not raise D-dimer. An acquired factor VIII inhibitor (E) prolongs only PTT and causes bleeding without thrombosis or fibrinogen consumption. DIC is the unifying consumptive process.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important principle in treating his DIC?
options: Empiric heparin for all DIC regardless of bleeding
Treat the underlying cause (aggressive sepsis source control and antibiotics) while providing supportive blood-product replacement
Withhold all blood products to avoid fueling thrombosis
stages.explanation: DIC is a secondary phenomenon, so the cornerstone is correcting the trigger, here treating sepsis with antibiotics and source control, while supporting the patient with blood products as needed (platelets for severe thrombocytopenia/bleeding, fresh frozen plasma for factor depletion, and cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen below ~100-150), making (B) correct. Empiric heparin for everyone (A) is wrong; anticoagulation is reserved for thrombosis-predominant DIC and used cautiously given bleeding risk. Withholding all products (C) is dangerous in a bleeding, consumptive patient. Splenectomy (D) has no role. Corticosteroids (E) are not primary DIC therapy. Fixing the underlying cause is the only definitive treatment; everything else is supportive.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His fibrinogen is 90 mg/dL with active oozing. Which blood product most specifically replaces fibrinogen?
stages.explanation: Cryoprecipitate is the concentrated source of fibrinogen (along with factor VIII, von Willebrand factor, factor XIII, and fibronectin) and is used to correct hypofibrinogenemia in bleeding DIC, typically when fibrinogen is below about 100-150 mg/dL, making (B) correct. Packed red cells (A) replace oxygen-carrying capacity, not clotting factors. Platelets (C) address thrombocytopenia but not fibrinogen. Albumin (D) is a volume expander without coagulation factors. Normal saline (E) provides volume only. While fresh frozen plasma replaces all clotting factors broadly, cryoprecipitate is the most concentrated and specific fibrinogen replacement, the key distinction tested here for managing the low fibrinogen in his consumptive coagulopathy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite resuscitation he develops progressive digital ischemia from microthrombi (e.g., symmetric peripheral gangrene) while still bleeding. What is the most appropriate approach?
options: Continue aggressive treatment of sepsis and supportive product replacement; consider cautious low-dose heparin only if thrombosis clearly predominates and bleeding risk is acceptable
Stop antibiotics to reduce drug effects
Give high-dose vitamin K as definitive therapy
stages.explanation: When DIC produces a thrombosis-predominant picture such as acral ischemia or purpura fulminans, the priority remains controlling the driver (sepsis) with ongoing supportive replacement; cautious low-dose heparin may be considered if thrombosis clearly dominates and bleeding risk is acceptable, making (B) correct. Full-dose thrombolysis (A) is dangerous in a bleeding, thrombocytopenic patient. Immediate amputation (C) is premature; perfusion may recover with source control and demarcation guides later surgery. Stopping antibiotics (D) would worsen the underlying sepsis driving DIC. High-dose vitamin K (E) does not reverse consumptive coagulopathy and is not definitive. Balancing thrombosis and bleeding while treating the cause is the central management challenge in severe DIC.

cases.diagnosis: Disseminated intravascular coagulation secondary to gram-negative sepsis
pearls: DIC is a consumptive coagulopathy: prolonged PT/PTT, LOW fibrinogen, HIGH D-dimer, falling platelets, and schistocytes, with both bleeding and thrombosis.
Sepsis-induced tissue factor drives systemic thrombin generation and microthrombi; secondary fibrinolysis raises D-dimer.
Treatment is to correct the underlying cause (source control, antibiotics) plus supportive products: FFP for factors, cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen, platelets for bleeding.
Cryoprecipitate is the most concentrated fibrinogen replacement; reserve cautious heparin for thrombosis-predominant DIC.

cases.title: Sudden Bruising and Tiny Red Spots in a Young Boy
cases.chiefComplaint: Pinpoint red spots and easy bruising that appeared over the last few days.
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his mother after she noticed scattered pinpoint red spots on his shins and several bruises on his arms and trunk over the past 3 days. He had a runny nose, low-grade fevers, and a sore throat about 2 weeks ago that resolved on its own. Since then he has been his usual energetic self—running around, eating well, and attending school without complaint. He has had one brief nosebleed but no blood in the urine or stool, no black tarry stools, and no headache or vision changes. He takes no medications, has had no recent travel, and has not started any new foods or supplements. He has received all routine vaccinations on schedule. He has no prior bleeding history, and his birth and circumcision were uncomplicated. There is no family history of bleeding disorders, easy bruising, or hematologic malignancy. He lives with both parents and a younger sister, all healthy. Review of systems is otherwise negative, with no weight loss, night sweats, bone pain, or recurrent infections.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, playful boy in no distress. Skin shows numerous nonblanching petechiae over both lower legs and scattered ecchymoses on the arms and trunk, with no palpable purpura. A few small petechiae are present on the buccal mucosa, but there is no active gingival bleeding. There is no lymphadenopathy in the cervical, axillary, or inguinal regions. The abdomen is soft and nontender, with no hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. Joints are normal without swelling or limited motion. The conjunctivae are pink. Neurologic exam is normal with no focal deficits or meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation is MOST reassuring that this is an isolated, benign process rather than a sinister cause of bruising?
options: The presence of petechiae on the buccal mucosa
Isolated mucocutaneous bleeding with no hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, or constitutional symptoms
The recent viral upper respiratory illness
stages.explanation: Isolated mucocutaneous bleeding with a normal exam—no organomegaly, lymphadenopathy, weight loss, or bone pain—is the key reassuring feature, because it argues strongly against marrow infiltration (leukemia) or systemic disease. Petechiae on the mucosa (wet purpura) actually signal a higher bleeding risk, not reassurance. A preceding viral illness is typical of immune thrombocytopenia but does not by itself exclude malignancy. Age 7 is within the typical range for both childhood ITP and acute leukemia, so it is nondiscriminating. The combination of an otherwise well child with a benign exam is what makes a primary platelet problem far more likely than a malignant or infiltrative process.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the single MOST appropriate initial test to order?
options: Complete blood count with peripheral smear
stages.explanation: A CBC with peripheral smear is the essential first test: it confirms the platelet count, verifies that the other cell lines are normal, and lets you rule out blasts, schistocytes, or platelet clumping. A bone marrow biopsy is not required for typical childhood ITP with isolated thrombocytopenia and a benign exam; it is reserved for atypical features. Antiplatelet antibody testing has poor sensitivity and specificity and does not guide management. PT/PTT assess the coagulation cascade and are typically normal in ITP, which is a platelet quantity problem, so they are not the first step here.

stages.label: LABS & SMEAR
stages.content:
WBC 7.1 ×10³/µL (normal differential)
Hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL
Platelets 14 ×10³/µL
MCV 84 fL
Peripheral smear: markedly decreased platelets with occasional large (young) platelets; normal RBC and WBC morphology, no blasts, no schistocytes
PT 12 sec / PTT 30 sec (normal)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the clinical picture and labs, what is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Isolated thrombocytopenia with large young platelets, a normal hemoglobin and white count, no blasts, and a benign exam following a viral illness is classic for immune (idiopathic) thrombocytopenic purpura. ALL would typically show abnormal cell lines (anemia, abnormal WBC, blasts) and often organomegaly or bone pain. HUS features microangiopathic hemolytic anemia with schistocytes, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury—none present here. Henoch-Schönlein purpura causes palpable (not flat) purpura on the buttocks/legs with a normal platelet count, plus arthralgia and abdominal pain. The destruction of antibody-coated platelets in the spleen explains both the low count and the compensatory large platelets.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has only minor skin and mucosal bleeding and is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Observation with close monitoring and bleeding precautions
stages.explanation: For a child with newly diagnosed ITP and only minor (skin/mild mucosal) bleeding, observation with bleeding precautions and close follow-up is preferred, because childhood ITP is usually self-limited and resolves spontaneously within weeks to months. Platelet transfusion is reserved for life-threatening hemorrhage (e.g., intracranial bleeding); transfused platelets are rapidly destroyed and are not used prophylactically. Splenectomy is a last-line option for chronic, refractory disease, not initial therapy. Cyclophosphamide is an immunosuppressant reserved for refractory chronic ITP and is inappropriate and toxic for a newly diagnosed, mildly affected child. Pharmacologic therapy (steroids, IVIG, anti-D) is reserved for significant bleeding.

cases.diagnosis: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura (childhood, acute)
pearls: Childhood ITP classically follows a viral illness and presents with isolated thrombocytopenia in an otherwise well child.
A benign exam (no hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, or constitutional symptoms) plus normal other cell lines makes leukemia unlikely and obviates routine marrow biopsy.
Most cases of acute childhood ITP resolve spontaneously; observation with bleeding precautions is appropriate when bleeding is minor.
Platelet transfusion is reserved for life-threatening hemorrhage because transfused platelets are rapidly consumed.

cases.title: Persistent Easy Bruising in a Middle-Aged Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Easy bruising and occasional gum bleeding that have persisted for several months.
cases.history: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 4-month history of easy bruising on her arms and legs, occasional bleeding from the gums when she brushes her teeth, and heavier-than-usual menstrual periods. She feels otherwise well, with no fevers, night sweats, weight loss, fatigue out of proportion, or bone pain. She denies melena, hematochezia, hematuria, headaches, or visual changes. She takes no regular medications, specifically no NSAIDs, aspirin, heparin, or herbal supplements, and reports no recent new drugs. She has no history of liver disease, alcohol use is minimal, and she has never had a blood transfusion. There is no recent viral illness or travel. She has no personal history of autoimmune disease, but her sister has Hashimoto thyroiditis. She has no prior abnormal blood counts on routine labs done 2 years ago. She is married, works as an accountant, and has two healthy children delivered without bleeding complications. Review of systems is otherwise negative.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Skin shows scattered ecchymoses of varying ages on the forearms and shins and a few petechiae on the lower legs. The oral mucosa shows a small amount of dried blood at the gumline but no active bleeding or wet purpura. There is no cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy. The abdomen is soft and nontender with no hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. There is no jaundice, no spider angiomata, and no palmar erythema. Joints are normal. Conjunctivae are pink, and the neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first diagnostic step?
options: CBC with peripheral smear and review of medications
stages.explanation: The first step is a CBC with peripheral smear plus a careful medication and history review, because this both quantifies the abnormality and screens for clues (pseudothrombocytopenia from clumping, blasts, schistocytes, or other cytopenias). Empiric steroids before establishing the diagnosis can mask underlying disease and are premature. A bone marrow biopsy is not the first step in an otherwise well adult with suspected isolated thrombocytopenia; it is considered selectively. Platelet aggregation studies evaluate qualitative platelet function (e.g., suspected platelet dysfunction or von Willebrand disease) and are not indicated for an isolated low count until the count itself is characterized.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
WBC 6.4 ×10³/µL (normal differential)
Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL
Platelets 22 ×10³/µL
MCV 88 fL
Peripheral smear: decreased platelets, occasional large platelets; no clumping, no blasts, no schistocytes; normal RBC and WBC morphology
PT 12.5 sec / PTT 31 sec (normal)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given isolated thrombocytopenia with an otherwise unremarkable workup, how is the diagnosis of immune thrombocytopenia in adults best characterized?
options: Confirmed by a positive antiplatelet antibody assay
Confirmed by characteristic findings on bone marrow biopsy
A diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out other causes of thrombocytopenia
Confirmed by an elevated reticulated platelet fraction alone
stages.explanation: Adult ITP is a diagnosis of exclusion: there is no single confirmatory test, so it is established by isolated thrombocytopenia plus exclusion of secondary causes (drugs, HIV, HCV, lupus, lymphoproliferative disorders, hypersplenism, pseudothrombocytopenia). Antiplatelet antibody assays lack adequate sensitivity and specificity and are not used to confirm or refute ITP. Bone marrow findings (normal or increased megakaryocytes) are supportive but nonspecific and not required for diagnosis in a typical adult. An elevated reticulated/immature platelet fraction suggests peripheral destruction but is supportive, not confirmatory. Reasonable secondary workup (HIV, HCV, and history/exam) is standard before settling on primary ITP.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has a platelet count of 22 ×10³/µL with mucocutaneous bleeding. She has no contraindication to steroids. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for newly diagnosed adult ITP requiring treatment is corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day or pulse dexamethasone), which suppress antibody-mediated platelet destruction and raise the count in most patients. IVIG is an alternative or add-on first-line agent when a more rapid rise is needed or steroids are contraindicated. Splenectomy is a second-line option reserved for steroid-refractory or relapsing disease. Rituximab and thrombopoietin receptor agonists (eltrombopag, romiplostim) are second-line therapies for patients who fail or cannot tolerate steroids. With moderate thrombocytopenia and only mucocutaneous bleeding, steroids are the appropriate initial choice.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: In which scenario would IVIG be preferred over (or added to) corticosteroids as initial therapy?
options: When the platelet count is mildly reduced and there is no bleeding
When a rapid rise in platelets is needed, such as active significant bleeding or urgent surgery
When the patient has a coexisting bacterial infection
When the patient prefers a longer duration of action
stages.explanation: IVIG is favored (often with steroids) when a rapid increase in platelet count is required—active significant bleeding, planned urgent surgery/procedure, or before delivery—because it raises counts faster than steroids alone, typically within 1–3 days, by blocking splenic Fc-mediated clearance. For mild thrombocytopenia without bleeding, observation is often appropriate and IVIG is unnecessary. A coexisting bacterial infection is not in itself an indication for IVIG over steroids. IVIG's effect is actually transient (days to a few weeks), so 'longer duration' is incorrect—steroids provide more sustained control. The defining advantage of IVIG is speed.

cases.diagnosis: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura (adult, primary)
pearls: Adult ITP is a diagnosis of exclusion; there is no confirmatory test, and antiplatelet antibody assays are not recommended.
Screen for secondary causes such as HIV, HCV, drugs, lupus, and lymphoproliferative disorders before diagnosing primary ITP.
First-line treatment for adults needing therapy is corticosteroids; IVIG is added when a rapid platelet rise is required.
Splenectomy, rituximab, and TPO receptor agonists are second-line options for steroid-refractory or relapsing disease.

cases.title: Falling Platelets and a Swollen Leg in a Critically Ill Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Critical care team notes a sharp drop in platelet count and a newly swollen, painful leg.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man is in the surgical ICU on postoperative day 7 after a coronary artery bypass graft. His recovery had been uncomplicated until the team noted a falling platelet count and, this morning, a swollen, tender right calf. He has been receiving subcutaneous unfractionated heparin for venous thromboembolism prophylaxis since the day of surgery, and he received intravenous heparin intraoperatively during bypass. His other medications include aspirin 81 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, metoprolol, and pantoprazole. He has type 2 diabetes and hypertension. He has no known drug allergies. He has no prior history of heparin exposure that he recalls, no personal or family history of clotting or bleeding disorders, and does not smoke. He has had no recent bleeding, fevers, or new lines placed. Review of systems is notable only for the new leg symptoms; he denies chest pain, dyspnea, or hemoptysis. His admission platelet count was 245 ×10³/µL and had been stable until day 5.
cases.exam: Alert man resting in bed in mild discomfort. Cardiovascular exam shows a regular rhythm with a healing midline sternotomy incision that is clean and dry without erythema or drainage. The right lower extremity is swollen from the calf distally with a 3 cm calf-circumference difference compared with the left, with tenderness along the deep venous tract and mild warmth; pedal pulses are intact. The left leg is normal. There is no active bleeding at line sites, no petechiae, and no mucosal bleeding. Lungs are clear, and the abdomen is benign. No skin necrosis at heparin injection sites.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the timing and the new thrombotic event, which clinical scoring tool best estimates the pretest probability of the suspected diagnosis?
stages.explanation: The 4T score estimates the pretest probability of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia using four domains: Thrombocytopenia magnitude, Timing of the platelet fall, Thrombosis or other sequelae, and oTher causes of thrombocytopenia. It directs whether to send confirmatory testing and when to act. The Wells score risk-stratifies DVT but does not address the cause of the thrombocytopenia, which is the central issue. CHA2DS2-VASc estimates stroke risk in atrial fibrillation. The PLASMIC score predicts severe ADAMTS13 deficiency in suspected TTP, a different microangiopathy. The 4T score is the validated tool specific to HIT and is the correct choice here.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Platelets 68 ×10³/µL (down from 245 on day 5; ~70% fall)
Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL
WBC 9.2 ×10³/µL
Peripheral smear: no schistocytes
PT 13 sec / PTT 33 sec / Fibrinogen 380 mg/dL (normal)
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Lower-extremity venous duplex: acute occlusive thrombus in the right popliteal vein

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His 4T score is intermediate-to-high (large platelet fall, onset day 5–7 with recent heparin, new proven thrombosis, no other clear cause). What is the best next step in confirming the diagnosis?
options: Send a heparin-PF4 antibody immunoassay (ELISA), with a serotonin release assay to confirm if positive
Repeat the platelet count in 48 hours and observe
Order a fibrinogen and D-dimer to diagnose DIC
stages.explanation: With an intermediate-to-high 4T score, send a heparin-PF4 (anti-PF4/heparin) antibody immunoassay; a positive or equivocal immunoassay is confirmed by a functional serotonin release assay (SRA), the gold standard. Crucially, you do not wait for results to act—empiric anticoagulation change begins now. A bone marrow biopsy has no role in HIT. Simply observing for 48 hours is dangerous given proven thrombosis and an intermediate-high score. A normal fibrinogen and the absence of schistocytes argue against DIC, and ordering DIC labs would not explain the platelet drop plus thrombosis pattern that points to HIT. The key is a paradoxical prothrombotic state from PF4-heparin antibody-induced platelet activation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Continue heparin and add a platelet transfusion
Stop all heparin and start a non-heparin anticoagulant such as argatroban
Stop heparin and start warfarin immediately
Stop heparin and start a prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin
stages.explanation: All heparin (including flushes and LMWH) must be stopped immediately, and because HIT is a prothrombotic state with active thrombosis, a non-heparin anticoagulant such as argatroban (a direct thrombin inhibitor) is started at therapeutic dose. Continuing heparin worsens the disease, and prophylactic platelet transfusion is avoided unless there is serious bleeding because it may fuel thrombosis. LMWH cross-reacts with HIT antibodies and is contraindicated. Warfarin must NOT be started while the platelet count is low: by depleting protein C it can precipitate warfarin-induced skin/limb necrosis (venous limb gangrene). Warfarin is bridged in only after platelets recover (typically >150 ×10³/µL) with overlap.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The platelet count recovers to 165 ×10³/µL on argatroban. The team wants to transition him to oral warfarin for long-term anticoagulation. What is the most important precaution?
options: Start warfarin at a high loading dose to reach target INR quickly
Overlap warfarin with the non-heparin anticoagulant for at least 5 days and until the INR is therapeutic, only after platelet recovery
Discontinue argatroban the moment warfarin is started
Switch back to heparin to bridge to warfarin
stages.explanation: Warfarin should be started only after the platelet count has substantially recovered, then overlapped with the non-heparin anticoagulant for at least 5 days AND until the INR is therapeutic, to prevent warfarin-induced venous limb gangrene from early protein C depletion. High loading doses are avoided because they drop protein C fastest and worsen this risk. Argatroban must not be stopped the instant warfarin begins—the overlap is essential, and note argatroban itself elevates the INR, so dosing/monitoring requires care during transition. Reintroducing heparin is contraindicated in HIT. The principle is patience: recover platelets first, then slow, overlapped warfarin initiation.

cases.diagnosis: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (type II) with thrombosis (HITT)
pearls: Suspect HIT when platelets fall ~30–50%+ on day 5–10 of heparin exposure, especially with new thrombosis; use the 4T score for pretest probability.
Confirm with a heparin-PF4 immunoassay and functional serotonin release assay, but do not delay treatment for results.
Stop ALL heparin (including LMWH and flushes) and start a non-heparin anticoagulant such as argatroban; avoid prophylactic platelet transfusions.
Do not start warfarin until platelets recover; overlap with a parenteral non-heparin agent to prevent warfarin-induced venous limb gangrene.

cases.title: Heavy Periods and Frequent Nosebleeds in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Lifelong heavy menstrual periods, frequent nosebleeds, and easy bruising.
cases.history: A 20-year-old woman presents for evaluation of long-standing heavy menstrual bleeding, frequent nosebleeds, and bruising that seems excessive for minor bumps. Her periods have been heavy since menarche at age 12, lasting 7–8 days with clots and soaking through protection, and she has been told she is borderline anemic in the past. She gets nosebleeds several times a month that can take 10–15 minutes to stop. She bled more than expected after a dental extraction 2 years ago. She has never had surgery otherwise. She takes no anticoagulants, no NSAIDs, and no aspirin; she occasionally uses acetaminophen. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke or use drugs and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is notable for a mother and a maternal aunt with heavy periods and easy bruising, suggesting an autosomal pattern. She has no joint swelling or deep muscle bleeds and no history of hemarthrosis. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable, with no weight loss, fevers, or recurrent infections.
cases.exam: Well-appearing young woman in no distress. Skin shows a few scattered ecchymoses on the extremities without petechiae. The nasal mucosa appears slightly friable but is not actively bleeding. The oral mucosa is moist with no active gingival bleeding. There is no lymphadenopathy and no hepatosplenomegaly. Joints are normal without swelling, effusion, or limited range of motion, and there is no evidence of prior hemarthropathy. Conjunctivae are pink. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
