stages.question: Her bleeding pattern is predominantly mucocutaneous (menorrhagia, epistaxis, easy bruising, post-dental bleeding). What type of hemostatic defect does this pattern most suggest?
options: A defect in secondary hemostasis (coagulation factor deficiency)
A defect in primary hemostasis (platelets or von Willebrand factor)
stages.explanation: Mucocutaneous bleeding—menorrhagia, recurrent epistaxis, easy bruising, and bleeding after minor procedures like dental work—points to a defect in primary hemostasis, which involves platelets and von Willebrand factor (vWF). Deep bleeding into joints and muscles (hemarthrosis, deep hematomas) and delayed bleeding after trauma characterize secondary hemostasis (coagulation factor) defects such as hemophilia. A pure fibrinolytic disorder is rare and not the best fit for this classic mucocutaneous picture with a positive family history. Vascular collagen disorders can cause bruising but would not explain the broader bleeding pattern and inheritance. The autosomal family history with mucosal bleeding is highly suggestive of von Willebrand disease.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which set of laboratory tests is the most appropriate initial screen for this suspected disorder?
options: Factor VIII activity, von Willebrand factor antigen, and ristocetin cofactor activity (with CBC, PT, PTT)
stages.explanation: The targeted initial workup for suspected von Willebrand disease is the vWF panel: factor VIII activity, von Willebrand factor antigen (vWF:Ag), and ristocetin cofactor activity (vWF:RCo, a measure of vWF function), along with a CBC and PT/PTT. A CBC and PT alone are insufficient and may be normal. Antiplatelet antibody testing evaluates immune thrombocytopenia, which does not fit this picture of normal platelet counts and mucosal bleeding. A bone marrow biopsy is not indicated for a suspected qualitative/quantitative vWF disorder with normal counts. Note the PTT may be normal or only mildly prolonged depending on the factor VIII level, so it cannot be relied on alone.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
CBC: Hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL, MCV 76 fL (mild iron-deficiency pattern), Platelets 240 ×10³/µL
PT 12 sec (normal)
PTT 38 sec (mildly prolonged)
Factor VIII activity 42% (low-normal/low)
von Willebrand factor antigen 38% (low)
Ristocetin cofactor activity (vWF:RCo) 30% (low)
vWF:RCo / vWF:Ag ratio 0.79

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given low vWF antigen, low ristocetin cofactor activity, low-normal factor VIII, and a ratio near 1, what is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A proportional reduction in vWF antigen and vWF activity (ristocetin cofactor) with a preserved RCo/Ag ratio (~>0.7) indicates a quantitative partial deficiency, i.e., type 1 von Willebrand disease, the most common type. Because vWF stabilizes factor VIII, the factor VIII level is mildly reduced and the PTT is mildly prolonged. Hemophilia A causes deep bleeding with markedly low factor VIII and normal vWF, and is X-linked (uncommon in women). Bernard-Soulier is a rare platelet GPIb defect with giant platelets and thrombocytopenia. ITP causes thrombocytopenia, which is absent here. A low RCo/Ag ratio would instead suggest a qualitative type 2 defect.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She needs treatment options for bleeding episodes and an upcoming minor dental procedure. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for mild type 1 von Willebrand disease?
stages.explanation: Desmopressin (DDAVP) is first-line for mild type 1 von Willebrand disease: it triggers release of stored vWF and factor VIII from endothelial Weibel-Palade bodies, raising both levels for minor bleeding or procedures. Antifibrinolytics (tranexamic acid) are a useful adjunct, especially for mucosal/dental bleeding. Recombinant factor VIII alone does not replace vWF and is not the preferred agent; vWF-containing concentrates are reserved for severe disease, type 3, or when DDAVP is ineffective/contraindicated. Platelet transfusion is not indicated for type 1 vWD. Aspirin is contraindicated because it impairs platelet function and would worsen bleeding. A DDAVP trial is often done to confirm responsiveness.

cases.diagnosis: Type 1 von Willebrand disease
pearls: Mucocutaneous bleeding (menorrhagia, epistaxis, easy bruising) suggests a primary hemostasis defect; vWD is the most common inherited bleeding disorder.
Diagnostic panel: vWF antigen, ristocetin cofactor activity, and factor VIII; a preserved RCo/Ag ratio (~>0.7) with low levels indicates type 1.
vWF stabilizes factor VIII, so factor VIII may be mildly low and the PTT mildly prolonged in vWD.
Desmopressin (DDAVP) is first-line for mild type 1 disease; tranexamic acid is a helpful adjunct and aspirin/NSAIDs should be avoided.

cases.title: A Swollen, Painful Knee in a Toddler
cases.chiefComplaint: A swollen, warm knee and refusal to bear weight after minor play.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic with a swollen, warm right knee and reluctance to walk that developed over a day after he was playing actively but without any major fall. His parents recall that as a toddler he had a large, slow-to-resolve bruise after a minor bump, and he bled longer than expected from a small mouth laceration last year. There was no excessive bleeding at circumcision, which was not performed, and the umbilical stump healed normally. He takes no medications, has no known drug allergies, and his immunizations are up to date. He has met developmental milestones. He has had no fevers, rashes, or recent illness, and no joint redness elsewhere. Family history is notable: his mother's brother (the boy's maternal uncle) has a 'bleeding problem' and required factor infusions, and a maternal grandmother's brother reportedly had similar issues, suggesting X-linked inheritance through the maternal line. The parents are nonconsanguineous. Review of systems is otherwise negative.
cases.exam: Alert toddler who is fussy and holds the right knee slightly flexed, resisting movement. The right knee is swollen, warm, and tense with a palpable effusion and pain on passive range of motion; there is no overlying erythema suggestive of cellulitis and no break in the skin. There are a few ecchymoses on the shins of varying ages. The remaining joints are normal. There is no lymphadenopathy and no hepatosplenomegaly. The skin shows no petechiae. Mucous membranes are moist without active bleeding. Neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The bleeding into a joint (hemarthrosis) and deep bruising suggest a defect in which part of hemostasis?
stages.explanation: Bleeding into joints (hemarthrosis) and deep soft-tissue/muscle bleeding are hallmarks of a secondary hemostasis defect—a coagulation factor deficiency such as hemophilia. Primary hemostasis defects (platelet or von Willebrand factor problems) instead produce mucocutaneous bleeding (petechiae, epistaxis, menorrhagia). Pure fibrinolytic disorders are rare and would not produce this classic hemarthrosis-with-family-history picture. A vascular integrity problem alone (e.g., scurvy, connective tissue disease) does not explain a tense hemarthrosis with an X-linked family pattern. The X-linked maternal-line inheritance plus deep bleeding strongly suggests hemophilia, prompting cascade-focused testing.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
CBC: Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, WBC 8.0 ×10³/µL, Platelets 290 ×10³/µL
PT 12 sec (normal)
PTT 62 sec (prolonged)
Fibrinogen 300 mg/dL (normal)
Peripheral smear: normal
Joint exam consistent with acute hemarthrosis

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: He has an isolated prolonged PTT with a normal PT and platelet count. What test best distinguishes a factor deficiency from an inhibitor as the cause?
options: A mixing study (patient plasma mixed 1:1 with normal plasma)
stages.explanation: A mixing study clarifies an isolated prolonged PTT: if the PTT corrects after mixing patient plasma 1:1 with normal plasma, a factor deficiency is present (the normal plasma supplies the missing factor). If it fails to correct, an inhibitor (e.g., an antibody or antiphospholipid) is suspected. Bleeding time assesses platelet function/primary hemostasis and is normal here, so it is unhelpful for this cascade problem. D-dimer reflects fibrinolysis/thrombosis and would not localize a factor versus inhibitor cause. The platelet count is already normal and stable. The mixing study is the pivotal next step before specific factor assays.

stages.label: MIXING STUDY & FACTOR ASSAYS
stages.content:
Mixing study: PTT corrects to 34 sec after 1:1 mix with normal plasma (suggests factor deficiency)
Factor VIII activity 3% (markedly low)
Factor IX activity 95% (normal)
von Willebrand factor antigen 100% (normal)
Factor VIII inhibitor (Bethesda) assay: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the correcting mixing study and a markedly low factor VIII with normal factor IX and vWF, what is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A markedly reduced factor VIII activity (3%) with normal factor IX and normal vWF, an isolated prolonged PTT that corrects on mixing, and X-linked inheritance establishes hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency). Hemophilia B is factor IX deficiency, which is normal here. Von Willebrand disease would show reduced vWF (and often a mildly low factor VIII due to lost stabilization) with a mucocutaneous pattern, not isolated deep bleeding. Factor XII deficiency prolongs the PTT but does NOT cause clinical bleeding, so it cannot explain hemarthrosis. The correcting mixing study plus the specific factor VIII deficiency is diagnostic of hemophilia A.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for this acute hemarthrosis?
options: Aspirin for pain and joint aspiration
stages.explanation: Acute bleeding in hemophilia A is treated with factor VIII concentrate replacement to raise factor levels and control the hemarthrosis, alongside rest and analgesia. Aspirin is contraindicated because it impairs platelet function and worsens bleeding, and routine joint aspiration is generally avoided acutely. Platelet transfusion does not address a clotting factor deficiency. Desmopressin can raise factor VIII modestly and is useful in mild hemophilia A, but with a factor VIII of 3% (severe disease), DDAVP alone is inadequate—factor concentrate is required. Adjuncts include antifibrinolytics for mucosal bleeding and prophylactic factor regimens to prevent recurrent hemarthroses and joint damage.

cases.diagnosis: Hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency)
pearls: Hemarthrosis and deep muscle/soft-tissue bleeding indicate a secondary hemostasis (coagulation factor) defect.
Hemophilia A produces an isolated prolonged PTT with normal PT and platelets; the PTT corrects on a mixing study (factor deficiency).
It is X-linked recessive—look for affected males in the maternal line; confirm with low factor VIII and normal factor IX.
Treat acute bleeds with factor VIII concentrate (DDAVP only helps mild disease); avoid aspirin/NSAIDs and consider prophylaxis to protect joints.

cases.title: A Teenager With a Painful, Swelling Thigh
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening pain and swelling in the thigh that developed without significant injury.
cases.history: A 15-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with 2 days of progressive pain, swelling, and firmness in his left thigh that began spontaneously, without a clear traumatic event beyond ordinary activity. He reports a similar but smaller episode in his calf a year ago that resolved slowly. As a younger child he had prolonged bleeding after a tooth extraction and a few episodes of joint swelling that his family attributed to 'growing pains.' He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He denies fevers, chills, or recent illness. He has not been on anticoagulants and uses no NSAIDs or aspirin. Family history is significant: a maternal uncle has a lifelong bleeding disorder requiring factor infusions, and the inheritance pattern appears to skip females and affect males, consistent with X-linked transmission. He is otherwise healthy, plays on the school soccer team, does not smoke, drink, or use drugs. Review of systems is negative for mucosal bleeding, hematuria, melena, headache, or neurologic symptoms.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable but well-appearing adolescent. The left anterior thigh is swollen, firm, and tender with a palpable deep mass effect consistent with an intramuscular hematoma; there is mild warmth but no overlying erythema or fluctuance and no skin breach. Distal pulses are intact, sensation is normal, and there is no compartment-syndrome tightness on serial checks at presentation. There are no petechiae and no active mucosal bleeding. Other joints are without effusion. There is no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are normal, and the neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His spontaneous deep muscle hematoma and prior joint bleeds, with an X-linked family history, point toward what category of disorder?
options: A platelet/von Willebrand factor (primary hemostasis) disorder
stages.explanation: Deep muscle hematomas and hemarthroses, particularly with an X-linked pattern affecting males through the maternal line, indicate a secondary hemostasis defect—a coagulation factor deficiency such as hemophilia. Primary hemostasis disorders (platelet or vWF) cause mucocutaneous bleeding such as petechiae and epistaxis, not deep muscle bleeds. Vasculitis (e.g., HSP) causes palpable purpura and a normal platelet/coagulation profile, not deep hematomas. A 'fibrinolytic shutdown' is prothrombotic, not a bleeding diathesis. The clinical and inheritance pattern strongly suggests hemophilia, and the next step is cascade-focused coagulation testing.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
CBC: Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, WBC 7.5 ×10³/µL, Platelets 270 ×10³/µL
PT 12 sec (normal)
PTT 58 sec (prolonged)
Fibrinogen 320 mg/dL (normal)
Ultrasound left thigh: large intramuscular hematoma in the quadriceps, no arterial injury

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Given the isolated prolonged PTT with normal PT and platelets, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Mixing study followed by specific factor assays (VIII and IX)
Genetic testing as the first step
stages.explanation: An isolated prolonged PTT with normal PT and platelets should be evaluated with a mixing study; correction implies factor deficiency, prompting specific factor VIII and IX assays to distinguish hemophilia A from B. Bleeding time evaluates platelet/primary hemostasis and would be normal here, providing no useful information. Empiric platelet transfusion does not treat a clotting factor deficiency and is inappropriate. Genetic testing can confirm a mutation later but is not the initial step—the functional factor assays establish the diagnosis and guide immediate treatment. The mixing study plus factor levels is the standard, efficient pathway.

stages.label: FACTOR ASSAYS
stages.content:
Mixing study: PTT corrects with 1:1 normal plasma (factor deficiency pattern)
Factor VIII activity 90% (normal)
Factor IX activity 4% (markedly low)
von Willebrand factor antigen 105% (normal)
Factor XI activity 100% (normal)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With a markedly low factor IX, normal factor VIII, and a correcting mixing study in an X-linked pattern, what is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A markedly reduced factor IX activity with a normal factor VIII, normal vWF, an isolated PTT that corrects on mixing, and X-linked inheritance defines hemophilia B (Christmas disease), a factor IX deficiency. Hemophilia A is a factor VIII deficiency, which is normal here. Factor XI deficiency (hemophilia C) is autosomal and the factor XI level is normal in this case. Von Willebrand disease causes mucocutaneous bleeding with reduced vWF, not deep hematomas with normal vWF. Hemophilia A and B are clinically indistinguishable by bleeding phenotype—only the specific factor assays differentiate them, underscoring why both VIII and IX must be measured.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for his acute deep muscle bleed?
options: Fresh frozen plasma as the preferred agent
stages.explanation: Acute bleeding in hemophilia B is treated with factor IX concentrate to correct the specific deficiency, along with rest, analgesia (avoiding NSAIDs/aspirin), and monitoring for compartment syndrome. Desmopressin raises factor VIII (useful in mild hemophilia A) but has no meaningful effect on factor IX, so it is ineffective here. Factor VIII concentrate treats hemophilia A and would not correct a factor IX deficiency. Fresh frozen plasma contains factor IX but in low, nonconcentrated amounts and risks volume overload; purified/recombinant factor IX concentrate is preferred. Tranexamic acid can be a useful adjunct for mucosal bleeding, and prophylaxis is considered for severe disease.

cases.diagnosis: Hemophilia B (factor IX deficiency)
pearls: Hemophilia B is clinically identical to hemophilia A (deep joint/muscle bleeds, X-linked); only factor assays distinguish them.
Both show an isolated prolonged PTT that corrects on a mixing study; measure factor VIII AND IX to differentiate.
Treat acute bleeds in hemophilia B with factor IX concentrate—DDAVP and factor VIII concentrate do not work for it.
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs, watch deep muscle bleeds for compartment syndrome, and consider prophylaxis in severe disease.

cases.title: New Spontaneous Bruising in an Elderly Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Large spontaneous bruises and a painful arm hematoma appearing over the past two weeks.
cases.history: A 74-year-old woman admitted for management of a community-acquired pneumonia is noted to have developed extensive new bruising and a large, painful hematoma in her right forearm over the past 2 weeks, without trauma. She has no lifelong bleeding history: she underwent an uncomplicated cholecystectomy at age 50 and three childbirths without abnormal bleeding, arguing against an inherited disorder. She takes amlodipine and atorvastatin and finished a course of antibiotics; she is on prophylactic enoxaparin during this admission. She denies aspirin, NSAIDs, warfarin, or other anticoagulants prior to admission. She has no known drug allergies. She has mild osteoarthritis and a remote history of a 'thyroid problem.' Family history is unremarkable for bleeding disorders. She does not smoke and rarely drinks. Review of systems is notable for fatigue but no melena, hematuria, hemoptysis, weight loss, night sweats, or lymph node swelling. There is no recent malignancy diagnosis, though her age and new acquired bleeding raise concern for an underlying condition.
cases.exam: Elderly woman appearing tired but in no acute distress. Skin shows multiple large ecchymoses on the trunk and extremities and a tense, tender hematoma along the right forearm with mild swelling; distal pulses and sensation are intact. There are no petechiae and no wet purpura. There is no lymphadenopathy and no hepatosplenomegaly. Joints show no acute effusions. Mucous membranes are moist without active bleeding. Lungs have crackles at the right base consistent with resolving pneumonia. The neurologic exam is nonfocal and there is no evidence of intracranial bleeding.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly suggests an ACQUIRED rather than inherited bleeding disorder in this patient?
options: New-onset bleeding at age 74 with no prior personal or family bleeding history
stages.explanation: New-onset bleeding in an older adult with no prior personal or family bleeding history—including uneventful past surgery and childbirths—strongly favors an acquired disorder rather than an inherited one, which would have manifested earlier in life. Ecchymoses are nonspecific. A prolonged PTT is a clue but does not by itself distinguish acquired from inherited causes. Prophylactic enoxaparin can contribute to bleeding but would not explain a markedly prolonged PTT or a large spontaneous deep hematoma of this severity, and LMWH minimally affects the PTT. The late-onset, de novo bleeding pattern is the key pointer toward an acquired etiology such as an acquired factor inhibitor.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
CBC: Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL (down from 12.0), WBC 8.2 ×10³/µL, Platelets 260 ×10³/µL
PT 12 sec (normal)
PTT 70 sec (markedly prolonged)
Fibrinogen 350 mg/dL (normal)
Peripheral smear: no schistocytes, normal platelet number
Thrombin time: normal

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: She has an isolated prolonged PTT with a normal platelet count. A mixing study is performed. What result would point toward an inhibitor rather than a factor deficiency?
options: The PTT fully corrects immediately on 1:1 mixing
The PTT fails to correct (or corrects then prolongs after incubation)
The PT becomes prolonged after mixing
The thrombin time becomes prolonged after mixing
stages.explanation: In a mixing study, failure of the PTT to correct (or correction immediately followed by re-prolongation after 1–2 hours of incubation at 37°C) indicates an inhibitor—an antibody that neutralizes a clotting factor—rather than a simple deficiency. A factor deficiency corrects fully and stably with normal plasma. A time- and temperature-dependent pattern is classic for a factor VIII autoantibody (acquired hemophilia A). The PT and thrombin time are not the discriminating tests for an isolated PTT inhibitor here. The incubated mixing study distinguishing a non-correcting inhibitor from a correcting deficiency is the pivotal step before specific factor and Bethesda inhibitor assays.

stages.label: MIXING STUDY & INHIBITOR ASSAY
stages.content:
Mixing study: PTT 70 → 48 sec immediately, then prolongs to 66 sec after 2-hour incubation (time-dependent inhibitor pattern)
Factor VIII activity 4% (low)
Factor IX, XI, XII activities: normal
Factor VIII inhibitor (Bethesda) assay: 18 Bethesda units (positive)
Lupus anticoagulant / dRVVT: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on a non-correcting (time-dependent) mixing study, low factor VIII, and a positive Bethesda inhibitor with a negative lupus anticoagulant, what is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A time-dependent, non-correcting mixing study with a low factor VIII and a positive Bethesda assay (anti-factor VIII autoantibody) in an older adult with new bleeding defines acquired hemophilia A. Congenital hemophilia A would present from childhood and would correct on mixing (deficiency, not inhibitor). Antiphospholipid syndrome can prolong the PTT via lupus anticoagulant, but that causes thrombosis (not bleeding), and the lupus anticoagulant is negative here. DIC features consumptive coagulopathy with prolonged PT, low fibrinogen, low platelets, and schistocytes—none present. Acquired hemophilia is often idiopathic but may be associated with malignancy, autoimmune disease, or the postpartum state, warranting a search for underlying causes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has active significant bleeding with a high-titer factor VIII inhibitor (18 BU). What is the most appropriate management?
options: High-dose factor VIII concentrate or desmopressin alone
A bypassing agent (e.g., recombinant factor VIIa or activated prothrombin complex concentrate) plus immunosuppression
Vitamin K and fresh frozen plasma
stages.explanation: For active bleeding with a high-titer inhibitor, hemostasis is achieved with a bypassing agent—recombinant factor VIIa or activated prothrombin complex concentrate (aPCC/FEIBA)—which works around the neutralized factor VIII. Concurrently, immunosuppression (corticosteroids ± cyclophosphamide or rituximab) is started to eradicate the autoantibody. High-dose factor VIII or DDAVP is generally ineffective against a high-titer inhibitor because the antibody neutralizes it. Platelet transfusion does not address a factor inhibitor. Vitamin K and FFP treat deficiencies of vitamin K–dependent factors (prolonged PT) and will not overcome a factor VIII autoantibody. Treating bleeding and eradicating the inhibitor are parallel priorities.

cases.diagnosis: Acquired hemophilia A (factor VIII inhibitor)
pearls: New bleeding in an older adult with no prior bleeding history and an isolated prolonged PTT should raise suspicion for an acquired factor inhibitor.
An incubated mixing study that fails to correct (time-dependent) indicates an inhibitor; confirm with factor VIII level and Bethesda titer.
A negative lupus anticoagulant plus a positive Bethesda assay distinguishes acquired hemophilia A from antiphospholipid syndrome.
Treat active bleeding with bypassing agents (rFVIIa or aPCC) and eradicate the antibody with immunosuppression; search for associated malignancy/autoimmunity.

cases.title: Bleeding From the Umbilicus in a Newborn
cases.chiefComplaint: Oozing from the umbilical stump and blood in the stool in a several-day-old infant.
cases.history: A 6-day-old male neonate is brought in for persistent oozing from the umbilical stump and a small amount of blood noted in the stool. He was born at home at term to a 28-year-old mother after an uncomplicated pregnancy and spontaneous vaginal delivery; the family declined the routine newborn injections and eye ointment, and the infant did not receive vitamin K prophylaxis at birth. He is exclusively breastfed and has been feeding adequately, though the mother reports he seems a bit sleepier today. There has been no fever, vomiting, or diarrhea. The mother took no anticoagulants, antiepileptics, or antibiotics during pregnancy and has no bleeding disorder; family history is negative for hemophilia or other inherited bleeding conditions. There were no birth complications and no instrumentation. The infant has had no surgeries. On further history, there has also been some bleeding after a heel-stick. Review of systems is otherwise limited by age but notable for the bleeding and mild lethargy, raising concern for possible intracranial involvement.
cases.exam: Term neonate, mildly lethargic but arousable, with slow oozing from the umbilical stump and a small ecchymosis at a recent heel-stick site. The anterior fontanelle is soft and flat. There is no jaundice and the skin is otherwise without petechiae or a generalized rash. The abdomen is soft and nondistended without hepatosplenomegaly. Mucous membranes are moist. There is a small amount of dark blood in the diaper. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. Neurologic exam shows normal tone with no bulging fontanelle or focal deficits at this time, but lethargy warrants close monitoring.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single historical detail most strongly explains this bleeding presentation?
options: Omission of vitamin K prophylaxis at birth
stages.explanation: Omission of vitamin K prophylaxis is the key historical detail: neonates are born with low vitamin K stores, and without the routine intramuscular vitamin K injection they cannot adequately carboxylate the vitamin K–dependent clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X), causing hemorrhagic disease of the newborn (vitamin K deficiency bleeding). Exclusive breastfeeding compounds the risk because breast milk is low in vitamin K, but it is a contributing factor rather than the primary cause. A home birth matters only insofar as prophylaxis was missed. Male sex is relevant for X-linked hemophilia, which is excluded by the negative family history and the multifactorial bleeding pattern explained more simply by missing vitamin K.

stages.label: COAGULATION LABS
stages.content:
CBC: Hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL, WBC 9.5 ×10³/µL, Platelets 280 ×10³/µL
PT 32 sec (markedly prolonged)
PTT 70 sec (prolonged)
Fibrinogen 250 mg/dL (normal)
Thrombin time: normal
Peripheral smear: normal, no schistocytes

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The PT is markedly prolonged with a prolonged PTT, normal platelets, normal fibrinogen, and a normal thrombin time. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Vitamin K deficiency bleeding of the newborn
stages.explanation: Prolongation of both the PT (most sensitive—reflects factor VII, the shortest half-life vitamin K–dependent factor) and PTT, with normal platelets, fibrinogen, and thrombin time in an unsupplemented neonate, is classic for vitamin K deficiency bleeding. DIC would show low platelets, low fibrinogen, and schistocytes from consumption—absent here. Hemophilia A causes an isolated prolonged PTT (normal PT) and would not prolong the PT. ITP causes isolated thrombocytopenia, but the platelet count is normal. The combined factor deficiency affecting both extrinsic and common pathways (II, VII, IX, X) explains the dual PT/PTT prolongation, with PT affected first and most.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate treatment for this actively bleeding neonate?
options: Parenteral (IV/IM) vitamin K, plus fresh frozen plasma for active bleeding
Observation and re-checking labs in 24 hours
stages.explanation: An actively bleeding neonate with vitamin K deficiency should receive parenteral vitamin K (IV or IM) to replenish the cofactor needed for factor carboxylation, plus fresh frozen plasma to immediately replace the deficient clotting factors and stop active bleeding, since vitamin K alone takes hours to correct the PT. Oral vitamin K is too slow and unreliable for active bleeding. Platelet transfusion is inappropriate because the platelet count is normal and the defect is in coagulation factors. Observation is unsafe given active bleeding and the risk of catastrophic intracranial hemorrhage in late/severe vitamin K deficiency bleeding; prompt correction is essential.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: What single intervention would have prevented this presentation?
options: Maternal vitamin K supplementation during pregnancy alone
A single intramuscular dose of vitamin K given to the newborn at birth
Switching the infant to formula feeding
Routine fresh frozen plasma at birth
stages.explanation: A single intramuscular dose of vitamin K administered to every newborn at birth is the standard, highly effective prevention for vitamin K deficiency bleeding, including its early, classic, and late forms. Maternal supplementation during pregnancy alone does not reliably raise neonatal vitamin K stores because vitamin K crosses the placenta poorly. Switching to formula increases dietary vitamin K but is not the recommended preventive strategy and does not address the immediate neonatal deficiency at birth. Routine FFP at birth is unnecessary, carries transfusion risks, and is not a prevention strategy. The IM injection is the simple, evidence-based intervention that was missed in this home birth.

cases.diagnosis: Vitamin K deficiency bleeding of the newborn (hemorrhagic disease of the newborn)
pearls: Neonates have low vitamin K stores; missing the routine IM vitamin K injection (especially with breastfeeding) causes vitamin K deficiency bleeding.
Vitamin K deficiency prolongs BOTH PT and PTT (PT first and most) with normal platelets, fibrinogen, and thrombin time.
Active bleeding requires parenteral vitamin K PLUS fresh frozen plasma for immediate factor replacement.
A single IM dose of vitamin K at birth prevents the disease; late vitamin K deficiency bleeding can cause fatal intracranial hemorrhage.

cases.title: A Swollen, Painful Leg in a Young Woman on the Pill
cases.chiefComplaint: Painful swelling of one calf that has worsened over several days.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman presents with 4 days of progressive pain, swelling, and warmth in her left calf. She has had no recent surgery, no prolonged immobilization beyond a desk job, and no long-haul travel. She started a combined estrogen-progestin oral contraceptive about 8 months ago. She has never been pregnant and has had no prior clots. She takes no other medications, does not smoke, and has no known malignancy, recent trauma, or central lines. She has no known drug allergies. Family history is notable: her father had a deep vein thrombosis in his 40s and a paternal aunt had a pulmonary embolism, suggesting a possible inherited thrombophilia with an autosomal pattern. She is otherwise healthy and active. Review of systems is negative for chest pain, shortness of breath, hemoptysis, or syncope, and she denies fevers, recent illness, or constitutional symptoms. She is concerned because her father's clot was 'in the family.'
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing young woman in mild discomfort. The left lower extremity is swollen with a 3 cm calf-circumference difference compared with the right, with warmth, mild erythema, and tenderness along the deep venous distribution; Homans sign is present but nonspecific. Distal pulses are intact and capillary refill is normal. The right leg is normal. There is no skin ulceration, no varicosities, and no cords. The lungs are clear and the cardiac exam is normal with no right-heart strain signs. The abdomen is benign. There is no lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her presentation suggests a deep vein thrombosis. What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Thrombophilia gene panel before any imaging
Compression ultrasound (venous duplex) of the leg
stages.explanation: Compression (duplex) ultrasonography of the leg is the first-line, noninvasive test to confirm a suspected DVT; loss of vein compressibility indicates thrombosis. A thrombophilia gene panel does not diagnose the acute clot and, importantly, is not done during the acute event or while on anticoagulation because results can be misleading—testing is deferred. CT pulmonary angiography evaluates pulmonary embolism, which is not the current presentation (no respiratory symptoms). A bone marrow biopsy has no role in diagnosing a DVT. Confirm the clot first with ultrasound, then treat, and consider thrombophilia evaluation later under appropriate conditions.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Venous duplex: noncompressible left femoral and popliteal veins with acute thrombus (proximal DVT)
CBC: Hemoglobin 13.0 g/dL, WBC 7.0 ×10³/µL, Platelets 250 ×10³/µL
PT 12 sec / PTT 30 sec (normal)
D-dimer: elevated
Pregnancy test: negative

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Considering her age, the relatively unprovoked clot (only risk factor is the oral contraceptive), and the strong family history, which inherited thrombophilia is the most common and the leading suspect?
options: Factor V Leiden (activated protein C resistance)
stages.explanation: Factor V Leiden is by far the most common inherited thrombophilia, especially in individuals of European descent, and is the leading suspect for an unprovoked/estrogen-associated venous thrombosis with a positive family history. It results from a point mutation making factor Va resistant to cleavage by activated protein C (APC resistance), promoting clot formation. Antithrombin and protein C deficiencies cause thrombophilia but are far less common. The prothrombin G20210A mutation is the second most common inherited thrombophilia but still less prevalent than factor V Leiden. The combination of estrogen exposure and a heritable APC-resistant state markedly elevates thrombotic risk.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: After completing anticoagulation, testing is performed. Which result would confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: An abnormal activated protein C resistance assay confirmed by factor V Leiden genetic testing
stages.explanation: Factor V Leiden is confirmed by demonstrating activated protein C (APC) resistance on a functional assay, with confirmation by genetic testing for the factor V Leiden (R506Q) mutation. A positive lupus anticoagulant indicates antiphospholipid syndrome, a different (acquired) thrombophilia. Low antithrombin activity would indicate antithrombin deficiency, and elevated homocysteine suggests hyperhomocysteinemia—both are alternative causes but not the most common and not what the APC resistance/family pattern points to here. Remember thrombophilia testing should be deferred until after the acute event and off anticoagulation (which can distort functional assays), so timing matters for accurate interpretation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond anticoagulating the acute DVT, what is the most important additional counseling point?
options: She can safely continue her combined oral contraceptive
She should discontinue the estrogen-containing contraceptive and use a non-estrogen method
She requires lifelong high-intensity anticoagulation regardless of risk factors
She should avoid all future pregnancies
stages.explanation: She should discontinue the estrogen-containing oral contraceptive and switch to a non-estrogen method (e.g., progestin-only or a copper IUD), because exogenous estrogen markedly compounds the thrombotic risk in factor V Leiden and removing it is a key, modifiable prevention step. Continuing the combined pill would be unsafe after an estrogen-provoked clot in a thrombophilic patient. Lifelong high-intensity anticoagulation is not mandated for a single provoked event in a heterozygous carrier; duration is individualized. Avoiding all pregnancies is incorrect—pregnancy is possible with appropriate thromboprophylaxis and counseling. The central, actionable point is eliminating the estrogen exposure.

cases.diagnosis: Factor V Leiden thrombophilia
pearls: Factor V Leiden is the most common inherited thrombophilia; factor Va resists cleavage by activated protein C (APC resistance).
Suspect it in young patients with unprovoked or estrogen-associated VTE and a positive family history.
Confirm with an APC resistance assay and factor V Leiden genetic testing—but defer thrombophilia testing until after the acute event and off anticoagulation.
Stopping estrogen-containing contraceptives is a critical modifiable risk-reduction step in affected patients.

cases.title: Repeated Pregnancy Loss and a New Clot
cases.chiefComplaint: A new leg clot in a woman with a history of multiple miscarriages.
cases.history: A 33-year-old woman is admitted with a confirmed proximal deep vein thrombosis. Her history is notable for three pregnancy losses—two first-trimester miscarriages and one second-trimester fetal demise—and an episode of superficial thrombophlebitis 2 years ago. She has no recent surgery, immobilization, or long travel, and she is not currently pregnant. She takes no estrogen-containing contraceptives currently and no other medications. She has occasional joint aches and a faint facial rash with sun exposure, but no formal autoimmune diagnosis. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke. Family history includes a mother with lupus. Review of systems is positive for mild fatigue and intermittent livedo-like skin mottling, but negative for chest pain, dyspnea, hemoptysis, neurologic deficits, or active bleeding. Of note, a basic preoperative coagulation panel several months ago had shown an unexplained prolonged PTT that was attributed to a lab artifact at the time.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in mild discomfort. The right lower extremity is swollen with calf tenderness and warmth consistent with the confirmed DVT; distal pulses are intact. The skin shows faint livedo reticularis over the thighs and a subtle malar erythema. There are no petechiae, no active bleeding, and no oral ulcers seen today. There is no synovitis on joint exam, no lymphadenopathy, and no hepatosplenomegaly. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal with no signs of right-heart strain. The neurologic exam is nonfocal. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of features best frames the unifying diagnosis to pursue?
options: Isolated DVT with no other clues
Recurrent pregnancy loss plus venous thrombosis plus an unexplained prolonged PTT
Bleeding tendency with a prolonged PTT
stages.explanation: The unifying triad to pursue is recurrent pregnancy loss, venous (or arterial) thrombosis, and a paradoxically prolonged PTT—classic for antiphospholipid syndrome. The prolonged PTT here is not from a bleeding tendency; antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant) interfere with phospholipid-dependent in-vitro clotting assays, prolonging the PTT, yet clinically cause thrombosis, not bleeding. Framing this as an isolated DVT misses the obstetric history and prior abnormal PTT that tie the picture together. A bleeding tendency is the wrong interpretation of the prolonged PTT. Thrombocytosis with arterial thrombosis suggests a myeloproliferative disorder, which does not fit. The combination strongly points to an acquired thrombophilia.

stages.label: COAGULATION LABS
stages.content:
CBC: Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, WBC 6.5 ×10³/µL, Platelets 110 ×10³/µL (mild thrombocytopenia)
PT 12 sec (normal)
PTT 52 sec (prolonged)
Mixing study: PTT fails to correct with 1:1 normal plasma (inhibitor pattern)
Dilute Russell viper venom time (dRVVT): prolonged, with correction on addition of excess phospholipid
Venous duplex: confirmed proximal DVT

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: The PTT does not correct on mixing, but adding excess phospholipid (dRVVT) corrects it. What does this pattern indicate?
stages.explanation: Failure to correct on a 1:1 mixing study indicates an inhibitor rather than a deficiency, and correction with excess phospholipid (the phospholipid-neutralization/dRVVT confirmation) identifies it as a phospholipid-dependent inhibitor—the lupus anticoagulant. A specific factor deficiency would correct on mixing. Heparin contamination would prolong the PTT and the thrombin time and is excluded by the phospholipid-dependence pattern. A factor VIII autoantibody (acquired hemophilia) causes bleeding with a time-dependent inhibitor and a low factor VIII, not a phospholipid-correctable inhibitor with thrombosis. The phospholipid-dependence is the signature that distinguishes lupus anticoagulant from other inhibitors.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is required to formally confirm the diagnosis of antiphospholipid syndrome?
options: A single positive lupus anticoagulant test at the time of clot
Persistent antibody positivity (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, or anti-β2-glycoprotein I) on testing repeated at least 12 weeks apart, plus a clinical event
stages.explanation: Antiphospholipid syndrome requires both a clinical criterion (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity) AND persistent laboratory positivity for at least one antiphospholipid antibody—lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, or anti-β2-glycoprotein I—confirmed on two occasions at least 12 weeks apart. A single positive test can be transient (e.g., from infection) and is insufficient. A positive ANA suggests lupus but does not diagnose APS. An elevated D-dimer merely reflects active clot and is nonspecific. The 12-week persistence requirement avoids overdiagnosis from transient antibodies, and the clinical-plus-laboratory combination is essential for the formal diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her antiphospholipid antibodies are persistently positive and she has confirmed thrombosis. What is the most appropriate long-term anticoagulation?
options: A direct oral anticoagulant (e.g., rivaroxaban) as first-line
Warfarin (vitamin K antagonist) with a target INR of 2–3
No anticoagulation, only avoidance of estrogen
stages.explanation: For thrombotic antiphospholipid syndrome, long-term warfarin (a vitamin K antagonist) targeting an INR of 2–3 is the preferred anticoagulant. Direct oral anticoagulants are NOT recommended as first-line in APS—particularly in triple-positive or arterial disease—because trials showed higher recurrent thrombosis rates compared with warfarin. Aspirin alone is inadequate for established venous thrombosis in APS (though it is used for obstetric APS with low-dose aspirin plus heparin during pregnancy). No anticoagulation is unsafe given a confirmed clot and persistent high-risk antibodies. Note the lupus anticoagulant can interfere with INR monitoring, so chromogenic factor X levels may be needed to guide warfarin dosing.

cases.diagnosis: Antiphospholipid syndrome
pearls: Suspect antiphospholipid syndrome with the triad of thrombosis, recurrent pregnancy loss, and a paradoxically prolonged PTT.
Lupus anticoagulant prolongs the PTT in vitro (no correction on mixing, corrects with excess phospholipid) but causes thrombosis, not bleeding, in vivo.
Diagnosis requires a clinical event PLUS persistently positive antibody testing repeated at least 12 weeks apart.
Long-term anticoagulation is warfarin (INR 2–3); DOACs are not first-line, especially in triple-positive or arterial APS.

cases.title: Painful Swollen Leg After a Long Flight
cases.chiefComplaint: Right calf pain and swelling for two days.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents with two days of progressive aching pain and swelling of the right lower leg. Symptoms began the day after he returned from a 14-hour intercontinental flight. He denies chest pain, dyspnea, hemoptysis, or syncope. There has been no recent trauma, surgery, immobilization beyond the flight, or prior clots. He has not noticed fevers or skin breakdown. Past medical history includes hypertension and class I obesity (BMI 32). Medications are amlodipine 5 mg daily and a daily multivitamin; he takes no aspirin or anticoagulants. He has no known drug allergies. He works as an accountant, drinks two beers on weekends, and has never smoked. He is married with two children. Family history is notable for a brother who had a deep vein thrombosis at age 60 after knee surgery; there is no history of recurrent or unprovoked clots, miscarriage, or known thrombophilia. He has had no unintentional weight loss, night sweats, or change in bowel habits.
cases.exam: Comfortable, in no distress. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal with clear lungs and no tachypnea. The right calf is swollen with a circumference 4 cm greater than the left measured 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity. There is pitting edema, warmth, and erythema over the right calf with tenderness along the deep venous tract. Homans sign is present but nonspecific. Distal pulses are intact and capillary refill is normal. There is no cord, no superficial varicosity thrombophlebitis, and no ulceration. The left leg is normal. No inguinal lymphadenopathy or palpable abdominal mass.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the history and exam, what is the most appropriate next step to risk-stratify this patient for deep vein thrombosis?
options: Order an immediate D-dimer as the sole determinant of need for imaging
Calculate the Wells score for DVT to guide testing
Proceed directly to CT pulmonary angiography
Empirically start full-dose anticoagulation and discharge
stages.explanation: The Wells score for DVT stratifies pretest probability and dictates whether to use D-dimer or move straight to ultrasound. This patient has unilateral swelling, calf tenderness, >3 cm calf asymmetry, and pitting edema, scoring as likely DVT. Calculating Wells first is the correct framework. A D-dimer alone is not a standalone rule-in test and is only useful in low-probability patients; using it as the sole determinant is wrong. CT pulmonary angiography evaluates PE, not leg DVT, and he has no pulmonary symptoms. Empirically anticoagulating and discharging without confirming the diagnosis exposes him to bleeding risk without a documented indication and is premature.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His Wells score places him in the 'DVT likely' category. What is the best confirmatory test?
options: D-dimer; if negative, no further workup
Contrast venography of the lower extremity
Compression ultrasound of the right lower extremity
stages.explanation: In a patient who is 'DVT likely' by Wells, compression ultrasound is the first-line confirmatory test; it is noninvasive, sensitive for proximal DVT, and widely available. A negative D-dimer cannot exclude DVT in a high-probability patient, so relying on it here is incorrect. Contrast venography is the historical gold standard but is invasive, requires contrast, and is now reserved for rare equivocal cases. MR venography is useful for pelvic or iliac vein evaluation when ultrasound is inadequate but is not the initial test for a suspected femoropopliteal clot.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CBC: WBC 7.8 ×10³/µL, Hgb 14.6 g/dL, Platelets 245 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >90)
INR 1.0, aPTT 30 s
Compression ultrasound right leg: noncompressible right popliteal vein with intraluminal echogenic thrombus; absent flow on Doppler
Left leg ultrasound: normal, fully compressible veins
CXR: clear lungs, no infiltrate

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Ultrasound confirms an acute proximal DVT. He has normal renal function and no active bleeding. What is the preferred initial anticoagulation strategy?
options: Unfractionated heparin infusion with bridge to warfarin only
A direct oral anticoagulant such as rivaroxaban or apixaban
stages.explanation: For acute proximal DVT in a hemodynamically stable patient with good renal function and no contraindications, a direct oral anticoagulant (apixaban or rivaroxaban) is the preferred first-line therapy per current guidelines; both have single-drug regimens with no required parenteral lead-in. An IVC filter is reserved for patients who cannot be anticoagulated, not as primary therapy. Unfractionated heparin bridged to warfarin is an acceptable but more cumbersome legacy approach and is not preferred when a DOAC is appropriate. Aspirin alone is inadequate treatment for acute VTE and is only considered for extended secondary prevention in select cases.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His DVT was provoked by a transient major risk factor (long-haul travel) and he has no thrombophilia or prior clots. What is the recommended duration of anticoagulation?
options: Lifelong because of his brother's history
stages.explanation: A DVT provoked by a transient, reversible risk factor warrants a finite course of approximately three months, after which anticoagulation is stopped if no ongoing risk persists. Indefinite therapy is reserved for unprovoked or recurrent VTE, or clots provoked by persistent factors such as active cancer; applying it to all DVTs causes unnecessary bleeding risk. Six weeks is too short and is associated with higher recurrence. A single family history of a provoked clot in a sibling does not justify lifelong anticoagulation, as it is not a strong unprovoked-thrombophilia signal in this scenario.

cases.diagnosis: Provoked deep vein thrombosis of the right popliteal vein (travel-associated)
pearls: Use the Wells DVT score first: 'DVT likely' goes straight to compression ultrasound, while 'DVT unlikely' can be triaged with a high-sensitivity D-dimer.
DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban) are first-line for most acute proximal DVT in stable patients with adequate renal function.
VTE provoked by a transient major risk factor is treated for ~3 months; unprovoked or cancer-associated VTE warrants extended/indefinite therapy.
IVC filters are for patients with an absolute contraindication to anticoagulation, not as a substitute for it.

cases.title: Sudden Breathlessness and Sharp Chest Pain After Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden shortness of breath and sharp chest pain that worsens with breathing.
cases.history: A 48-year-old woman presents with abrupt onset of dyspnea and right-sided pleuritic chest pain that began this morning. She is postoperative day 6 from a total abdominal hysterectomy for fibroids and was discharged on day 3. Since then she has been largely sedentary at home recovering. She reports mild right calf discomfort over the past day. She denies fever, productive cough, or recent infection, but notes one episode of lightheadedness when standing. Past medical history includes obesity and menorrhagia. Medications are oxycodone-acetaminophen as needed and a combined oral contraceptive she resumed after surgery. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol rarely. She works as a teacher. Family history is unremarkable for clotting disorders, and she has had no prior thrombosis or miscarriages. She denies hemoptysis at home but felt a few drops of blood-tinged sputum on the way in. There has been no recent long-distance travel, weight loss, or night sweats.
cases.exam: Anxious and mildly tachypneic, speaking in full sentences. JVP is not elevated. Heart is tachycardic and regular with a loud P2 but no murmur or rub. Lungs are clear bilaterally without crackles or wheeze; there is reproducible chest wall tenderness only minimally. The right calf is mildly swollen and tender compared with the left, with about 2 cm of asymmetry. No leg erythema or cords. There is no peripheral edema, cyanosis, or accessory muscle use at rest. Abdominal incision is clean, dry, and intact without surrounding erythema or drainage. No focal neurologic deficit.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given her postoperative state and presentation, which scoring tool best estimates her pretest probability of pulmonary embolism?
stages.explanation: The Wells score for PE stratifies pretest probability and guides whether to obtain a D-dimer or proceed to imaging. She has tachycardia, recent surgery (immobilization within 4 weeks), clinical signs of DVT, and PE as the most likely diagnosis, placing her in a high-probability group. CHA2DS2-VASc estimates stroke risk in atrial fibrillation, not PE. CURB-65 grades community-acquired pneumonia severity, which is not her syndrome. The HEART score risk-stratifies undifferentiated chest pain for acute coronary syndrome, not venous thromboembolism, and would misdirect the workup here.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her Wells score categorizes her as 'PE likely' (high probability). What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Send a D-dimer and discharge if negative
Order a transthoracic echocardiogram as the definitive test
stages.explanation: In a 'PE likely' patient, CT pulmonary angiography is the imaging test of choice to confirm or exclude PE directly. A D-dimer is intended to rule out PE in low/intermediate-probability patients; in a high-probability patient it should not be used to defer imaging, and a negative result would not be reassuring enough to discharge. Echocardiography may reveal right heart strain and aids risk stratification but is not the definitive diagnostic test for PE. A leg ultrasound alone could miss PE if negative and would not establish the pulmonary diagnosis, so CTPA is preferred.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CBC: WBC 9.1 ×10³/µL, Hgb 12.8 g/dL, Platelets 260 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL (eGFR >90)
Troponin: mildly elevated at 0.06 ng/mL (ref <0.04)
BNP 180 pg/mL
ECG: sinus tachycardia, S1Q3T3 pattern, no ST elevation
CT pulmonary angiogram: filling defects in the right main and lobar pulmonary arteries; right ventricle:left ventricle diameter ratio >1.0 suggesting RV strain

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: The CTPA confirms PE with CT evidence of RV enlargement, and her troponin is elevated, but her blood pressure is 118/72 and she is perfusing well. How is her PE best classified?
options: Low-risk PE suitable for outpatient management
stages.explanation: She is normotensive but has both RV dysfunction (CT RV:LV >1.0, elevated BNP) and myocardial injury (elevated troponin), which defines submassive (intermediate-risk) PE. Massive (high-risk) PE requires hemodynamic instability such as sustained hypotension or shock, which she does not have. Low-risk PE has neither RV strain nor biomarker elevation and may be managed as outpatient; her positive markers exclude this category. Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension is a long-standing complication of organized thrombus with pulmonary hypertension, not an acute presentation like hers.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is hemodynamically stable with intermediate-risk PE. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Therapeutic anticoagulation with close monitoring for deterioration
Inferior vena cava filter without anticoagulation
stages.explanation: Hemodynamically stable intermediate-risk PE is treated with prompt therapeutic anticoagulation (e.g., LMWH or a DOAC) plus close monitoring, reserving rescue thrombolysis for those who decompensate. Up-front systemic thrombolysis is indicated for massive (high-risk) PE with hemodynamic instability; in submassive PE it increases major bleeding without clear mortality benefit and is not routine. An IVC filter without anticoagulation is only for patients who cannot be anticoagulated, which does not apply here. Observation with serial D-dimers is inappropriate once PE is confirmed and would leave a dangerous clot untreated. She should also stop her estrogen-containing contraceptive.

cases.diagnosis: Acute submassive (intermediate-risk) pulmonary embolism, postoperative/estrogen-provoked
pearls: S1Q3T3 on ECG is classic but neither sensitive nor specific; sinus tachycardia is the most common ECG finding in PE.
Use Wells PE: 'PE unlikely' permits D-dimer rule-out, while 'PE likely' goes directly to CT pulmonary angiography.
Submassive PE = normotensive but with RV strain (echo/CT) and/or positive troponin/BNP; manage with anticoagulation and watch for decompensation.
Reserve systemic thrombolysis for high-risk (massive) PE with hemodynamic instability; discontinue estrogen-containing contraceptives after a provoked VTE.

cases.title: Itching After Showers and Burning Red Hands
cases.chiefComplaint: Intense itching after warm showers and intermittent burning, red, painful hands and feet.
cases.history: A 62-year-old man presents to clinic with several months of severe generalized itching that predictably begins minutes after a warm shower or bath, without any rash. He also describes episodes of burning, throbbing pain and redness in his fingertips and toes that improve with cooling and aspirin. He reports headaches, dizziness, and a ruddy, plethoric facial complexion noted by his wife. He denies weight loss, fevers, or night sweats but has felt fatigued. Past medical history includes hypertension and a remote smoking history of 20 pack-years, quit ten years ago. Medications are lisinopril 10 mg daily; he takes occasional aspirin for the hand pain. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol socially. He works as a retired machinist. Family history includes a father with 'thick blood' requiring periodic bloodletting, otherwise noncontributory. On review of systems he notes blurred vision at times and a sensation of abdominal fullness with early satiety. He denies hematuria, melena, or recent dehydration.
cases.exam: Plethoric, ruddy facies with conjunctival suffusion. Oxygen saturation is normal on room air. No respiratory distress and lungs are clear; there is no clubbing or cyanosis. Cardiac exam is normal. The abdomen is soft with a palpable spleen tip 3 cm below the left costal margin and no hepatomegaly or ascites. The fingertips show erythema with normal pulses and capillary refill, without ulceration or digital ischemia at rest. There is no lymphadenopathy. Skin shows excoriations from scratching but no primary rash, petechiae, or purpura. Neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His CBC shows an elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit. What is the most useful initial step to distinguish a primary from a secondary erythrocytosis?
options: Order a sleep study for obstructive sleep apnea
Perform a bone marrow biopsy immediately
stages.explanation: A serum EPO level efficiently separates primary from secondary causes: in polycythemia vera, autonomous clonal red cell production suppresses EPO to low or low-normal levels, whereas secondary erythrocytosis (hypoxia, tumors, smoking) drives a high EPO. Checking EPO first focuses the workup. A sleep study and carboxyhemoglobin pursue secondary hypoxic causes that are far less likely given his pruritus, erythromelalgia, splenomegaly, and plethora. A bone marrow biopsy is part of confirming PV but is not the most useful first discriminating step; serologic and molecular testing typically precede or accompany it.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
CBC: Hgb 19.8 g/dL, Hematocrit 59%, WBC 12.4 ×10³/µL, Platelets 520 ×10³/µL
Serum erythropoietin: 2 mU/mL (low; ref 4-26)
O2 saturation 97% on room air
LDH 260 U/L (mildly elevated)
Iron studies: low ferritin
JAK2 V617F mutation: positive
Uric acid 8.1 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With an elevated red cell mass, low EPO, splenomegaly, and a positive JAK2 V617F mutation, what is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Low EPO, erythrocytosis, splenomegaly, and a JAK2 V617F mutation satisfy the criteria for polycythemia vera, a clonal myeloproliferative neoplasm; aquagenic pruritus and erythromelalgia are characteristic. Secondary polycythemia would show a high EPO driven by hypoxia, and his saturation is normal. Relative polycythemia reflects plasma volume contraction with normal red cell mass and would not cause a JAK2 mutation or splenomegaly. Chronic myeloid leukemia is driven by BCR-ABL with marked leukocytosis and basophilia, not isolated erythrocytosis with low EPO and JAK2 positivity, and would lack aquagenic pruritus as the dominant feature.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is 62 years old with no prior thrombosis. What is the cornerstone of initial therapy to reduce his thrombotic risk?
options: Therapeutic phlebotomy to a hematocrit goal below 45% plus low-dose aspirin
Iron supplementation to correct the low ferritin
stages.explanation: The foundation of PV management is therapeutic phlebotomy targeting a hematocrit below 45%, combined with low-dose aspirin, which together reduce thrombotic events. Stem cell transplantation is not first-line for PV and is reserved for transformation to myelofibrosis or acute leukemia. Corticosteroids have no role in controlling the clonal erythrocytosis. Iron supplementation is contraindicated because the iron deficiency from phlebotomy actually limits red cell production; giving iron would worsen the erythrocytosis. Cytoreduction such as hydroxyurea is added for high-risk patients (age >60 or prior thrombosis), as in this man.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is the leading cause of morbidity and mortality in polycythemia vera and the main reason for aggressive hematocrit control?
stages.explanation: Thrombosis, both arterial (stroke, MI) and venous (including unusual sites such as the hepatic veins causing Budd-Chiari), is the leading cause of morbidity and mortality in PV, driven by hyperviscosity and increased platelet activity, which is why hematocrit control and aspirin are central. Hemolytic anemia is not a feature of PV. Tumor lysis syndrome occurs with treatment of bulky, rapidly proliferating malignancies, not chronic PV. Pulmonary fibrosis is unrelated; the late marrow complication of PV is progression to myelofibrosis or, less commonly, acute leukemia rather than lung fibrosis.

cases.diagnosis: Polycythemia vera (JAK2 V617F-positive)
pearls: Aquagenic pruritus after warm water plus erythromelalgia and plethora is a classic triad pointing to polycythemia vera.
Low serum EPO supports a primary erythrocytosis; high EPO suggests a secondary (hypoxic or tumor-driven) cause.
JAK2 V617F is present in ~95% of PV cases; treat with phlebotomy to Hct <45% plus low-dose aspirin, adding hydroxyurea for high-risk patients.
Thrombosis is the chief cause of death in PV; never give iron to 'correct' the phlebotomy-induced iron deficiency.

cases.title: An Alarmingly High Platelet Count with Tingling Hands
cases.chiefComplaint: Burning, tingling hands and headaches found to have a very high platelet count.
cases.history: A 57-year-old woman is referred after routine bloodwork showed a markedly elevated platelet count. She describes intermittent burning and tingling in her hands and feet, occasional throbbing redness of her fingertips relieved by aspirin, and recurrent headaches with transient visual disturbances. She denies fevers, weight loss, night sweats, bleeding, or recent illness. She has had no prior thrombotic or bleeding events and is not pregnant. Past medical history includes hypothyroidism and iron-deficiency anemia treated successfully two years ago. Medications are levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She is a nonsmoker, drinks wine occasionally, and works as a librarian. Family history is unremarkable for blood disorders or clotting. On review of systems she notes mild dizziness and no recent infection, trauma, or surgery. Her last colonoscopy and mammogram were normal. She has not had recent blood loss, and her menstrual periods stopped at age 51.
cases.exam: Well-appearing and in no distress. There is no pallor, jaundice, or rash. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. The abdomen is soft with a spleen tip just palpable below the left costal margin and no hepatomegaly. Fingertips show mild erythema without ulceration; pulses and capillary refill are normal. There is no lymphadenopathy, petechiae, ecchymoses, or active bleeding. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with normal visual fields on confrontation at the time of the visit. There is no evidence of inflammatory arthritis or infection that would explain a reactive process.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her platelet count is markedly elevated. Before labeling this a primary disorder, what must be excluded first?
options: Reactive (secondary) thrombocytosis from iron deficiency, infection, inflammation, or malignancy
stages.explanation: Markedly elevated platelets are far more often reactive than clonal, so secondary causes such as iron deficiency, infection, chronic inflammation, post-splenectomy state, and occult malignancy must be excluded before diagnosing essential thrombocythemia. Given her prior iron deficiency, rechecking iron studies and inflammatory markers is essential. Pseudothrombocytopenia describes a falsely low (not high) platelet count from EDTA-induced clumping and is irrelevant here. DIC causes consumption with a low platelet count and bleeding, the opposite picture. Hemophilia A is a clotting factor deficiency causing bleeding and does not affect the platelet count.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
CBC: Hgb 13.6 g/dL, WBC 8.9 ×10³/µL, Platelets 920 ×10³/µL
Peripheral smear: numerous large and giant platelets, occasional platelet clumps, no blasts
Iron studies: ferritin 95 ng/mL, normal transferrin saturation
CRP 2 mg/L (normal), ESR normal
BCR-ABL1: negative
JAK2 V617F: positive
LDH 215 U/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Iron studies and inflammatory markers are normal, BCR-ABL is negative, and JAK2 V617F is positive with persistent thrombocytosis and giant platelets. What is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Sustained thrombocytosis with large/giant platelets, a positive JAK2 V617F mutation, negative BCR-ABL, and exclusion of reactive causes establishes essential thrombocythemia, a clonal myeloproliferative neoplasm. Reactive thrombocytosis is excluded because iron studies and inflammatory markers are normal and the count is persistent with a driver mutation. Chronic myeloid leukemia requires BCR-ABL positivity, which is negative here. Polycythemia vera is characterized by erythrocytosis with an elevated hematocrit; her hemoglobin is normal, making PV unlikely. CALR or MPL mutations can be tested if JAK2 were negative.

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: She is 57 years old with no prior thrombosis but is JAK2-positive with vasomotor symptoms. How should her thrombotic risk and initial therapy be approached?
options: She is high risk by age >60 alone and needs cytoreduction immediately
She is intermediate/high risk given JAK2 positivity; start low-dose aspirin and consider cytoreduction such as hydroxyurea
No treatment is ever needed in ET
stages.explanation: Risk in ET is stratified by age, prior thrombosis, and JAK2 status (IPSET-thrombosis). At 57 without prior thrombosis but JAK2-positive with vasomotor symptoms, she is at increased risk; low-dose aspirin relieves microvascular symptoms and reduces thrombosis, and cytoreduction (hydroxyurea, or interferon in younger patients) is reasonable. She is not high risk by age alone, since high-risk age is typically >60. Saying no treatment is ever needed is incorrect because untreated high-risk ET carries thrombotic and hemorrhagic risk. Warfarin is not the standard preventive therapy for ET; antiplatelet therapy and cytoreduction are.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: At extremely high platelet counts (often >1,000-1,500 ×10³/µL), which paradoxical complication can occur and should be considered before reflexively adding aspirin?
options: Acquired von Willebrand syndrome causing bleeding
stages.explanation: Very high platelet counts can adsorb and degrade large von Willebrand factor multimers, producing an acquired von Willebrand syndrome with a paradoxical bleeding tendency; aspirin can worsen this, so VWF activity should be checked before antiplatelet therapy at extreme counts. Tumor lysis syndrome arises from rapid tumor cell turnover during treatment, not from chronic thrombocytosis. Hemochromatosis is iron overload, unrelated to platelet count. Hyperviscosity from red cell excess characterizes polycythemia vera, not essential thrombocythemia, where the dominant cell line is platelets rather than red cells.

cases.diagnosis: Essential thrombocythemia (JAK2 V617F-positive)
pearls: Always exclude reactive thrombocytosis (iron deficiency, infection, inflammation, malignancy, post-splenectomy) before diagnosing essential thrombocythemia.
ET driver mutations: JAK2 V617F (~50-60%), CALR (~20-30%), or MPL; BCR-ABL must be negative to exclude CML.
Risk-stratify with IPSET-thrombosis (age, prior thrombosis, JAK2); treat with low-dose aspirin and add hydroxyurea for high-risk disease.
Extreme thrombocytosis can cause acquired von Willebrand syndrome and bleeding — check VWF activity before starting aspirin at very high counts.

cases.title: A Heavy Belly, Drenching Sweats, and a Failed Marrow Tap
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive abdominal fullness, fatigue, weight loss, and drenching night sweats.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents with six months of worsening fatigue, early satiety, and a sensation of left-sided abdominal heaviness. He reports an unintentional 8 kg weight loss, drenching night sweats, and low-grade fevers. He bruises easily and feels short of breath on exertion. He denies overt bleeding, bone pain, or recent infection. Past medical history includes gout and osteoarthritis. Medications are allopurinol 300 mg daily and acetaminophen as needed. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired carpenter, a former smoker (15 pack-years, quit 20 years ago), and drinks minimal alcohol. Family history is noncontributory for hematologic malignancy. On review of systems he notes occasional bone aching and increasing abdominal girth that makes meals uncomfortable. He has had no prior chemotherapy, radiation, or known benzene exposure. He reports no recent travel, tick bites, or risk factors for chronic infection, and his prior CBCs from two years ago were reportedly normal.
cases.exam: Cachectic, pale man in no acute distress. Conjunctival pallor is present. There is no jaundice or rash. Cardiopulmonary exam reveals a soft flow murmur consistent with anemia and clear lungs. The abdomen is notable for massive splenomegaly extending well below the umbilicus and across the midline, firm and nontender, with no ascites; the liver edge is palpable 2 cm below the costal margin. There are scattered ecchymoses on the extremities. No significant lymphadenopathy. No focal neurologic deficit. Mild bony tenderness over the sternum on palpation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His CBC shows anemia and his smear is described below. Which peripheral blood finding most strongly suggests a marrow infiltrative/fibrotic process?
options: Teardrop cells (dacrocytes) with a leukoerythroblastic picture
stages.explanation: Teardrop-shaped red cells (dacrocytes) together with a leukoerythroblastic smear (immature myeloid cells and nucleated red blood cells in the periphery) indicate marrow stress from infiltration or fibrosis forcing premature cell release, the hallmark of myelofibrosis. Spherocytes with reticulocytosis suggest hemolysis (hereditary spherocytosis or immune hemolysis). Target cells and basophilic stippling point to liver disease, thalassemia, or lead exposure. Schistocytes with thrombocytopenia indicate a microangiopathic process such as TTP or DIC, not marrow fibrosis. The teardrop/leukoerythroblastic combination is the classic clue here.

stages.label: LABS & SMEAR
stages.content:
CBC: Hgb 9.2 g/dL, WBC 13.5 ×10³/µL, Platelets 110 ×10³/µL
Peripheral smear: numerous teardrop cells (dacrocytes), nucleated RBCs, occasional myelocytes and metamyelocytes (leukoerythroblastic)
LDH 540 U/L (elevated)
Uric acid 9.0 mg/dL
BCR-ABL1: negative
Bone marrow aspirate: 'dry tap' — unable to aspirate
JAK2 V617F: positive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The marrow aspiration yields a 'dry tap.' What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Repeat the aspiration at a different site only
Bone marrow biopsy (trephine core) with reticulin/collagen fibrosis staining
Flow cytometry of peripheral blood alone
stages.explanation: A 'dry tap' typically reflects marrow fibrosis that prevents aspiration; a trephine core biopsy with reticulin and collagen (trichrome) staining is required to demonstrate the fibrosis and confirm primary myelofibrosis, along with megakaryocyte atypia. Simply repeating aspiration at another site will likely fail again because the problem is fibrosis, not technique. Splenectomy is a palliative/therapeutic option for refractory symptoms, not a diagnostic procedure, and carries high risk. Peripheral blood flow cytometry helps characterize acute leukemias and lymphoproliferative disorders but cannot demonstrate marrow fibrosis or establish this diagnosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The biopsy shows marked reticulin fibrosis with atypical megakaryocytes; JAK2 is positive and BCR-ABL is negative. What is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Marrow fibrosis with atypical/clustered megakaryocytes, a leukoerythroblastic smear with teardrop cells, massive splenomegaly from extramedullary hematopoiesis, constitutional symptoms, a JAK2 mutation, and negative BCR-ABL define primary myelofibrosis. Chronic myeloid leukemia requires BCR-ABL positivity, which is absent. Polycythemia vera presents with erythrocytosis, not the cytopenias and fibrosis seen here, though PV can later transform to a fibrotic phase. Hairy cell leukemia causes pancytopenia and splenomegaly but shows characteristic 'hairy' B cells and a dry tap from marrow infiltration rather than reticulin fibrosis with teardrop cells and a leukoerythroblastic picture.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has symptomatic splenomegaly and constitutional symptoms with intermediate/high-risk disease. He is 68 and otherwise fit. What therapies are most appropriate to discuss?
options: A JAK inhibitor (e.g., ruxolitinib) for symptom/spleen control, with evaluation for allogeneic stem cell transplant as the only curative option
BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor such as imatinib
stages.explanation: JAK inhibitors such as ruxolitinib reduce spleen size and constitutional symptoms in myelofibrosis and improve quality of life, while allogeneic stem cell transplantation remains the only potentially curative therapy and should be considered in fit intermediate/high-risk patients. Phlebotomy and aspirin treat polycythemia vera, not the cytopenic, fibrotic myelofibrosis seen here. Imatinib targets BCR-ABL in CML and has no role in BCR-ABL-negative myelofibrosis. Pure watchful waiting is appropriate only for asymptomatic low-risk disease; this man's symptoms, splenomegaly, and risk category warrant active treatment and transplant evaluation.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which long-term complication of primary myelofibrosis carries the worst prognosis and must be monitored for over time?
stages.explanation: Primary myelofibrosis can undergo leukemic transformation to acute myeloid leukemia, which carries a dismal prognosis and is a key reason for risk stratification and surveillance. Iron-deficiency anemia is not the characteristic terminal complication; the anemia of myelofibrosis stems from marrow failure and ineffective hematopoiesis. Myelofibrosis does not spontaneously resolve into normal marrow. It does not evolve into polycythemia vera; rather, the reverse can occur, with PV or ET progressing into a secondary myelofibrotic phase. Vigilance for blast transformation is essential.

cases.diagnosis: Primary myelofibrosis (JAK2 V617F-positive)
pearls: Teardrop cells (dacrocytes) plus a leukoerythroblastic smear and massive splenomegaly should trigger suspicion for myelofibrosis.
A 'dry tap' on aspiration is classic for myelofibrosis — diagnosis requires a trephine core biopsy with reticulin/collagen staining.
Driver mutations are JAK2 (~50-60%), CALR, or MPL, with BCR-ABL negative; massive splenomegaly reflects extramedullary hematopoiesis.
Ruxolitinib palliates spleen and symptoms; allogeneic transplant is the only cure. Leukemic transformation to AML is the feared, fatal endpoint.

cases.title: Fatigue, a Full Belly, and a Sky-High White Count
cases.chiefComplaint: Fatigue, left-upper-quadrant fullness, and an incidentally found very high white blood cell count.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man presents after a life-insurance physical revealed a markedly elevated white blood cell count. He reports several weeks of fatigue, early satiety, a 4 kg unintentional weight loss, and occasional drenching night sweats. He has noticed left-upper-quadrant discomfort and fullness. He denies fevers, bleeding, bone pain, or recurrent infections. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes only a daily multivitamin and has no known drug allergies. He is married, works as a software engineer, has never smoked, and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory for malignancy. On review of systems he notes mild dyspnea on exertion and easy fatigability but no chest pain, lymph node swelling, or rash. He has had no recent infections, vaccinations, or corticosteroid use that would explain a reactive leukocytosis, and he denies any history of splenic trauma. He reports no priapism but mentions occasional flushing.
cases.exam: Well-developed man in no distress. Mild conjunctival pallor. No jaundice or rash. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal with clear lungs. The abdomen reveals marked splenomegaly with the spleen palpable 8 cm below the left costal margin, firm and nontender; the liver is not enlarged. There is no significant peripheral lymphadenopathy. No sternal tenderness. Skin shows no petechiae or purpura. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. There are no signs of leukostasis such as confusion or visual disturbance at this time.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His differential shows a marked leukocytosis with a left shift and basophilia. Which feature most strongly favors a clonal myeloproliferative neoplasm over a reactive leukemoid reaction?
