options: Presence of toxic granulation and Döhle bodies
Absolute basophilia with the full spectrum of myeloid maturation and splenomegaly
stages.explanation: Absolute basophilia, a continuum of maturing myeloid forms (myelocytes, metamyelocytes, bands, neutrophils), and marked splenomegaly strongly suggest chronic myeloid leukemia rather than a reactive leukemoid reaction. Toxic granulation and Döhle bodies are signs of a reactive/infectious leukemoid reaction. A high (elevated) leukocyte alkaline phosphatase score favors a reactive process, whereas CML classically shows a low LAP score. A predominance of mature lymphocytes points to a lymphoproliferative disorder such as CLL, not a myeloid neoplasm. Basophilia plus full myeloid maturation is the key discriminator here.

stages.label: LABS & SMEAR
stages.content:
CBC: WBC 145 ×10³/µL, Hgb 11.0 g/dL, Platelets 480 ×10³/µL
Differential: neutrophils with full spectrum of maturation, myelocytes, metamyelocytes, 2% blasts, basophils 8%, eosinophils 4%
Leukocyte alkaline phosphatase score: low
LDH 480 U/L, uric acid 8.5 mg/dL
Peripheral blood FISH: BCR-ABL1 fusion positive
Karyotype: t(9;22)(q34;q11) Philadelphia chromosome

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: FISH confirms a BCR-ABL1 fusion and cytogenetics show the Philadelphia chromosome. With <10% blasts in blood and marrow, what is the diagnosis and phase?
stages.explanation: BCR-ABL1/Philadelphia chromosome positivity with marked granulocytosis, basophilia, splenomegaly, and fewer than 10% blasts defines chronic-phase CML. Acute myeloid leukemia would show 20% or more blasts with maturation arrest, not the full myeloid spectrum seen here. Blast crisis is defined by 20% or more blasts in blood or marrow (or extramedullary blast proliferation); his 2% blasts place him firmly in chronic phase. A leukemoid reaction is BCR-ABL-negative with a high LAP score and an identifiable reactive cause, none of which fit this molecularly confirmed clonal disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment for chronic-phase CML that has transformed this from a fatal disease into a chronic, manageable one?
options: Allogeneic stem cell transplant as first-line for all patients
A BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor such as imatinib
stages.explanation: BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitors such as imatinib (or second-generation agents like dasatinib/nilotinib) are first-line therapy and have made chronic-phase CML a highly manageable disease with near-normal life expectancy in responders. Allogeneic transplant is no longer first-line; it is reserved for TKI-resistant disease or advanced phases because of its toxicity. Hydroxyurea may transiently lower counts but does not target the driving fusion and is not durable therapy. Splenectomy and interferon are largely historical and are not the standard of care now that targeted TKIs exist.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After starting a TKI, how is treatment response best monitored over time?
options: Quantitative BCR-ABL1 transcript levels by PCR with molecular milestones
Repeat bone marrow biopsy every week
stages.explanation: Response is tracked with quantitative RT-PCR for BCR-ABL1 transcripts on the international scale, aiming for hematologic, cytogenetic, and ultimately deep molecular milestones (e.g., major molecular response) at defined intervals. Leukocyte alkaline phosphatase is a crude diagnostic clue, not a monitoring tool. Weekly bone marrow biopsies are excessively invasive and unnecessary; marrow is used selectively, not routinely on that schedule. Daily CBCs indefinitely are impractical and do not capture molecular response; CBCs are followed but the definitive long-term monitor is the BCR-ABL transcript level.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic myeloid leukemia, chronic phase (BCR-ABL1/Philadelphia chromosome positive)
pearls: CML triad: marked neutrophilic leukocytosis with full myeloid maturation, basophilia, and splenomegaly.
A LOW leukocyte alkaline phosphatase score and BCR-ABL1/t(9;22) distinguish CML from a reactive leukemoid reaction.
First-line therapy is a BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor (imatinib); transplant is reserved for resistant or advanced-phase disease.
Monitor response with quantitative BCR-ABL1 PCR; rising transcripts or rising blasts (≥20%) signal progression to accelerated phase or blast crisis.

cases.title: Bruising, Fevers, and Strange Cells in the Blood
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of worsening fatigue, easy bruising, and fevers.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man is admitted with three weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising, gum bleeding when brushing his teeth, and intermittent fevers. He reports breathlessness climbing stairs and a recent gum infection that responded poorly to antibiotics. He denies weight loss, lymph node swelling, or bone pain at rest. Past medical history includes coronary artery disease and prior smoking. Medications are aspirin 81 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, and metoprolol. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired electrician, quit smoking 8 years ago after 30 pack-years, and drinks rarely. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no recent travel or known toxic exposures other than remote workplace solvents. On review of systems he notes dizziness on standing and pallor noticed by his daughter. He has had no prior chemotherapy or radiation and no known myelodysplastic syndrome, though a CBC a year ago was reportedly normal. He denies headache, confusion, or visual changes.
cases.exam: Pale, fatigued man who is alert and oriented. Multiple ecchymoses and scattered petechiae over the lower extremities. Gingival hypertrophy with oozing at the gumline. Conjunctival pallor present. Cardiopulmonary exam shows tachycardia with a soft systolic flow murmur and clear lungs. The abdomen is soft without marked hepatosplenomegaly. No significant lymphadenopathy. There is no focal neurologic deficit and no nuchal rigidity. Mild sternal tenderness on palpation. Oral mucosa shows a small area of candidiasis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His CBC shows pancytopenia with circulating immature cells. What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic step?
options: Review the peripheral smear and proceed to bone marrow aspiration/biopsy with flow cytometry and cytogenetics
Start empiric corticosteroids for presumed ITP
Order an abdominal CT to look for splenomegaly first
Transfuse platelets and discharge with outpatient follow-up
stages.explanation: Pancytopenia with circulating blasts mandates urgent peripheral smear review followed by bone marrow aspiration and biopsy with flow cytometry, cytogenetics, and molecular studies to diagnose and subtype an acute leukemia. Empiric steroids for ITP are inappropriate when pancytopenia and blasts point to marrow failure, not isolated immune platelet destruction. An abdominal CT for splenomegaly does not establish the diagnosis and delays definitive marrow evaluation. Transfusing platelets is supportive but discharging a febrile, pancytopenic patient with blasts is dangerous; he needs admission and an urgent diagnostic workup.

stages.label: LABS & SMEAR
stages.content:
CBC: WBC 2.8 ×10³/µL, Hgb 8.0 g/dL, Platelets 28 ×10³/µL
Differential: 30% circulating blasts
Peripheral smear: large blasts with prominent nucleoli and pink cytoplasmic rod-shaped inclusions (Auer rods)
Bone marrow: hypercellular with >20% myeloblasts; myeloperoxidase-positive blasts
Flow cytometry: CD13+, CD33+, CD34+, MPO+ blasts
Coagulation: PT, aPTT, fibrinogen normal; D-dimer mildly elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The marrow shows >20% myeloblasts with Auer rods that are myeloperoxidase-positive. What is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: 20% or more myeloblasts in the marrow with myeloperoxidase positivity and Auer rods (azurophilic crystalline granules) define acute myeloid leukemia. Acute lymphoblastic leukemia shows TdT-positive, MPO-negative lymphoblasts without Auer rods and is more common in children. Chronic myeloid leukemia in chronic phase shows mature granulocytosis with basophilia and fewer than 10% blasts, not a blast-predominant marrow. Myelodysplastic syndrome has dysplasia with fewer than 20% blasts; crossing the 20% threshold with Auer rods reclassifies the process as AML. The MPO-positive, Auer-rod-bearing blasts are the defining clue.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is febrile and neutropenic at diagnosis. Beyond planning induction chemotherapy, what is the most urgent supportive priority?
options: Begin broad-spectrum empiric antibiotics for neutropenic fever after cultures
Withhold antibiotics until a source is confirmed
Administer iron supplementation for the anemia
stages.explanation: Neutropenic fever is a medical emergency; after obtaining blood cultures, broad-spectrum empiric antibiotics (e.g., an antipseudomonal beta-lactam such as cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam) must be started promptly because untreated infection can be rapidly fatal in the absence of functioning neutrophils. Withholding antibiotics until a source is found is dangerous and contraindicated in febrile neutropenia. Prophylactic anticoagulation is inappropriate in a thrombocytopenic, bleeding-prone patient. Iron supplementation does not address the marrow failure causing the anemia and is not the urgent priority; transfusion support and infection control take precedence while leukemia-directed therapy is arranged.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before and during induction chemotherapy for AML, which metabolic emergency from rapid cell turnover must be anticipated and prevented?
stages.explanation: Tumor lysis syndrome results from massive release of intracellular contents during rapid leukemic cell death, producing hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hyperuricemia, and hypocalcemia, with risk of acute kidney injury and arrhythmia; it is prevented with aggressive hydration and hypouricemic therapy (allopurinol or rasburicase). Diabetic ketoacidosis, myxedema coma, and Addisonian crisis are endocrine emergencies unrelated to leukemic cell lysis. Anticipating and prophylaxing against tumor lysis is a core part of safely delivering induction chemotherapy, especially with a high tumor burden.

cases.diagnosis: Acute myeloid leukemia (MPO-positive blasts with Auer rods)
pearls: Auer rods are pathognomonic for myeloid blasts; MPO positivity confirms myeloid lineage and distinguishes AML from ALL.
AML is defined by ≥20% myeloblasts in blood or marrow; pancytopenia with circulating blasts demands urgent marrow biopsy with cytogenetics.
Neutropenic fever is an emergency: draw cultures, then start broad-spectrum empiric antibiotics immediately.
Anticipate tumor lysis syndrome with hydration and allopurinol/rasburicase before and during induction chemotherapy.

cases.title: Catastrophic Bleeding with Deranged Clotting Labs
cases.chiefComplaint: Spontaneous bruising, gum and nose bleeding, and heavy vaginal bleeding.
cases.history: A 39-year-old woman is admitted to the ICU with several days of worsening spontaneous bruising, epistaxis, gum bleeding, and now heavy vaginal bleeding. She also reports profound fatigue and dyspnea. Over the past two weeks she felt increasingly unwell with low-grade fevers. She denies recent trauma, anticoagulant use, or known liver disease. Past medical history is unremarkable and she takes only an oral contraceptive; no known drug allergies. She is a nonsmoker, a teacher, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems she notes lightheadedness and one episode of blood in her urine. She has had no recent infections requiring antibiotics, no prior chemotherapy or radiation, and no history of bleeding disorders or easy bruising before this illness. She is not pregnant. She has not traveled recently and has no risk factors for viral hepatitis. She reports no headache or focal weakness but feels her bleeding is rapidly worsening.
cases.exam: Pale, anxious woman with active oozing from venipuncture sites and the gingiva. Diffuse ecchymoses and petechiae over the trunk and extremities. Conjunctival pallor and scleral icterus absent. Tachycardic with a flow murmur; lungs clear. Abdomen soft without marked hepatosplenomegaly. There is active vaginal bleeding noted on exam. No focal neurologic deficit, but she is mildly confused. No nuchal rigidity. Fundoscopic exam shows a small retinal hemorrhage. The bleeding appears disproportionate to any local cause and is consistent with a systemic coagulopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her labs reveal pancytopenia with blasts plus a coagulopathy. Which laboratory pattern indicates the disseminated intravascular coagulation driving her bleeding?
options: Prolonged PT and aPTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, and falling platelets
Isolated prolonged aPTT that corrects with mixing
Elevated fibrinogen with a normal D-dimer
stages.explanation: DIC is characterized by consumption of clotting factors and platelets: prolonged PT and aPTT, a falling fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer/fibrin degradation products, and progressive thrombocytopenia, producing the bleeding seen here. Normal PT/aPTT with isolated thrombocytopenia suggests ITP, not a consumptive coagulopathy. An isolated prolonged aPTT that corrects with mixing suggests a factor deficiency, not DIC. Elevated fibrinogen with a normal D-dimer is the opposite of DIC, where fibrinogen falls and D-dimer rises. The combined factor and platelet consumption with high D-dimer is the signature of DIC.

stages.label: LABS & SMEAR
stages.content:
CBC: WBC 1.9 ×10³/µL, Hgb 7.6 g/dL, Platelets 18 ×10³/µL
Coagulation: PT 22 s, aPTT 48 s, fibrinogen 90 mg/dL (low), D-dimer markedly elevated
Peripheral smear: hypergranular promyelocytes, some with bundles of Auer rods (faggot cells)
Bone marrow: abnormal promyelocytes; MPO strongly positive
Flow cytometry: CD33+, CD13+, HLA-DR negative, CD34 often negative
Molecular: PML-RARA fusion / t(15;17) positive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The smear shows hypergranular promyelocytes with bundles of Auer rods and molecular testing reveals t(15;17)/PML-RARA. What is the diagnosis?
options: Chronic myeloid leukemia in blast crisis
stages.explanation: Hypergranular promyelocytes with bundled Auer rods (faggot cells), strong MPO positivity, HLA-DR negativity, and the t(15;17)/PML-RARA fusion define acute promyelocytic leukemia, a subtype of AML notorious for causing DIC. CML in blast crisis would be BCR-ABL-positive without the PML-RARA fusion. Acute lymphoblastic leukemia shows TdT-positive, MPO-negative lymphoblasts and does not produce promyelocytes or faggot cells. Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura causes microangiopathic hemolysis with schistocytes and a normal PT/aPTT, not the promyelocyte proliferation and consumptive coagulopathy seen here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: APL is a hematologic emergency because of fatal hemorrhage from DIC. What is the single most time-critical intervention?
options: Wait for full cytogenetic confirmation before any treatment
Start all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) immediately on clinical/morphologic suspicion, alongside aggressive blood product support
stages.explanation: ATRA must be started immediately upon morphologic or molecular suspicion of APL, before final confirmation, because it differentiates the malignant promyelocytes and rapidly mitigates the life-threatening DIC; pairing it with aggressive transfusion support (platelets, cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen, FFP) addresses the coagulopathy. Waiting for full cytogenetic confirmation risks fatal hemorrhage during the delay. Standard 7+3 alone, without ATRA (and typically arsenic trioxide), misses the targeted differentiating therapy that defines APL treatment. Giving platelets alone and observing fails to treat the underlying leukemia or the consumptive coagulopathy and is inadequate.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Several days after starting ATRA she develops fever, dyspnea, hypoxia, pulmonary infiltrates, and weight gain. What complication has developed and how is it managed?
options: Tumor lysis syndrome; give rasburicase only
Differentiation (APL/retinoic acid) syndrome; start corticosteroids (dexamethasone) promptly
stages.explanation: Differentiation syndrome (formerly retinoic acid syndrome) arises as promyelocytes differentiate, releasing cytokines that cause fever, capillary leak, fluid retention, dyspnea, hypoxia, and pulmonary infiltrates; prompt corticosteroids (dexamethasone) are the treatment, with temporary ATRA interruption if severe. Tumor lysis is a metabolic emergency without the capillary-leak pulmonary picture, and rasburicase alone would not treat this syndrome. Labeling it hospital-acquired pneumonia and permanently stopping ATRA would withdraw curative therapy unnecessarily. Anticoagulation for presumed PE is dangerous in a DIC-prone, thrombocytopenic patient and does not address the actual differentiation syndrome.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: With timely ATRA-based therapy (often combined with arsenic trioxide), how does APL's prognosis compare with other AML subtypes?
options: APL has the worst prognosis of all AML subtypes
APL, once the highest early-death subtype, becomes one of the most curable AML subtypes with prompt targeted therapy
Prognosis is identical to all other AML subtypes
APL is incurable and uniformly fatal
stages.explanation: Although APL historically had a high rate of early death from hemorrhage, prompt ATRA plus arsenic trioxide has transformed it into one of the most curable AML subtypes, with high long-term remission rates, which is precisely why immediate recognition and treatment matter. It does not have the worst prognosis once treated, nor is it identical to other AML subtypes, which generally have lower cure rates. It is far from incurable; the entire emphasis on early ATRA reflects that with timely therapy most patients are cured.

cases.diagnosis: Acute promyelocytic leukemia with DIC (t(15;17)/PML-RARA)
pearls: Faggot cells (promyelocytes with bundled Auer rods) plus DIC and t(15;17)/PML-RARA define APL — a medical emergency.
Start ATRA immediately on suspicion, before genetic confirmation, to halt the lethal DIC; support aggressively with platelets, cryoprecipitate, and FFP.
Watch for differentiation syndrome (fever, hypoxia, infiltrates, fluid overload) and treat promptly with dexamethasone.
ATRA plus arsenic trioxide has turned APL from the deadliest into one of the most curable AML subtypes.

cases.title: A Tired Child with Bone Pain and Swollen Glands
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of fatigue, leg pain, and refusal to walk, with swollen neck glands.
cases.history: A 5-year-old boy is admitted after several weeks of progressive fatigue, pallor, intermittent low-grade fevers, and complaints of leg and back pain that have made him reluctant to walk or play. His parents note new bruising and swollen glands in his neck. He has had two minor nosebleeds. There is no history of recent major infection beyond a cold a month ago. Birth and developmental history are normal; immunizations are up to date. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He lives with his parents and a younger sibling and attends kindergarten. Family history is noncontributory for malignancy or blood disorders. On review of systems his parents report decreased appetite, mild weight loss, and increased napping. He has had no headaches, vomiting, or visual changes. There is no history of toxic exposures, and no prior episodes of unusual bleeding or recurrent infections before this illness.
cases.exam: Pale, tired-appearing child who is fussy but consolable. Scattered ecchymoses and petechiae over the shins. Bilateral nontender cervical lymphadenopathy and a few enlarged axillary nodes. Conjunctival pallor present. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia with a soft flow murmur; lungs clear. Abdomen reveals hepatosplenomegaly with the spleen palpable 4 cm below the costal margin. He has point tenderness over the long bones and is reluctant to bear weight. No focal neurologic deficit, no nuchal rigidity, and no testicular enlargement on exam.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His CBC shows anemia, thrombocytopenia, and an abnormal lymphoid population. In a child with bone pain, lymphadenopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure and treat as a viral illness with follow-up in two weeks
Peripheral smear review and bone marrow aspiration/biopsy with flow cytometry and cytogenetics
Start oral iron and recheck in one month
Order plain radiographs of the legs as the definitive test
stages.explanation: The combination of cytopenias, bone pain, lymphadenopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly in a child is highly concerning for acute leukemia, mandating peripheral smear review and bone marrow aspiration/biopsy with flow cytometry, cytogenetics, and molecular studies to confirm and subtype it. Reassurance as a viral illness ignores the red-flag constellation and dangerously delays diagnosis. Oral iron treats iron-deficiency anemia, not pancytopenia from marrow infiltration. Plain leg radiographs may show leukemic lines but are not diagnostic and would not establish the leukemia; marrow evaluation is the definitive step.

stages.label: LABS & SMEAR
stages.content:
CBC: WBC 22 ×10³/µL, Hgb 7.2 g/dL, Platelets 35 ×10³/µL
Differential: numerous lymphoblasts
Peripheral smear: small-to-intermediate blasts with scant cytoplasm and condensed chromatin
Bone marrow: >90% lymphoblasts replacing normal marrow
Flow cytometry: CD10+, CD19+, TdT positive; MPO negative
Cytogenetics: hyperdiploidy (favorable)
Uric acid 7.8 mg/dL, LDH 600 U/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The marrow is replaced by TdT-positive, CD19+/CD10+, MPO-negative blasts. What is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: TdT positivity with B-lineage markers (CD19, CD10) and MPO negativity in a child whose marrow is replaced by lymphoblasts defines B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia, the most common childhood malignancy. Acute myeloid leukemia would be MPO-positive with myeloid markers and often Auer rods, which are absent. Burkitt leukemia shows mature B-cell, surface immunoglobulin-positive, TdT-negative cells with a 'starry sky' and MYC translocation, not TdT-positive precursors. Infectious mononucleosis produces reactive atypical lymphocytes, not a monomorphic TdT-positive blast population replacing the marrow.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Childhood ALL has a propensity to seed the central nervous system, which is a sanctuary site. What is a standard component of therapy to address this?
options: Routine whole-body radiation for all patients
CNS-directed therapy with intrathecal chemotherapy (CNS prophylaxis)
No CNS therapy unless symptoms appear
stages.explanation: Because leukemic cells hide in the CNS where systemic chemotherapy penetrates poorly, CNS-directed prophylaxis with intrathecal chemotherapy (e.g., methotrexate, often with cytarabine/steroid) is a standard part of ALL treatment and dramatically reduces CNS relapse. Routine whole-body radiation is not standard and carries excess long-term toxicity in children. Waiting for CNS symptoms before treating allows overt CNS relapse, defeating the purpose of prophylaxis. Splenectomy plays no role in ALL therapy. Intrathecal CNS prophylaxis (sometimes with cranial radiation in high-risk cases) is the established approach.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His leukemia shows hyperdiploidy and he is in the favorable age range. How does this affect his prognosis?
options: It carries a poor prognosis requiring immediate transplant
Pediatric B-ALL with favorable features (age 1-10, hyperdiploidy) has a high cure rate with chemotherapy
Prognosis is unaffected by age or cytogenetics
It is incurable without bone marrow transplant in all cases
stages.explanation: Pediatric B-cell precursor ALL with favorable features such as age between 1 and 10 years and hyperdiploidy (or ETV6-RUNX1) has an excellent prognosis, with cure rates exceeding 85-90% using risk-adapted multiagent chemotherapy, generally without upfront transplant. These features are favorable, not poor, so immediate transplant is not indicated. Age and cytogenetics are central to risk stratification and clearly do affect prognosis. Most children are cured with chemotherapy alone; transplant is reserved for high-risk or relapsed disease, not required in all cases.

cases.diagnosis: B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia (TdT-positive, hyperdiploid, favorable risk)
pearls: ALL is the most common childhood cancer; suspect it with cytopenias, bone pain, lymphadenopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly.
Lymphoblasts are TdT-positive and MPO-negative; B-ALL expresses CD19/CD10, distinguishing it from MPO-positive AML.
CNS is a sanctuary site — intrathecal CNS prophylaxis is standard to prevent CNS relapse.
Favorable prognosis in pediatric B-ALL: age 1-10, hyperdiploidy or ETV6-RUNX1; cure rates exceed 85% with chemotherapy.

cases.title: An Incidental High Lymphocyte Count in a Well Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Asymptomatic, referred for a persistently high lymphocyte count on routine bloodwork.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man is referred after routine bloodwork done before a knee replacement showed a persistently elevated lymphocyte count over two visits. He feels well, with no fevers, drenching night sweats, or unintentional weight loss, and denies recurrent infections, easy bruising, or bleeding. He has noticed a few small, painless lumps in his neck. Past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and osteoarthritis. Medications are amlodipine 5 mg, atorvastatin 20 mg, and acetaminophen as needed. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired bank manager, a former light smoker who quit 25 years ago, and drinks a glass of wine most evenings. Family history is noncontributory for hematologic malignancy. On review of systems he reports normal energy and appetite and no early satiety. He has had no recent infections, vaccinations, or illnesses to explain a reactive lymphocytosis, and his lymphocyte count was also elevated on a prior test a year earlier.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, robust older man in no distress. There are a few small (1-1.5 cm), mobile, nontender cervical and axillary lymph nodes. No pallor, jaundice, petechiae, or ecchymoses. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. The abdomen is soft; the spleen tip is just palpable below the left costal margin and the liver is not enlarged. No bony tenderness. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. There are no signs of active infection or systemic illness, and the overall picture is of an asymptomatic patient with mild lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His smear shows mature-appearing lymphocytes and numerous smudge cells. What is the most appropriate next test to characterize the lymphocytosis?
options: Excisional lymph node biopsy as the first step
Bone marrow biopsy as the first step
stages.explanation: Peripheral blood flow cytometry is the first-line test to characterize a mature lymphocytosis and can diagnose chronic lymphocytic leukemia by demonstrating a clonal CD5+/CD19+/CD23+ B-cell population, avoiding more invasive procedures. An excisional node biopsy is reserved for suspected transformation (Richter) or when flow is nondiagnostic, not as the first step in classic CLL. A bone marrow biopsy is not required to diagnose CLL when blood flow cytometry is diagnostic. A Monospot would be appropriate for a young patient with reactive atypical lymphocytes and mononucleosis symptoms, not a 70-year-old with chronic mature lymphocytosis and smudge cells.

stages.label: LABS & FLOW
stages.content:
CBC: WBC 28 ×10³/µL, Hgb 13.8 g/dL, Platelets 210 ×10³/µL
Differential: absolute lymphocyte count 22 ×10³/µL, mature small lymphocytes
Peripheral smear: numerous smudge (basket) cells
Flow cytometry: clonal B cells co-expressing CD5, CD19, CD23, with dim surface immunoglobulin and kappa light-chain restriction
LDH normal; haptoglobin normal; DAT (Coombs) negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Flow cytometry shows a clonal B-cell population co-expressing CD5, CD19, and CD23 with light-chain restriction. What is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A clonal B-cell population co-expressing CD5, CD19, and CD23 with dim surface immunoglobulin and light-chain restriction, together with smudge cells and a persistent mature lymphocytosis, is diagnostic of chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Mantle cell lymphoma is also CD5-positive but is typically CD23-negative and cyclin D1/t(11;14)-positive, distinguishing it from CLL. Reactive lymphocytosis is polyclonal without light-chain restriction and usually has an identifiable trigger. Acute lymphoblastic leukemia consists of immature TdT-positive blasts, not the mature, clonal small lymphocytes seen here. The CD5+/CD23+ immunophenotype is the key.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is asymptomatic with early-stage disease (lymphocytosis and minimal lymphadenopathy, normal hemoglobin and platelets). What is the appropriate management?
options: Active surveillance ('watch and wait') with periodic monitoring
Start prophylactic antibiotics and immediate splenectomy
stages.explanation: Early-stage, asymptomatic CLL is managed with active surveillance ('watch and wait'), because initiating treatment early does not improve survival and exposes patients to unnecessary toxicity. Treatment is reserved for indications such as progressive or symptomatic disease, bulky or rapidly enlarging nodes, marrow failure (anemia/thrombocytopenia), or autoimmune complications. Immediate chemoimmunotherapy in an asymptomatic patient is not indicated. Allogeneic transplant is reserved for high-risk, relapsed/refractory disease, not early-stage observation. Prophylactic antibiotics and splenectomy are not standard initial management of asymptomatic early CLL.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Over years of follow-up, which complications of CLL should be monitored for?
options: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia, recurrent infections from hypogammaglobulinemia, and Richter transformation
stages.explanation: CLL is associated with autoimmune cytopenias (especially autoimmune hemolytic anemia and immune thrombocytopenia), increased infection risk from hypogammaglobulinemia and immune dysfunction, and Richter transformation into an aggressive large B-cell lymphoma, all of which warrant ongoing surveillance. Hemochromatosis and cirrhosis are iron-overload/liver conditions unrelated to CLL biology. Diabetes insipidus is a posterior pituitary disorder with no link to CLL. CLL does not progress to chronic myeloid leukemia, which is a distinct BCR-ABL-driven myeloid neoplasm; the feared transformation in CLL is Richter (large-cell) transformation.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CD5+/CD19+/CD23+ clonal B cells)
pearls: Smudge (basket) cells on smear with a persistent mature lymphocytosis in an older adult suggest CLL.
Diagnose CLL with peripheral blood flow cytometry: clonal CD5+/CD19+/CD23+ B cells with light-chain restriction.
Early-stage asymptomatic CLL is observed ('watch and wait'); treat only for symptomatic/progressive disease or marrow failure.
Watch for autoimmune hemolytic anemia, infections from hypogammaglobulinemia, and Richter transformation to aggressive lymphoma.

cases.title: Fatigue, Easy Bruising, and a Heavy Left Side
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive fatigue, early satiety, and easy bruising over the past four months.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents with four months of worsening fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, and a dragging sensation in the left upper abdomen with early satiety. He has had several episodes of gum bleeding and bruises easily. He reports two sinus infections and a prolonged bronchitis in the last three months that were slow to resolve. He denies fevers, drenching night sweats, or weight loss, though his appetite has fallen. Past medical history is notable only for hypertension treated with amlodipine 5 mg daily. He takes no antiplatelet agents or anticoagulants. He has no known drug allergies. He works as a carpenter, drinks alcohol socially, and has never smoked. He has no occupational solvent or radiation exposure that he can recall. Family history is unremarkable for hematologic malignancy. Review of systems is otherwise negative for hematochezia, hematuria, joint pain, or rashes. He has not traveled recently and has no pets.
cases.exam: The patient is pale and appears tired but not acutely ill. Conjunctivae are pale; oropharynx shows scattered petechiae on the soft palate. There is no cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable. The abdomen is soft; the spleen is markedly enlarged, palpable approximately 8 cm below the left costal margin, firm and nontender, crossing the midline. The liver edge is not enlarged. Skin shows several ecchymoses on the forearms and shins without a precipitating trauma history. There is no sternal tenderness. Neurologic exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial laboratory study is most likely to characterize the cause of this patient's pancytopenia and splenomegaly?
options: Complete blood count with peripheral smear review
stages.explanation: A CBC with manual peripheral smear is the essential first step; it confirms pancytopenia and may reveal the characteristic abnormal mononuclear cells with cytoplasmic projections, directing further work-up. SPEP/immunofixation evaluates plasma cell dyscrasias, which do not produce massive splenomegaly with cytopenias this way. JAK2 testing screens for myeloproliferative neoplasms such as polycythemia vera or primary myelofibrosis; while myelofibrosis causes splenomegaly, the clinical picture of monocytopenia and recurrent infection points elsewhere, and the smear should be reviewed first. A Coombs test evaluates autoimmune hemolysis, which would not explain leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and massive splenomegaly together.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS & SMEAR
stages.content:
WBC 2.1 ×10³/µL (neutrophils 18%, monocytes 1%)
Hemoglobin 8.6 g/dL, MCV 90 fL
Platelets 62 ×10³/µL
Reticulocyte count 0.8%
Absolute monocyte count 0.02 ×10³/µL (profound monocytopenia)
Peripheral smear: medium-sized lymphoid cells with abundant pale-blue cytoplasm and fine ''hairy'' cytoplasmic projections
LDH 210 U/L
SPEP: no monoclonal spike

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A bone marrow aspirate is attempted but yields a ''dry tap.'' Which finding on the core biopsy and special studies would most specifically confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Sheets of plasma cells with kappa light-chain restriction
Cells positive for tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase (TRAP) and BRAF V600E, with CD11c/CD25/CD103 expression
Reed-Sternberg cells that are CD15 and CD30 positive
stages.explanation: The constellation of pancytopenia, monocytopenia, massive splenomegaly, a dry tap from marrow fibrosis, and TRAP-positive cells with bright CD11c, CD25, CD103, and CD123 plus the BRAF V600E mutation is diagnostic of hairy cell leukemia. Plasma cells with light-chain restriction define multiple myeloma, which does not cause hairy cells or monocytopenia. BCR-ABL1 defines chronic myeloid leukemia, which classically causes leukocytosis with a myeloid left shift, not pancytopenia. Reed-Sternberg cells (CD15+/CD30+) define Hodgkin lymphoma, which presents with lymphadenopathy rather than a dry tap and monocytopenia.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Bone marrow flow cytometry confirms a CD11c+/CD25+/CD103+ B-cell population and BRAF V600E is detected. Before starting systemic therapy, which step is most important in this patient who has recurrent infections?
options: Perform urgent splenectomy as first-line definitive treatment
Treat any active infection and assess for cytopenia-related complications before initiating purine analog therapy
Start immediate allogeneic stem cell transplantation
stages.explanation: Patients with hairy cell leukemia are frequently neutropenic and infection-prone; active infections should be controlled before purine analog (cladribine or pentostatin) therapy, which causes profound and prolonged immunosuppression and lymphopenia. Indefinite empiric antibiotics are not standard and breed resistance. Splenectomy was a historical option but is no longer first-line now that purine analogs produce durable remissions; it is reserved for selected refractory or massive symptomatic spleen cases. Allogeneic transplant is not indicated for newly diagnosed hairy cell leukemia, which is highly chemosensitive and indolent.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Active infection has resolved. Which is the most appropriate first-line systemic therapy for this patient's hairy cell leukemia?
options: Imatinib, a BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor
stages.explanation: A purine nucleoside analog, most commonly cladribine (with rituximab increasingly added), is first-line for symptomatic classic hairy cell leukemia and produces high rates of durable complete remission. R-CHOP treats aggressive B-cell lymphomas such as DLBCL, not the indolent, exquisitely purine-analog-sensitive hairy cell leukemia. Imatinib targets BCR-ABL1 in CML and has no role here. Lenalidomide-dexamethasone treats plasma cell dyscrasias such as myeloma, not hairy cell leukemia. BRAF inhibitors such as vemurafenib are reserved for relapsed/refractory disease.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two weeks after cladribine, the patient develops fever to 38.7 °C with an absolute neutrophil count of 0.3 ×10³/µL. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Reassure and recheck CBC in one week
Admit for prompt empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics covering gram-negative organisms including Pseudomonas
Repeat the cladribine dose to accelerate remission
stages.explanation: This is febrile neutropenia, a medical emergency. Cladribine causes profound and prolonged neutropenia and lymphopenia, and a neutropenic patient with fever requires immediate empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics with antipseudomonal coverage (e.g., cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam) after cultures. Delaying a week risks rapid sepsis and death. Repeating cladribine during cytopenic fever would worsen marrow suppression and is contraindicated. Platelet transfusion does not address the infectious emergency, and discharge of a febrile neutropenic patient is unsafe. G-CSF support and antimicrobial prophylaxis are commonly considered during recovery.

cases.diagnosis: Hairy cell leukemia (classic, BRAF V600E-positive)
pearls: Classic hairy cell leukemia is the classic cause of pancytopenia with monocytopenia, massive splenomegaly, and a fibrosis-related ''dry tap.''
Hairy cells are TRAP-positive and express CD11c, CD25, CD103, and CD123; nearly all carry the BRAF V600E mutation.
Purine analogs (cladribine or pentostatin), often with rituximab, are first-line and produce durable remissions; splenectomy is now rarely needed.
Profound, prolonged neutropenia after purine analog therapy makes febrile neutropenia a key complication to anticipate and treat emergently.

cases.title: An Older Man With Stubborn Anemia
cases.chiefComplaint: Three months of worsening fatigue and breathlessness despite iron and B12 supplementation.
cases.history: A 75-year-old man is referred for persistent anemia. Over three months he has had progressive fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, and lightheadedness. His primary physician started oral ferrous sulfate and then monthly vitamin B12 injections without improvement; he was later transfused two units of red cells with only transient benefit. He denies melena, hematochezia, hematuria, or heavy alcohol use. He had a normal colonoscopy 18 months ago. Past medical history includes coronary artery disease, hypertension, and prostate cancer treated with radiation eight years ago. Medications are aspirin 81 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, lisinopril 20 mg, and metoprolol 50 mg daily; he has no anticoagulants. He reports no NSAID use. He has no drug allergies. He is a retired machinist with prior benzene-containing solvent exposure and a 20-pack-year remote smoking history, quit 15 years ago. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems reveals occasional gum bleeding but no fevers, night sweats, weight loss, or bone pain.
cases.exam: The patient is pale and appears his stated age. Conjunctivae are pale. There is no scleral icterus, glossitis, or angular cheilitis. No cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy is palpable. Heart sounds are regular with a soft II/VI systolic flow murmur at the left sternal border. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft and nontender; the spleen and liver are not enlarged. There are a few scattered petechiae on the lower legs but no large ecchymoses. Neurologic exam shows no posterior column signs, normal proprioception, and no peripheral neuropathy. There is no sternal tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Iron studies and B12/folate were already replete. What is the most appropriate next investigation to characterize this refractory macrocytic anemia?
options: Empiric trial of high-dose oral folate
Peripheral blood smear with reticulocyte count and review of all cell lines
Upper endoscopy to search for occult bleeding
stages.explanation: A peripheral smear with reticulocyte count, examining all three lineages, is the key next step in refractory macrocytic anemia that has not responded to iron and B12. It can reveal dysplastic changes (oval macrocytes, hypogranular/hypolobated neutrophils, Pelger-Huet forms) and an inappropriately low reticulocyte count suggesting marrow failure. Another empiric folate trial is futile when folate is already replete. Endoscopy targets blood loss, but this is a non-bleeding, hypoproliferative macrocytic anemia. An EPO level alone is insufficient to characterize a multilineage marrow disorder, though it is useful later for treatment planning.

stages.label: BLOOD WORK & SMEAR
stages.content:
WBC 3.2 ×10³/µL (ANC 1.1 ×10³/µL)
Hemoglobin 8.2 g/dL, MCV 108 fL
Platelets 95 ×10³/µL
Reticulocyte count 0.5% (inappropriately low)
Ferritin 410 ng/mL, transferrin saturation 38%
Vitamin B12 620 pg/mL, folate 14 ng/mL
LDH 240 U/L, haptoglobin normal
Smear: oval macrocytes, hypogranular neutrophils, occasional bilobed (pseudo-Pelger-Huet) neutrophils

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given pancytopenia with dysplastic features and adequate vitamin stores, which test is most likely to establish the diagnosis?
options: Bone marrow aspirate and biopsy with cytogenetics and iron (Prussian blue) stain
Flow cytometry for paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria only
stages.explanation: Bone marrow aspirate and biopsy with cytogenetics is the gold standard, demonstrating a hypercellular marrow with multilineage dysplasia, blasts under 20%, and Prussian blue staining revealing ring sideroblasts (iron-laden mitochondria encircling the nucleus). Cytogenetics (e.g., del(5q), -7) refine risk. PNH flow can mimic some cytopenias but does not explain dysplasia. BCR-ABL1 would diagnose CML, which causes leukocytosis, not pancytopenia with dysplasia. Methylmalonic acid would confirm B12 deficiency, already excluded by normal B12 and absent neuropathy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The marrow shows 4% blasts, multilineage dysplasia, 22% ring sideroblasts, and an SF3B1 mutation; cytogenetics are normal. Calculating prognosis with the revised International Prognostic Scoring System (IPSS-R) places him in the lower-risk category. For his symptomatic anemia, which first-line therapy is most appropriate?
options: Immediate allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant
Erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (e.g., epoetin alfa), guided by serum EPO level and transfusion burden
Hydroxyurea to lower the blast count
stages.explanation: For lower-risk MDS with symptomatic anemia and a low serum EPO level, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents are first-line and can reduce transfusion needs; luspatercept is especially effective in MDS with ring sideroblasts/SF3B1 mutation. Allogeneic transplant is the only cure but is reserved for higher-risk disease given its toxicity, especially at age 75. Intensive AML induction is for higher-risk MDS or transformation, not lower-risk disease with 4% blasts. Hydroxyurea controls proliferative myeloid disorders and does not treat the cytopenias of lower-risk MDS.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: He has now received eight red cell transfusions over five months. His ferritin is 1,900 ng/mL. Which complication should be monitored and managed?
options: Transfusion-related acute lung injury from chronic transfusion
Secondary iron overload, with consideration of iron chelation in transfusion-dependent lower-risk MDS
Dilutional hyponatremia from packed red cells
stages.explanation: Chronic transfusion delivers roughly 200-250 mg of iron per unit, causing secondary iron overload. In transfusion-dependent lower-risk MDS with rising ferritin and a reasonable life expectancy, iron chelation (e.g., deferasirox) is considered to limit cardiac, hepatic, and endocrine iron toxicity. TRALI is an acute, single-transfusion reaction, not a cumulative complication. Vitamin K deficiency is unrelated to transfusion. Packed red cells are not a significant free-water load and do not characteristically cause dilutional hyponatremia.

cases.diagnosis: Myelodysplastic syndrome (lower-risk, MDS with ring sideroblasts / SF3B1-mutated)
pearls: Refractory macrocytic anemia with cytopenias and a low reticulocyte count despite replete B12/folate should prompt evaluation for myelodysplastic syndrome.
Hallmark smear findings include oval macrocytes, hypogranular and pseudo-Pelger-Huet neutrophils; marrow shows ring sideroblasts and multilineage dysplasia with under 20% blasts.
Risk stratify with IPSS-R: lower-risk disease is treated supportively (ESAs, luspatercept for ring sideroblast/SF3B1 subtype), while higher-risk disease prompts hypomethylating agents or transplant.
Chronic transfusion leads to iron overload; monitor ferritin and consider chelation in transfusion-dependent lower-risk patients.

cases.title: Bone Pain, Confusion, and Sluggish Kidneys
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening lower back pain, fatigue, and confusion over six weeks.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man is admitted with six weeks of progressive lower back and rib pain, profound fatigue, constipation, and increasing confusion noted by his wife. He has had reduced urine output and excessive thirst. He denies trauma, fevers, or night sweats but reports a 5 kg unintentional weight loss. Past medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes; he takes amlodipine 10 mg, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and recently started ibuprofen for the back pain. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired accountant, drinks minimal alcohol, and quit smoking 20 years ago. Family history is notable for a brother with prostate cancer. Review of systems is positive for paresthesias in the feet and recurrent pneumonia twice in the past year, but negative for hematochezia, hemoptysis, or focal weakness. He has not traveled recently. He uses no anticoagulants. He notes his rings and shoes still fit but that he has felt mentally foggy and sleepy during the day.
cases.exam: The patient is somnolent but rousable and oriented to person and place only. Mucous membranes are dry. There is no lymphadenopathy. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable. The abdomen is soft with hypoactive bowel sounds. There is point tenderness over the mid-thoracic spine and several ribs on palpation. Conjunctivae are pale. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. Neurologic exam shows mild proximal weakness and reduced vibratory sense in the toes; reflexes are symmetric. Skin shows no rash or purpura. There is no peripheral edema.

stages.label: ADMISSION LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL, MCV 92 fL (rouleaux on smear)
WBC 5.6 ×10³/µL, Platelets 180 ×10³/µL
Calcium 12.8 mg/dL, Albumin 3.2 g/dL
Creatinine 2.6 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
BUN 42 mg/dL
Total protein 9.8 g/dL, globulin gap elevated
Urinalysis: dipstick protein negative, but 24-h urine protein elevated

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The combination of anemia, hypercalcemia, renal failure, and bone pain with an elevated total protein and rouleaux suggests a plasma cell disorder. Which test best confirms a monoclonal protein?
options: Serum and urine protein electrophoresis with immunofixation and serum free light chains
Peripheral blood flow cytometry for CD19/CD20
stages.explanation: Serum and urine protein electrophoresis with immunofixation plus serum free light chain assay is the definitive way to detect and characterize a monoclonal (M) protein, explaining the elevated total protein, rouleaux, and CRAB features. The negative urine dipstick despite heavy 24-hour proteinuria is a classic clue, because dipsticks detect albumin, not the Bence Jones (free light chain) protein that immunofixation reveals. Flow cytometry detects circulating B cells but is not the standard for paraproteins. Iron studies do not explain hypercalcemia and renal failure. ANA evaluates autoimmune disease, not a plasma cell dyscrasia.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: SPEP shows an IgG kappa M-spike of 4.2 g/dL and urine immunofixation confirms Bence Jones (kappa) protein. Which additional finding would complete the diagnosis of symptomatic multiple myeloma?
options: More than 10% clonal plasma cells in the bone marrow, with end-organ damage (CRAB)
A serum IgM paraprotein with hyperviscosity
Reed-Sternberg cells in a lymph node
A positive Coombs test with spherocytes
stages.explanation: Multiple myeloma requires clonal bone marrow plasma cells (typically >=10%) or a plasmacytoma plus a myeloma-defining event; here the CRAB criteria (hyperCalcemia, Renal insufficiency, Anemia, Bone lesions) are present, confirming symptomatic disease. An IgM paraprotein with hyperviscosity defines Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, not myeloma. Reed-Sternberg cells indicate Hodgkin lymphoma. A positive Coombs with spherocytes indicates autoimmune hemolytic anemia. The patient's IgG kappa spike, Bence Jones proteinuria, and CRAB features fit myeloma.

stages.label: IMAGING & MARROW
stages.content:
Low-dose whole-body CT: multiple lytic lesions in the skull, vertebrae, and ribs; a compression deformity at T8
Bone marrow biopsy: 45% clonal plasma cells, kappa-restricted
FISH: t(11;14) detected, standard risk
Beta-2 microglobulin 5.8 mg/L, albumin 3.2 g/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is the most urgent initial management priority on admission for this patient?
options: Aggressive isotonic IV hydration for hypercalcemia and acute kidney injury, plus stopping the NSAID
Begin a bisphosphonate alone and observe
Order an urgent bone marrow transplant evaluation before stabilization
stages.explanation: The immediate priorities are correcting hypercalcemia and AKI with aggressive isotonic saline and discontinuing the nephrotoxic NSAID; volume repletion is the cornerstone of hypercalcemia treatment and protects the kidneys before light-chain-directed therapy. Definitive anti-myeloma chemotherapy (e.g., bortezomib-based, which is renally safe and rapidly reduces light chains) follows stabilization. A bisphosphonate helps hypercalcemia but acts slowly and must be dose-adjusted and used cautiously in renal failure; it is not the first move. Transplant evaluation is appropriate later but never precedes acute stabilization.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After stabilization, he begins bortezomib-lenalidomide-dexamethasone. As a 66-year-old with good performance status, what is the appropriate role of autologous stem cell transplantation?
options: He is too old; transplant is contraindicated over age 65
Autologous transplant is not used in myeloma
He should be referred for autologous stem cell transplant consideration after induction, as a transplant-eligible candidate
Only allogeneic transplant is appropriate in myeloma
stages.explanation: Transplant eligibility in myeloma is based on performance status, organ function, and frailty rather than a strict age cutoff; a fit 66-year-old is typically a candidate for autologous stem cell transplant after induction, which deepens and prolongs remission, followed by maintenance lenalidomide. Age over 65 alone is not a contraindication. Autologous transplant is a standard consolidation in myeloma, not an excluded therapy. Allogeneic transplant carries high transplant-related mortality and is investigational in myeloma, reserved for rare high-risk or trial settings.

cases.diagnosis: Multiple myeloma (IgG kappa, symptomatic with CRAB features)
pearls: Think myeloma with the CRAB tetrad: hyperCalcemia, Renal failure, Anemia, and Bone lytic lesions, often with rouleaux and an elevated globulin gap.
A negative urine dipstick with heavy 24-hour proteinuria signals Bence Jones (free light chain) protein, which dipsticks miss; confirm with urine immunofixation.
Diagnosis needs clonal marrow plasma cells (or plasmacytoma) plus a myeloma-defining event; whole-body low-dose CT, MRI, or PET assess lytic disease.
Acute management of hypercalcemia and AKI is aggressive IV saline and removing nephrotoxins; bortezomib-based regimens are renally safe, and fit patients proceed to autologous transplant.

cases.title: An Unexpected Finding on Routine Bloodwork
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred after a small abnormal protein band was found on labs ordered for fatigue.
cases.history: A 72-year-old woman is referred to hematology after routine labs for mild fatigue revealed a slightly elevated total protein with an abnormal band on serum protein electrophoresis. She feels generally well, walks two miles most days, and has no bone pain, fevers, night sweats, or weight loss. She denies numbness, recurrent infections, easy bruising, or change in urine output. Past medical history includes hypothyroidism and osteoarthritis. Medications are levothyroxine 75 mcg daily and acetaminophen as needed; she takes no anticoagulants and has no drug allergies. She is a retired schoolteacher, never smoked, and drinks one glass of wine weekly. Family history includes a sister with breast cancer but no plasma cell or lymphoid malignancies. Review of systems is otherwise negative; appetite and energy are near baseline, and she attributes her fatigue to a busy month. She has no history of recurrent pneumonia, fractures, or kidney stones. Her last bone density scan two years ago showed osteopenia.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. There is no pallor, scleral icterus, or oral lesion. No cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy is palpable. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are normal. The abdomen is soft and nontender with no hepatosplenomegaly. There is no spinal or rib tenderness. Skin shows no purpura or rash. Neurologic exam is normal with intact sensation, normal strength, and symmetric reflexes. There is no peripheral edema and no macroglossia.

stages.label: REFERRAL LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL, MCV 88 fL
WBC 6.4 ×10³/µL, Platelets 245 ×10³/µL
Calcium 9.4 mg/dL, Albumin 4.1 g/dL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
SPEP: monoclonal IgG kappa spike measuring 1.1 g/dL
Serum free light chains: kappa/lambda ratio mildly elevated at 2.1
Urinalysis: no protein

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has a small M-protein but no symptoms. Which set of findings must be assessed to distinguish a benign monoclonal gammopathy from myeloma?
options: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein
CRAB features (calcium, renal function, anemia, bone lesions) plus M-protein size and marrow plasma cell percentage
stages.explanation: Distinguishing MGUS from smoldering or active myeloma rests on three pillars: the size of the serum M-protein (<3 g/dL for MGUS), the clonal marrow plasma cell percentage (<10% for MGUS), and the absence of myeloma-defining end-organ damage (no CRAB and no myeloma-defining biomarkers). Her IgG kappa spike of 1.1 g/dL with no CRAB fits MGUS. ESR/CRP are nonspecific inflammatory markers. ANA and rheumatoid factor evaluate autoimmune disease. JAK2 and BCR-ABL1 screen for myeloproliferative neoplasms, not plasma cell dyscrasias.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her labs show no CRAB features, M-protein 1.1 g/dL, normal hemoglobin, calcium, and creatinine. What is the most likely diagnosis, and is a bone marrow biopsy mandatory now?
options: Smoldering multiple myeloma; biopsy is urgent
Monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS); biopsy may be deferred in this low-risk profile
stages.explanation: This is MGUS: an M-protein under 3 g/dL, no CRAB, and a low-risk profile (IgG isotype, M-protein <1.5 g/dL, near-normal free light chain ratio). In such low-risk patients, marrow biopsy and skeletal imaging can often be deferred with surveillance. Smoldering myeloma requires an M-protein >=3 g/dL or >=10% marrow plasma cells without end-organ damage. Active myeloma requires CRAB or myeloma-defining events, which are absent. Waldenstrom involves an IgM paraprotein with lymphoplasmacytic marrow infiltration, not IgG kappa.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this low-risk MGUS?
options: Periodic surveillance with repeat SPEP, CBC, calcium, and creatinine (e.g., in 6 months, then annually if stable)
stages.explanation: MGUS is a premalignant condition managed with watchful surveillance, not treatment; low-risk patients typically have SPEP and basic labs rechecked at about 6 months, then annually if stable, because the risk of progression to myeloma or related malignancy is roughly 1% per year. Lenalidomide-dexamethasone and transplant are myeloma treatments and would be overtreatment with unnecessary toxicity. Monthly IVIG is reserved for recurrent infections from hypogammaglobulinemia, not for routine MGUS, which this asymptomatic patient does not have.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: At her follow-up, she asks what symptoms should prompt earlier evaluation. Which counseling point is most appropriate?
options: Reassure her that MGUS never progresses, so no follow-up is needed
Advise her to return promptly for new bone pain, fractures, anemia symptoms, increased thirst/urination, or recurrent infections
Tell her she will certainly develop myeloma within a year
Recommend she avoid all vaccinations permanently
stages.explanation: Patients with MGUS should be counseled on red-flag symptoms suggesting progression to myeloma or a related disorder, prompting earlier evaluation: new or worsening bone pain or fractures, symptomatic anemia, polyuria/polydipsia or confusion (hypercalcemia), and recurrent infections. MGUS does progress at roughly 1% per year, so lifelong surveillance is recommended, not abandonment of follow-up. Stating she will certainly develop myeloma is inaccurate and alarming. Avoiding vaccinations is unwarranted; routine immunizations remain appropriate and beneficial.

cases.diagnosis: Monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (low-risk IgG kappa MGUS)
pearls: MGUS is defined by an M-protein <3 g/dL, clonal marrow plasma cells <10%, and absence of CRAB or myeloma-defining events.
Low-risk MGUS (IgG isotype, M-protein <1.5 g/dL, normal free light chain ratio) often does not require marrow biopsy or skeletal survey at diagnosis.
Management is surveillance, not treatment; the risk of progression to myeloma or related malignancy is about 1% per year.
Counsel patients to report new bone pain, fractures, anemia, hypercalcemia symptoms, or recurrent infections that may herald progression.

cases.title: Headache, Blurred Vision, and Oozing Gums
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of headache, blurry vision, dizziness, and spontaneous gum and nose bleeding.
cases.history: A 69-year-old man reports several weeks of progressive headache, intermittent blurred vision, dizziness, and a vague feeling of confusion. He has had recurrent epistaxis and bleeding gums and notes worsening fatigue and breathlessness. He denies chest pain or focal weakness but describes numbness and a burning sensation in both feet. He has lost about 4 kg without trying and has occasional low-grade night sweats. Past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension and benign prostatic hyperplasia; medications are amlodipine 5 mg and tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily. He takes no anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents and has no drug allergies. He is a retired postal worker, drinks rarely, and quit smoking 25 years ago. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for cold-induced bluish discoloration of his fingertips and for early satiety. He has had no recent infections, hematochezia, or hematuria. He has not traveled internationally and denies tick exposure.
cases.exam: The patient appears fatigued. Fundoscopic exam reveals dilated, tortuous ''sausage-link'' retinal veins with scattered flame hemorrhages. Oral mucosa shows oozing from the gingival margins. There is mild bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable. The abdomen is soft; the spleen tip is palpable and the liver edge is mildly enlarged. Neurologic exam shows a stocking-distribution sensory neuropathy in both feet with reduced vibratory sense; strength is preserved. There is no focal motor deficit. Skin shows scattered ecchymoses and acrocyanosis of the fingertips. There is no macroglossia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The retinal ''sausage-link'' veins, mucosal bleeding, and neurologic symptoms suggest hyperviscosity. Which initial test best identifies the responsible paraprotein?
stages.explanation: Serum protein electrophoresis with immunofixation identifies and types the monoclonal protein; a large IgM paraprotein is the classic cause of the hyperviscosity syndrome seen here (headache, visual changes, mucosal bleeding, neurologic symptoms). D-dimer and fibrinogen assess DIC or thrombosis, not paraproteinemia. ESR is often markedly elevated with paraproteins but is nonspecific and does not type the protein. A smear for schistocytes evaluates microangiopathic hemolysis, which does not explain a large monoclonal protein with hyperviscosity.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 9.0 g/dL (rouleaux on smear)
WBC 6.0 ×10³/µL, Platelets 130 ×10³/µL
SPEP: monoclonal IgM spike 5.5 g/dL
Serum IgM markedly elevated; IgG and IgA low
Serum viscosity 5.2 centipoise (normal 1.4-1.8)
Calcium 9.2 mg/dL, Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Cryoglobulins present; cold agglutinin titer elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Bone marrow biopsy is planned. Which combination of findings would confirm Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia?
options: Sheets of CD138+ plasma cells with lytic bone lesions
Lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate with an IgM monoclonal protein and the MYD88 L265P mutation
Reed-Sternberg cells in a background of mixed inflammatory cells
stages.explanation: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia is a lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma defined by marrow infiltration with lymphoplasmacytic cells secreting a monoclonal IgM; the MYD88 L265P mutation is present in over 90% of cases and supports the diagnosis. Sheets of CD138+ plasma cells with lytic lesions indicate multiple myeloma (and IgM myeloma is rare, without lymphoplasmacytic morphology). The Philadelphia chromosome defines CML. Reed-Sternberg cells indicate Hodgkin lymphoma. The IgM paraprotein, hyperviscosity, and lymphoplasmacytic marrow fit Waldenstrom.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His viscosity is 5.2 cP with active visual symptoms and mucosal bleeding. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Transfuse two units of packed red cells to correct the anemia first
Urgent plasmapheresis (plasma exchange) to rapidly lower the IgM and reverse hyperviscosity
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation for presumed thrombosis
stages.explanation: Symptomatic hyperviscosity is an emergency treated with urgent plasmapheresis, which rapidly removes the large intravascular IgM and relieves visual and neurologic symptoms and mucosal bleeding while definitive therapy is arranged. Transfusing red cells before lowering viscosity is dangerous because it raises whole-blood viscosity and can worsen symptoms. Corticosteroids alone act too slowly for an acute hyperviscosity crisis. Anticoagulation is contraindicated in a bleeding patient and does not address the paraprotein. After stabilization, rituximab-based therapy (e.g., with bendamustine, or a BTK inhibitor) treats the underlying disease.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After plasmapheresis stabilizes him, definitive systemic therapy is planned. Which consideration is most important when choosing an anti-IgM regimen in Waldenstrom?
options: Rituximab can cause a transient IgM ''flare,'' so it may be deferred or used cautiously until viscosity is controlled
Allogeneic transplant is the standard first-line therapy
Splenectomy should precede all medical therapy
Hydroxyurea is the preferred first agent to control IgM
stages.explanation: Rituximab can trigger a transient surge in IgM (an ''IgM flare''), which may acutely worsen hyperviscosity; therefore it is often deferred or given cautiously, ideally after viscosity is controlled with plasmapheresis, and may be combined with chemotherapy (e.g., bendamustine) or a BTK inhibitor such as ibrutinib (especially effective in MYD88-mutated disease). Allogeneic transplant is not standard first-line therapy in this indolent disease. Splenectomy is not a primary treatment for Waldenstrom. Hydroxyurea is not a preferred agent for lowering the IgM paraprotein.

cases.diagnosis: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia (lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma with IgM hyperviscosity)
pearls: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia is a lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma producing monoclonal IgM; over 90% carry the MYD88 L265P mutation.
Hyperviscosity syndrome (headache, blurred vision, ''sausage-link'' retinal veins, mucosal bleeding, neurologic changes) is the hallmark emergency.
Urgent plasmapheresis rapidly lowers IgM and reverses hyperviscosity; avoid red cell transfusion before viscosity is controlled.
Rituximab can cause an IgM flare that worsens viscosity, so it is timed carefully; BTK inhibitors are highly active in MYD88-mutated disease.

cases.title: A Young Man With Night Sweats and a Neck Lump
cases.chiefComplaint: A painless neck lump for two months with drenching night sweats and weight loss.
cases.history: A 24-year-old man presents with a firm, painless left neck mass first noticed two months ago that has slowly enlarged. He reports drenching night sweats requiring a change of bedclothes, intermittent low-grade fevers, and an unintentional 6 kg weight loss over three months. He has also noticed generalized itching. Interestingly, the neck mass becomes painful within minutes of drinking alcohol. He denies sore throat, dental infection, or recent travel. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no regular medications and has no drug allergies. He is a graduate student, drinks alcohol socially, has never smoked, and uses no recreational drugs. He has one male partner and reports consistent condom use. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for cough, dyspnea, abdominal pain, or focal weakness, though he notes occasional early satiety. He had infectious mononucleosis in his teens. He has no pets and no tuberculosis exposure that he is aware of.
cases.exam: The patient is well-developed and in no distress. There is a 3 cm firm, rubbery, nontender, mobile lymph node in the left supraclavicular/lower cervical region, plus smaller palpable left axillary nodes. The oropharynx is clear without exudate. There is no jugular venous distension or facial plethora. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. The abdomen is soft; the spleen tip is just palpable. There are excoriations on the trunk consistent with pruritus. There is no hepatomegaly, no rash, and no peripheral edema. Neurologic exam is normal. No inguinal lymphadenopathy is appreciated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young man has a firm, painless cervical node with B symptoms and alcohol-induced node pain. What is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis?
options: Empiric two-week course of antibiotics and reassess
Start broad-spectrum antivirals for presumed EBV
stages.explanation: Excisional (or generous core) lymph node biopsy is required to diagnose lymphoma because architecture is essential to identify Reed-Sternberg cells and the surrounding reactive background; fine-needle aspiration usually cannot demonstrate this architecture and frequently yields nondiagnostic results. Empiric antibiotics waste time when B symptoms, weight loss, and a persistent firm node strongly suggest malignancy rather than a reactive infectious node. Antivirals are not indicated; although prior EBV is associated with Hodgkin lymphoma, treating EBV does not address the suspected malignancy and delays diagnosis.

stages.label: BIOPSY & LABS
stages.content:
Excisional biopsy: scattered large binucleate cells with prominent eosinophilic ''owl-eye'' nucleoli (Reed-Sternberg cells) in a mixed inflammatory background
Immunohistochemistry: tumor cells CD15+ and CD30+, CD20-, CD45-
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, WBC 9.2 ×10³/µL (eosinophilia present), Platelets 320 ×10³/µL
ESR 68 mm/hr
LDH mildly elevated; HIV antibody negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CD15+/CD30+ Reed-Sternberg cells in a mixed inflammatory background confirm the diagnosis. What is it?
stages.explanation: Reed-Sternberg cells expressing CD15 and CD30 (and characteristically negative for CD20 and CD45) within a mixed reactive background of lymphocytes and eosinophils define classic Hodgkin lymphoma. Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma is a sheet of CD20+ large B cells without Reed-Sternberg cells. Reactive lymphadenitis lacks malignant Reed-Sternberg cells and would not cause progressive B symptoms and weight loss. Chronic lymphocytic leukemia is a clonal small mature B-lymphocyte proliferation seen on blood counts/flow cytometry, not an excisional node with Reed-Sternberg cells.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which study is essential for staging before treatment is planned?
options: Bone marrow biopsy in all patients regardless of imaging
PET/CT of the neck, chest, abdomen, and pelvis to determine the Ann Arbor stage
stages.explanation: PET/CT is the standard staging study for Hodgkin lymphoma, mapping all involved nodal and extranodal sites to assign the Ann Arbor stage (modified with B-symptom designation) and to serve as the baseline for interim response assessment. Routine bone marrow biopsy is now often omitted when PET/CT adequately assesses marrow involvement. Lumbar puncture is not part of routine Hodgkin staging because CNS involvement is rare. Mammography is unrelated to staging this lymphoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Staging shows nodal disease on both sides of the diaphragm with B symptoms (advanced stage). Which first-line chemotherapy regimen is standard, and what long-term toxicity must be counseled?
options: R-CHOP, with cardiomyopathy as the main late risk
ABVD (or A+AVD), with bleomycin-related pulmonary toxicity and doxorubicin cardiotoxicity among the key risks
Single-agent rituximab, with infusion reactions only
stages.explanation: ABVD (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine), or brentuximab-AVD in advanced disease, is the standard backbone for classic Hodgkin lymphoma. Key toxicities to counsel include bleomycin-induced pulmonary fibrosis, doxorubicin cardiotoxicity, and infertility, plus long-term secondary malignancy risk. R-CHOP treats CD20+ aggressive B-cell lymphomas such as DLBCL, not Hodgkin (whose Reed-Sternberg cells are CD20-negative). FOLFOX is a colorectal cancer regimen. Single-agent rituximab is inadequate for classic Hodgkin lymphoma given the CD20-negative malignant cells.

cases.diagnosis: Classic Hodgkin lymphoma (advanced stage with B symptoms)
pearls: A young patient with a firm, painless cervical/supraclavicular node, B symptoms, and alcohol-induced node pain should raise suspicion for Hodgkin lymphoma.
Excisional biopsy is required to see architecture; Reed-Sternberg cells are CD15+ and CD30+ but CD20- and CD45-.
PET/CT defines the Ann Arbor stage and is the modern staging and response-assessment tool, often replacing routine marrow biopsy.
ABVD (or brentuximab-AVD) is first-line; counsel on bleomycin lung toxicity, anthracycline cardiotoxicity, infertility, and secondary malignancies.

cases.title: A Fast-Growing Mass and Mounting Fevers
cases.chiefComplaint: A rapidly enlarging neck mass over three weeks with fevers and night sweats.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman is admitted for a rapidly enlarging right neck mass that has roughly doubled in size over three weeks. She reports daily fevers, drenching night sweats, and a 7 kg weight loss in two months. She has fatigue and decreased appetite but denies localized pain. She has no cough or dysphagia, though the mass feels increasingly tight. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes and hypothyroidism; medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily and levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. She has no known drug allergies and takes no anticoagulants. She is a retired nurse, drinks rarely, and never smoked. Family history includes a mother with lymphoma. Review of systems is positive for early satiety and intermittent abdominal fullness, but negative for hematochezia, neurologic symptoms, or rash. She has not traveled recently and has no known immunodeficiency, though she had shingles two years ago. She reports no recent infections and no sick contacts.
cases.exam: The patient appears fatigued and mildly ill. There is a 6 cm firm, fixed, nontender mass in the right cervical chain with associated supraclavicular adenopathy; the overlying skin is intact. There is no airway stridor at rest. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal aside from mild tachycardia. The abdomen is soft; the spleen is palpable 4 cm below the costal margin and the liver edge is enlarged. There is no peripheral lymphedema. Skin shows no purpura. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. There is no jugular venous distension and no facial plethora.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A rapidly enlarging nodal mass with B symptoms in an older adult suggests an aggressive lymphoma. What is the most appropriate diagnostic step?
options: Excisional or generous core-needle biopsy with immunohistochemistry and flow cytometry
Serial observation for six weeks to see if it regresses
stages.explanation: An excisional or generous core-needle biopsy with immunohistochemistry, flow cytometry, and molecular studies is required to subclassify an aggressive lymphoma and guide therapy. Observation is inappropriate for a rapidly growing mass with B symptoms, where delay risks airway and disease progression. Fine-needle aspiration alone usually cannot establish lymphoma subtype because it lacks architecture and adequate tissue for the necessary panel. Empiric corticosteroids can transiently shrink and obscure lymphoma histology, compromising the diagnostic biopsy, so they should be avoided until tissue is obtained.

stages.label: BIOPSY & LABS
stages.content:
Core biopsy: diffuse sheets of large atypical lymphoid cells effacing nodal architecture
Immunohistochemistry: CD20+, CD45+, CD3-; Ki-67 proliferation index ~80%
FISH: no MYC/BCL2/BCL6 ''double-hit'' rearrangement
Hemoglobin 10.6 g/dL, WBC 8.0 ×10³/µL, Platelets 210 ×10³/µL
LDH 540 U/L (elevated)
HIV negative, hepatitis B surface antigen negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Sheets of large CD20+ B cells with a high Ki-67 confirm the diagnosis. What is it?
stages.explanation: Diffuse sheets of large CD20+ B cells effacing nodal architecture with a high proliferation index (Ki-67 ~80%) define diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, the most common aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Follicular lymphoma grade 1 is an indolent, small-cleaved-cell, follicular-pattern lymphoma with a low Ki-67, not diffuse large cells. Hodgkin lymphoma shows CD15+/CD30+ Reed-Sternberg cells in a mixed background, not diffuse large CD20+ sheets. CLL is a proliferation of small mature B lymphocytes (CD5+/CD23+), not large transformed cells.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before starting chemoimmunotherapy, which pretreatment evaluations are most important in this patient?
options: PET/CT staging, echocardiogram (cardiac function), hepatitis B serologies, and consideration of CNS-prophylaxis risk
Genetic testing of all family members
stages.explanation: Pre-treatment work-up for DLBCL includes PET/CT for staging and the International Prognostic Index, an echocardiogram or MUGA to assess ejection fraction before anthracycline (doxorubicin) therapy, hepatitis B serologies because rituximab can reactivate HBV, and assessment of CNS relapse risk (CNS-IPI) to decide on prophylaxis. A CBC alone is insufficient. Genetic testing of relatives is not part of standard DLBCL care. Bone densitometry is not a relevant pretreatment requirement here, though steroid effects are monitored over time.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has adequate cardiac function and negative HBV serologies. What is the standard first-line therapy for DLBCL?
stages.explanation: R-CHOP (rituximab plus cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone) every 21 days is the standard curative-intent regimen for DLBCL; adding rituximab to CHOP substantially improved survival, and DLBCL is potentially curable even at advanced stage. ABVD treats Hodgkin lymphoma, whose cells are CD20-negative. Watchful waiting is appropriate for indolent lymphomas, not an aggressive, rapidly growing DLBCL that is curable and life-threatening if untreated. Single-agent ibrutinib is not standard first-line therapy and is inadequate as monotherapy for DLBCL.

cases.diagnosis: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphoma)
pearls: A rapidly enlarging nodal mass with B symptoms and elevated LDH in an older adult suggests an aggressive lymphoma like DLBCL.
Diagnosis requires excisional or generous core biopsy; DLBCL shows diffuse sheets of CD20+ large B cells with a high Ki-67; avoid steroids before biopsy.
Pretreatment work-up includes PET/CT staging, echocardiogram before anthracyclines, HBV serologies before rituximab, and CNS relapse risk assessment.
R-CHOP is curative-intent first-line therapy; check for MYC/BCL2/BCL6 double-hit rearrangements, which carry a worse prognosis.

cases.title: A Boy With a Swollen Belly That Grew Overnight
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapidly enlarging abdominal swelling with pain and vomiting over one week.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy is admitted for an abdominal mass that his parents say has grown noticeably over just one week, with worsening abdominal pain, decreased appetite, and several episodes of vomiting. He has had intermittent fevers and now reports that he has not had a bowel movement in two days. His parents also noticed a firm swelling along the right jaw. Past medical history is unremarkable; immunizations are up to date. He takes no medications and has no drug allergies. He lives with his parents and a younger sibling, attends school, and has had no recent travel; the family has no farm or animal exposure beyond a house cat. Family history is noncontributory.
