Review of systems is positive for fatigue and night sweats but negative for cough, dysuria, rash, or headache. The mass and the rapid trajectory have alarmed the parents, who note he was completely well a month ago. There is no history of recurrent infections or known immunodeficiency.
cases.exam: The boy is uncomfortable and guarding. The abdomen is distended with a large, firm right-lower-quadrant mass and diffuse tenderness; bowel sounds are reduced. There is a firm, nontender swelling along the right mandible. There is no airway compromise. Cardiopulmonary exam shows tachycardia with clear lungs. There is mild hepatosplenomegaly. No peripheral lymphadenopathy is appreciated elsewhere. Skin shows no petechiae. Neurologic exam is nonfocal with no cranial nerve deficits. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. There is no scleral icterus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A child with an explosively growing abdominal mass and jaw swelling needs urgent work-up. Beyond imaging and biopsy planning, which baseline labs are most critical to obtain immediately?
options: Thyroid function tests and vitamin D level
Tumor lysis panel: potassium, phosphorus, calcium, uric acid, LDH, and renal function
stages.explanation: A rapidly proliferating tumor like this places the child at extreme risk for tumor lysis syndrome, so the immediate labs are a tumor lysis panel: potassium, phosphorus, calcium, uric acid, LDH, plus creatinine/BUN to assess renal function. These detect the hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia, and hyperuricemia that can occur even before therapy in high-burden disease and that worsen acutely once treatment begins. Thyroid tests, vitamin D, iron studies, B12, lipids, and HbA1c are irrelevant to the acute oncologic emergency and would delay recognition of life-threatening metabolic derangements.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Potassium 5.6 mEq/L, Phosphorus 6.8 mg/dL
Calcium 7.8 mg/dL, Uric acid 11.2 mg/dL
LDH 1,850 U/L, Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (elevated for age)
WBC 12.0 ×10³/µL, Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, Platelets 140 ×10³/µL
CT abdomen: bulky ileocecal/mesenteric mass with lymphadenopathy
Biopsy: medium-sized monomorphic lymphoid cells with a ''starry-sky'' pattern of tingible-body macrophages; Ki-67 nearly 100%; MYC rearrangement (t(8;14)) detected

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The starry-sky histology, near-100% Ki-67, and MYC (t(8;14)) rearrangement establish the diagnosis. What is it?
stages.explanation: Burkitt lymphoma is a highly aggressive B-cell lymphoma defined by a MYC translocation (most commonly t(8;14) with the immunoglobulin heavy-chain locus), a starry-sky pattern from tingible-body macrophages engulfing apoptotic debris, and a near-100% proliferation index. The sporadic form classically presents in children as an abdominal/ileocecal mass, while the endemic (EBV-associated, African) form favors the jaw. Follicular lymphoma is indolent with a low Ki-67. Hodgkin lymphoma has Reed-Sternberg cells. Neuroblastoma is a small-round-blue-cell tumor of neural crest origin (Homer-Wright rosettes, elevated catecholamines), not a MYC-rearranged B-cell lymphoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the high tumor burden and elevated uric acid, which measure is most important to start before and during cytotoxic chemotherapy?
options: Fluid restriction to protect the kidneys
Aggressive IV hydration plus rasburicase (urate oxidase) and close electrolyte monitoring
Allopurinol alone with no IV fluids
Loop diuretics as the primary strategy
stages.explanation: Preventing and treating tumor lysis syndrome requires aggressive IV hydration to maintain high urine output plus rasburicase, which enzymatically converts existing uric acid to soluble allantoin and is preferred over allopurinol when uric acid is already high (as here, 11.2 mg/dL). Frequent electrolyte monitoring and management of hyperkalemia and hyperphosphatemia are essential. Fluid restriction is dangerous and worsens crystal nephropathy. Allopurinol only blocks new uric acid formation and is insufficient for established hyperuricemia. Loop diuretics are not first-line and can worsen volume depletion and urate precipitation if the patient is not well hydrated.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Twelve hours after starting chemotherapy, his potassium rises to 6.8 mEq/L with peaked T waves on ECG. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Repeat the potassium in the morning and observe
Give IV calcium gluconate for cardiac membrane stabilization, then insulin/glucose (and other measures) to shift and remove potassium; consider urgent dialysis
Administer oral potassium binder and discharge
Increase the chemotherapy dose to lyse the tumor faster
stages.explanation: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate IV calcium gluconate to stabilize the myocardium, followed by insulin with glucose (and beta-agonists) to shift potassium intracellularly, plus measures to remove potassium; refractory tumor-lysis hyperkalemia and renal compromise may require urgent hemodialysis. Waiting until morning risks fatal arrhythmia. A binder alone acts too slowly for symptomatic hyperkalemia with ECG changes, and discharge is unsafe. Increasing chemotherapy would worsen tumor lysis and hyperkalemia.

cases.diagnosis: Burkitt lymphoma (sporadic, MYC-rearranged) with tumor lysis syndrome
pearls: Burkitt lymphoma is among the fastest-growing human tumors; sporadic disease presents in children as an abdominal/ileocecal mass, while the endemic EBV form favors the jaw.
Histology shows a starry-sky pattern with a near-100% Ki-67, and the defining lesion is a MYC translocation, most commonly t(8;14).
Tumor lysis syndrome (hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hyperuricemia, hypocalcemia) is the key emergency; prevent with aggressive hydration and rasburicase when uric acid is high.
Hyperkalemia with ECG changes requires immediate calcium gluconate for membrane stabilization, then potassium-shifting and removal, with dialysis if refractory.

cases.title: Painless Swellings That Come and Go
cases.chiefComplaint: Painless, slowly enlarging lymph nodes in the neck and groin noticed over several months.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man reports several months of painless, slowly enlarging lumps in his neck, armpits, and groin. He says they seem to wax and wane slightly in size but have not resolved. He feels generally well with normal energy and no fevers, no drenching night sweats, and no weight loss. He has no bone pain, abdominal pain, or pruritus. Past medical history includes hyperlipidemia and GERD; medications are atorvastatin 20 mg and omeprazole 20 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies and takes no anticoagulants. He is an accountant, drinks alcohol moderately, and has a 10-pack-year remote smoking history, quit 12 years ago. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable, with no recurrent infections, easy bruising, or early satiety. He has not traveled internationally and has no occupational chemical exposures. He sought care mainly because the lumps have not gone away and his spouse encouraged him to be checked.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and comfortable. There are multiple discrete, rubbery, nontender, mobile lymph nodes in the cervical (up to 2 cm), bilateral axillary, and inguinal regions. The oropharynx is clear. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. The abdomen is soft; the spleen tip is just palpable, with no hepatomegaly. There is no peripheral edema and no skin rash or purpura. Neurologic exam is normal. There is no jugular venous distension. He has no pallor or scleral icterus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An adult with chronic, painless, generalized lymphadenopathy and no B symptoms needs tissue diagnosis. What is the most appropriate step?
options: Excisional lymph node biopsy with immunohistochemistry and cytogenetics
Antibiotic trial and reassess in three months
stages.explanation: Persistent, painless, generalized lymphadenopathy in an adult warrants excisional lymph node biopsy with immunohistochemistry and cytogenetics to identify an indolent lymphoma and grade it; architecture is needed to recognize the follicular pattern. An antibiotic trial is inappropriate when nodes have persisted for months without infectious features. Fine-needle aspiration alone usually cannot establish lymphoma subtype or grade because it lacks architecture. Reassurance without diagnosis is unsafe given the duration and distribution, even though the patient currently feels well.

stages.label: BIOPSY & STAGING
stages.content:
Excisional biopsy: nodular (follicular) proliferation of small cleaved centrocytes and centroblasts; low grade (grade 1-2)
Immunohistochemistry: CD20+, CD10+, BCL2+, BCL6+
FISH: t(14;18) with BCL2-IGH rearrangement
Ki-67 proliferation index ~10% (low)
Hemoglobin 14.0 g/dL, WBC 6.8 ×10³/µL, Platelets 250 ×10³/µL, LDH normal
PET/CT: nodal disease above and below the diaphragm; no large bulky mass; marrow uninvolved on biopsy

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The follicular architecture, CD10+/BCL2+ phenotype, and t(14;18) confirm the diagnosis. What is it?
stages.explanation: A nodular (follicular) proliferation of small cleaved centrocytes with a CD10+/BCL6+/BCL2+ phenotype and the t(14;18) BCL2-IGH translocation, with a low Ki-67, defines follicular lymphoma, the most common indolent non-Hodgkin lymphoma. DLBCL shows diffuse sheets of large cells with a high Ki-67. Burkitt lymphoma has a near-100% Ki-67 and a MYC translocation. Reactive follicular hyperplasia is BCL2-negative in germinal centers and lacks the clonal t(14;18); the BCL2 positivity and t(14;18) here mark a clonal malignancy rather than a reactive process.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is asymptomatic with low-grade, low-tumor-burden follicular lymphoma and no cytopenias or compressive symptoms. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Active surveillance (''watch and wait'') with periodic clinical and lab follow-up
Aggressive multi-agent chemotherapy as for Burkitt lymphoma
stages.explanation: For asymptomatic, low-tumor-burden follicular lymphoma, active surveillance (''watch and wait'') is appropriate because early treatment has not been shown to improve overall survival in this indolent, generally incurable but very treatable disease; therapy is reserved for symptoms, bulky or compressive disease, cytopenias, or rapid progression. Immediate R-CHOP exposes an asymptomatic patient to unnecessary toxicity. Autologous transplant is not first-line in this setting. Burkitt-style intensive chemotherapy is wildly inappropriate for an indolent, low-Ki-67 lymphoma.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Two years later he develops rapidly enlarging adenopathy in one site, new B symptoms, and a sharply rising LDH. What complication must be excluded?
options: Histologic transformation to an aggressive lymphoma (e.g., diffuse large B-cell lymphoma)
Reactive hyperplasia from a viral illness
stages.explanation: Indolent follicular lymphoma can undergo histologic transformation to an aggressive lymphoma, typically diffuse large B-cell lymphoma; the classic clues are rapid growth at one site, new B symptoms, and a disproportionate rise in LDH. This requires re-biopsy (often guided by PET-avid sites) and a switch to aggressive-lymphoma therapy such as R-CHOP. Bacterial lymphadenitis and viral reactive hyperplasia do not cause a sharp LDH rise with B symptoms in this context. Iron deficiency anemia does not explain rapidly enlarging adenopathy and constitutional symptoms.

cases.diagnosis: Follicular lymphoma (low-grade, indolent non-Hodgkin lymphoma)
pearls: Follicular lymphoma is the most common indolent NHL: chronic, painless, waxing-and-waning generalized lymphadenopathy without B symptoms.
Hallmarks are a follicular pattern, CD10+/BCL2+/BCL6+ phenotype, the t(14;18) BCL2-IGH translocation, and a low Ki-67.
Asymptomatic, low-tumor-burden disease is managed with watch-and-wait, since early treatment does not improve survival in this incurable but indolent disease.
Suspect histologic transformation to DLBCL when one site grows rapidly with new B symptoms and a sharply rising LDH; re-biopsy and escalate therapy.

cases.title: Swollen Legs, a Big Tongue, and a Failing Heart
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive leg swelling, fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and a feeling that his tongue has enlarged.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man is admitted for several months of worsening bilateral leg swelling, fatigue, and exertional dyspnea, now with orthostatic lightheadedness. He has noticed his tongue feels larger and he occasionally bites it, and his speech sounds different. He reports easy bruising, including periorbital bruising after minor straining, and foamy urine. He has lost 5 kg. Past medical history includes hypertension, but his blood pressures have recently been running low and his antihypertensives were stopped. Medications now are only a multivitamin; he takes no anticoagulants and has no drug allergies. He is a retired electrician, drinks rarely, and never smoked. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for numbness in the hands suggesting carpal tunnel, and for diarrhea alternating with constipation; it is negative for chest pain, fevers, and hemoptysis. He has not traveled recently. He notes that he becomes dizzy on standing and has nearly fainted twice, which prompted this admission.
cases.exam: The patient appears chronically ill with periorbital ecchymoses (''raccoon eyes''). The tongue is enlarged with lateral dental indentations (macroglossia). There is jugular venous distension and bilateral pitting edema to the thighs. Heart sounds are distant; lungs have bibasilar crackles. The abdomen is soft with mild hepatomegaly. There is a positive Tinel sign bilaterally at the wrists and a stocking-distribution sensory neuropathy. Skin shows scattered ecchymoses. On standing, blood pressure falls with reproduction of lightheadedness (orthostatic hypotension). There is no lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Macroglossia, periorbital purpura, nephrotic-range proteinuria, restrictive heart disease, neuropathy, and orthostasis suggest a systemic infiltrative disorder. Which initial work-up best screens for a clonal plasma cell process?
options: Serum and urine protein electrophoresis with immunofixation and serum free light chains
stages.explanation: Serum and urine immunofixation with the serum free light chain assay is the most sensitive screen for the clonal light-chain process underlying AL amyloidosis; the free light chain ratio is abnormal in the great majority of cases and helps quantify the clone. Coronary angiography evaluates obstructive coronary disease, not infiltrative restrictive cardiomyopathy. TSH screens for thyroid disease, which does not cause this multisystem picture with monoclonal protein. ANA and complements screen for autoimmune disease, not a plasma cell dyscrasia, and would not explain macroglossia and periorbital purpura.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Serum free light chains: lambda markedly elevated, kappa/lambda ratio very low (lambda clone)
Urine: 6.5 g protein/24 h (nephrotic range), albumin predominant
NT-proBNP markedly elevated; troponin mildly elevated
ECG: low QRS voltage; Echo: thickened ventricular walls with a ''granular sparkling'' myocardium and preserved-to-reduced EF (restrictive physiology)
SPEP: small monoclonal lambda band
Bone marrow: 8% clonal plasma cells, lambda-restricted

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which biopsy finding would definitively confirm AL amyloidosis?
options: Sheets of plasma cells exceeding 60% in the marrow
Congo red staining showing apple-green birefringence under polarized light, with typing confirming AL (lambda light chain) amyloid
Reed-Sternberg cells on lymph node biopsy
stages.explanation: Amyloidosis is confirmed by Congo red staining of involved tissue (or an abdominal fat pad aspirate), which shows apple-green birefringence under polarized light; typing (by mass spectrometry or immunohistochemistry) then confirms AL (immunoglobulin light-chain, here lambda) amyloid rather than transthyretin or other types. Over 60% marrow plasma cells would indicate plasma cell myeloma, a different threshold and not the confirmatory amyloid test. Noncaseating granulomas indicate sarcoidosis. Reed-Sternberg cells indicate Hodgkin lymphoma. The lambda light-chain clone plus apple-green birefringence defines AL amyloidosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Congo red is positive and typing confirms AL (lambda) amyloid. Which is the most important early management consideration for his cardiac involvement?
options: Aggressive nodal-dose diuresis combined with high-dose beta-blockade and an ACE inhibitor
Cautious diuresis for volume overload while avoiding agents poorly tolerated in amyloid cardiomyopathy (e.g., many beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, calcium channel blockers, and digoxin)
Start full-dose nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers for rate control
stages.explanation: Amyloid cardiomyopathy is a restrictive, preload-dependent disease, so management is cautious diuresis for congestion while avoiding drugs that are poorly tolerated: beta-blockers and calcium channel blockers can drop the rate-dependent cardiac output, ACE inhibitors/ARBs often cause profound hypotension, and digoxin binds amyloid fibrils with increased toxicity risk. Aggressive beta-blockade plus ACE inhibition can precipitate dangerous hypotension. Coronary stenting addresses obstructive CAD, not infiltrative disease. Nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers are specifically hazardous in amyloid cardiomyopathy. Definitive therapy targets the plasma cell clone (e.g., daratumumab-CyBorD).

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: He is stabilized hemodynamically. Which is the appropriate direction for definitive disease-directed therapy?
options: Plasma-cell-directed therapy (e.g., daratumumab plus bortezomib-cyclophosphamide-dexamethasone), with autologous transplant reserved for eligible patients
Watchful waiting with no clone-directed treatment
Renal transplantation as the primary first treatment
stages.explanation: Definitive AL amyloidosis therapy targets the underlying plasma cell clone to halt toxic light-chain production; daratumumab added to bortezomib-cyclophosphamide-dexamethasone (Dara-CyBorD) is a standard first-line regimen, with autologous stem cell transplant reserved for carefully selected, organ-function-eligible patients. Antibiotics have no role. Watchful waiting is inappropriate because untreated AL amyloidosis, especially with cardiac involvement, has a poor prognosis and progressive organ failure. Renal transplant does not address the source of amyloidogenic light chains and would not be the primary treatment; the clone must be controlled first.

cases.diagnosis: AL (light-chain) amyloidosis with cardiac, renal, and neurologic involvement
pearls: Suspect AL amyloidosis with the multisystem combination of nephrotic-range proteinuria, restrictive cardiomyopathy, macroglossia, periorbital purpura, and peripheral/autonomic neuropathy.
Screen with serum/urine immunofixation and free light chains; confirm with Congo red apple-green birefringence and type the amyloid (AL vs transthyretin).
Amyloid cardiomyopathy is preload-dependent: diurese cautiously and avoid beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, and digoxin.
Definitive treatment targets the plasma cell clone (e.g., daratumumab-based regimens), with autologous transplant for selected eligible patients.

cases.title: Painless Neck Lumps and Months of Night Sweats
cases.chiefComplaint: Enlarging, painless neck and groin lumps with fatigue and night sweats over three months.
cases.history: A 65-year-old man presents with three months of progressively enlarging, nontender lymph nodes in his neck, axillae, and groin. He reports drenching night sweats, a 6 kg unintentional weight loss, and worsening fatigue. Over the past month he has had abdominal fullness, early satiety, and looser, more frequent stools with occasional bright-red blood. He denies fevers, chest pain, or dyspnea. Past medical history includes hypertension on lisinopril 20 mg daily and well-controlled type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg twice daily. He takes no anticoagulants. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired accountant, drinks two beers weekly, and quit smoking 20 years ago (15 pack-years). There is no known farm or chemical exposure. Family history is notable for a sister with breast cancer; no hematologic malignancy. Review of systems is positive for mild pruritus but negative for bone pain, headaches, or visual changes. He reports no recent infections, travel, or sick contacts.
cases.exam: The patient is thin but not acutely ill. There is bilateral cervical, axillary, and inguinal lymphadenopathy with multiple firm, mobile, nontender nodes up to 3 cm. Waldeyer ring appears mildly enlarged on oropharyngeal inspection. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable. The abdomen is soft; the spleen is palpable 4 cm below the left costal margin and the liver edge is felt 2 cm below the costal margin, both nontender. There is no sternal tenderness, jaundice, or peripheral edema. Skin shows no rash or petechiae. Neurologic exam is intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which diagnostic procedure is most appropriate to characterize this patient's generalized lymphadenopathy?
options: Fine-needle aspiration of a cervical node
Excisional lymph node biopsy of an accessible enlarged node
Empiric trial of broad-spectrum antibiotics with reassessment in 4 weeks
Serum lactate dehydrogenase and PET scan alone, deferring tissue sampling
stages.explanation: Excisional (or generous core) lymph node biopsy is required to diagnose and subclassify lymphoma because architectural pattern and immunophenotype are essential. Fine-needle aspiration yields cells without preserved architecture and frequently misses or misclassifies lymphoma, so it is inadequate for initial diagnosis. Empiric antibiotics waste time in a patient with B symptoms, weight loss, and hepatosplenomegaly that strongly suggest malignancy rather than infection. LDH and PET help with staging and prognosis but cannot establish a tissue diagnosis or subtype; treatment cannot be chosen without histology and immunohistochemistry.

stages.label: BIOPSY & LABS
stages.content:
Excisional cervical node: diffuse/nodular infiltrate of small-to-medium atypical lymphoid cells with irregular nuclei
Immunohistochemistry: CD20+, CD5+, CD23-, cyclin D1 (BCL1) strongly positive
FISH: t(11;14)(q13;q32) IGH::CCND1 detected
Ki-67 proliferation index 45%
WBC 22.0 ×10³/µL with circulating atypical lymphocytes
Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL, Platelets 110 ×10³/µL
LDH 480 U/L (elevated)
Colonoscopy: multiple mucosal polypoid lesions (lymphomatous polyposis)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the immunophenotype and cytogenetics, which diagnosis is most consistent with these findings?
options: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia / small lymphocytic lymphoma
stages.explanation: The combination CD20+, CD5+, CD23-negative, cyclin D1 overexpression, and t(11;14)(q13;q32) producing IGH::CCND1 fusion is the defining signature of mantle cell lymphoma; gastrointestinal involvement as lymphomatous polyposis is a classic feature. CLL/SLL is also CD5+ but is CD23-positive and cyclin D1-negative. Follicular lymphoma is CD5-negative, CD10-positive, and driven by t(14;18) with BCL2, not cyclin D1. Marginal zone lymphoma is typically CD5- and CD23-negative without cyclin D1 expression. Cyclin D1 positivity with t(11;14) is the single most specific discriminator here, separating mantle cell lymphoma from the other CD5+ small B-cell neoplasm, CLL/SLL.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before initiating therapy, which study is most important for completing staging and prognostic assessment in this patient?
options: Bone marrow biopsy with PET/CT and assessment of the MIPI score and Ki-67
Echocardiogram and pulmonary function testing only
JAK2 V617F and BCR-ABL1 molecular testing
stages.explanation: Mantle cell lymphoma is staged with PET/CT and bone marrow biopsy, and prognosis is refined with the Mantle Cell Lymphoma International Prognostic Index (MIPI) plus the Ki-67 proliferation index, which guide intensity of therapy. Baseline echocardiogram and PFTs are reasonable pre-chemotherapy but do not stage the lymphoma or assign prognosis. JAK2 and BCR-ABL1 testing evaluate myeloproliferative neoplasms and CML, which are unrelated to this diagnosis. SPEP/immunofixation evaluates plasma cell dyscrasias; mantle cell lymphoma does not produce a clinically relevant monoclonal paraprotein, so it adds nothing to staging here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Staging confirms widespread stage IV disease with marrow involvement and Ki-67 of 45%. The patient is fit with good performance status. Which initial treatment approach is most appropriate?
options: Active surveillance (watch and wait) without therapy
Intensive cytarabine-containing chemoimmunotherapy with rituximab, followed by consideration of autologous stem cell transplant consolidation
stages.explanation: For a fit patient with symptomatic, widespread, biologically aggressive mantle cell lymphoma (high Ki-67, B symptoms, extranodal disease), intensive cytarabine-containing chemoimmunotherapy with rituximab followed by autologous stem cell transplant consolidation maximizes depth and duration of remission. Watch-and-wait is reserved for the uncommon, indolent, asymptomatic, low-burden presentation, not this aggressive bulky case. Rituximab monotherapy is insufficient as initial control of high-burden disease. Splenectomy does not treat systemic nodal, marrow, and gastrointestinal disease and is not a primary therapy for mantle cell lymphoma.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The patient relapses 18 months after first-line therapy with rapidly progressive nodal disease. Which targeted agent class is most appropriate for relapsed mantle cell lymphoma?
options: Imatinib, a BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor
A Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor such as acalabrutinib or zanubrutinib
stages.explanation: BTK inhibitors (acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib, ibrutinib) are standard, highly active therapy for relapsed/refractory mantle cell lymphoma; CAR T-cell therapy is another option for BTK-exposed disease. Imatinib targets BCR-ABL1 in CML and has no role here. ATRA is differentiation therapy specific to acute promyelocytic leukemia. Hydroxyurea is a nonspecific cytoreductive agent used in myeloproliferative neoplasms and offers no durable benefit in relapsed mantle cell lymphoma. Targeting B-cell receptor signaling through BTK exploits the dependence of mantle cell lymphoma on this pathway, producing high response rates after chemoimmunotherapy failure.

cases.diagnosis: Mantle cell lymphoma (stage IV, cyclin D1+/t(11;14), high Ki-67)
pearls: Mantle cell lymphoma is CD5+ but CD23-negative and cyclin D1-positive with t(11;14)(q13;q32) IGH::CCND1, distinguishing it from CD23+ CLL/SLL.
Gastrointestinal lymphomatous polyposis and leukemic peripheral blood involvement are characteristic of mantle cell lymphoma.
Fit patients receive cytarabine-based chemoimmunotherapy plus rituximab and autologous transplant consolidation; BTK inhibitors and CAR T-cell therapy treat relapse.
Prognosis is stratified by the MIPI score and Ki-67 proliferation index; high Ki-67 predicts aggressive behavior.

cases.title: Years of Scaly, Itchy Patches That Won't Clear
cases.chiefComplaint: Multiple chronic, itchy, scaly skin patches and plaques that have failed years of treatment.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man presents with a 5-year history of pruritic, scaly, erythematous patches over his trunk, buttocks, and proximal thighs that wax and wane but never fully resolve. The lesions favor sun-protected areas and have slowly thickened into raised plaques over the last year. He has been treated by several clinicians for presumed eczema and psoriasis with topical steroids and emollients, with only partial, temporary relief. He denies fever, drenching night sweats, or weight loss but reports significant nighttime itching that disturbs sleep. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes only over-the-counter antihistamines and uses topical triamcinolone 0.1% ointment. He has no known drug allergies. He works as an electrician, is married, and has a 10 pack-year remote smoking history. He drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is negative for skin cancer or lymphoma. Review of systems is negative for lymph node swelling that he has noticed, joint pain, or new moles. He has had no new soaps, detergents, or occupational chemical exposures that correlate with the rash.
cases.exam: Skin shows multiple well-demarcated, erythematous to violaceous patches and indurated plaques with fine scale distributed over the lower trunk, buttocks, and medial thighs in a 'bathing trunk' pattern, sparing the face. Several plaques show variation in thickness and an atrophic, wrinkled (poikilodermatous) quality. There is no generalized erythroderma and no skin ulceration. There is no palpable cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the examination, including abdominal and neurologic exams, is normal. Nails and oral mucosa are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given chronic patches and plaques refractory to treatment for presumed eczema and psoriasis, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Punch skin biopsy of a representative plaque for histopathology and immunophenotyping
Empiric escalation to high-potency topical clobetasol for 8 more weeks
Patch testing for allergic contact dermatitis
stages.explanation: Chronic, treatment-refractory patches and plaques in a 'bathing trunk' distribution with poikiloderma raise concern for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, and a punch skin biopsy with histopathology and T-cell immunophenotyping/clonality is the essential diagnostic step. Further empiric high-potency steroid escalation only delays diagnosis after years of failed topical therapy. Patch testing evaluates allergic contact dermatitis, which does not produce indurated poikilodermatous plaques over 5 years. PET/CT is a staging tool used after a tissue diagnosis is established; it cannot diagnose the disease and is not the first step before biopsy.

stages.label: SKIN BIOPSY
stages.content:
Histopathology: band-like infiltrate of atypical lymphocytes in the upper dermis with epidermotropism
Atypical cells with cerebriform (convoluted) nuclei
Intraepidermal collections of atypical lymphocytes (Pautrier microabscesses)
Immunophenotype: CD3+, CD4+, CD8-, with partial loss of CD7
T-cell receptor gene rearrangement: clonal
Peripheral blood Sezary cell count: <5% (not elevated)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The biopsy shows epidermotropic atypical CD4+ T cells with cerebriform nuclei and Pautrier microabscesses. Which diagnosis is most consistent?
stages.explanation: Mycosis fungoides is the most common cutaneous T-cell lymphoma; its hallmark histology is an epidermotropic infiltrate of atypical CD4+ T lymphocytes with cerebriform nuclei and intraepidermal clusters called Pautrier microabscesses, with a clonal T-cell receptor rearrangement. Psoriasis shows regular acanthosis, parakeratosis, and neutrophilic Munro microabscesses, not atypical lymphocytes. Nummular eczema is a reactive spongiotic dermatitis without a monoclonal atypical T-cell infiltrate. Cutaneous B-cell lymphoma is a CD20+ B-cell process lacking epidermotropism and Pautrier microabscesses; the CD3+/CD4+ phenotype here confirms a T-cell, not B-cell, lineage.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After confirming the diagnosis, which evaluation is most important to determine stage and guide therapy?
options: Complete skin assessment (body surface area, patch/plaque/tumor), lymph node exam, and peripheral blood for Sezary cells with imaging as indicated
Immediate allogeneic stem cell transplant evaluation
Serum IgE level and food allergy panel
stages.explanation: Mycosis fungoides is staged by the TNMB system, which requires quantifying skin involvement (patches, plaques, tumors, and body surface area), assessing lymph nodes, evaluating peripheral blood for circulating Sezary cells (the B classification), and imaging when nodal or visceral disease is suspected. This staging determines whether skin-directed or systemic therapy is appropriate. Allogeneic transplant is reserved for advanced, refractory disease, not initial staging. Serum IgE and food allergy panels evaluate atopic disease, not lymphoma. A Coombs test evaluates autoimmune hemolysis and is irrelevant to staging cutaneous lymphoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Staging confirms limited patch/plaque disease (stage IB) without nodal, visceral, or significant blood involvement. Which is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
options: Skin-directed therapy such as topical corticosteroids, topical chemotherapy/retinoids, or phototherapy (NB-UVB/PUVA)
stages.explanation: Early-stage (IA-IIA) mycosis fungoides limited to skin is treated with skin-directed therapies: topical corticosteroids, topical nitrogen mustard or retinoids, and phototherapy with narrow-band UVB or PUVA, sometimes with localized radiation. These control disease with low toxicity and excellent prognosis. Multiagent chemotherapy such as CHOP is reserved for advanced or transformed disease and does not improve survival in early stages while adding toxicity. Allogeneic transplant is for advanced, refractory disease. Splenectomy has no role in mycosis fungoides. Matching therapy intensity to limited skin-only stage is the key principle.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Years later the patient develops diffuse erythema involving over 80% of his body surface with intense pruritus, and a high circulating atypical T-cell (Sezary) count is found. What does this transformation most likely represent?
options: Progression to Sezary syndrome, the leukemic/erythrodermic variant
stages.explanation: Erythroderma involving most of the body surface plus a high burden of circulating malignant cerebriform (Sezary) cells defines Sezary syndrome, the aggressive leukemic/erythrodermic variant of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma that can arise from progression of mycosis fungoides; it carries a worse prognosis and requires systemic therapy (e.g., extracorporeal photopheresis, retinoids/bexarotene, interferon, mogamulizumab, or romidepsin). A drug reaction to phototherapy would not produce clonal circulating Sezary cells. Widespread erythroderma with a rising malignant cell count is the opposite of resolution. Psoriasis does not generate circulating clonal atypical T cells; the leukemic blood involvement confirms lymphoma progression, not a benign dermatosis.

cases.diagnosis: Mycosis fungoides (cutaneous T-cell lymphoma), early stage (IB)
pearls: Mycosis fungoides classically presents as chronic, refractory, pruritic patches and plaques in sun-protected 'bathing trunk' areas, often misdiagnosed for years as eczema or psoriasis.
Hallmark histology: epidermotropic atypical CD4+ T cells with cerebriform nuclei and intraepidermal Pautrier microabscesses, with clonal TCR rearrangement.
Stage with the TNMB system; circulating Sezary cells define blood involvement and the leukemic Sezary syndrome variant (erythroderma + Sezary cells).
Early skin-limited disease is treated with skin-directed therapy (topicals, phototherapy, local radiation); systemic chemotherapy is reserved for advanced/transformed disease.

cases.title: Sudden Kidney Failure After Starting Cancer Treatment
cases.chiefComplaint: Decreased urine output, muscle cramps, and lethargy two days after starting chemotherapy.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma (bulky abdominal disease, markedly elevated baseline LDH) was admitted and began induction chemotherapy two days ago. Overnight he developed nausea, muscle cramps, perioral tingling, and progressive lethargy. His urine output has fallen to less than 200 mL over the last 12 hours. He reports palpitations. He denies chest pain or seizures. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable; he takes no chronic medications and has no known drug allergies. He received allopurinol 300 mg daily and intravenous hydration starting at diagnosis. He has no prior kidney disease. He is a nonsmoker, drinks alcohol rarely, and works as a software engineer. Family history is noncontributory. Nursing notes that he has not received any potassium-containing fluids. On review, his pre-chemotherapy labs were notable only for an elevated uric acid and LDH; renal function was normal at admission. He has had no diarrhea, vomiting blood, or recent contrast exposure.
cases.exam: The patient is lethargic but arousable and oriented. There is no jugular venous distension; mucous membranes are slightly dry. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia with a regular rhythm and no murmur. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is distended with a large, firm, nontender mass in the left upper quadrant. There is mild diffuse muscle tenderness and a positive Chvostek sign. Extremities show no edema; capillary refill is 2 seconds. Neurologic exam shows generalized weakness without focal deficit. There is no flank tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given oliguria, cramps, and palpitations two days after chemotherapy for a bulky high-grade lymphoma, which set of laboratory studies is most urgent?
options: Electrolytes including potassium and phosphate, calcium, uric acid, creatinine, and an ECG
stages.explanation: The clinical picture is classic for tumor lysis syndrome: rapid cell turnover after chemotherapy releases potassium, phosphate, and nucleic acids (uric acid), and phosphate binds calcium causing hypocalcemia, while urate and calcium-phosphate crystals cause acute kidney injury. The most urgent labs are potassium, phosphate, calcium, uric acid, and creatinine with an immediate ECG to detect life-threatening hyperkalemia. Blood cultures and procalcitonin address sepsis, not the metabolic emergency here. A coagulation panel alone does not capture the relevant electrolyte derangements. Thyroid testing is irrelevant to this acute, predictable post-chemotherapy metabolic syndrome and would dangerously delay care.

stages.label: LABS & ECG
stages.content:
Potassium 6.8 mEq/L
Phosphate 8.4 mg/dL
Calcium 6.6 mg/dL (ionized low)
Uric acid 13.5 mg/dL
Creatinine 3.2 mg/dL (baseline 0.9)
BUN 48 mg/dL
LDH 2200 U/L
Urine output <0.3 mL/kg/hr
ECG: peaked T waves and widened QRS
G6PD screen: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: These findings confirm which diagnosis, and by what defining mechanism?
options: Tumor lysis syndrome from massive release of intracellular contents
stages.explanation: The combination of hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia, hyperuricemia, and acute kidney injury arising shortly after cytotoxic therapy for a bulky, high-turnover malignancy defines tumor lysis syndrome (Cairo-Bishop criteria). Dying tumor cells dump potassium, phosphate, and nucleic acids; phosphate chelates calcium (hypocalcemia), and uric acid and calcium-phosphate precipitate in tubules, causing AKI. Contrast nephropathy would not produce hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and a uric acid of 13.5. Rhabdomyolysis causes hyperkalemia and hyperphosphatemia but with a very high CK and is not triggered by chemotherapy here. Allopurinol interstitial nephritis would not cause hyperphosphatemia and severe hyperuricemia simultaneously.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With potassium 6.8 mEq/L and ECG changes, which intervention should be given FIRST?
options: Intravenous calcium gluconate to stabilize the cardiac membrane
Urgent CT abdomen to assess tumor burden
stages.explanation: Severe hyperkalemia with ECG changes (peaked T waves, widened QRS) is immediately life-threatening, so intravenous calcium gluconate is given first to stabilize the cardiac membrane, followed by insulin with glucose and other potassium-shifting and -removing measures. Rasburicase rapidly lowers uric acid and is essential for tumor lysis syndrome but does not address the acute cardiac risk of hyperkalemia in the next minutes. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate acts too slowly and is not first-line for acute, dangerous hyperkalemia. Imaging the tumor does nothing to treat the metabolic emergency and would dangerously delay cardioprotection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After membrane stabilization and potassium-lowering measures, which agent is preferred to manage the uric acid of 13.5 mg/dL in this patient with established AKI?
options: Sodium bicarbonate infusion to alkalinize the urine
stages.explanation: Rasburicase, a recombinant urate oxidase, enzymatically converts existing uric acid to soluble allantoin, rapidly lowering established hyperuricemia, and is preferred in high-risk or established tumor lysis syndrome with AKI. Allopurinol only blocks new uric acid formation (xanthine oxidase inhibition) and does not reduce the uric acid already present, so it is inadequate for an acute level of 13.5. Probenecid is uricosuric and contraindicated with renal impairment and acute urate load. Routine urinary alkalinization is no longer recommended because it promotes calcium-phosphate precipitation and is unnecessary once rasburicase is used. Note: rasburicase is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency, which was screened and normal here.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Despite rasburicase, fluids, and potassium-lowering therapy, the patient remains oliguric with potassium 6.9 mEq/L, phosphate 9 mg/dL, and worsening volume overload. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Administer a large normal saline bolus and observe
Start oral phosphate binders and discharge to the ward
stages.explanation: Refractory hyperkalemia, severe hyperphosphatemia, oliguric AKI, and volume overload that fail medical management are indications for urgent hemodialysis (renal replacement therapy), which efficiently clears potassium, phosphate, and uric acid in tumor lysis syndrome. A large saline bolus in an oliguric, volume-overloaded patient risks pulmonary edema and will not correct life-threatening hyperkalemia. Repeating rasburicase addresses uric acid but not the refractory hyperkalemia and fluid overload. Phosphate binders act too slowly for this severity, and transferring an unstable patient to the ward is unsafe; this patient needs ongoing ICU-level renal replacement therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Tumor lysis syndrome with acute kidney injury
pearls: Suspect tumor lysis syndrome after treating bulky, high-turnover malignancies (Burkitt, ALL, high-grade lymphoma) when potassium, phosphate, and uric acid rise with hypocalcemia and AKI.
Hyperkalemia with ECG changes is the immediate killer; give IV calcium gluconate first, then insulin/glucose and potassium removal.
Rasburicase rapidly degrades existing uric acid and is preferred over allopurinol in high-risk/established TLS, but is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency.
Avoid routine urinary alkalinization (promotes calcium-phosphate deposition); use aggressive hydration and escalate to dialysis for refractory metabolic derangements or volume overload.

cases.title: Fever in a Patient Midway Through Chemotherapy
cases.chiefComplaint: Fever and chills ten days after a chemotherapy cycle.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman with breast cancer receiving dose-dense chemotherapy presents to the emergency department with a temperature of 38.6 C at home, chills, and malaise that began this afternoon. Her last chemotherapy infusion was 10 days ago, placing her in the expected count nadir. She reports mild fatigue and a slightly sore throat but denies cough, dysuria, abdominal pain, diarrhea, or rash. She has a tunneled central venous catheter (port) without overlying redness that she has noticed. Past medical history includes the breast cancer and mild hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. She takes ondansetron as needed and has no known drug allergies. She is married, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is significant for breast cancer in her mother. Review of systems is otherwise negative for headache, neck stiffness, shortness of breath, or focal pain. She has not been around sick contacts and has not traveled. She received pegfilgrastim with her last cycle.
cases.exam: The patient appears mildly ill but alert and well-perfused. There is no oral mucositis ulceration, though the posterior pharynx is mildly erythematous without exudate. The chest port site is clean, without erythema, tenderness, or discharge. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac exam shows sinus tachycardia without murmur. The abdomen is soft and nontender. There is no perianal tenderness or fluctuance, no skin rash or cellulitis, and no neurologic deficit. Capillary refill is brisk and there are no signs of shock.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A febrile chemotherapy patient at the count nadir arrives in the ED. Which is the most critical immediate step?
options: Obtain a CBC with differential and blood cultures, then administer empiric antibiotics within one hour
Wait for the absolute neutrophil count to confirm neutropenia before any antibiotics
Give acetaminophen and observe for 6 hours
Order a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis before starting any therapy
stages.explanation: Fever during the chemotherapy nadir is a medical emergency: blood cultures (peripheral and from any central line) and a CBC with differential should be obtained, but empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics must be started within one hour and should not wait for confirmatory counts because untreated febrile neutropenia can progress rapidly to septic shock. Waiting for the ANC delays life-saving therapy. Antipyretics and observation alone are dangerous and inappropriate. Comprehensive CT imaging may follow if a source is sought but must never delay timely empiric antibiotics in a potentially neutropenic, septic patient.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 0.8 ×10³/µL
Absolute neutrophil count 180 cells/µL
Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL
Platelets 95 ×10³/µL
Lactate 1.8 mmol/L
Chest X-ray: no infiltrate
Urinalysis: unremarkable
Blood cultures: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The ANC is 180 cells/µL with a temperature of 38.7 C. How is this best classified?
stages.explanation: Febrile neutropenia is defined as a single oral temperature of 38.3 C or a temperature of 38.0 C sustained over an hour with an absolute neutrophil count below 500 cells/µL (or expected to fall below 500). This patient has a temperature of 38.7 C with an ANC of 180, meeting the definition, and is a true oncologic emergency. A simple viral URI cannot be assumed in a neutropenic host where bacterial sepsis must be excluded and treated empirically. Levothyroxine does not cause drug fever in this setting. Catheter-related thrombosis causes swelling and pain, not isolated fever, and the port site is clean.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which empiric antibiotic regimen is most appropriate for this patient with febrile neutropenia and no identified source?
options: An antipseudomonal beta-lactam such as cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam
stages.explanation: Empiric therapy for febrile neutropenia must cover Pseudomonas aeruginosa and other gram-negative organisms, so an antipseudomonal beta-lactam such as cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam, or a carbapenem is the standard backbone. Oral azithromycin does not provide reliable antipseudomonal or broad gram-negative coverage and is inappropriate for an unstable-risk neutropenic host. Vancomycin alone covers gram-positives but misses Pseudomonas; it is added only for specific indications such as suspected line infection, skin/soft-tissue infection, hemodynamic instability, or known resistant organisms, not as monotherapy. Empiric antifungal therapy is reserved for persistent fever after several days of broad-spectrum antibacterials, not initial management.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After the first antibiotic dose, the patient is hemodynamically stable. Which factor most supports inpatient admission rather than outpatient oral management?
options: Her profound neutropenia (ANC 180) and anticipated prolonged nadir
Her family history of breast cancer
Her use of ondansetron at home
stages.explanation: Risk stratification (e.g., MASCC score) guides disposition: deep, anticipated-prolonged neutropenia such as an ANC of 180 places her at higher risk and favors inpatient IV antibiotics and monitoring rather than outpatient oral therapy, which is reserved for carefully selected low-risk patients with brief expected neutropenia and stable comorbidities. Hypothyroidism on stable replacement does not drive admission. A family history of breast cancer is not a disposition factor. Home ondansetron use is irrelevant to infection risk. Depth and expected duration of neutropenia, comorbidities, and clinical stability are the key determinants of admission versus outpatient management.

cases.diagnosis: Febrile neutropenia (chemotherapy-induced)
pearls: Febrile neutropenia = temperature 38.3 C once (or 38.0 C for 1 hour) with ANC <500 cells/µL; it is an oncologic emergency.
Draw blood cultures and start empiric antipseudomonal antibiotics (cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam, or a carbapenem) within one hour — do not wait for the ANC.
Add vancomycin only for specific indications (suspected line/skin infection, hemodynamic instability, resistant organisms), not routinely.
Use risk stratification (MASCC) to choose inpatient IV vs. outpatient oral therapy; deep, prolonged neutropenia warrants admission.

cases.title: Facial Swelling and Bulging Neck Veins
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive facial and neck swelling with a feeling of head fullness over two weeks.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man presents with two weeks of progressive facial puffiness, swelling of the neck and upper arms, and a sensation of head fullness and pressure that worsens when he bends forward or lies flat. He has noticed dilated veins over his chest and a hoarse voice, along with a dry cough and mild shortness of breath. He denies hemoptysis, chest pain, or fever. He reports a 5 kg weight loss and decreased appetite over two months. Past medical history includes COPD and hypertension; medications are tiotropium inhaler, albuterol as needed, and amlodipine 10 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He has a 45 pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke. He drinks alcohol occasionally and worked in construction. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for mild dysphagia and headache that is worse in the morning, and negative for orthopnea from heart failure, leg swelling, or syncope. He has had no recent infections or central line placement.
cases.exam: The patient has facial plethora and edema, periorbital swelling, and swelling of the neck and both upper extremities. There are prominent dilated, nonpulsatile veins over the anterior chest wall and distended neck veins that do not collapse with inspiration. The face appears cyanotic when he bends forward (positive Pemberton-like response). Lungs reveal decreased breath sounds and dullness over the right upper lung field. Cardiac exam is unremarkable without murmur or pericardial rub. There is no lower-extremity edema. Neurologic exam is nonfocal, though he reports mild lightheadedness. There is no stridor at rest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Facial/upper-body swelling with distended neck veins and dilated chest wall veins in a heavy smoker most strongly suggests which syndrome, and what initial imaging is best?
options: Right heart failure; obtain an echocardiogram
Superior vena cava syndrome; obtain contrast-enhanced CT of the chest
Nephrotic syndrome; obtain a renal ultrasound
Angioedema; obtain a C1 esterase inhibitor level
stages.explanation: Facial and upper-extremity edema, distended neck veins that do not collapse with inspiration, dilated chest wall collateral veins, and head fullness in a heavy smoker are classic for superior vena cava syndrome, usually from a mediastinal mass obstructing the SVC. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest defines the site, degree of obstruction, and the underlying mass. Right heart failure causes lower-extremity edema and hepatic congestion, not isolated upper-body and facial swelling with chest collaterals. Nephrotic syndrome causes generalized/dependent edema with proteinuria. Angioedema is acute and episodic without dilated chest wall collaterals or a mediastinal mass.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Contrast-enhanced CT chest: 6 cm right upper lobe/mediastinal mass compressing and narrowing the superior vena cava
Extensive venous collateral formation over the chest wall
Mediastinal lymphadenopathy
No pericardial effusion
No pulmonary embolism
No airway compression to a critical degree at rest

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: CT confirms SVC obstruction by a mediastinal mass. In a hemodynamically stable patient without airway compromise or cerebral edema, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Immediate emergent radiation therapy before any tissue sampling
Obtain a tissue biopsy to establish the histologic diagnosis before definitive treatment
Start lifelong systemic anticoagulation as definitive therapy
stages.explanation: In a stable patient with superior vena cava syndrome and no airway compromise or cerebral edema, establishing a tissue diagnosis by biopsy is the priority because treatment differs dramatically by tumor type (e.g., small cell lung cancer and lymphoma are chemosensitive, whereas non-small cell lung cancer may require radiation). Emergent radiation before biopsy can obscure histology and is reserved for true life-threatening compromise. Anticoagulation treats associated thrombus but is not definitive therapy for tumor-related obstruction. Surgical SVC bypass is reserved for refractory cases; it is not the initial step. Stable SVC syndrome allows time to obtain diagnostic tissue.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which clinical finding would convert this from an urgent to an EMERGENT situation requiring immediate intervention before biopsy?
options: Stridor with airway compromise or signs of cerebral edema (severe headache, confusion, obtundation)
stages.explanation: Most superior vena cava syndrome is subacute and allows time for biopsy, but life-threatening features — stridor or central airway obstruction and cerebral edema (severe headache, altered mental status, obtundation) — constitute a true emergency requiring immediate intervention such as endovascular stenting or emergent radiation, sometimes before tissue diagnosis. Mild facial swelling, distended neck veins, and hoarseness are characteristic of the syndrome but are not by themselves immediately life-threatening and do not mandate emergent treatment ahead of biopsy. Recognizing airway and neurologic compromise is the key trigger to escalate from urgent to emergent management.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Biopsy returns and shows non-small cell lung carcinoma. Besides directing systemic therapy, which intervention provides rapid symptomatic relief of the SVC obstruction in this patient?
options: Endovascular SVC stenting and/or radiation therapy
stages.explanation: Endovascular SVC stenting provides the most rapid relief of obstructive symptoms and can be combined with radiation therapy; for chemosensitive tumors, chemotherapy also shrinks the mass, but non-small cell lung cancer responds well to radiation and stenting for fast decompression. High-dose diuresis does not relieve mechanical venous obstruction and risks hypovolemia. Therapeutic phlebotomy treats polycythemia, not mechanical SVC compression. Beta-blockade has no role in relieving venous obstruction. Combining definitive antitumor therapy with mechanical/radiation decompression best addresses both the cause and the acute symptoms of SVC syndrome.

cases.diagnosis: Superior vena cava syndrome from a mediastinal non-small cell lung carcinoma
pearls: SVC syndrome: facial/upper-body edema, distended noncollapsing neck veins, dilated chest wall collaterals, and head fullness — most often from a malignant mediastinal mass.
Contrast-enhanced chest CT defines the obstruction and mass; obtain a tissue biopsy before definitive therapy in stable patients since treatment depends on histology.
Escalate to emergent intervention (stenting/radiation) for airway compromise (stridor) or cerebral edema.
Endovascular stenting gives the fastest symptom relief; combine with radiation and tumor-directed systemic therapy.

cases.title: Worsening Back Pain With Weak, Numb Legs
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe mid-back pain with progressive leg weakness and inability to urinate.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents with three weeks of progressively severe thoracic back pain that is worse at night and when lying flat, now accompanied by two days of bilateral leg weakness, numbness from the mid-abdomen down, and an inability to urinate for the past 12 hours. He also notes new constipation and saddle numbness. He denies trauma or fever. Past medical history is significant for prostate cancer diagnosed four years ago, treated with radiation; his last PSA was rising at the most recent visit. Other history includes hypertension on lisinopril 20 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired teacher, a former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 15 years ago), and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history includes a brother with prostate cancer. Review of systems is positive for fatigue and the urinary symptoms above and negative for chest pain, abdominal pain, or recent infection. He reports no prior episodes of back pain of this severity and no anticoagulant use.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable from pain. There is focal tenderness to percussion over the mid-thoracic spine. Motor exam shows 3/5 strength in bilateral lower extremities with increased tone, hyperreflexia at the knees and ankles, and bilateral upgoing plantar responses (Babinski). There is a sensory level at approximately T10 with decreased pinprick and light touch below it. Saddle anesthesia is present and rectal tone is reduced. The bladder is palpable and distended. Upper-extremity strength, reflexes, and sensation are normal. There is no spinal deformity or overlying skin lesion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A patient with known prostate cancer has back pain, bilateral leg weakness, a sensory level, and urinary retention. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Schedule outpatient MRI within two weeks and prescribe oral analgesics
Administer IV dexamethasone immediately and obtain emergent MRI of the entire spine
Order lumbar plain radiographs and discharge with physical therapy
stages.explanation: This is malignant spinal cord compression, an oncologic emergency. High-dose IV corticosteroids (dexamethasone) should be given immediately to reduce vasogenic edema and preserve neurologic function while emergent MRI of the entire spine is obtained to localize the level(s) of compression. Delaying MRI for weeks risks permanent paralysis. Plain radiographs miss epidural disease and cannot exclude cord compression, and discharge is unsafe. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated when cord compression is suspected because it can worsen neurologic status and does not address the emergency. Rapid steroids plus urgent imaging is the standard.

stages.label: MRI & LABS
stages.content:
MRI whole spine: epidural soft-tissue mass at T9-T10 with high-grade spinal cord compression
Multiple osteoblastic (sclerotic) vertebral metastases
No cord signal change yet (no established myelomalacia)
PSA 240 ng/mL (markedly elevated)
Alkaline phosphatase 310 U/L (elevated)
Post-void bladder scan: 600 mL retained urine

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: MRI confirms high-grade cord compression at T9-T10 from osteoblastic metastases. Besides continuing dexamethasone, which immediate supportive measure is required?
options: Urinary catheterization to relieve the obstructed bladder
Oral bisphosphonate started today as the priority intervention
stages.explanation: The patient has 600 mL of retained urine from a neurogenic bladder due to cord compression; urinary (Foley) catheterization is immediately required to relieve the obstructed, distended bladder and prevent further injury and discomfort. Fluid restriction does not address the mechanical retention and risks hypovolemia. Anticoagulation is not indicated for cord compression and would add bleeding risk without benefit. Bone-targeted agents (bisphosphonates or denosumab) are appropriate later for skeletal-related events but are not the immediate priority over relieving acute urinary retention and arranging definitive decompressive therapy. Address the acute bladder problem promptly while definitive treatment is organized.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which definitive treatment(s) should be arranged urgently for this cord compression from prostate cancer?
options: Observation, since steroids alone are definitive
Urgent radiation therapy, with surgical decompression considered for instability, single-level compression in a good-prognosis patient, or radioresistant tumor
Chemotherapy alone over the next several weeks
Repeat MRI in one month before any treatment
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment of malignant cord compression is urgent radiation therapy, with surgical decompression/stabilization considered for spinal instability, a single level of compression in a patient with good prognosis and life expectancy, or a radioresistant tumor — followed by radiation. Steroids buy time and reduce edema but are not definitive. Chemotherapy alone is too slow for an acute compressive emergency, though prostate cancer is hormone-sensitive and androgen-deprivation therapy is added systemically. Waiting a month for repeat imaging before treatment risks irreversible paraplegia. The window to preserve ambulation is short, so radiation (with possible surgery) must be arranged urgently.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which factor at presentation is the strongest predictor of whether this patient will regain or retain the ability to walk?
options: His pretreatment neurologic/ambulatory status and the speed of treatment initiation
The number of osteoblastic lesions on MRI
stages.explanation: The single strongest predictor of post-treatment ambulatory function in malignant spinal cord compression is the patient's neurologic status at presentation and how rapidly treatment begins — patients who are still ambulatory and treated quickly have far better outcomes than those who present paraplegic. PSA reflects tumor burden and prostate cancer activity but does not predict neurologic recovery. Age alone is a weaker determinant than functional status. The number of osteoblastic lesions indicates metastatic extent but does not directly predict ambulation recovery. This is why immediate steroids, urgent imaging, and prompt definitive therapy are emphasized — time is cord.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given that biopsy years ago confirmed prostate adenocarcinoma and PSA is now 240 ng/mL, which systemic therapy should be added to treat the underlying metastatic disease driving this complication?
options: Androgen-deprivation therapy (with consideration of an androgen-receptor pathway inhibitor)
stages.explanation: Metastatic prostate cancer is hormone-driven, so androgen-deprivation therapy (GnRH agonist/antagonist or orchiectomy), typically combined with an androgen-receptor pathway inhibitor such as abiraterone or enzalutamide, is the systemic backbone that controls the disease causing the bony metastases and cord compression; a bone-targeted agent (denosumab or zoledronic acid) reduces skeletal-related events. Trastuzumab targets HER2-positive breast and gastric cancers, not prostate cancer. Insulin has no antineoplastic role here. Antibiotics treat infection, which is not the problem; this is metastatic malignancy. Local radiation/surgery relieve the compression, but systemic androgen-deprivation therapy addresses the underlying driver of widespread metastatic prostate cancer.

cases.diagnosis: Malignant spinal cord compression from metastatic prostate cancer
pearls: Back pain (worse when lying flat/at night) with leg weakness, a sensory level, and bladder/bowel dysfunction in a cancer patient = malignant spinal cord compression until proven otherwise.
Give IV dexamethasone immediately and obtain emergent MRI of the WHOLE spine; do not delay for outpatient imaging.
Definitive therapy is urgent radiation; consider surgery for instability, single-level compression with good prognosis, or radioresistant tumors.
Pretreatment ambulatory status and time to treatment are the strongest predictors of walking outcome — diagnose and treat fast.

cases.title: New Confusion and Stubborn Constipation
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive confusion, constipation, and excessive thirst over one week.
cases.history: A 65-year-old woman admitted for evaluation of a lung mass has developed worsening confusion, severe constipation, nausea, polyuria, and increasing thirst over the past week. Her family reports she has been lethargic and 'not herself,' with poor concentration. She has had a 7 kg weight loss and a chronic cough over two months. She denies fevers or focal weakness. Past medical history includes COPD and hypertension; medications are tiotropium, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, and amlodipine 5 mg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She has a 50 pack-year smoking history. She lives with her husband, drinks alcohol rarely, and is a retired factory worker. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for bone aches, fatigue, and decreased appetite and negative for hematuria, diarrhea, or recent immobilization. On admission a chest CT showed a cavitary central lung mass with hilar lymphadenopathy; biopsy results are pending. She has not been taking calcium or vitamin D supplements.
cases.exam: The patient is drowsy and disoriented to time and place but arousable, with no focal neurologic deficit and a nonfocal cranial nerve exam. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is decreased. Cardiac exam shows a regular rhythm without murmur. Lungs have decreased breath sounds at the right apex with scattered wheezes. The abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with reduced bowel sounds and no focal tenderness; there is no organomegaly. There is no lymphadenopathy palpable in the neck. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished. There is no peripheral edema and no asterixis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A patient with a lung mass develops confusion, constipation, polyuria, and dehydration. Which laboratory study is most likely to explain this constellation?
options: Serum calcium (with albumin or ionized calcium)
stages.explanation: The classic combination of altered mental status, constipation, polyuria with dehydration, and bone aches in a patient with malignancy points to hypercalcemia ('stones, bones, groans, psychiatric overtones'), so serum calcium corrected for albumin (or ionized calcium) is the key test. Serum ammonia evaluates hepatic encephalopathy, which is not suggested here (no liver disease, no asterisks). Serum cortisol evaluates adrenal insufficiency, which causes hyponatremia and hypotension rather than this picture with polyuria and constipation. CSF analysis would be pursued for meningitis or carcinomatous meningitis, but the metabolic profile here strongly favors hypercalcemia first.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Corrected calcium 14.2 mg/dL (normal 8.5-10.5)
Phosphate 2.2 mg/dL (low)
Intact PTH 6 pg/mL (suppressed)
PTH-related peptide (PTHrP) markedly elevated
25-OH vitamin D normal
Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (mildly elevated)
Lung biopsy: squamous cell carcinoma
ECG: shortened QT interval

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Calcium is 14.2 mg/dL with suppressed PTH and elevated PTHrP in a patient with squamous cell lung cancer. What is the mechanism of this hypercalcemia?
options: Osteolytic bone metastases releasing calcium directly
stages.explanation: Elevated PTHrP with suppressed intact PTH and low phosphate in a squamous cell carcinoma is the hallmark of humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy, in which tumor-secreted PTH-related peptide mimics PTH at its receptor to raise calcium. Primary hyperparathyroidism would show an elevated (not suppressed) PTH. Vitamin D toxicity causes hypercalcemia with an elevated 25-OH vitamin D, which is normal here. Local osteolytic hypercalcemia is mediated by cytokines from bone metastases (common in breast cancer and myeloma) and would not produce a markedly elevated circulating PTHrP; the PTHrP elevation pinpoints the humoral mechanism in this squamous cell lung cancer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate FIRST-line therapy for this symptomatic, dehydrated patient with calcium 14.2 mg/dL?
options: Loop diuretics as the initial step before any fluids
Hemodialysis as the routine first measure
stages.explanation: Severe symptomatic hypercalcemia is initially treated with aggressive intravenous isotonic (normal) saline to restore volume and promote calciuresis, since these patients are profoundly volume-depleted from calcium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. Loop diuretics are not given upfront; they are reserved for volume overload after rehydration and are no longer routine. Oral phosphate does not rapidly lower dangerous hypercalcemia and can precipitate calcium-phosphate complexes. Hemodialysis is reserved for severe hypercalcemia with renal failure or when other measures fail. Volume repletion with saline is the essential first step, followed by agents that lower calcium.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After saline rehydration, which agent provides the most durable reduction in calcium for this malignancy-driven hypercalcemia?
options: An intravenous bisphosphonate (e.g., zoledronic acid) or denosumab
stages.explanation: After volume repletion, an IV bisphosphonate such as zoledronic acid (or denosumab, especially in renal impairment or refractory cases) inhibits osteoclastic bone resorption and provides durable calcium lowering over days; calcitonin can be added for rapid but short-lived early reduction. Thiazide diuretics raise calcium by increasing renal reabsorption and are contraindicated. Calcium carbonate would worsen hypercalcemia. Vitamin D supplementation increases intestinal calcium absorption and is also contraindicated. The combination of saline, then a bisphosphonate or denosumab (with calcitonin bridging if needed), is the standard durable approach while definitive cancer treatment is pursued.

cases.diagnosis: Hypercalcemia of malignancy (PTHrP-mediated) from squamous cell lung cancer
pearls: Hypercalcemia presents with 'stones, bones, groans, and psychiatric overtones' plus polyuria and dehydration; check corrected/ionized calcium.
Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy shows elevated PTHrP with suppressed PTH and low phosphate, classically with squamous cell carcinomas.
First-line treatment is aggressive IV isotonic saline; avoid thiazides and routine upfront loop diuretics.
After rehydration, IV bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid) or denosumab give durable lowering; calcitonin bridges the early gap.

cases.title: An Incidental Lung Spot in a Lifelong Nonsmoker
cases.chiefComplaint: A lung nodule found incidentally on a chest CT done for an unrelated reason.
cases.history: A 59-year-old woman is referred after a peripheral right lower lobe lung nodule was found incidentally on a CT performed during an emergency visit for a kidney stone. She feels well, with no cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, dyspnea, or weight loss. She has never smoked and reports minimal secondhand smoke exposure. Past medical history includes hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily and osteopenia. She takes a daily multivitamin and has no known drug allergies. She is of East Asian descent, works as an attorney, exercises regularly, and drinks wine occasionally. Family history includes a mother with breast cancer and no known lung cancer. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no bone pain, headaches, or neurologic symptoms. She has no occupational asbestos or radon exposure that she is aware of and has had no prior imaging for comparison. She is up to date on screening mammography and colonoscopy.
cases.exam: The patient appears well and is in no distress. There is no cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally with no wheezes, crackles, or focal dullness. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm without murmur. There is no clubbing, cyanosis, or peripheral edema. The abdomen is soft and nontender. Neurologic exam is intact. There are no skin or breast abnormalities noted, and no signs of weight loss or cachexia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 2.5 cm spiculated peripheral lung nodule is seen on CT with no prior comparison imaging. What is the most appropriate next step to characterize it?
options: PET/CT for metabolic and staging assessment and to guide biopsy
Begin empiric antibiotics for presumed pneumonia
stages.explanation: A 2.5 cm spiculated solid nodule carries a high probability of malignancy and warrants further characterization with PET/CT, which assesses metabolic activity and screens for nodal and distant involvement to guide biopsy and staging. A 12-month surveillance CT is appropriate only for small, low-risk nodules, not a spiculated 2.5 cm lesion. Proceeding directly to lobectomy without tissue diagnosis is not standard when a less invasive biopsy and staging can be done, especially since management may shift with molecular testing. Empiric antibiotics are inappropriate for a spiculated nodule with no infectious symptoms and only delay diagnosis.

stages.label: STAGING & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
PET/CT: FDG-avid 2.5 cm right lower lobe nodule, no FDG-avid lymphadenopathy, no distant metastases
CT-guided biopsy: adenocarcinoma
Brain MRI: no metastases
Molecular testing: EGFR exon 19 deletion detected
ALK, ROS1, KRAS: negative
PD-L1: low expression

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Biopsy shows adenocarcinoma with an EGFR exon 19 deletion in this never-smoker. Which statement best characterizes this disease?
options: It is small cell lung cancer requiring immediate chemoradiation
It is non-small cell lung cancer (adenocarcinoma); EGFR mutations are more common in never-smokers and predict response to targeted therapy
EGFR mutation indicates it will not respond to any systemic therapy
Squamous cell histology is confirmed, mandating immunotherapy alone
stages.explanation: This is non-small cell lung cancer of adenocarcinoma subtype. Activating EGFR mutations (such as exon 19 deletions and L858R) are enriched in never-smokers, women, and patients of East Asian descent and predict excellent response to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors. Small cell lung cancer is a distinct neuroendocrine tumor strongly tied to smoking and is not what the biopsy shows. An EGFR mutation indicates a targetable driver, not chemoresistance — it specifically predicts sensitivity to targeted agents. The histology is adenocarcinoma, not squamous, and PD-L1 is low, so single-agent immunotherapy is not the optimal frontline choice given the actionable driver mutation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Staging confirms early-stage, localized disease without nodal or distant spread. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment for this localized tumor?
options: Surgical resection (lobectomy) with mediastinal lymph node sampling
Lifelong EGFR inhibitor without any local therapy
stages.explanation: For early-stage, localized non-small cell lung cancer in a fit patient, surgical resection (anatomic lobectomy) with mediastinal lymph node sampling/dissection is the definitive curative-intent treatment; adjuvant therapy, including adjuvant osimertinib for resected EGFR-mutant disease, is added based on final stage. Palliative radiation alone is for advanced or unresectable disease, not curable localized tumors. An EGFR inhibitor is highly effective in advanced/metastatic EGFR-mutant disease and as adjuvant therapy after surgery, but it is not a substitute for definitive local resection in early-stage disease. Observation is inappropriate for a biopsy-proven malignancy that is resectable.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If this patient instead had metastatic disease at diagnosis, which first-line systemic therapy would be most appropriate given the EGFR exon 19 deletion?
options: An EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor such as osimertinib
stages.explanation: In metastatic EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung cancer, a third-generation EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor such as osimertinib is preferred first-line therapy, offering high response rates, central nervous system penetration, and superior outcomes versus chemotherapy. Platinum-doublet chemotherapy is reserved for tumors without targetable drivers or after TKI progression. Single-agent checkpoint inhibitors perform poorly in EGFR-mutant tumors, which generally have low immunotherapy benefit. Imatinib targets BCR-ABL, KIT, and PDGFR (CML, GIST), not EGFR, and has no role here. Matching the targeted agent to the driver mutation is the core principle of precision oncology in lung adenocarcinoma.

cases.diagnosis: Non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma with EGFR exon 19 deletion (never-smoker)
pearls: Lung adenocarcinoma is the most common lung cancer in never-smokers and tends to be peripheral; obtain molecular testing on all advanced/non-squamous NSCLC.
Activating EGFR mutations (exon 19 deletion, L858R) are enriched in never-smokers, women, and East Asian patients and predict response to EGFR TKIs.
Early-stage NSCLC is treated with curative-intent lobectomy plus lymph node sampling; adjuvant osimertinib follows resected EGFR-mutant disease.
Metastatic EGFR-mutant NSCLC is treated first-line with osimertinib; checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy is generally ineffective in EGFR-mutant tumors.

cases.title: A Heavy Smoker With Drooping Eyes and Low Sodium
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of progressive leg weakness, fatigue, and a recently discovered low sodium level.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man is admitted with three weeks of proximal leg weakness making it hard to rise from a chair, fatigue, dry mouth, and mild double vision. He reports a chronic cough with occasional blood-streaked sputum and a 6 kg weight loss over two months. He notes that his strength seems to improve slightly with repeated effort. He denies headache or seizures. Past medical history includes COPD and hypertension; medications are tiotropium, albuterol as needed, and lisinopril 20 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He has a 60 pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke. He drinks alcohol occasionally and is a retired miner. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for constipation, erectile dysfunction, and the symptoms above and negative for fever, neck stiffness, or recent illness.
