Outpatient labs prompting admission showed a low serum sodium. He has had no recent medication changes and is not on diuretics.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and oriented. There is proximal lower-extremity weakness (4-/5) with diminished knee and ankle reflexes that augment (become brisker) after sustained muscle contraction. There is mild ptosis. Pupils are sluggishly reactive and his mouth is dry. Lungs reveal decreased breath sounds and dullness over the right hilum. Cardiac exam is unremarkable. The abdomen is soft and nontender; bowel sounds are reduced. Volume status appears euvolemic with moist-to-dry mucous membranes, no edema, and normal JVP. Sensation is intact and there is no neck stiffness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A heavy smoker with a hilar lung lesion presents with hyponatremia. He is euvolemic with normal blood pressure. Which evaluation best clarifies the cause of the low sodium?
options: Serum and urine osmolality with urine sodium
Empiric fluid restriction without further testing
stages.explanation: In a euvolemic patient with hyponatremia and a lung mass, serum and urine osmolality plus urine sodium are essential to identify the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH): low serum osmolality with inappropriately concentrated urine (high urine osmolality) and elevated urine sodium. Empiric fluid restriction may be part of treatment but should not replace establishing the diagnosis and excluding mimics. A random cortisol alone cannot characterize the hyponatremia and ignores the osmolality data needed to diagnose SIADH. Brain imaging is not the first step for euvolemic hyponatremia without focal neurologic emergency; the metabolic workup comes first.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Serum sodium 122 mEq/L
Serum osmolality 258 mOsm/kg (low)
Urine osmolality 480 mOsm/kg (inappropriately concentrated)
Urine sodium 60 mEq/L
Normal cortisol and TSH
CT chest: 4 cm central right hilar mass with bulky mediastinal lymphadenopathy
Biopsy: small round blue cells, neuroendocrine markers positive (synaptophysin, chromogranin)
Anti-voltage-gated calcium channel (P/Q-type) antibodies: positive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the central mass with neuroendocrine small round blue cells, SIADH, and proximal weakness that improves with repetition plus positive VGCC antibodies, what is the unifying diagnosis?
options: Non-small cell lung cancer with myasthenia gravis
Small cell lung cancer with paraneoplastic SIADH and Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome
stages.explanation: A central lung mass composed of small round blue cells positive for neuroendocrine markers is small cell lung cancer, which is strongly associated with smoking and with paraneoplastic syndromes. SIADH (euvolemic hyponatremia with concentrated urine) and Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome — proximal weakness that improves with repeated effort plus autonomic features (dry mouth, constipation), caused by antibodies against presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels — are both classic. Myasthenia gravis (anti-AChR) causes fatigable weakness that worsens with use, the opposite pattern, and is tied to thymoma. Tuberculosis and sarcoidosis do not produce neuroendocrine small-cell histology with VGCC antibodies and this paraneoplastic constellation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which intervention will most effectively improve both the SIADH and the Lambert-Eaton weakness over time?
options: Treating the underlying small cell lung cancer with chemotherapy (platinum-etoposide) and radiation
stages.explanation: Both SIADH and Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome are paraneoplastic and tied to the tumor, so effective treatment of the underlying small cell lung cancer with platinum-etoposide chemotherapy (plus radiation for limited-stage disease) provides the most durable improvement in both syndromes. High-volume normal saline can worsen hyponatremia in SIADH because the kidney retains free water and excretes the sodium; SIADH is managed with fluid restriction and sometimes a vaptan or salt/urea. Pyridostigmine helps myasthenia gravis far more than Lambert-Eaton (where 3,4-diaminopyridine is preferred symptomatically). Thymectomy treats myasthenia gravis with thymoma, not small cell lung cancer.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Staging shows disease confined to one hemithorax that fits within a tolerable single radiation field (limited-stage small cell lung cancer). Which treatment approach is most appropriate?
options: Surgery alone is the standard for nearly all small cell lung cancer
Concurrent platinum-etoposide chemotherapy with thoracic radiation, with consideration of prophylactic cranial irradiation
stages.explanation: Limited-stage small cell lung cancer is treated with concurrent platinum-etoposide chemotherapy plus thoracic radiation, and prophylactic cranial irradiation is considered in responders because of the high rate of brain micrometastases. Surgery has only a narrow role in the rare very early-stage node-negative tumor, not for the typical bulky central disease seen here. EGFR inhibitors target EGFR-mutant non-small cell adenocarcinoma and have no role in small cell lung cancer, a neuroendocrine tumor without that driver. Observation is inappropriate for an aggressive, rapidly proliferating, highly chemosensitive malignancy where prompt treatment improves outcomes and palliates paraneoplastic syndromes.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The sodium is 122 mEq/L and the patient is asymptomatic from the hyponatremia itself. What is the safest initial approach to the SIADH, and what must be avoided?
options: Fluid restriction with gradual correction; avoid raising sodium too rapidly to prevent osmotic demyelination
Rapid correction to normal sodium within a few hours with hypertonic saline
stages.explanation: For chronic, asymptomatic SIADH-related hyponatremia, fluid restriction with gradual correction is safest; sodium should be raised slowly (generally no more than about 6-8 mEq/L per 24 hours) to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome from overly rapid correction of chronic hyponatremia. Rapid correction with hypertonic saline is reserved for severe symptomatic hyponatremia (seizures, coma) and still must be carefully rate-limited; doing so here risks demyelination. Encouraging free water worsens SIADH by adding water the kidney cannot excrete. Thiazides cause hyponatremia and are contraindicated. Gentle, monitored correction is the rule in chronic SIADH.

cases.diagnosis: Small cell lung cancer with paraneoplastic SIADH and Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome
pearls: Small cell lung cancer is a central, smoking-related neuroendocrine tumor (synaptophysin/chromogranin positive) notorious for paraneoplastic syndromes.
SIADH = euvolemic hyponatremia, low serum osmolality, inappropriately concentrated urine, and high urine sodium; treat with fluid restriction, not saline loading.
Lambert-Eaton (anti-VGCC) causes proximal weakness that IMPROVES with repetition plus autonomic symptoms — opposite of myasthenia gravis (anti-AChR), which worsens with use.
Correct chronic hyponatremia slowly (≤6-8 mEq/L/24h) to avoid osmotic demyelination; treating the tumor addresses the paraneoplastic syndromes.

cases.title: A Lump Found on a Routine Mammogram
cases.chiefComplaint: An abnormal screening mammogram showing a new breast mass.
cases.history: A 55-year-old postmenopausal woman is referred after her routine screening mammogram showed a new spiculated mass in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast. She had not noticed any lump herself but on examination after the report can feel a firm area. She denies breast pain, nipple discharge, skin changes, or systemic symptoms. She reports no weight loss or bone pain. Menarche was at age 12 and menopause at age 51; she had her first child at age 31 and breastfed. She used hormone replacement therapy briefly. Past medical history includes hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily; she takes a daily multivitamin and has no known drug allergies. She is married, works as a teacher, does not smoke, and drinks one glass of wine most evenings. Family history is significant for breast cancer in her mother (diagnosed at age 60) and a maternal aunt. Review of systems is otherwise negative. She is up to date on colorectal screening and has had normal prior mammograms.
cases.exam: On focused breast exam there is a firm, hard, irregular, immobile mass approximately 2 cm in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast, nontender, fixed to surrounding tissue. The overlying skin shows no dimpling, erythema, peau d'orange, or nipple retraction, and there is no nipple discharge. No axillary or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy is palpable. The left breast is normal. The remainder of the examination, including lungs, heart, abdomen, and spine, is unremarkable, with no hepatomegaly or bony tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 55-year-old woman has a new spiculated mass on screening mammography and a palpable hard, immobile lump. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Reassurance and repeat mammogram in one year
Diagnostic mammogram and targeted ultrasound followed by core needle biopsy
Fine-needle aspiration cytology alone for definitive diagnosis
stages.explanation: A suspicious mass on screening should be worked up with diagnostic mammography and targeted ultrasound to fully characterize it (the 'triple assessment'), followed by image-guided core needle biopsy, which provides histology, grade, and receptor status needed to plan treatment. Reassurance with a one-year repeat is unsafe for a spiculated, hard, fixed mass. Fine-needle aspiration yields cytology without architecture or reliable receptor testing and is inadequate as the definitive diagnostic test for a suspicious solid mass. Immediate mastectomy without a tissue diagnosis is never appropriate; surgery is planned only after biopsy confirms cancer and defines its biology.

stages.label: BIOPSY RESULTS
stages.content:
Diagnostic mammogram: spiculated 2 cm mass, BI-RADS 5
Ultrasound: irregular hypoechoic mass with posterior acoustic shadowing
Core needle biopsy: invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 2
Estrogen receptor (ER): positive (90%)
Progesterone receptor (PR): positive (70%)
HER2: negative (IHC 0)
Ki-67: 15%

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The biopsy confirms ER-positive, PR-positive, HER2-negative invasive ductal carcinoma. How is this subtype best characterized?
options: Hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer (luminal type)
stages.explanation: ER-positive, PR-positive, HER2-negative disease is hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer, the most common (luminal) subtype, which is responsive to endocrine therapy. Triple-negative disease is ER-, PR-, and HER2-negative and cannot be treated with endocrine or HER2-directed therapy. HER2-enriched cancer overexpresses HER2 (IHC 3+ or amplified), which is negative here, so HER2-targeted agents like trastuzumab are not indicated. Inflammatory breast cancer is a clinical diagnosis with rapid onset, erythema, and peau d'orange skin changes, none of which are present in this slowly detected, non-inflamed mass. Receptor status directly determines systemic therapy choices.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before definitive local therapy, which is the most appropriate next step in this clinically node-negative patient?
options: Clinical/imaging staging and surgical management with sentinel lymph node biopsy at the time of breast surgery
Routine whole-body PET/CT and bone scan for all patients regardless of stage
Immediate chemotherapy before any surgery for all hormone receptor-positive tumors
Bilateral mastectomy as the only acceptable option
stages.explanation: For early-stage, clinically node-negative breast cancer, surgical management (breast-conserving surgery or mastectomy) includes sentinel lymph node biopsy to assess nodal status, which guides staging and adjuvant therapy. Extensive systemic staging with PET/CT and bone scans is reserved for higher-stage disease or symptoms/signs of metastasis, not routine early-stage cancer. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy is not automatically required for all hormone receptor-positive tumors; many are managed with surgery first, then endocrine therapy with chemotherapy added based on stage and genomic risk. Bilateral mastectomy is not mandated; breast conservation with radiation is an equivalent option for appropriate candidates.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After breast-conserving surgery with negative sentinel nodes, which adjuvant systemic therapy is most appropriate for this ER/PR-positive, HER2-negative cancer?
options: No systemic therapy is ever indicated
Adjuvant endocrine therapy (e.g., an aromatase inhibitor) for this postmenopausal woman
Platinum-based chemotherapy as the sole adjuvant therapy
stages.explanation: Hormone receptor-positive breast cancer is treated with adjuvant endocrine therapy; in a postmenopausal woman an aromatase inhibitor (or tamoxifen) for at least 5 years substantially lowers recurrence risk. Trastuzumab targets HER2 and is not indicated in HER2-negative disease. Saying no systemic therapy is needed is incorrect, since endocrine therapy clearly benefits hormone receptor-positive tumors. Platinum-based chemotherapy is not the standard sole adjuvant therapy here; the decision to add chemotherapy in node-negative, ER-positive disease is guided by genomic assays (e.g., Oncotype DX recurrence score), and endocrine therapy remains the backbone for this receptor profile.

cases.diagnosis: Invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast, ER/PR-positive, HER2-negative
pearls: Work up a suspicious breast mass with triple assessment (exam, diagnostic mammogram/ultrasound, core needle biopsy); avoid FNA-alone and never operate before a tissue diagnosis.
Always determine ER, PR, and HER2 status — they define subtype and drive systemic therapy.
Early, clinically node-negative breast cancer is staged surgically with sentinel lymph node biopsy; reserve PET/bone scan for higher-stage or symptomatic disease.
ER/PR-positive, HER2-negative disease is treated with adjuvant endocrine therapy (aromatase inhibitor in postmenopausal women); genomic assays guide whether to add chemotherapy.

cases.title: A Red, Swollen Breast That Won't Clear
cases.chiefComplaint: Six weeks of progressive redness, swelling, and warmth of the left breast not improved by antibiotics.
cases.history: A 47-year-old premenopausal woman presents with six weeks of left breast redness, swelling, and warmth. Her primary doctor diagnosed mastitis four weeks ago and prescribed dicloxacillin, then cephalexin, with no improvement. She reports the skin now looks dimpled like an orange peel and the breast feels heavy and tender, but she denies a discrete lump or nipple discharge. She has had no fevers since the first week and is not breastfeeding (her youngest child is 9). She notes a 4 kg unintentional weight loss. PMH: hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily; G3P3. Menarche age 12. No prior breast biopsies. Allergies: none. Social: never-smoker, one glass of wine weekly, works as a teacher. Family history: maternal aunt with breast cancer at 58; no known BRCA testing. She has not had a mammogram in three years. She is anxious because the redness keeps spreading despite two antibiotic courses, and a coworker told her it 'looked like an infection.'
cases.exam: The left breast is diffusely enlarged compared with the right, with erythema involving more than one-third of the skin, warmth, and prominent peau d'orange. There is skin thickening and tenderness but no fluctuant, pointing abscess. The nipple is slightly retracted. No dominant discrete mass is palpable within the engorged breast. There is a firm, mobile 1.5 cm left axillary lymph node. The right breast is normal. No supraclavicular adenopathy. No hepatomegaly; lungs clear.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After two failed antibiotic courses for presumed mastitis, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Switch to clindamycin and reassess in two weeks
Order incision and drainage of the breast
Obtain diagnostic mammogram and breast ultrasound with skin punch biopsy
Reassure that nonpuerperal mastitis can take months to resolve
stages.explanation: Erythema involving a large fraction of the breast with peau d'orange that fails to respond to appropriate antibiotics must be treated as inflammatory breast cancer until proven otherwise; the next step is imaging plus full-thickness skin punch biopsy (and core biopsy of any underlying mass/node) to look for tumor and dermal lymphatic invasion. Switching antibiotics or 'watchful waiting' wastes time on a rapidly progressive cancer. Incision and drainage is for a documented abscess, which she lacks. Reassurance is dangerous given weight loss, peau d'orange, and an axillary node.

stages.label: IMAGING & BIOPSY
stages.content:
Diagnostic mammogram: diffuse skin thickening and increased breast density, no spiculated mass; trabecular thickening.
Breast ultrasound: skin thickening, edema, and a 2.1 cm hypoechoic mass at 2 o'clock; abnormal left axillary node (cortex 6 mm).
Skin punch biopsy: invasive ductal carcinoma with tumor emboli in dermal lymphatics.
Core biopsy of mass: invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3; ER 0%, PR 0%, HER2 IHC 3+.
No distant lesions on staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis; bone scan negative.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which biopsy finding most specifically establishes the diagnosis of inflammatory breast cancer?
options: Invasive ductal carcinoma on core biopsy
Tumor emboli within dermal lymphatics on skin punch biopsy
An abnormal axillary lymph node on ultrasound
stages.explanation: Inflammatory breast cancer is fundamentally a clinical diagnosis (rapid erythema/edema over at least one-third of the breast, peau d'orange), but the pathologic hallmark is dermal lymphatic invasion by tumor emboli, which explains the skin findings. Invasive ductal carcinoma is the underlying histology of most breast cancers and is not specific to IBC. HER2 positivity guides therapy but does not define IBC. An abnormal axillary node indicates regional spread, common in IBC but also seen in other locally advanced cancers; it is not diagnostic of the inflammatory subtype.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: By definition, what is the minimum clinical (T) stage of inflammatory breast cancer at diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Inflammatory breast cancer is classified as T4d by definition because of dermal lymphatic involvement and skin changes; this makes it at minimum clinical stage IIIB even with a small or non-palpable underlying mass. It is therefore never Tis, T1, or T2 regardless of the size of any discrete lesion. Recognizing the automatic T4d designation is high-yield: it explains why IBC is always treated as locally advanced disease and why trimodality therapy is required rather than upfront breast-conserving surgery.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the correct treatment sequence for this non-metastatic, HER2-positive inflammatory breast cancer?
options: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy with HER2-targeted therapy, then modified radical mastectomy, then radiation
Upfront modified radical mastectomy, then chemotherapy
Radiation alone followed by endocrine therapy
stages.explanation: Inflammatory breast cancer requires trimodality therapy: neoadjuvant systemic therapy first, then non-skin-sparing modified radical mastectomy, then post-mastectomy radiation. For HER2-positive disease, chemotherapy is combined with anti-HER2 agents (trastuzumab plus pertuzumab). Breast-conserving surgery is contraindicated in IBC because of diffuse dermal lymphatic involvement. Upfront mastectomy is wrong because surgery before systemic therapy yields worse outcomes and high local recurrence. Radiation alone or endocrine therapy alone is inadequate, and endocrine therapy is useless here given ER 0%/PR 0%.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which surgical procedure is specifically contraindicated and should NOT be offered for inflammatory breast cancer?
options: Modified radical mastectomy after neoadjuvant therapy
Skin-sparing or nipple-sparing mastectomy with immediate reconstruction
stages.explanation: Skin-sparing/nipple-sparing mastectomy and immediate reconstruction are contraindicated in inflammatory breast cancer because diffuse dermal lymphatic tumor involvement means the skin must be removed to achieve local control; sparing skin risks leaving tumor behind and high recurrence. The standard operation is a non-skin-sparing modified radical mastectomy (which includes axillary node dissection) after neoadjuvant systemic therapy. Axillary dissection is appropriate and necessary because of the high nodal burden, so it is not contraindicated. Reconstruction, if desired, is delayed until after completion of radiation. Sentinel node biopsy is also unreliable in IBC, so formal axillary dissection is preferred.

cases.diagnosis: Inflammatory breast cancer (HER2-positive, hormone receptor-negative), clinical stage IIIB (T4dN1M0)
pearls: Any breast erythema/edema that fails to respond to a course of antibiotics must be biopsied to exclude inflammatory breast cancer—do not keep cycling antibiotics.
IBC is a clinical diagnosis; dermal lymphatic tumor emboli on skin punch biopsy are the pathologic correlate but are not required to make the diagnosis.
IBC is staged T4d by definition (minimum stage IIIB) regardless of underlying mass size.
Treatment is trimodality: neoadjuvant systemic therapy, then modified radical mastectomy (no breast conservation), then radiation.

cases.title: Fatigue and a Change in the Bathroom Routine
cases.chiefComplaint: Three months of worsening fatigue and looser, more frequent stools.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man presents with three months of fatigue, mild exertional dyspnea climbing stairs, and a change in bowel habits with looser, more frequent stools and occasional thin caliber. He denies overt rectal bleeding but says stools are sometimes darker. He has had mild crampy lower abdominal discomfort and a 3 kg weight loss. PMH: hypertension and type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg twice daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily. He has never had a colonoscopy. Allergies: none. Social: 20-pack-year smoking history, quit 5 years ago; drinks beer on weekends; eats a red-meat-heavy diet, low fiber. Family history: father had 'colon trouble' in his 60s, details unknown; mother alive. Review of systems negative for hematemesis, melena described as overt, fevers, or night sweats. He has felt 'washed out' and his wife noticed he looks pale. He takes no NSAIDs and no anticoagulants. No prior GI bleeding.
cases.exam: He appears pale with pale conjunctivae. Cardiopulmonary exam reveals a soft systolic flow murmur and no rales. Abdomen is soft with mild left-lower-quadrant tenderness, no rebound or guarding, no palpable mass, and normoactive bowel sounds. No hepatosplenomegaly. Digital rectal exam reveals brown stool that is guaiac positive, no palpable rectal mass, and no external hemorrhoids. No lymphadenopathy. No koilonychia, but mild glossitis is noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given iron deficiency anemia features and a change in bowel habits in a 61-year-old man, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Empiric oral iron and recheck CBC in 3 months
Prescribe a fiber supplement and reassess symptoms
stages.explanation: Iron deficiency anemia in a man (or postmenopausal woman) and a change in bowel habits mandate evaluation for a GI malignancy; colonoscopy is the test of choice because it visualizes the entire colon and allows biopsy. Empiric iron without investigation can mask and delay diagnosis of colorectal cancer. Capsule endoscopy evaluates the small bowel and is reserved for obscure bleeding after colonoscopy (and EGD) are unrevealing. Fiber supplementation treats a symptom while ignoring a red-flag presentation, which is inappropriate.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hgb 9.4 g/dL
MCV 74 fL
Ferritin 8 ng/mL
Iron saturation 9%
Platelets 410 ×10³/µL
Fecal occult blood: positive
CEA 6.8 ng/mL (elevated)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Colonoscopy reveals a circumferential, constricting lesion in the descending colon. On barium imaging this would most classically appear as which finding?
options: Lead-pipe colon with loss of haustra
String sign of a narrowed terminal ileum
stages.explanation: A circumferential constricting colon cancer classically produces an apple-core (napkin-ring) lesion on barium studies, reflecting annular tumor growth that narrows the lumen. Lead-pipe colon (loss of haustra) is the hallmark of chronic ulcerative colitis. Thumbprinting reflects bowel wall edema from ischemic colitis. The string sign denotes terminal ileal narrowing in Crohn disease. The apple-core appearance correlates with this patient's left-sided obstructive symptoms (change in caliber, looser stools) typical of distal colon cancers.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: After biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma, which study is most important to complete staging before treatment planning?
options: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
stages.explanation: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis is the standard staging study for colorectal cancer to assess for liver and lung metastases and local extent. The liver is the most common site of colorectal metastasis (portal venous drainage). A brain PET is not part of routine staging absent neurologic symptoms. A bone scan is not routine for colorectal cancer, which uncommonly metastasizes to bone. Repeat colonoscopy is for surveillance after resection or to clear a proximal synchronous lesion if the scope could not pass, not for systemic staging.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Staging shows a node-positive descending colon adenocarcinoma without distant metastases (stage III). What is the appropriate management?
options: Surgical resection (colectomy) followed by adjuvant chemotherapy
Radiation alone to the primary tumor
stages.explanation: Stage III colon cancer is treated with surgical resection (segmental colectomy with regional lymphadenectomy) followed by adjuvant chemotherapy (e.g., FOLFOX) to address micrometastatic disease and improve survival. Neoadjuvant chemoradiation is the paradigm for rectal cancer, not colon cancer, because the rectum is extraperitoneal and radiation reduces local recurrence there. Chemotherapy alone leaves the primary tumor and risks obstruction/perforation. Radiation alone is not curative for colon adenocarcinoma and plays essentially no role in standard colon cancer management.

cases.diagnosis: Descending colon adenocarcinoma (stage III, node-positive) presenting with iron deficiency anemia
pearls: Iron deficiency anemia in a man or postmenopausal woman is a GI malignancy until proven otherwise—get a colonoscopy.
Left-sided colon cancers obstruct early (change in caliber/bowel habits, apple-core lesion); right-sided cancers bleed occultly and present with anemia.
Stage III colon cancer: resection then adjuvant FOLFOX; neoadjuvant chemoradiation is for rectal cancer, not colon.
The liver is the most common metastatic site for colorectal cancer via portal venous drainage—always stage with CT chest/abdomen/pelvis.

cases.title: Yellow Eyes Without the Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of progressive yellowing of the eyes and skin with dark urine and pale stools.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man is admitted with two weeks of painless jaundice noted first by his daughter, accompanied by dark 'tea-colored' urine, pale clay-colored stools, and generalized itching. He reports a 7 kg unintentional weight loss over two months and new poor appetite. He denies fever, chills, or right-upper-quadrant pain, and has no history of gallstones or hepatitis. He notes new mild epigastric discomfort that radiates to the back, worse when lying flat. PMH: type 2 diabetes diagnosed only 4 months ago, hypertension; medications include metformin 500 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: 40-pack-year smoker, still smoking; drinks two beers nightly. Family history: father died of 'stomach cancer.' Review of systems notable for fatigue and the new-onset diabetes. He has no tattoos, no IV drug use, no recent travel, and no acetaminophen overuse. He is frustrated that he 'feels fine except for the color.'
cases.exam: He is icteric with scleral icterus and diffuse skin jaundice; excoriations are present from scratching. Abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness; a nontender, smooth, distended gallbladder is palpable in the right upper quadrant (Courvoisier sign). No hepatomegaly, no splenomegaly, no ascites or fluid wave. No abdominal mass palpable. No asterixis. No spider angiomata or palmar erythema. No supraclavicular or periumbilical nodes. Stool in the rectum is pale.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A palpable, nontender, distended gallbladder with painless jaundice (Courvoisier sign) most strongly suggests which underlying process?
options: Malignant obstruction of the distal common bile duct
stages.explanation: Courvoisier sign—a palpable, nontender, distended gallbladder with painless jaundice—suggests malignant obstruction of the distal common bile duct, classically pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. The reasoning: gallstone disease produces a chronically scarred, fibrotic gallbladder that cannot distend, and stones usually cause pain, so a distended painless gallbladder points away from stones and toward slow malignant obstruction. Acute cholecystitis is painful with a positive Murphy sign. Viral hepatitis A causes hepatocellular jaundice (tender liver, transaminase elevation) without an obstructive distended gallbladder. Hemolysis causes unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia without pale stools or biliary obstruction.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Total bilirubin 11.2 mg/dL
Direct bilirubin 8.6 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 540 U/L
ALT 78 U/L
AST 70 U/L
Lipase 60 U/L (normal)
CA 19-9 720 U/mL (markedly elevated)
HbA1c 8.1%

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the obstructive pattern and elevated CA 19-9, what is the best initial imaging study to evaluate the suspected mass?
options: Multiphase (pancreatic protocol) contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen
stages.explanation: A pancreatic-protocol multiphase contrast-enhanced CT is the best study to characterize a suspected pancreatic head mass, define ductal dilation, and assess vascular involvement (resectability). Ultrasound can confirm biliary dilation and is a reasonable first screen but is operator-dependent and poorly visualizes the pancreas behind bowel gas; it is insufficient for staging. A plain radiograph adds nothing for soft-tissue masses. Brain MRI is irrelevant without neurologic symptoms. The obstructive cholestatic labs (high direct bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase with modest transaminases) plus markedly elevated CA 19-9 strongly support pancreatic adenocarcinoma.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: CT shows a 3.2 cm pancreatic head mass with double-duct sign and no vascular encasement or metastases. What is the most appropriate management for this resectable tumor?
options: ERCP with stenting and then observation
Surgical resection (pancreaticoduodenectomy / Whipple procedure), typically with perioperative chemotherapy
stages.explanation: A resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma (no metastases, no major vascular encasement) is treated with pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) combined with perioperative or adjuvant chemotherapy, the only potentially curative pathway. ERCP with stenting relieves obstruction/itching but is palliative and does not treat the cancer; it is reserved for cholangitis or to bridge to surgery, not as definitive therapy. Palliative chemotherapy alone is for metastatic or unresectable disease. Radiation alone is not standard curative-intent therapy. The double-duct sign (dilated common bile and pancreatic ducts) is classic for a pancreatic head mass.

stages.label: PEARL
stages.question: Which feature in this man's recent history is a recognized early/associated clue to pancreatic adenocarcinoma?
stages.explanation: New-onset diabetes in an older adult—especially with weight loss—can be a paraneoplastic harbinger of pancreatic adenocarcinoma, which impairs glucose metabolism; this man was diagnosed only four months ago. Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor and chronic pancreatitis also raises risk. Hypertension is not specifically associated. Metformin is actually associated with possibly lower cancer risk and is the treatment, not a clue. A prior normal colonoscopy is unrelated to pancreatic cancer risk. Recognizing new diabetes plus weight loss as a possible cancer signal can prompt earlier evaluation.

cases.diagnosis: Pancreatic head adenocarcinoma causing painless obstructive jaundice (resectable)
pearls: Courvoisier sign (palpable nontender distended gallbladder + painless jaundice) points to malignant distal CBD obstruction, classically pancreatic head cancer.
Obstructive labs (high direct bilirubin, high alkaline phosphatase, modest transaminases) + double-duct sign + elevated CA 19-9 = pancreatic head adenocarcinoma.
New-onset diabetes with weight loss in an older adult can be a paraneoplastic clue to pancreatic cancer; smoking is the leading modifiable risk factor.
Resectable pancreatic head cancer is treated with a Whipple (pancreaticoduodenectomy) plus chemotherapy; biliary stenting is palliative, not curative.

cases.title: When a Stable Cirrhotic Suddenly Slips
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of increasing abdominal girth, fatigue, and intermittent confusion in a man with known cirrhosis.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man with hepatitis C-related cirrhosis (previously compensated, Child-Pugh A) presents with two weeks of increasing abdominal distension, worsening fatigue, decreased appetite, and episodes of confusion noted by his wife. He had been stable for years with controlled ascites on low-dose diuretics. He now reports right-upper-quadrant fullness and a 4 kg weight gain from fluid despite poor intake, plus a 3 kg loss of muscle mass. He achieved sustained virologic response after direct-acting antiviral therapy three years ago but missed his last two surveillance ultrasounds. PMH: HCV cirrhosis, esophageal varices (banded once), type 2 diabetes. Medications: spironolactone 100 mg daily, furosemide 40 mg daily, nadolol 20 mg daily, insulin glargine. Allergies: none. Social: former IV drug use, abstinent 15 years; quit alcohol 10 years ago; ex-smoker. Family history: noncontributory. He has had no GI bleeding, no fever, and no new medications; he wonders why his 'fluid is back so fast.'
cases.exam: He is thin with temporal wasting and mild scleral icterus. He has spider angiomata on the chest and palmar erythema. Abdomen is distended with shifting dullness and a fluid wave; the liver edge is firm and nodular with a palpable mass in the right upper quadrant. There is mild splenomegaly. A subtle bruit is auscultated over the liver. He has trace asterixis. No abdominal tenderness or rebound; no peripheral edema beyond mild ankle swelling. Mentation is slow but oriented.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A previously compensated cirrhotic now decompensating (new ascites, encephalopathy, weight loss) should be evaluated first for which complication?
stages.explanation: New decompensation (refractory/rapidly recurrent ascites, encephalopathy, weight loss, a palpable hepatic mass, and a hepatic bruit) in a stable cirrhotic strongly suggests hepatocellular carcinoma, especially with missed surveillance. HCC must be excluded urgently because it changes prognosis and management. Dietary sodium indiscretion and diuretic underdosing can worsen ascites but do not explain weight loss, a hepatic mass, or a bruit. Acute hepatitis A could decompensate a cirrhotic but is far less likely than HCC given the chronic mass and the lack of acute hepatitic features; surveillance imaging is the priority.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
AFP 980 ng/mL (markedly elevated; prior baseline 6 ng/mL)
Total bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL
INR 1.4
Albumin 3.0 g/dL
Platelets 88 ×10³/µL
Multiphase CT abdomen: 5 cm right-lobe liver mass with arterial-phase hyperenhancement and portal-venous washout; patent main portal vein; no extrahepatic disease

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: In a cirrhotic patient, a liver lesion showing arterial-phase hyperenhancement with portal-venous washout on multiphase imaging allows what conclusion?
options: It is a benign hemangioma requiring no further workup
Biopsy is mandatory before any diagnosis can be made
It can be diagnosed as hepatocellular carcinoma without biopsy
It must be a metastasis from a colon primary
stages.explanation: In a patient with cirrhosis, a lesion with the classic radiographic signature—arterial-phase hyperenhancement followed by venous/delayed washout (LI-RADS 5)—can be diagnosed as hepatocellular carcinoma noninvasively, without biopsy, because the imaging is highly specific. Biopsy is therefore usually avoided (risk of bleeding and needle-tract seeding) unless imaging is indeterminate. A hemangioma shows peripheral nodular discontinuous enhancement with centripetal fill-in, not washout. Colorectal metastases are typically hypovascular and would be unusual to diagnose this way; the markedly elevated AFP further supports HCC.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: Which staging system is used for hepatocellular carcinoma because it incorporates tumor burden, liver function, and performance status to guide therapy?
stages.explanation: The Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer (BCLC) system is preferred for HCC because it links tumor stage, underlying liver function (Child-Pugh/MELD), and performance status directly to evidence-based treatment, from resection/transplant/ablation through TACE to systemic therapy. TNM alone ignores liver reserve, which dominates HCC prognosis. Ann Arbor staging is for lymphomas. Gleason grading is for prostate cancer. Using BCLC, this man's underlying cirrhosis and platelet count matter as much as the 5 cm tumor when choosing therapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Considering his single 5 cm tumor, cirrhosis, and preserved performance status, which treatment offers the best chance of cure?
options: Liver transplantation or surgical resection if criteria are met
stages.explanation: For early-stage HCC, the potentially curative options are surgical resection, liver transplantation, and ablation. A single 5 cm lesion in a cirrhotic could meet Milan criteria for transplant (single tumor ≤5 cm), which treats both the cancer and the underlying cirrhosis—offering the best chance of cure if he is a candidate; resection is an option only if liver function and remnant allow. Atezolizumab-bevacizumab is first-line systemic therapy for advanced/unresectable disease, not curative. TACE is a bridge or palliative locoregional therapy, not curative. Best supportive care is inappropriate given a potentially curable tumor.

cases.diagnosis: Hepatocellular carcinoma arising in HCV cirrhosis (early-stage, within transplant criteria)
pearls: New decompensation in a previously stable cirrhotic (ascites, encephalopathy, weight loss, hepatic bruit/mass) should prompt urgent evaluation for HCC.
In cirrhosis, arterial-phase hyperenhancement with venous washout (LI-RADS 5) is diagnostic of HCC without biopsy; biopsy risks bleeding and tract seeding.
Stage HCC with BCLC, which integrates tumor burden, liver function, and performance status—liver reserve drives prognosis as much as tumor size.
Curative options for early HCC are resection, transplant (Milan criteria), and ablation; atezolizumab-bevacizumab is systemic therapy for advanced disease.

cases.title: Full After Just a Few Bites
cases.chiefComplaint: Three months of early satiety, vague upper abdominal discomfort, and weight loss.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents with three months of feeling full after only a few bites, progressive loss of appetite, and an 8 kg unintentional weight loss. He has nagging epigastric discomfort unrelieved by over-the-counter antacids and intermittent nausea. He denies overt hematemesis but reports occasional dark stools and increasing fatigue. PMH: chronic Helicobacter pylori gastritis (never treated), GERD, osteoarthritis. Medications: omeprazole 20 mg daily, occasional naproxen. Allergies: none. Social: 30-pack-year smoker; emigrated from Japan at age 40; diet historically high in smoked and salted foods; moderate alcohol. Family history: brother had 'stomach cancer.' Review of systems notable for early satiety, dysphagia to solids developing over the last few weeks, and night sweats denied. He has not had an upper endoscopy. He attributes symptoms to 'just getting older and stress' but his wife insisted he come in after noticing his belt was several notches tighter.
cases.exam: He is thin and pale. There is a firm, fixed left supraclavicular lymph node (Virchow node). Abdominal exam reveals mild epigastric tenderness without a clearly palpable mass; no hepatomegaly initially appreciated, no ascites. A periumbilical nodule is not palpated. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable except mild tachycardia. Rectal exam shows guaiac-positive dark stool and no shelf. Conjunctivae are pale; no jaundice. No spider angiomata.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a 68-year-old with new dyspepsia plus alarm features (weight loss, early satiety, anemia, dysphagia), what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Empiric high-dose PPI and reassess in 8 weeks
Test-and-treat for H. pylori with stool antigen only
stages.explanation: New dyspepsia with alarm features—age over 60, unintentional weight loss, early satiety, anemia/GI bleeding, dysphagia, and a possible Virchow node—mandates prompt upper endoscopy with biopsy to exclude gastric cancer. Empiric PPI or H. pylori test-and-treat is appropriate only for younger patients without alarm features and would dangerously delay diagnosis here. Stool antigen testing for H. pylori does not visualize or biopsy the stomach. Abdominal ultrasound poorly evaluates the gastric mucosa. EGD provides direct visualization and tissue, the only way to diagnose and grade gastric adenocarcinoma.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & LABS
stages.content:
EGD: diffusely thickened, rigid, non-distensible stomach wall with loss of normal rugal folds (linitis plastica appearance); friable mucosa
Biopsy: poorly cohesive adenocarcinoma with signet-ring cells
H. pylori: positive
Hgb 9.8 g/dL, MCV 78 fL
Ferritin 11 ng/mL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The rigid, non-distensible 'leather bottle' stomach with diffusely infiltrating signet-ring carcinoma is best described by which term?
stages.explanation: Linitis plastica ('leather bottle stomach') describes a diffusely infiltrating gastric adenocarcinoma, often of the signet-ring (diffuse) type, that thickens and stiffens the entire stomach wall with loss of rugal folds. Ménétrier disease causes giant hypertrophic gastric folds with protein loss but is not malignant infiltration. A Krukenberg tumor is metastatic signet-ring carcinoma to the ovary—a site of spread, not the primary appearance. A Sister Mary Joseph nodule is a periumbilical metastatic deposit. The Virchow node and signet-ring histology fit diffuse-type gastric cancer with early metastatic spread.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: The presence of a firm left supraclavicular (Virchow) node in this patient most likely indicates what?
options: A reactive node from H. pylori gastritis
Localized regional disease still amenable to curative gastrectomy alone
stages.explanation: Virchow node (left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy) reflects spread via the thoracic duct and signifies distant metastatic disease in gastric cancer, marking it as advanced/incurable by surgery alone. It is not a benign reactive node, and its presence excludes the patient from upfront curative resection as the sole therapy. It does not represent merely regional disease. While supraclavicular nodes can occur in tuberculosis, in this oncologic context with signet-ring carcinoma it indicates metastasis. Confirmatory cross-sectional imaging (CT) and often staging laparoscopy/PET follow to map the full extent.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which factor identified here is a modifiable contributor that also warrants treatment in this patient?
stages.explanation: Chronic Helicobacter pylori infection is the leading modifiable risk factor for gastric adenocarcinoma (driving chronic atrophic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia, dysplasia) and should be eradicated; treating it also reduces risk in the patient and informs family screening. Japanese ancestry and family history are non-modifiable risk markers, as is age. While eradication will not cure established cancer, addressing H. pylori is the actionable risk factor and standard of care. Other modifiable risks include smoking and diets high in salted/smoked/nitrate-rich foods, both present in this patient.

cases.diagnosis: Diffuse-type gastric adenocarcinoma (signet-ring, linitis plastica) with metastatic Virchow node
pearls: New dyspepsia plus any alarm feature (age >60, weight loss, dysphagia, anemia, early satiety, mass/node) requires prompt EGD with biopsy.
Linitis plastica = diffusely infiltrating signet-ring gastric carcinoma producing a rigid 'leather bottle' stomach with loss of rugal folds.
Gastric cancer metastatic nodes/sites: Virchow (left supraclavicular), Sister Mary Joseph (periumbilical), Krukenberg (ovary), Blumer shelf (rectal).
H. pylori is the leading modifiable risk factor—eradicate it; salt/smoked-food diets and smoking also raise risk.

cases.title: Trouble Getting Food Down
cases.chiefComplaint: Four months of progressively worsening difficulty swallowing, now affecting both solids and liquids.
cases.history: A 62-year-old man presents with four months of dysphagia that started with solid foods like steak and bread, requiring him to chew longer and drink water to wash food down, and has now progressed to difficulty with liquids. He reports food 'sticking' behind the sternum, regurgitation of undigested food, and a 9 kg unintentional weight loss. He has long-standing heartburn for over 15 years, intermittently treated. PMH: GERD with a prior endoscopy years ago that 'showed some Barrett changes' for which he stopped follow-up; obesity (BMI 34); hypertension. Medications: omeprazole 40 mg daily (inconsistently), amlodipine 5 mg. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: 25-pack-year smoker; drinks two to three beers nightly. Family history: father with myocardial infarction. Review of systems notable for early satiety, mild odynophagia, and a hoarse voice in the last few weeks. He denies hematemesis but notes occasional dark stools and worsening fatigue. He delayed care because he 'figured it was just reflux acting up.'
cases.exam: He appears thinner than his stated baseline with temporal wasting. There is no oral thrush or oropharyngeal lesion. Neck exam reveals no palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy and no thyromegaly. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. Abdomen is soft, nontender, without hepatomegaly or palpable mass. Rectal exam: dark guaiac-positive stool. No jaundice. Voice is mildly hoarse; gag reflex intact. Conjunctivae mildly pale.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 62-year-old with progressive dysphagia to solids then liquids and weight loss most urgently needs which test?
options: Trial of high-dose PPI for 8 weeks
stages.explanation: Progressive dysphagia that advances from solids to liquids with weight loss is a mechanical/obstructive 'red flag' pattern that requires upper endoscopy with biopsy to exclude esophageal cancer and obtain tissue. EGD both visualizes and biopsies the lesion. A barium esophagram can characterize strictures and is sometimes done first for high-grade obstruction, but it cannot biopsy and is not the definitive test. An empiric PPI trial would dangerously delay cancer diagnosis given alarm features. Manometry evaluates motility disorders (e.g., achalasia), where dysphagia to solids AND liquids is typically simultaneous from onset, not progressive solids-then-liquids.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & BIOPSY
stages.content:
EGD: ulcerated, partially obstructing mass in the distal esophagus at the gastroesophageal junction; background salmon-colored mucosa proximal to the lesion
Biopsy of mass: invasive adenocarcinoma
Biopsy of background mucosa: intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells (Barrett esophagus)
Hgb 10.6 g/dL, MCV 80 fL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: This distal esophageal adenocarcinoma arose from which predisposing condition, and what is the underlying mucosal change?
options: Barrett esophagus with intestinal metaplasia (columnar epithelium with goblet cells)
Caustic stricture with squamous cell carcinoma
stages.explanation: Chronic GERD causes Barrett esophagus—metaplastic replacement of normal squamous epithelium by columnar epithelium with goblet cells (intestinal metaplasia)—which predisposes to esophageal adenocarcinoma, typically in the distal esophagus/GE junction. The biopsy here confirms both adenocarcinoma and background Barrett metaplasia. Achalasia, Plummer-Vinson syndrome (iron-deficiency with esophageal webs), and caustic strictures are risk factors for squamous cell carcinoma, which usually arises in the proximal/mid esophagus. Recognizing the GERD-Barrett-adenocarcinoma sequence explains both this patient's location (distal) and histology (adenocarcinoma).

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: After biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma, which test best assesses the depth of tumor invasion (T) and regional nodal involvement (N)?
stages.explanation: Endoscopic ultrasound is the most accurate test for locoregional (T and N) staging of esophageal cancer because it images the depth of wall invasion and adjacent lymph nodes and permits fine-needle aspiration of suspicious nodes. PET/CT is added to detect distant metastases (M). A plain chest radiograph and abdominal ultrasound lack the resolution for T/N staging. Esophageal pH monitoring quantifies acid reflux and has no role in cancer staging. Accurate T/N staging determines whether the patient receives upfront surgery versus neoadjuvant chemoradiation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Staging shows a locally advanced (T3) node-positive esophageal adenocarcinoma without distant metastases. What is the standard approach?
stages.explanation: For locally advanced, resectable (e.g., T3 and/or node-positive) esophageal cancer, neoadjuvant chemoradiation followed by esophagectomy improves survival over surgery alone and is standard. Immediate esophagectomy alone is reserved for early-stage disease. Endoscopic mucosal resection is curative only for very early, mucosa-confined (T1a/high-grade dysplasia) lesions, not a T3 node-positive tumor. Palliative stenting relieves dysphagia in metastatic/unresectable disease but does not treat curable locoregional cancer. This patient still has no distant metastases, so curative-intent trimodality therapy is appropriate.

cases.diagnosis: Distal esophageal adenocarcinoma arising in Barrett esophagus (locally advanced)
pearls: Progressive dysphagia (solids then liquids) plus weight loss is mechanical obstruction until proven otherwise—get EGD with biopsy, not a PPI trial.
GERD → Barrett (intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells) → adenocarcinoma of the distal esophagus; squamous cell cancer links to smoking, alcohol, achalasia, and webs in the proximal esophagus.
EUS is best for T/N (locoregional) staging; add PET/CT to detect distant metastasis.
Locally advanced resectable esophageal cancer: neoadjuvant chemoradiation then esophagectomy; EMR only for mucosa-confined early lesions.

cases.title: An Abnormal Number on a Routine Blood Test
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred for an elevated screening prostate-specific antigen and an abnormal rectal exam.
cases.history: A 67-year-old man is referred after a screening PSA returned at 8.5 ng/mL (prior value 2.1 ng/mL two years ago). He is largely asymptomatic but admits to mild nocturia (twice nightly) and a slightly weaker urinary stream over the past year, which he attributed to aging. He denies hematuria, dysuria, bone pain, or weight loss. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily. He takes no finasteride or other 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker (15 pack-years), social alcohol, retired electrician. Family history: father diagnosed with prostate cancer at age 72; no known breast/ovarian cancer. He has had no recent urinary tract infection, prostatitis, ejaculation, or vigorous bicycling before the PSA draw. He feels well and is surprised by the abnormal result; he came mainly because his wife urged him to follow up.
cases.exam: He is well-appearing and in no distress. Abdomen is soft and nontender with no palpable bladder. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Digital rectal exam reveals a firm, irregular, hard nodule in the right lobe of the prostate; the gland is non-tender and not boggy, with no warmth. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam of the lower extremities is normal with intact strength and no focal deficits or spinal tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given an elevated PSA (8.5 ng/mL) and a hard prostatic nodule on DRE, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start empiric antibiotics for prostatitis and recheck PSA
Reassure and repeat PSA in one year
Prostate MRI and/or biopsy (commonly MRI-guided/targeted biopsy)
stages.explanation: An elevated and rising PSA combined with a hard, irregular nodule on DRE is highly concerning for prostate cancer and requires tissue diagnosis. Current practice often uses multiparametric prostate MRI to risk-stratify and guide targeted biopsy, followed by prostate biopsy to confirm cancer and obtain a Gleason grade. Empiric antibiotics are appropriate when prostatitis is suspected (tender, boggy gland, urinary symptoms with infection), not here. Reassurance/repeat PSA ignores a palpable nodule and a doubling PSA. A 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor treats BPH and would lower PSA, masking and delaying a cancer diagnosis.

stages.label: BIOPSY RESULTS
stages.content:
Multiparametric prostate MRI: PI-RADS 5 lesion in the right peripheral zone; no seminal vesicle invasion; no pelvic adenopathy
Targeted biopsy: prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason 4+3=7 (Grade Group 3) in 4 of 6 right-sided cores
Left-sided cores: benign
PSA 8.5 ng/mL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The biopsy reads Gleason 4+3=7. What do the two numbers represent?
options: The two highest-grade patterns present, regardless of how common
Tumor size in centimeters and number of positive cores
The most common (primary) pattern plus the second most common (secondary) pattern
stages.explanation: The Gleason score sums the grade of the most prevalent (primary) histologic pattern and the second most prevalent (secondary) pattern, each scored 1–5 by glandular differentiation. Thus 4+3=7 means pattern 4 predominates with pattern 3 secondary—worse than 3+4=7 because the higher-grade pattern is dominant (Grade Group 3 vs 2). It is not simply the two highest patterns regardless of frequency (that is a modification used only at biopsy when a small high-grade focus exists). It does not denote tumor size, core counts, PSA velocity, or PSA density. The primary-then-secondary order is high-yield.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: With PSA 8.5, Grade Group 3 (Gleason 7), and a palpable nodule, this is intermediate-risk disease. What staging study is indicated to evaluate for bone metastasis if higher risk features were present?
options: Bone scan (and/or PSMA PET) for high-risk or symptomatic patients
stages.explanation: Prostate cancer characteristically metastasizes to bone, producing osteoblastic (sclerotic) lesions, so a technetium bone scan—or increasingly a PSMA PET—is used to detect skeletal metastases in high-risk disease (e.g., PSA >20, Grade Group 4–5, or T3+) or when bone pain is present. Mammography, echocardiography, and carotid ultrasound have no role in prostate cancer staging. Recognizing the osteoblastic, bone-tropic behavior of prostate cancer explains why an elevated alkaline phosphatase or new back pain in these patients prompts skeletal imaging.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this man with localized intermediate-risk prostate cancer and good life expectancy, which are the principal curative-intent options?
options: Radical prostatectomy or definitive radiation therapy
Active surveillance alone for all intermediate-risk disease
Immediate androgen deprivation therapy as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Localized intermediate-risk prostate cancer in a man with reasonable life expectancy is managed with curative-intent local therapy: radical prostatectomy or definitive radiation therapy (often with a course of androgen deprivation for unfavorable intermediate/high risk). Active surveillance is appropriate for low-risk (and select favorable intermediate-risk) disease, not as the default for Grade Group 3. Androgen deprivation therapy alone is not curative and is used for metastatic disease or as an adjunct to radiation. Docetaxel chemotherapy is reserved for metastatic/castration-resistant disease, not first-line localized cancer.

cases.diagnosis: Localized prostate adenocarcinoma, Gleason 4+3=7 (Grade Group 3), intermediate-risk
pearls: A hard, irregular prostate nodule plus elevated/rising PSA warrants MRI and biopsy—do not mask it with a 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor (which halves PSA).
Gleason score = primary (most common) pattern + secondary pattern; 4+3=7 is worse than 3+4=7 because the dominant pattern is higher grade.
Prostate cancer metastasizes to bone as osteoblastic (sclerotic) lesions—use bone scan/PSMA PET for high-risk or symptomatic patients.
Localized intermediate/high-risk disease: radical prostatectomy or radiation (± ADT); active surveillance is for low/favorable-risk disease.

cases.title: Blood in the Urine and a Fullness in the Side
cases.chiefComplaint: Six weeks of intermittent blood in the urine with dull right flank pain.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents with six weeks of intermittent gross hematuria (painless, then with a dull right flank ache) and a sense of right-sided abdominal fullness. He reports fatigue, a 5 kg weight loss, and intermittent low-grade fevers. He denies dysuria, urinary frequency, or recent infection. Notably, his face has looked 'ruddier' lately and he feels his head is 'fuller.' PMH: hypertension, obesity, chronic kidney disease stage 2. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: 35-pack-year smoker, still smoking; works in a printing shop with solvent exposure; moderate alcohol. Family history: no known hereditary renal syndromes. Review of systems notable for night sweats and new left-sided scrotal swelling that does not reduce when lying down. He has had no trauma. He initially ignored the hematuria because it was painless and came and went, but the flank discomfort and a lump he now feels in his abdomen prompted the visit.
cases.exam: He is plethoric (ruddy facies). Abdomen is soft with a palpable, firm, non-tender mass in the right flank/upper quadrant that moves slightly with respiration; no hepatosplenomegaly. There is mild right costovertebral angle fullness without marked tenderness. Examination of the scrotum reveals a non-reducing left-sided varicocele. No peripheral edema. Lungs clear. No supraclavicular nodes. Cardiac exam normal apart from mild hypertension.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 60-year-old smoker with gross hematuria, flank pain, and a palpable flank mass (classic triad) most urgently warrants which study?
options: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis (CT urogram)
stages.explanation: The classic triad of hematuria, flank pain, and a palpable flank mass is highly suspicious for renal cell carcinoma, and contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen/pelvis (CT urogram) is the best test to characterize a renal mass, assess enhancement, and stage local/vascular extension (e.g., renal vein/IVC tumor thrombus). Ultrasound can detect a mass and distinguish cystic from solid but is insufficient for full characterization and staging. A KUB cannot evaluate soft-tissue masses. Empiric antibiotics are wrong: he lacks dysuria/pyuria, has weight loss and a mass, and treating presumed infection would delay a cancer diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 18.4 g/dL (elevated)
Hematocrit 55%
Calcium 10.8 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
Urinalysis: gross hematuria, no pyuria, no bacteria
CT abdomen/pelvis: 8 cm heterogeneous, enhancing right renal mass with tumor thrombus extending into the right renal vein; no distant metastasis
Left varicocele noted; erythropoietin level elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His elevated hemoglobin/hematocrit with a high erythropoietin level in the setting of a renal mass represents which phenomenon?
options: Paraneoplastic polycythemia from tumor erythropoietin production
stages.explanation: Renal cell carcinoma can secrete erythropoietin, causing a paraneoplastic (secondary) polycythemia with elevated EPO—exactly this patient's high hemoglobin/hematocrit and elevated EPO. RCC is famous for paraneoplastic syndromes: EPO (polycythemia), PTHrP (hypercalcemia, seen here), renin (hypertension), and Stauffer syndrome (nonmetastatic hepatic dysfunction). Relative polycythemia from dehydration would have a normal/low EPO and no mass-related cause. Polycythemia vera is a primary marrow disorder with a LOW EPO and JAK2 mutation. Chronic hypoxemia raises EPO but would show low oxygen saturation and a pulmonary cause, absent here (SpO2 98%).

stages.label: CLINICAL CLUE
stages.question: The new, non-reducing LEFT-sided varicocele in this man with a renal mass should raise suspicion for what?
options: A benign idiopathic varicocele needing no workup
Obstruction of the left renal vein (e.g., by tumor thrombus or mass effect)
stages.explanation: A new, non-reducing left-sided varicocele can signal left renal vein obstruction because the left gonadal vein drains into the left renal vein (the right drains directly into the IVC). Tumor thrombus or mass effect obstructing the left renal vein impairs gonadal venous drainage, producing a varicocele that does not decompress when supine—an important red flag in RCC. A benign idiopathic varicocele is usually long-standing and reduces when lying down. Testicular torsion is an acute painful emergency, not a varicocele. An inguinal hernia presents differently and reduces with position/pressure.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this localized 8 cm RCC with renal vein thrombus but no distant metastases, what is the primary treatment?
options: Surgical resection (radical nephrectomy, with removal of tumor thrombus)
stages.explanation: Localized renal cell carcinoma—even large or with renal vein/IVC tumor thrombus but no distant metastasis—is treated surgically with radical nephrectomy (extracting the tumor thrombus as needed), the mainstay of curative therapy. RCC is notoriously chemoresistant, so conventional chemotherapy is not used. RCC is also relatively radioresistant, so radiation is not primary curative therapy (used palliatively, e.g., for bone/brain metastases). Active surveillance may be considered only for small (<4 cm) renal masses in select patients, not an 8 cm tumor with venous thrombus. Targeted/immunotherapy is reserved for metastatic disease.

cases.diagnosis: Renal cell carcinoma (localized, with renal vein tumor thrombus) and paraneoplastic polycythemia
pearls: Classic RCC triad (hematuria, flank pain, palpable mass) appears in a minority but is highly suggestive—characterize with contrast-enhanced CT.
RCC paraneoplastic syndromes: EPO (polycythemia), PTHrP (hypercalcemia), renin (HTN), Stauffer syndrome (hepatic dysfunction without mets).
A new non-reducing LEFT varicocele suggests left renal vein obstruction (left gonadal vein drains into the left renal vein).
RCC is chemo- and radio-resistant; localized disease (even with venous thrombus) is treated with radical nephrectomy.

cases.title: Painless Pink Urine in a Longtime Smoker
cases.chiefComplaint: Several episodes of painless visible blood in the urine over the past month.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man presents after noticing visibly bloody (pink-to-red) urine several times over the past month. The hematuria is painless, occurs throughout the stream, and sometimes includes small clots. He denies dysuria, urinary frequency, fever, flank pain, or recent vigorous exercise or trauma. He has mild nocturia attributed to age. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, BPH. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily. He is not on anticoagulation. Allergies: none. Social: 45-pack-year smoker, still smoking; worked 20 years in a chemical/dye plant with aniline dye and aromatic amine exposure; moderate alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems otherwise negative; no weight loss, no bone pain. He delayed seeking care because the bleeding came and went and never hurt, and he assumed it would resolve. His wife finally insisted he get checked after seeing red urine in the toilet.
cases.exam: He is well-appearing and in no distress. Abdomen is soft and nontender with no palpable masses and no suprapubic fullness. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness bilaterally. Genitourinary exam is unremarkable; no penile lesions. Digital rectal exam shows a mildly enlarged, smooth, non-nodular prostate consistent with BPH and no rectal mass. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy and no lower extremity edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 66-year-old smoker with painless gross hematuria and aromatic amine exposure needs which combination as the most appropriate workup?
options: Urinalysis and a course of empiric antibiotics
Cystoscopy plus CT urography (and urine cytology)
PSA and repeat urinalysis in 6 months
stages.explanation: Painless gross hematuria in an older smoker is bladder cancer until proven otherwise, and the standard evaluation is cystoscopy (to inspect the bladder/urethra and biopsy lesions) plus CT urography (to image the upper tracts/ureters), commonly with urine cytology. Empiric antibiotics are wrong without evidence of infection and would delay diagnosis. Renal ultrasound alone misses bladder and ureteral lesions and is inadequate for hematuria of malignant concern. Deferring with PSA and a repeat urinalysis ignores a major red flag. Cystoscopy is essential because CT urography can miss small flat bladder tumors (e.g., carcinoma in situ).

stages.label: CYSTOSCOPY & CYTOLOGY
stages.content:
CT urography: bladder wall thickening with a 2 cm enhancing mass on the right lateral bladder wall; upper tracts normal; no hydronephrosis
Urine cytology: atypical/malignant urothelial cells present
Cystoscopy: papillary, frond-like tumor on the right lateral bladder wall
Hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which exposure in this patient's history is the strongest risk factor for urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma of the bladder?
options: Cigarette smoking and aromatic amine (aniline dye) exposure
stages.explanation: Cigarette smoking is the single biggest risk factor for urothelial carcinoma, and occupational exposure to aromatic amines/aniline dyes (rubber, dye, leather, printing industries) is a classic additional cause—this patient has both. Hypertension and BPH are not causes of bladder cancer. Tamsulosin treats BPH symptoms and is unrelated. Other risks include chronic cyclophosphamide exposure and, for squamous cell bladder cancer specifically, chronic Schistosoma haematobium infection and chronic indwelling catheters. Identifying smoking and aromatic amines explains both the diagnosis and a key counseling point (smoking cessation).

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After cystoscopy reveals the tumor, what is the essential next procedure for both diagnosis and initial treatment/staging of the bladder lesion?
stages.explanation: Transurethral resection of bladder tumor (TURBT) is both diagnostic and therapeutic: it removes the visible tumor and, critically, samples the muscularis propria to determine whether the cancer is non-muscle-invasive or muscle-invasive—the pivotal distinction that drives all subsequent management. Immediate radical cystectomy is premature before pathologic staging and is reserved for muscle-invasive disease. Systemic chemotherapy (neoadjuvant) is used for muscle-invasive cancer, determined only after TURBT. Pelvic radiation is part of bladder-preservation protocols, again only after staging. TURBT first is the high-yield answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: TURBT shows high-grade non-muscle-invasive urothelial carcinoma (does not invade the muscularis propria). What is the appropriate adjuvant intravesical therapy?
stages.explanation: For high-grade non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer, intravesical BCG immunotherapy after complete TURBT reduces recurrence and progression and is standard. Radical cystectomy is reserved for muscle-invasive disease or for BCG-refractory high-risk non-muscle-invasive cancer, not first-line here. Systemic cisplatin-based (neoadjuvant) chemotherapy is for muscle-invasive disease before cystectomy. Whole-pelvis radiation is part of bladder-preservation (trimodality) therapy for muscle-invasive cancer, not non-muscle-invasive disease. The non-muscle-invasive versus muscle-invasive distinction (established by TURBT) is what selects intravesical BCG here.

cases.diagnosis: High-grade non-muscle-invasive urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma of the bladder
pearls: Painless gross hematuria in an older smoker is bladder cancer until proven otherwise—evaluate with cystoscopy + CT urography (± cytology).
Top risk factors for urothelial carcinoma: smoking and aromatic amines/aniline dyes; cyclophosphamide; Schistosoma haematobium causes squamous cell bladder cancer.
TURBT is diagnostic AND therapeutic and must sample muscularis propria to separate non-muscle-invasive from muscle-invasive disease.
High-grade non-muscle-invasive disease: intravesical BCG; muscle-invasive disease: neoadjuvant cisplatin chemo then radical cystectomy.

cases.title: A Swelling Belly and Constant Bloating
cases.chiefComplaint: Two months of progressive abdominal bloating, early satiety, and increasing abdominal girth.
cases.history: A 63-year-old postmenopausal woman is admitted with two months of persistent abdominal bloating, early satiety, vague pelvic discomfort, and a noticeably enlarging abdomen with a 4 kg weight gain despite poor appetite. She reports urinary frequency from pelvic pressure and mild constipation. She was treated for 'irritable bowel' without relief. She denies vaginal bleeding. PMH: hypertension; she is G0 (nulliparous), reached menarche at 11 and menopause at 54, and never used oral contraceptives. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: never-smoker, rare alcohol, retired accountant. Family history: mother with breast cancer at 60 and a maternal aunt with ovarian cancer; no genetic testing done. Review of systems notable for fatigue and shortness of breath on exertion in the last week. She initially attributed the bloating to weight gain and diet, but the rapidly enlarging abdomen and breathlessness prompted admission. Her family history of breast and ovarian cancer worries her.
cases.exam: She is uncomfortable lying flat. The abdomen is distended with a positive fluid wave and shifting dullness consistent with ascites; there is diffuse mild tenderness and a vague firm fullness in the upper abdomen suggestive of an omental mass. A firm, fixed mass is palpable in the right adnexa/pelvis on bimanual exam; the cul-de-sac feels nodular. Breath sounds are diminished at the right lung base (possible effusion). No supraclavicular nodes. No peripheral edema. No jaundice.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A postmenopausal woman with bloating, early satiety, ascites, and a fixed adnexal mass should first be evaluated with which study?
stages.explanation: Persistent bloating, early satiety, pelvic/abdominal pain, and urinary urgency in a postmenopausal woman are the classic (often missed) symptoms of ovarian cancer, especially with ascites and a fixed adnexal mass. The best initial evaluation is transvaginal/pelvic ultrasound to characterize the mass plus serum CA-125. Colonoscopy and upper endoscopy may be considered to exclude a GI primary later but are not the first step given the adnexal mass. Immediate diagnostic laparoscopy is premature before imaging and tumor markers; in ovarian cancer, definitive diagnosis and staging are typically achieved at exploratory laparotomy with cytoreductive surgery rather than diagnostic laparoscopy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CA-125 850 U/mL (markedly elevated)
CEA 2.1 ng/mL (normal)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: complex solid-cystic right adnexal mass (9 cm), large-volume ascites, omental caking, peritoneal nodularity, small right pleural effusion; no parenchymal liver or lung metastases
Paracentesis cytology: adenocarcinoma cells consistent with Müllerian/ovarian origin
Albumin 3.2 g/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT finding of thickened, nodular, infiltrated omentum ('omental caking') in this patient signifies what?
options: Peritoneal/omental metastatic spread of ovarian carcinoma
stages.explanation: Omental caking—diffuse thickening and nodular infiltration of the omentum by tumor—indicates transcoelomic peritoneal spread of ovarian carcinoma, the hallmark dissemination pattern that also produces malignant ascites. Combined with an adnexal mass, markedly elevated CA-125, and malignant cytology, it confirms advanced ovarian cancer. A lipoma or simple fatty omentum is fat-density and non-nodular, not infiltrative. Tuberculous peritonitis can rarely mimic carcinomatosis but is far less likely here given the adnexal mass, CA-125, and malignant cytology. Recognizing omental caking explains the diffuse abdominal symptoms and the surgical goal of omentectomy during debulking.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: Ovarian cancer with peritoneal implants beyond the pelvis and a malignant pleural effusion is staged and managed by which approach?
options: It is early-stage and curable with cystectomy alone
It is advanced (stage III–IV); managed with surgical cytoreduction (debulking) and platinum-based chemotherapy
Radiation therapy is the mainstay of treatment
Observation, since CA-125 will normalize on its own
stages.explanation: Peritoneal implants beyond the pelvis (omental caking, peritoneal nodularity) denote at least stage III, and a malignant pleural effusion denotes stage IV. Advanced epithelial ovarian cancer is managed with the combination of surgical cytoreduction (debulking—removing as much tumor as possible, often with hysterectomy/BSO/omentectomy) and platinum-based chemotherapy (carboplatin + paclitaxel), with neoadjuvant chemotherapy used when upfront optimal debulking is not feasible. It is not early-stage or curable with cystectomy. Radiation is not the mainstay for epithelial ovarian cancer. Observation is inappropriate for a malignancy; CA-125 is used to monitor response, not to expect spontaneous resolution.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given her family history of breast and ovarian cancer, which additional intervention is most appropriate at diagnosis?
options: Germline (and tumor) BRCA1/BRCA2 and homologous recombination testing, with genetic counseling
Immediate prophylactic mastectomy before any cancer treatment
stages.explanation: All patients with epithelial ovarian cancer should undergo germline and tumor BRCA1/BRCA2 (and broader homologous recombination deficiency) testing with genetic counseling, because results carry treatment implications (PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy benefits BRCA-mutated/HRD-positive tumors) and family/screening implications—pertinent given her mother's breast cancer and aunt's ovarian cancer. Saying no genetic evaluation is needed is incorrect and outdated. Prophylactic mastectomy is not an acute intervention and is decided later based on personal/genetic risk, not before treating the active ovarian cancer. A bone marrow biopsy has no role here. Genetic testing is high-yield and standard of care.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After completing initial therapy, what is the primary role of serial CA-125 measurements in this patient?
options: To screen the general population for ovarian cancer
To monitor treatment response and detect recurrence during follow-up
To establish the histologic subtype of the tumor
stages.explanation: In a patient with known epithelial ovarian cancer, serial CA-125 is used to monitor response to chemotherapy and to detect recurrence during surveillance—a falling level suggests response and a rising level can herald relapse. CA-125 is NOT a good general-population screening test (poor specificity; elevated in endometriosis, fibroids, pregnancy, peritoneal inflammation), so it is not recommended for asymptomatic screening. It cannot establish the histologic subtype, which requires pathology, nor can it determine BRCA status, which requires genetic/molecular testing. Using CA-125 appropriately for monitoring (not screening) is a high-yield distinction.

cases.diagnosis: Advanced epithelial ovarian carcinoma (stage IV) with malignant ascites and omental caking
pearls: Persistent bloating, early satiety, pelvic pain, and urinary urgency in a postmenopausal woman are the classic, often-missed symptoms of ovarian cancer.
Initial workup of a suspicious adnexal mass: transvaginal ultrasound + CA-125; omental caking signals transcoelomic peritoneal spread.
Advanced epithelial ovarian cancer is treated with surgical cytoreduction (debulking) plus platinum-based chemotherapy (carboplatin/paclitaxel).

All epithelial ovarian cancers warrant BRCA/HRD testing—it guides PARP inhibitor maintenance and informs hereditary breast-ovarian cancer screening; nulliparity and early menarche/late menopause raise risk while OCPs lower it.

cases.title: Bleeding After Intercourse
cases.chiefComplaint: Bleeding after intercourse and spotting between periods for the past three months.
cases.history: A 44-year-old woman presents with three months of intermittent postcoital bleeding and irregular intermenstrual spotting. She reports a thin, malodorous, occasionally blood-tinged vaginal discharge. There is no dysuria, fever, or weight loss, though she notes mild deep dyspareunia. Her last Pap smear was over six years ago and she recalls it being 'abnormal,' but she did not return for follow-up colposcopy. Menarche was at age 12; she has had four lifetime sexual partners and first intercourse at age 16. She is G3P3, all vaginal deliveries. PMH is notable for a 20-pack-year smoking history. She takes no regular medications and uses condoms inconsistently. She has no known drug allergies. She works as a cashier, drinks alcohol socially, and has never received the HPV vaccine.
