Family history is unremarkable for gynecologic malignancy. Review of systems is otherwise negative including no flank pain, hematuria, or change in bowel habits.
cases.exam: The patient appears well and in no distress. Abdomen is soft and nontender without palpable masses. Speculum examination reveals a firm, friable exophytic lesion on the anterior cervix measuring approximately 2.5 cm that bleeds readily on contact. There is scant blood-tinged discharge but no active hemorrhage. Bimanual examination demonstrates a mobile uterus of normal size; the parametria are soft and nonindurated, and there is no nodularity in the rectovaginal septum. Inguinal lymph nodes are not enlarged. No costovertebral angle tenderness or lower-extremity edema is present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A visible, friable cervical lesion is identified on speculum exam. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Repeat cervical cytology (Pap smear) of the lesion
Cervical biopsy of the visible lesion
Empiric metronidazole for presumed bacterial vaginosis
Reassurance and repeat exam in three months
stages.explanation: A grossly visible, friable cervical lesion mandates direct biopsy, not cytology. Pap smear is a screening test for asymptomatic patients; a negative cytology can never rule out cancer in a patient with a visible mass, so biopsy provides definitive histologic diagnosis. Repeat cytology (A) is wrong because it may be falsely negative due to necrosis and overlying inflammation and delays diagnosis. Empiric metronidazole (C) treats bacterial vaginosis but ignores a clearly suspicious mass and could dangerously delay cancer diagnosis. Reassurance with delayed follow-up (D) is unacceptable given a friable, bleeding lesion in a woman with prior abnormal cytology lost to follow-up. Biopsy of any visible cervical lesion is the standard of care.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY & IMAGING
stages.content:
Cervical biopsy: Invasive squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated
p16 immunostain: Diffusely positive (HPV-associated)
HPV testing: Positive for high-risk type 16
Exam under anesthesia: Tumor confined to cervix, 2.6 cm, no parametrial or vaginal involvement
Pelvic MRI: 2.6 cm enhancing cervical mass; no parametrial extension; no pelvic lymphadenopathy
CT chest/abdomen: No distant metastatic disease
Hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL

stages.label: PATHOGENESIS
stages.question: Which molecular mechanism best explains this HPV-16-associated squamous cell carcinoma?
options: Viral E6 and E7 proteins inactivate p53 and Rb tumor suppressors
Reciprocal translocation creating a BCR-ABL fusion oncoprotein
Germline APC mutation causing loss of cell-cycle checkpoints
Constitutive activation of the BRAF V600E kinase pathway
stages.explanation: High-risk HPV (types 16 and 18) drives cervical carcinogenesis through oncoproteins E6 and E7. E6 promotes degradation of p53, disabling apoptosis and DNA-damage response, while E7 binds and inactivates Rb, releasing E2F and driving unchecked cell-cycle progression; resulting p16 overexpression is the basis of the positive p16 stain. BCR-ABL (B) is the t(9;22) fusion of CML, unrelated to cervical cancer. Germline APC mutation (C) causes familial adenomatous polyposis and colorectal cancer, not HPV-driven squamous lesions. BRAF V600E (D) characterizes melanoma and some thyroid and colon cancers, not cervical squamous carcinoma. Understanding E6/p53 and E7/Rb is high-yield for HPV pathogenesis.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: Based on a 2.6 cm tumor confined to the cervix without parametrial, vaginal, or nodal involvement, what is the FIGO stage?
options: Stage IB2 (clinical lesion >2 to ≤4 cm confined to cervix)
Stage IIB (parametrial invasion not reaching pelvic wall)
stages.explanation: A clinically visible tumor 2.6 cm in greatest dimension confined to the cervix without parametrial extension is FIGO stage IB2 (lesions >2 cm to ≤4 cm). Stage IA1 (A) is microscopic disease with ≤3 mm stromal invasion and no visible lesion, which does not fit a 2.6 cm exophytic mass. Stage IIB (C) requires parametrial invasion, explicitly absent on MRI and exam. Stage IIIC1 (D) requires pelvic lymph node metastasis, which imaging did not show. Accurate FIGO staging now incorporates imaging and pathology and directly guides whether surgery or chemoradiation is chosen.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this woman with FIGO stage IB2 squamous cell carcinoma, what is the most appropriate primary treatment?
options: Radical hysterectomy with pelvic lymphadenectomy (or definitive chemoradiation)
Total abdominal hysterectomy without lymph node assessment
stages.explanation: Stage IB2 disease is treated with curative intent by either radical hysterectomy with pelvic lymphadenectomy or definitive cisplatin-based chemoradiation; both offer comparable survival, and choice depends on patient and tumor factors. Conization alone (A) is appropriate only for stage IA1 microinvasive disease, not a 2.6 cm invasive tumor. Simple total hysterectomy without lymph node evaluation (C) is inadequate because it fails to remove parametrial tissue and assess nodes that guide adjuvant therapy. Palliative single-agent chemotherapy (D) is reserved for metastatic or unresectable recurrent disease, not a localized curable tumor. Lymph node status critically determines need for adjuvant chemoradiation.

cases.diagnosis: Invasive squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix (HPV-16 associated), FIGO stage IB2
pearls: Any grossly visible cervical lesion requires biopsy, not cytology—a normal Pap cannot exclude cancer when a mass is present.
High-risk HPV E6 inactivates p53 and E7 inactivates Rb; diffuse p16 positivity is the immunohistochemical signature.
FIGO IB2 (tumor >2 to ≤4 cm) is treated with radical hysterectomy plus pelvic lymphadenectomy or definitive cisplatin-based chemoradiation.
Smoking and prior abnormal Pap lost to follow-up are major risk factors; HPV vaccination is primary prevention.

cases.title: Bleeding Years After Menopause
cases.chiefComplaint: New vaginal bleeding eight years after her last menstrual period.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman presents with two episodes of painless vaginal bleeding over the past month. She underwent natural menopause at age 50 and has had no bleeding since until now. The bleeding was light to moderate, with no associated pain, discharge, fever, or weight loss. She denies trauma and is not on hormone therapy. PMH includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and obesity (BMI 38). Her gynecologic history is notable for nulliparity, late menopause, and a history of polycystic ovary syndrome with chronic anovulation in her younger years. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg daily; she has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is significant for breast cancer in her mother at age 62 and colon cancer in a maternal uncle. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable, with no urinary symptoms, no rectal bleeding, and stable weight.
cases.exam: The patient is obese and in no acute distress. Cardiopulmonary examination is normal. Abdomen is soft, obese, and nontender with no palpable masses. External genitalia are normal. Speculum examination shows a normal-appearing cervix and a small amount of dark blood in the vaginal vault without an identifiable cervical or vaginal lesion. Bimanual examination reveals a normal-sized, mobile, nontender uterus with no adnexal masses. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy and no lower-extremity edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this postmenopausal woman with new vaginal bleeding, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step?
options: Reassurance, as postmenopausal bleeding is usually benign atrophy
Endometrial biopsy (or transvaginal ultrasound to assess endometrial thickness)
Hysterectomy for definitive diagnosis and treatment
Empiric vaginal estrogen and follow-up in six months
stages.explanation: Postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise and always requires evaluation, most directly with endometrial biopsy; transvaginal ultrasound measuring endometrial thickness is an acceptable initial test, with a stripe ≤4 mm making cancer unlikely. Reassurance (A) is wrong because although atrophy is the most common cause, malignancy must be excluded first. Hysterectomy (C) is overly aggressive before a tissue diagnosis is obtained. Empiric vaginal estrogen (D) is inappropriate and could worsen an estrogen-driven malignancy while delaying diagnosis. Tissue sampling is the key step for any postmenopausal bleeding.

stages.label: BIOPSY & IMAGING
stages.content:
Transvaginal ultrasound: Endometrial thickness 14 mm with heterogeneous echotexture
Endometrial biopsy: Endometrioid adenocarcinoma, FIGO grade 1
No myometrial mass on ultrasound
CT abdomen/pelvis: No lymphadenopathy or distant metastasis
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
CA-125: 18 U/mL (normal)

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which feature in this patient's history most directly increased her risk of this cancer through unopposed estrogen?
options: Nulliparity, obesity, and chronic anovulation from PCOS
Use of combined oral contraceptives in her 20s
stages.explanation: Endometrioid (type I) endometrial carcinoma is driven by chronic unopposed estrogen exposure. Nulliparity, obesity (peripheral aromatization of androgens to estrogen in adipose tissue), and PCOS-related chronic anovulation (no progesterone from a corpus luteum) all increase lifetime estrogen exposure and risk. Combined oral contraceptives (B) are actually protective because the progestin component opposes estrogen. Multiparity (C) is protective, as pregnancy provides progesterone-dominant states. Smoking (D) is paradoxically associated with a modestly reduced endometrial cancer risk and is not a driver here. Recognizing the unopposed-estrogen risk profile is central to type I endometrial cancer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this woman with FIGO grade 1 endometrioid adenocarcinoma and no evidence of metastasis, what is the most appropriate primary treatment?
options: Progestin therapy alone with serial biopsies
Total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and surgical staging
stages.explanation: The standard treatment for endometrial carcinoma is total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy plus surgical staging (peritoneal washings and lymph node assessment), which is both therapeutic and provides definitive staging to guide adjuvant therapy. Progestin therapy alone (A) is reserved for young women with grade 1 disease who wish to preserve fertility, which does not apply to a 58-year-old. Primary radiation (B) is reserved for poor surgical candidates. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy (D) is used for advanced or metastatic disease, not early-stage localized cancer. Surgery is the cornerstone of management for operable endometrial cancer.

stages.label: SCREENING
stages.question: Given her family history of colon and uterine-related cancers, which inherited syndrome warrants consideration and tumor screening?
stages.explanation: Lynch syndrome (HNPCC), caused by germline mismatch-repair gene mutations (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2), markedly increases risk of colorectal AND endometrial cancer; endometrial cancer is often the sentinel malignancy in affected women. Her personal endometrial cancer plus family colon cancer warrants mismatch-repair/microsatellite-instability tumor testing. Li-Fraumeni (A) features sarcomas, breast cancer, brain tumors, and adrenocortical carcinoma, not this pattern. FAP (C) causes hundreds of colonic polyps and is not classically associated with endometrial cancer. BRCA (D) raises breast and ovarian cancer risk, not endometrial. Universal mismatch-repair screening of endometrial tumors is now standard.

cases.diagnosis: Endometrioid adenocarcinoma of the endometrium (FIGO grade 1), type I, unopposed-estrogen related
pearls: Postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise—always evaluate with endometrial biopsy or transvaginal ultrasound (stripe >4 mm warrants sampling).
Type I endometrioid cancer arises from unopposed estrogen: obesity, nulliparity, PCOS/anovulation, late menopause, and tamoxifen are key risk factors.
Standard treatment is total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and surgical staging.
Endometrial cancer can be the sentinel malignancy of Lynch syndrome—screen tumors for mismatch-repair deficiency.

cases.title: A Painless Lump
cases.chiefComplaint: A painless lump in the right testicle noticed three weeks ago.
cases.history: A 27-year-old man presents after noticing a firm, painless swelling in his right testicle while showering about three weeks ago. He reports the testicle feels heavier and is not tender. He denies trauma, fever, dysuria, urethral discharge, or recent illness. There is no back pain, cough, or weight loss, though he reports mild fullness in the lower abdomen. He is sexually active with one female partner and uses condoms. PMH is notable for a history of right cryptorchidism surgically corrected (orchiopexy) at age 4. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He works as an electrician, smokes occasionally, and drinks alcohol on weekends. Family history is negative for testicular or other cancers. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no gynecomastia symptoms, no hemoptysis, and no neurologic complaints. He has never had a similar problem and has no history of sexually transmitted infections.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. There is a firm, nontender, approximately 3 cm intratesticular mass on the right that does not transilluminate and is distinct from the epididymis. The left testicle is normal. There is no scrotal erythema or warmth and no cremasteric reflex abnormality. No inguinal lymphadenopathy is palpable. Abdominal exam reveals mild fullness but no discrete palpable mass and no hepatomegaly. There is no gynecomastia and no supraclavicular adenopathy. Lungs are clear to auscultation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A firm, non-transilluminating intratesticular mass is palpated. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Trans-scrotal needle biopsy of the mass
Reassurance and reexamination in three months
stages.explanation: Scrotal ultrasound is the first imaging study for any palpable testicular mass; it distinguishes solid intratesticular masses (highly suspicious for malignancy) from benign processes like hydrocele, spermatocele, or epididymitis. A solid intratesticular mass is cancer until proven otherwise. Trans-scrotal needle biopsy (B) is contraindicated because it risks tumor seeding and altering lymphatic drainage; diagnosis is made by radical inguinal orchiectomy. Empiric antibiotics (C) are inappropriate for a painless, firm, non-transilluminating solid mass with no signs of infection. Reassurance (D) dangerously delays diagnosis of a potentially curable but aggressive tumor in a young man.

stages.label: ULTRASOUND & TUMOR MARKERS
stages.content:
Scrotal ultrasound: 3.1 cm solid, hypoechoic, hypervascular intratesticular mass in the right testis; left testis normal
Beta-hCG: 480 mIU/mL (elevated)
AFP: 320 ng/mL (elevated)
LDH: 410 U/L (elevated)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: 2 cm retroperitoneal (para-aortic) lymph node; no pulmonary or visceral metastases

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Both AFP and beta-hCG are elevated. What does this marker pattern indicate about the tumor histology?
stages.explanation: An elevated AFP is incompatible with pure seminoma and signals a nonseminomatous germ cell tumor (NSGCT) component—AFP is produced by yolk sac tumor and embryonal carcinoma elements, while beta-hCG can be elevated in both but is markedly raised in choriocarcinoma. Therefore elevated AFP, with or without elevated hCG, defines a nonseminomatous or mixed germ cell tumor and mandates NSGCT-pattern management. Pure seminoma (A) never elevates AFP; it may mildly raise hCG. Leydig cell tumor (C) is a sex cord-stromal tumor producing androgens/estrogens, not AFP/hCG. Testicular lymphoma (D), typical of older men, does not produce these germ cell markers. Marker patterns are essential for classification and prognosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate initial intervention for this testicular germ cell tumor?
options: Upfront BEP chemotherapy before any surgery
stages.explanation: Radical inguinal orchiectomy is both the initial diagnostic and therapeutic procedure for a suspected testicular germ cell tumor; the inguinal approach allows high ligation of the spermatic cord and avoids violating scrotal lymphatics. Trans-scrotal orchiectomy (A) is contraindicated because it risks tumor seeding and alters lymphatic drainage to inguinal nodes. Primary retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (B) is not the first step and is reserved for selected NSGCT cases after orchiectomy. Upfront chemotherapy (D) before removing the primary tumor is not standard; orchiectomy first establishes histology and removes the primary, then adjuvant chemotherapy (e.g., BEP) is given based on stage and markers.

stages.label: RISK FACTOR
stages.question: Which element of this patient's history is the best-established risk factor for his testicular cancer?
stages.explanation: Cryptorchidism (undescended testis) is the strongest established risk factor for testicular germ cell tumors, increasing risk severalfold even after orchiopexy, and risk is elevated in both the affected and contralateral testis. Smoking (A) is not a recognized significant risk factor for testicular cancer. Condom use (C) is unrelated to germ cell tumor risk. Electrical work (D) has no established association. Other risk factors include prior testicular cancer, family history, white race, and Klinefelter syndrome. Even with surgical correction in childhood, the prior cryptorchidism persists as the key risk factor here.

cases.diagnosis: Nonseminomatous (mixed) testicular germ cell tumor, right testis, with retroperitoneal nodal involvement
pearls: Any solid intratesticular mass is cancer until proven otherwise—evaluate with scrotal ultrasound and proceed to radical INGUINAL orchiectomy; never trans-scrotal biopsy.
Elevated AFP rules out pure seminoma and indicates a nonseminomatous germ cell tumor; LDH reflects tumor bulk.
Cryptorchidism is the strongest risk factor and persists even after orchiopexy, affecting both testes.
Germ cell tumors are among the most curable solid malignancies; BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin) chemotherapy is highly effective.

cases.title: A Mole That Changed
cases.chiefComplaint: A mole on his back that has grown darker and irregular over several months.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man presents because a mole on his upper back has changed over the past five months. His wife noticed it has become larger, darker, and more irregular, and it occasionally itches and recently bled once after he scratched it. He has many moles and a history of significant sun exposure, including blistering sunburns as a teenager and frequent use of tanning beds in his 30s. He works outdoors in construction. PMH is unremarkable. He takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. He has fair skin (Fitzpatrick type II), red hair, and light eyes. He smokes half a pack per day and drinks alcohol socially. Family history is significant for melanoma in his father, who was diagnosed at age 60. Review of systems is negative for headache, vision changes, weight loss, bone pain, or shortness of breath. He has not previously had any skin biopsies and does not perform regular skin self-examinations.
cases.exam: The patient has fair skin with numerous nevi. On the left upper back there is an 8 mm pigmented lesion with asymmetric borders, irregular notched margins, and color variegation ranging from brown to black with a small area of bluish discoloration. The surface is slightly raised with overlying crusting from prior bleeding. There is no surrounding erythema or ulceration of adjacent skin. No other concerning lesions are identified on full skin examination. There is no axillary, cervical, or inguinal lymphadenopathy. The liver is not enlarged, and the remainder of the examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the changing pigmented lesion with concerning features, what is the most appropriate biopsy technique?
options: Shave biopsy of the most pigmented area
Excisional biopsy with narrow margins removing the full lesion to depth
Punch biopsy of the lesion center only
Wide local excision with 2 cm margins immediately
stages.explanation: Suspected melanoma should be evaluated with a full-thickness excisional biopsy with narrow (1–3 mm) margins that removes the entire lesion down to subcutaneous fat. This allows accurate measurement of Breslow depth, the single most important prognostic factor that guides definitive surgical margins and staging. A shave biopsy (A) risks transecting the lesion base and underestimating depth. A punch biopsy of the center (C) may miss the deepest portion and is reserved for large lesions where complete excision is impractical. Immediate wide local excision with 2 cm margins (D) is premature before depth is known—definitive margins are determined by Breslow thickness after diagnosis.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
Excisional biopsy: Malignant melanoma, superficial spreading type
Breslow depth: 1.8 mm
Ulceration: Absent
Mitotic rate: 3 per mm²
BRAF V600E mutation: Detected
LDH: Normal
No clinical lymphadenopathy on exam

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: For this 1.8 mm-thick melanoma without ulceration and no palpable nodes, which procedure best assesses for regional nodal spread?
options: Sentinel lymph node biopsy at the time of wide local excision
PET scan alone to evaluate nodes
No nodal evaluation is needed for any melanoma
stages.explanation: Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is recommended for intermediate-thickness melanomas (generally >0.8–1.0 mm) without clinically apparent nodal disease, and a 1.8 mm tumor clearly qualifies. SLNB accurately stages the regional basin and provides important prognostic information with minimal morbidity. Routine complete lymph node dissection (A) is not done prophylactically given its morbidity and lack of survival benefit when nodes are clinically negative. PET scan alone (C) lacks sensitivity for microscopic nodal disease and cannot replace SLNB. No nodal evaluation (D) is wrong—thin melanomas may not need SLNB, but a 1.8 mm lesion warrants it. Breslow depth drives the decision to perform SLNB.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Based on a Breslow depth of 1.8 mm, what surgical margin is recommended for wide local excision of the primary site?
stages.explanation: Recommended wide local excision margins are determined by Breslow thickness: in situ melanoma 0.5–1 cm; ≤1 mm a 1 cm margin; >1–2 mm a 1–2 cm margin; and >2 mm a 2 cm margin. For a 1.8 mm melanoma, a 1–2 cm margin is appropriate, with 2 cm being the standard choice when feasible, making 2 cm the best answer here. A 0.5 cm margin (A) is only adequate for in situ disease. A 1 cm margin (B) is for thin (≤1 mm) lesions. A 3 cm margin (D) is excessive and not supported, as margins beyond 2 cm provide no additional survival benefit while increasing morbidity.

stages.label: TARGETED THERAPY
stages.question: The tumor harbors a BRAF V600E mutation. In the setting of advanced or metastatic melanoma, this finding enables which class of targeted therapy?
options: BRAF inhibitors (e.g., vemurafenib/dabrafenib) often combined with MEK inhibitors
Tyrosine kinase inhibitors targeting BCR-ABL (e.g., imatinib)
stages.explanation: Roughly half of cutaneous melanomas harbor activating BRAF V600 mutations, which constitutively activate the MAPK pathway. These tumors respond to BRAF inhibitors (vemurafenib, dabrafenib), and combining a BRAF inhibitor with a MEK inhibitor (e.g., trametinib) improves efficacy and reduces resistance—relevant for advanced/metastatic disease. Imatinib (B) targets BCR-ABL/KIT and is used in CML and GIST, not BRAF-mutant melanoma. Trastuzumab (C) targets HER2-amplified breast/gastric cancers. Aromatase inhibitors (D) treat hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer. Immune checkpoint inhibitors are also central to advanced melanoma, but BRAF status specifically enables BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Cutaneous malignant melanoma, superficial spreading type, Breslow depth 1.8 mm, BRAF V600E mutant
pearls: Use the ABCDE criteria (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variegation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution) to flag suspicious lesions for excisional biopsy.
Breslow depth is the most important prognostic factor and determines both wide-excision margins and the need for sentinel lymph node biopsy.
Excisional biopsy to subcutaneous fat is required to accurately measure depth—avoid shave biopsies of suspected melanoma.
BRAF V600E mutations enable targeted BRAF/MEK inhibitor therapy in advanced disease; checkpoint inhibitors are also mainstays.

cases.title: Worsening Headaches and a Weak Hand
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of progressively worsening headaches with new left-hand weakness.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man is admitted after six weeks of progressively worsening headaches, worse in the early morning and with coughing, now accompanied by new left-hand clumsiness and weakness noticed over the past week. His wife reports he has seemed confused and 'not himself,' with word-finding difficulty and one episode of vomiting without nausea this morning. He had a brief witnessed generalized seizure at home, prompting admission. He denies fever, neck stiffness, or recent travel. PMH includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications are amlodipine 5 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg daily; no known drug allergies. He is a retired accountant, a lifelong nonsmoker, and drinks one glass of wine occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is notable for unintentional 4 kg weight loss attributed to poor appetite, but no night sweats, cough, or hemoptysis. He has had no prior cancer diagnoses, no immunosuppression, and no risk factors for HIV. There is no history of head trauma.
cases.exam: The patient is drowsy but arousable and oriented to person and place only. There is mild expressive aphasia. Cranial nerves show subtle left central facial droop; fundoscopy reveals blurred disc margins suggesting papilledema. Motor exam demonstrates 4/5 strength in the left upper extremity with a left pronator drift; left-sided reflexes are brisk with an upgoing left plantar response. Sensation is intact. There is no neck stiffness or photophobia. No lymphadenopathy, no hepatosplenomegaly, and lungs are clear. The remainder of the general examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given progressive focal deficits, morning headache, papilledema, and a seizure, what is the most appropriate initial imaging?
options: MRI brain with and without contrast
Non-contrast head CT only, then discharge if negative
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain is the imaging study of choice for a suspected intracranial mass, providing superior characterization of tumor location, enhancement pattern, edema, and involvement of structures like the corpus callosum. Signs of raised intracranial pressure and focal deficits point to a space-occupying lesion. Lumbar puncture (A) is contraindicated before imaging when increased intracranial pressure is suspected, given the risk of cerebral herniation. Non-contrast CT alone with discharge (C) is inadequate—CT may detect a mass but lacks the detail of MRI and discharging a patient with these findings is unsafe. Carotid duplex (D) evaluates extracranial carotid stenosis, irrelevant to this presentation.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with contrast: 4.5 cm heterogeneously ring-enhancing mass in the right frontal lobe with central necrosis
The lesion crosses the corpus callosum to the contralateral hemisphere (butterfly pattern)
Extensive surrounding vasogenic edema with 6 mm midline shift
MR spectroscopy: Elevated choline-to-NAA ratio
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: No primary tumor or systemic malignancy identified
Dexamethasone started for edema

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A ring-enhancing lesion crossing the corpus callosum in this 61-year-old is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Solitary brain metastasis from lung cancer
stages.explanation: A large ring-enhancing lesion with central necrosis that crosses the corpus callosum in a butterfly pattern in an older adult is classic for glioblastoma multiforme (WHO grade 4 astrocytoma), supported by elevated choline/NAA on spectroscopy. A pyogenic abscess (A) typically shows restricted diffusion, smooth thin-walled enhancement, and accompanies infection/fever, absent here. A solitary metastasis (C) is possible, but the negative systemic workup and corpus callosum crossing favor primary GBM; metastases more often occur at the gray-white junction and multiply. A tumefactive MS plaque (D) usually shows an incomplete (open) ring of enhancement in younger patients with demyelinating history. The butterfly pattern is the key clue.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After surgical resection confirms glioblastoma, what is the standard adjuvant therapy regimen?
options: Concurrent and adjuvant temozolomide with radiation therapy
stages.explanation: The standard of care after maximal safe surgical resection of glioblastoma is concurrent chemoradiation with temozolomide followed by adjuvant temozolomide (the Stupp protocol), which improves survival compared with radiation alone. Whole-brain radiation alone (A) is inferior and not the standard for a localized resectable primary GBM; focal radiation plus temozolomide is preferred. Methotrexate-based therapy (C) is the regimen for primary CNS lymphoma, a different entity. Observation (D) is inappropriate for an aggressive grade 4 tumor. MGMT promoter methylation status predicts temozolomide responsiveness and is an important biomarker for prognosis and treatment decisions.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which molecular marker, if present, predicts a better response to temozolomide and improved prognosis in glioblastoma?
stages.explanation: MGMT (O6-methylguanine-DNA methyltransferase) promoter methylation silences the DNA-repair enzyme that would otherwise reverse temozolomide-induced alkylation, so methylated tumors are more sensitive to temozolomide and carry a better prognosis. EGFR amplification (A) is common in primary GBM and generally associated with aggressive behavior, not improved response. TERT promoter mutations (C) are frequent in GBM but do not predict temozolomide benefit and are often associated with worse outcomes. Chromosome 10 loss (D), including PTEN, is common in GBM and not a favorable predictive marker. IDH-mutant gliomas also carry better prognosis, but MGMT methylation is the key temozolomide-response predictor.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The morning after admission the patient becomes more somnolent with a fixed, dilated right pupil and worsening left hemiparesis. What is the most likely process?
options: New ischemic stroke in the posterior circulation
stages.explanation: A unilateral fixed, dilated pupil with declining consciousness and contralateral worsening weakness in a patient with a large hemispheric mass and midline shift signals uncal (transtentorial) herniation, where the medial temporal lobe compresses the ipsilateral oculomotor nerve (causing the blown pupil) and the midbrain. This is a neurosurgical emergency requiring osmotic therapy (mannitol or hypertonic saline), elevated head of bed, and urgent neurosurgical decompression. A posterior circulation stroke (B) would not explain the ipsilateral blown pupil from mass effect in this context. Bacterial meningitis (C) presents with fever and meningismus, absent here. Nonconvulsive status (D) would not produce a fixed dilated pupil from structural compression.

cases.diagnosis: Glioblastoma multiforme (WHO grade 4 astrocytoma) with butterfly extension across the corpus callosum
pearls: A ring-enhancing necrotic lesion crossing the corpus callosum (butterfly glioma) in an older adult is classic for glioblastoma multiforme.
Avoid lumbar puncture when raised intracranial pressure is suspected—obtain contrast MRI first to prevent herniation.
Standard therapy is maximal safe resection followed by the Stupp protocol: concurrent radiation plus temozolomide, then adjuvant temozolomide.
MGMT promoter methylation predicts temozolomide response; a unilateral fixed dilated pupil signals uncal herniation requiring emergent osmotic therapy and decompression.

cases.title: A Lump in the Neck
cases.chiefComplaint: A painless lump in the front of her neck noticed two months ago.
cases.history: A 38-year-old woman presents after feeling a painless lump in the front of her neck while applying lotion two months ago. The lump has not changed appreciably in size and causes no pain, difficulty swallowing, or voice change. She denies palpitations, heat or cold intolerance, weight change, tremor, or diarrhea, and feels generally well. She has no history of neck irradiation, though she recalls living abroad as a child. PMH is unremarkable. She takes only a daily multivitamin and has no known drug allergies. She is married with two children, works as a teacher, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with thyroid cancer; there is no family history of pheochromocytoma or other endocrine tumors. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no fevers, night sweats, or fatigue. She has never had a thyroid problem before and is not pregnant. Menstrual cycles are regular.
cases.exam: The patient is euthyroid-appearing and in no distress. There is a firm, nontender, approximately 1.8 cm nodule in the right thyroid lobe that moves with swallowing. The remainder of the thyroid is normal in size without diffuse enlargement. There is no palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. There is no exophthalmos, lid lag, or tremor; the skin is normally moist and warm. Cardiac rhythm is regular without tachycardia. Deep tendon reflexes are normal without delayed relaxation. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For this euthyroid-appearing patient with a solitary thyroid nodule, what is the most appropriate initial pair of tests?
options: Radioactive iodine uptake scan and thyroglobulin level
CT neck with contrast and serum calcitonin
stages.explanation: Evaluation of a thyroid nodule begins with serum TSH and a dedicated thyroid ultrasound. TSH assesses functional status; a low TSH suggests a possibly hyperfunctioning ('hot') nodule warranting a radioiodine scan, whereas a normal or high TSH prompts ultrasound-based risk stratification and likely fine-needle aspiration. A radioiodine uptake scan first (B) is only indicated when TSH is suppressed, and thyroglobulin is a surveillance marker, not a diagnostic one. CT with iodinated contrast (C) can interfere with subsequent radioiodine therapy and calcitonin is not a routine first-line screen for sporadic nodules. Immediate thyroidectomy (D) is premature before cytologic diagnosis.

stages.label: ULTRASOUND & CYTOLOGY
stages.content:
Serum TSH: 1.8 mIU/L (normal)
Thyroid ultrasound: 1.8 cm hypoechoic solid right-lobe nodule with irregular margins and punctate microcalcifications; no abnormal cervical nodes
Fine-needle aspiration cytology: Cells with nuclear grooves, intranuclear pseudoinclusions, and 'Orphan Annie eye' nuclei; psammoma bodies present
Diagnosis on cytology: Papillary thyroid carcinoma

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The cytology shows nuclear grooves, intranuclear pseudoinclusions, 'Orphan Annie eye' nuclei, and psammoma bodies. Which thyroid cancer does this describe?
stages.explanation: Papillary thyroid carcinoma is defined by its nuclear features: 'Orphan Annie eye' nuclei (empty-appearing with dispersed chromatin), nuclear grooves, and intranuclear pseudoinclusions, often with psammoma bodies (laminated calcifications). It is the most common thyroid cancer and spreads via lymphatics with an excellent prognosis. Follicular carcinoma (A) is diagnosed by capsular/vascular invasion on histology (not cytology) and cannot be diagnosed by FNA alone. Medullary carcinoma (C) arises from parafollicular C cells, shows amyloid stroma, and produces calcitonin. Anaplastic carcinoma (D) is an aggressive, undifferentiated tumor of elderly patients with rapid growth. The described nuclear features are pathognomonic for papillary carcinoma.

stages.label: SPREAD
stages.question: How does papillary thyroid carcinoma most characteristically spread?
options: Hematogenous spread to bone and lung early
Lymphatic spread to cervical lymph nodes
Direct extension into the spinal cord
stages.explanation: Papillary thyroid carcinoma characteristically spreads through lymphatics to regional cervical lymph nodes, which is common yet carries a favorable prognosis. In contrast, follicular thyroid carcinoma tends to spread hematogenously to bone and lung, which is the classic board distinction. Early hematogenous spread to bone and lung (A) typifies follicular, not papillary, carcinoma. Direct spinal cord extension (C) is not characteristic of differentiated thyroid cancer. Transcoelomic peritoneal seeding (D) is seen with ovarian and other abdominal cancers, not thyroid cancer. Remembering 'Papillary—Palpable nodes (lymphatic); Follicular—hematogenous' is a useful mnemonic.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for this 1.8 cm papillary thyroid carcinoma?
options: Surgical resection (lobectomy or total thyroidectomy), with radioiodine if indicated
Active surveillance with annual ultrasound only
stages.explanation: Surgery is the mainstay for papillary thyroid carcinoma; for a 1.8 cm tumor, either thyroid lobectomy or total thyroidectomy is appropriate depending on risk features, followed by radioactive iodine ablation in higher-risk cases and TSH-suppressive levothyroxine. External-beam radiation (B) is not primary therapy for differentiated thyroid cancer and is reserved for unresectable or aggressive disease. Cytotoxic chemotherapy (C) plays essentially no role in standard differentiated thyroid cancer. Active surveillance (D) is an option only for very small (subcentimeter) low-risk papillary microcarcinomas, not a 1.8 cm tumor with suspicious features. Differentiated thyroid cancer has an excellent prognosis with appropriate surgery.

cases.diagnosis: Papillary thyroid carcinoma of the right lobe
pearls: Work up a thyroid nodule with serum TSH plus ultrasound; FNA is the key diagnostic test for suspicious nodules.
Papillary carcinoma shows 'Orphan Annie eye' nuclei, nuclear grooves, pseudoinclusions, and psammoma bodies—and spreads via lymphatics.
Papillary spreads to cervical nodes (good prognosis); follicular spreads hematogenously to bone and lung.
Treatment is surgical (lobectomy or total thyroidectomy) with radioiodine and TSH suppression in selected cases; prognosis is excellent.

cases.title: Flushing, Diarrhea, and a Neck Nodule
cases.chiefComplaint: A neck lump and episodes of facial flushing with watery diarrhea.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents with a firm neck lump noticed over the past few months, accompanied by recurrent episodes of facial flushing and frequent watery diarrhea. He denies palpitations, headaches with sweating spells, or syncope. He has lost 3 kg unintentionally. PMH is notable for nephrolithiasis (calcium stones) and treated hypertension. He takes amlodipine 5 mg daily; no known drug allergies. He is a nonsmoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. Critically, his family history is striking: his father died of a 'thyroid cancer' in his 40s, and a paternal cousin had surgery for an adrenal tumor causing dangerous blood pressure spikes. He has two teenage children who have not been screened. He works as an engineer. Review of systems is positive for the flushing and diarrhea but negative for hemoptysis, bone pain, or neurologic symptoms. He has no history of neck irradiation. He notes occasional tingling around his lips but no tetany, and has not had recent infections.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no acute distress. There is a firm, nontender 2 cm nodule in the left thyroid lobe that moves with swallowing, with a palpable 1.5 cm firm left cervical lymph node. There is no exophthalmos or lid lag. Cardiac rhythm is regular. Abdominal exam is benign without organomegaly. There is no truncal obesity, striae, or skin hyperpigmentation. Neurologic exam is normal with no tremor. Notably, there are no mucosal neuromas on the lips or tongue and no marfanoid habitus. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a thyroid nodule with a suspicious cervical node and a striking family history, which tumor marker is most specific for the suspected diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Medullary thyroid carcinoma arises from parafollicular C cells, which secrete calcitonin, making serum calcitonin the most specific and sensitive tumor marker for diagnosis and monitoring; CEA is a useful secondary marker. Thyroglobulin (A) is produced by follicular cells and is a marker for differentiated (papillary/follicular) thyroid cancer, NOT medullary carcinoma, which does not arise from follicular epithelium. TSH (C) reflects thyroid function and is not a tumor marker for medullary carcinoma. Alpha-fetoprotein (D) is a marker for hepatocellular carcinoma and certain germ cell tumors, unrelated to thyroid C cells. The family history of thyroid cancer plus an adrenal pressor tumor strongly suggests a hereditary medullary carcinoma syndrome.

stages.label: LABS & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
Serum calcitonin: 1,250 pg/mL (markedly elevated)
CEA: 28 ng/mL (elevated)
Fine-needle aspiration: Spindle/polygonal cells in nests; amyloid stroma that stains with Congo red
Immunohistochemistry: Calcitonin positive
Serum calcium: 11.4 mg/dL (elevated)
PTH: 96 pg/mL (elevated)
Plasma metanephrines: Elevated
Germline RET proto-oncogene mutation: Detected

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The FNA shows nests of cells with amyloid stroma that is Congo-red positive and calcitonin-positive. What is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Medullary thyroid carcinoma derives from calcitonin-producing parafollicular C cells; the hallmark histology is nests of polygonal/spindle cells in an amyloid stroma (derived from calcitonin) that stains apple-green under polarized light with Congo red, and tumor cells stain positive for calcitonin. Papillary carcinoma (A) shows 'Orphan Annie' nuclei and psammoma bodies, not amyloid, and arises from follicular cells. Anaplastic carcinoma (C) is an undifferentiated, pleomorphic, rapidly growing tumor without amyloid or calcitonin positivity. Hashimoto thyroiditis (D) is an autoimmune lymphocytic infiltrate with Hürthle cells, not a malignancy with amyloid. The amyloid stroma plus calcitonin positivity is pathognomonic.

stages.label: SYNDROME
stages.question: A germline RET mutation, elevated calcium with high PTH, and elevated metanephrines indicate which inherited syndrome?
stages.explanation: MEN 2A (Sipple syndrome), caused by germline RET proto-oncogene mutations, comprises medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma (elevated metanephrines, explaining his hypertension and the cousin's adrenal pressor tumor), and primary hyperparathyroidism (hypercalcemia with elevated PTH, explaining his calcium stones). MEN 1 (A) involves the '3 Ps'—Parathyroid, Pancreatic, and Pituitary tumors—caused by MEN1/menin mutations, not RET, and lacks medullary thyroid cancer. MEN 2B (C) features medullary carcinoma and pheochromocytoma but with mucosal neuromas and marfanoid habitus and NO hyperparathyroidism—both absent/present-mismatched here. Von Hippel-Lindau (D) causes hemangioblastomas, renal cell carcinoma, and pheochromocytoma, not medullary thyroid cancer or hyperparathyroidism.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Before performing thyroidectomy in this patient, which condition must be identified and addressed first?
stages.explanation: A coexisting pheochromocytoma must be excluded and treated BEFORE any surgery, including thyroidectomy. Anesthesia and surgical manipulation can precipitate a catastrophic catecholamine surge and hypertensive crisis if an unrecognized pheochromocytoma is present, so it must be controlled first with alpha-blockade (e.g., phenoxybenzamine) followed by beta-blockade, and resected before or concurrently. Primary hyperparathyroidism (A) is also part of MEN 2A and is managed, but it is not the immediate life-threatening peri-operative concern. Vitamin D deficiency (C) and hypothyroidism (D) are not acute peri-operative emergencies in this context. The rule 'treat the pheo first' is a classic high-yield principle in MEN 2 management.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Regarding his unscreened teenage children, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: No action unless they develop symptoms
Germline RET mutation testing and genetic counseling, with prophylactic thyroidectomy if positive
Annual thyroid ultrasound only, without genetic testing
stages.explanation: First-degree relatives of a patient with a germline RET mutation should undergo genetic counseling and RET mutation testing; carriers are offered prophylactic total thyroidectomy because penetrance of medullary thyroid carcinoma approaches 100%, and timing is guided by the specific RET codon's risk level. Waiting for symptoms (A) is unacceptable given near-complete penetrance and the curability of early disease. Ultrasound alone without genetic testing (C) misses the opportunity to identify carriers and intervene prophylactically. Empiric calcitonin-lowering medication (D) does not exist as a preventive strategy and does not address the underlying cancer risk. Identifying RET carriers and offering prophylactic thyroidectomy is a landmark example of cancer prevention through genetic testing.

cases.diagnosis: Medullary thyroid carcinoma in the setting of MEN 2A (germline RET mutation)
pearls: Medullary thyroid carcinoma arises from parafollicular C cells; calcitonin (and CEA) is the tumor marker, and histology shows amyloid stroma (Congo-red positive).
MEN 2A = medullary thyroid carcinoma + pheochromocytoma + primary hyperparathyroidism, all driven by germline RET mutations.
Always exclude and treat pheochromocytoma (alpha- then beta-blockade) BEFORE any surgery to avoid hypertensive crisis.
Offer RET testing to first-degree relatives; carriers undergo prophylactic thyroidectomy because penetrance approaches 100%.

cases.title: Hoarseness and a Persistent Neck Mass
cases.chiefComplaint: A neck mass that has not resolved and three months of hoarseness.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man presents with a left-sided neck mass that he first noticed two months ago and that has not resolved, along with progressive hoarseness for about three months. He reports a sensation of something in his throat and mild odynophagia but no significant dysphagia to solids. He has had occasional blood-tinged sputum. He denies fever or night sweats but reports a 5 kg unintentional weight loss. PMH includes hypertension and GERD. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history and drinks four to five alcoholic beverages daily. Medications are lisinopril 10 mg daily and omeprazole 20 mg daily; no known drug allergies. He works as a long-haul truck driver. Family history is noncontributory. He has not had dental care in years and reports poor oral hygiene. Review of systems is otherwise notable for chronic cough attributed to smoking; no chest pain, no neurologic deficits, and no ear pain. He has not previously had any imaging of the neck or laryngoscopy.
cases.exam: The patient is a thin man with a hoarse voice. There is a firm, fixed, nontender 3 cm mass in the left upper cervical chain (level II/III). Oral examination shows poor dentition and leukoplakia on the left lateral tongue; no obvious oral cavity ulcer is seen. There is no thyromegaly. Indirect mirror examination suggests a left vocal cord lesion with reduced cord mobility. There is no other lymphadenopathy and no hepatosplenomegaly. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cranial nerve examination is otherwise intact, and there is no facial weakness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For this 58-year-old smoker and drinker with a fixed neck mass and hoarseness, what is the most appropriate evaluation?
options: Open excisional biopsy of the neck node first
Trial of antibiotics for presumed reactive lymphadenitis
Panendoscopy with biopsy of the primary lesion plus fine-needle aspiration of the node and cross-sectional imaging
Reassurance, as neck masses in adults are usually benign
stages.explanation: A persistent neck mass with hoarseness in an older patient with heavy tobacco and alcohol use is head and neck squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise. The correct workup is examination of the upper aerodigestive tract (laryngoscopy/panendoscopy) to identify and biopsy the primary lesion, fine-needle aspiration of the neck node, and contrast CT/MRI for staging. Open excisional biopsy of the node first (A) is discouraged because it can disrupt tissue planes, complicate later neck dissection, and worsen outcomes; FNA is preferred. Empiric antibiotics (B) waste time for a clearly suspicious fixed mass. Reassurance (D) is dangerous—a firm, fixed adult neck mass persisting beyond two weeks warrants malignancy workup.

stages.label: BIOPSY & IMAGING
stages.content:
Laryngoscopy: Ulcerated lesion on the left true vocal cord/supraglottis
Biopsy of primary lesion: Invasive squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, keratin pearls present
p16 immunostain: Negative
HPV testing: Negative
FNA of neck node: Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma
CT neck/chest with contrast: 2.5 cm laryngeal primary; 3 cm left level II node; no distant metastasis

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: Given the laryngeal primary, heavy tobacco/alcohol use, keratin pearls, and p16-negative HPV-negative status, what is the most likely driving etiology?
options: Cumulative tobacco and alcohol carcinogen exposure
stages.explanation: This is a classic tobacco- and alcohol-driven, p16-negative/HPV-negative head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, typically arising in the larynx, hypopharynx, or oral cavity, with keratinizing histology (keratin pearls). Tobacco and alcohol act synergistically as carcinogens. HPV-16 (A) drives a distinct subset of OROPHARYNGEAL (tonsil/base of tongue) cancers that are p16-positive and carry a better prognosis—the opposite of this p16-negative laryngeal tumor. Epstein-Barr virus (C) is associated with nasopharyngeal carcinoma, not laryngeal SCC, and is common in specific geographic populations. GERD alone (D) is a risk factor for esophageal adenocarcinoma, not the primary driver of this laryngeal squamous carcinoma. HPV status critically stratifies prognosis.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: How does this tumor's HPV/p16 status affect prognosis compared with HPV-positive oropharyngeal carcinoma?
options: HPV/p16-negative tumors carry a worse prognosis
HPV/p16-negative tumors carry a better prognosis
HPV status has no effect on prognosis
stages.explanation: HPV/p16-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinomas have a significantly BETTER prognosis and greater treatment responsiveness than HPV/p16-negative tumors. Therefore this p16-negative, tobacco/alcohol-related laryngeal carcinoma carries a comparatively WORSE prognosis, reflecting the field cancerization and genomic instability of carcinogen-driven disease. Stating HPV-negative tumors carry a better prognosis (B) is the reverse of the established data. Saying HPV status has no effect (C) is incorrect—it is one of the strongest prognostic and staging factors, and HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer now has its own staging system. Claiming HPV-negative tumors never metastasize (D) is false; this patient already has nodal metastasis. HPV status meaningfully drives prognosis and de-escalation trials.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this locally advanced laryngeal SCC with nodal involvement, which treatment approach is most appropriate?
options: Multimodality therapy: concurrent chemoradiation (or surgery) with multidisciplinary planning
Observation with repeat imaging in six months
stages.explanation: Locally advanced head and neck squamous cell carcinoma requires multidisciplinary, multimodality treatment—typically concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiation for organ (larynx) preservation, or primary surgery with neck dissection followed by adjuvant therapy, depending on resectability and patient factors. Single-agent palliative chemotherapy (A) is reserved for metastatic or incurable disease, not this potentially curable locoregional tumor. Observation (C) is inappropriate for an invasive cancer with nodal spread. Antiviral therapy (D) has no role, particularly in an HPV-negative tumor. Smoking cessation and addressing alcohol use are also essential, as continued exposure increases second primary tumors and worsens outcomes.

cases.diagnosis: HPV-negative laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma with cervical nodal metastasis (tobacco/alcohol related)
pearls: A firm, fixed neck mass persisting beyond 2 weeks in an older smoker/drinker is head and neck SCC until proven otherwise—do panendoscopy with biopsy and FNA, not open node biopsy first.
Tobacco and alcohol act synergistically; p16-negative/HPV-negative tumors classically involve the larynx, hypopharynx, and oral cavity with keratinizing histology.
HPV/p16-positive oropharyngeal SCC has a markedly better prognosis than HPV-negative disease.
Locally advanced HNSCC is treated with multimodality therapy (chemoradiation for organ preservation or surgery plus adjuvant therapy); smoking cessation is essential.

cases.title: Cancer Everywhere, Source Unknown
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening abdominal swelling, fatigue, and weight loss over two months.
cases.history: A 64-year-old woman is admitted with two months of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, fatigue, and 7 kg unintentional weight loss. She also reports dull right upper quadrant discomfort and dyspnea on exertion. She denies hematemesis, melena, vaginal bleeding, hemoptysis, or breast lumps. PMH includes hypothyroidism and osteoarthritis. Medications are levothyroxine 75 mcg daily and as-needed acetaminophen; no known drug allergies. She is a former smoker (15 pack-years, quit 10 years ago) and drinks alcohol rarely. She is up to date on screening: a normal mammogram one year ago, normal colonoscopy three years ago, and normal Pap smears. Family history includes a sister with breast cancer at age 70. She worked as a librarian. Review of systems is positive for fatigue and the abdominal symptoms but negative for fevers, night sweats, bone pain, headaches, and neurologic changes. She has no jaundice and no recent travel.
cases.exam: The patient is fatigued-appearing and mildly tachypneic. There is shifting dullness and a fluid wave consistent with ascites; the abdomen is distended and mildly tender in the right upper quadrant with a firm, nodular hepatic edge palpable below the costal margin. There is no palpable breast mass and no axillary, cervical, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Pelvic examination reveals no discrete adnexal mass. Lungs have decreased breath sounds at the right base. There is no jaundice, no spider angiomata, and no peripheral edema. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Imaging shows liver lesions and ascites suspicious for metastatic cancer. What is the most important next step to direct therapy?
options: Obtain a tissue biopsy of an accessible metastatic site for histology and immunohistochemistry
Start palliative hospice care without further workup
Repeat mammography and colonoscopy first regardless of biopsy
stages.explanation: When metastatic cancer is suspected, obtaining tissue from the most accessible site for histology and immunohistochemistry is the essential next step—it confirms malignancy, defines the tumor type (e.g., adenocarcinoma vs. squamous vs. neuroendocrine), and guides a focused search for the primary and appropriate therapy. Empiric chemotherapy without a diagnosis (A) is inappropriate and potentially harmful. Hospice without diagnosis (C) is premature, as some cancers of unknown primary are highly treatable. Reflexively repeating mammography and colonoscopy before biopsy (D) is less efficient; tissue diagnosis with IHC directs which targeted workup is worthwhile. Biopsy with IHC is the cornerstone of the cancer-of-unknown-primary evaluation.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY & IMAGING
stages.content:
Liver biopsy: Metastatic adenocarcinoma
Immunohistochemistry: CK7 positive, CK20 negative, TTF-1 negative
ER/PR: Negative; GCDFP-15 negative; mammaglobin negative
PAX8: Positive
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: Multiple liver lesions and ascites; no discrete lung, breast, GI, or pancreatic primary
Mammogram and colonoscopy (recent): Normal
CA-125: Elevated; CEA: Mildly elevated
Upper endoscopy: Normal

stages.label: DEFINITION
stages.question: Despite thorough imaging and endoscopy, no primary tumor is found. What best defines this clinical entity?
options: A premalignant condition requiring only surveillance
stages.explanation: Cancer of unknown primary (CUP) is defined as histologically confirmed metastatic cancer for which the anatomic site of origin remains unidentified after a standardized diagnostic workup (history, exam, imaging, and immunohistochemistry). This patient has biopsy-proven metastatic adenocarcinoma without an identifiable primary despite appropriate evaluation, fitting CUP. A benign reactive process (B) is excluded by malignant histology. Primary hepatocellular carcinoma (C) would arise from hepatocytes (often in cirrhosis) and would not be an adenocarcinoma with this IHC profile; the liver lesions here are metastases. A premalignant condition (D) is incompatible with established metastatic adenocarcinoma. CUP accounts for a small but important fraction of cancer diagnoses and relies on IHC-guided management.

stages.label: IHC INTERPRETATION
stages.question: The IHC profile is CK7-positive, CK20-negative, TTF-1-negative, PAX8-positive, with elevated CA-125. Which favorable subset does this suggest?
stages.explanation: CK7-positive/CK20-negative with PAX8 positivity and elevated CA-125 in a woman with peritoneal carcinomatosis/ascites points toward a Müllerian (ovarian/peritoneal-type) adenocarcinoma profile—a clinically important 'favorable' CUP subset treated like ovarian cancer with platinum-based chemotherapy. A colorectal primary (A) is typically CK20-positive, CK7-negative, and CDX2-positive—the opposite pattern. Lung adenocarcinoma (B) is usually TTF-1-positive, which is negative here. Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (D) is not a CK7/PAX8 adenocarcinoma and does not fit. Identifying favorable CUP subsets (such as Müllerian-type peritoneal carcinomatosis in women) is crucial because they respond to specific, effective regimens.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Recognizing this favorable Müllerian-type CUP subset, what is the most appropriate management approach?
options: Treat as ovarian/peritoneal carcinoma with platinum-based chemotherapy (and consider surgical cytoreduction)
Broad-spectrum antibiotics and supportive care only
Single empiric agent chosen at random
Radiation to the liver as definitive therapy
stages.explanation: Favorable CUP subsets are managed according to the presumed tumor type they most resemble. A woman with peritoneal carcinomatosis, ascites, a Müllerian IHC profile (PAX8-positive, CK7-positive/CK20-negative), and elevated CA-125 should be treated like ovarian/primary peritoneal carcinoma with platinum/taxane-based chemotherapy and consideration of surgical cytoreduction, which offers meaningful benefit. Antibiotics and supportive care only (B) ignore a treatable malignancy. A randomly chosen single empiric agent (C) abandons the rational, subset-directed strategy that improves outcomes. Liver radiation as definitive therapy (D) is inappropriate for disseminated peritoneal adenocarcinoma. Matching favorable CUP subsets to effective regimens is the core principle that changes prognosis.

stages.label: WORKUP RATIONALE
stages.question: What is the primary purpose of the immunohistochemistry panel (CK7, CK20, TTF-1, PAX8) in this CUP workup?
options: To grade the tumor's mitotic rate
To predict the likely tissue of origin and direct targeted treatment
To measure tumor marker levels in blood
To assess the patient's immune competence
stages.explanation: Immunohistochemistry in CUP uses lineage-specific markers to predict the likely tissue of origin and thereby direct a focused workup and tumor-type-specific therapy. The CK7/CK20 pattern narrows organ groups, TTF-1 suggests lung or thyroid, PAX8 suggests Müllerian/renal/thyroid lineage, and additional markers (ER/PR, GCDFP-15, CDX2) refine the differential. Grading mitotic rate (A) is a histologic feature assessed on routine stains, not the purpose of this lineage panel. Measuring serum tumor markers (C) is a separate blood test, not what IHC does on tissue. Assessing immune competence (D) is unrelated. IHC-guided identification of treatable, favorable subsets is what makes the CUP workup clinically meaningful.

cases.diagnosis: Cancer of unknown primary—favorable Müllerian (ovarian/peritoneal-type) adenocarcinoma subset
pearls: Cancer of unknown primary is metastatic cancer with no identifiable origin after a standard workup; tissue biopsy with immunohistochemistry is the cornerstone.
IHC predicts tissue of origin: CK7+/CK20− with PAX8+ and elevated CA-125 in a woman with peritoneal disease suggests a Müllerian/ovarian-type primary.
Recognize 'favorable' CUP subsets (e.g., peritoneal carcinomatosis in women, midline germ cell, isolated axillary nodes in women) because they respond to specific effective regimens.
Favorable subsets are treated like the tumor they resemble—here, platinum-based therapy as for ovarian/peritoneal carcinoma.

cases.title: Flushing, Diarrhea, and Wheezing Spells
cases.chiefComplaint: Episodes of facial flushing, watery diarrhea, and wheezing for several months.
cases.history: A 56-year-old woman presents with several months of episodic facial flushing, frequent watery diarrhea, and intermittent wheezing. The flushing episodes are sometimes triggered by eating, stress, or alcohol, last minutes, and involve the face and upper chest. She has had crampy abdominal pain and a 4 kg weight loss. She denies hematochezia, fever, or food allergies, and an inhaler trial for presumed asthma gave little relief. PMH is unremarkable. She takes no regular medications and uses a rescue albuterol inhaler with limited benefit; no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks wine occasionally, noting wine seems to provoke flushing. She works as a chef. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for palpitations during flushing spells and exertional dyspnea, but negative for headache with sweating, heat intolerance, or rashes. She has not had prior abdominal imaging or endoscopy. She reports the diarrhea persists even with fasting and has become more frequent over time.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing between episodes. During the visit she has a transient episode of facial flushing. There is mild expiratory wheezing on lung auscultation. Cardiac exam reveals a soft holosystolic murmur best heard at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration, suggesting a right-sided (tricuspid) valvular lesion. The abdomen is soft with mild diffuse tenderness; the liver edge is palpable and firm with a nodular contour approximately 3 cm below the costal margin. There is no jaundice. There is no goiter and no tremor. There is no lower-extremity edema. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given episodic flushing, secretory diarrhea, wheezing, and a nodular liver, which biochemical test is most appropriate to confirm the suspected syndrome?
stages.explanation: The triad of flushing, secretory diarrhea, and wheezing with right-sided cardiac valve disease and liver lesions is classic for carcinoid syndrome, caused by serotonin-secreting neuroendocrine tumors. The best confirmatory test is a 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA, the major serotonin metabolite, which is highly specific (patients should avoid serotonin-rich foods before testing). Plasma metanephrines (B) screen for pheochromocytoma, which causes flushing with episodic hypertension and headache/sweating, not secretory diarrhea and wheezing. Serum gastrin (C) evaluates Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (peptic ulcers, diarrhea), not flushing/wheezing. Urinary cortisol (D) screens for Cushing syndrome. Chromogranin A is a useful adjunct neuroendocrine marker, but 5-HIAA is the classic confirmatory test for carcinoid syndrome.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
24-hour urinary 5-HIAA: Markedly elevated
Serum chromogranin A: Elevated
CT abdomen: Multiple hypervascular liver lesions and a mass in the distal ileum with mesenteric desmoplastic reaction
Liver biopsy: Well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor; chromogranin and synaptophysin positive
NT-proBNP: Elevated
Echocardiogram: Tricuspid regurgitation with thickened, retracted valve leaflets (carcinoid heart disease)

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why does this patient develop the full carcinoid syndrome only now, given the primary tumor is in the ileum?
options: The tumor has spread to the lungs, bypassing detoxification
Liver metastases allow serotonin to bypass hepatic first-pass metabolism and reach the systemic circulation
The kidney has stopped clearing serotonin
The primary ileal tumor secretes catecholamines
stages.explanation: Midgut (ileal) carcinoid tumors drain via the portal vein, so the liver normally metabolizes secreted serotonin/vasoactive substances (first-pass effect), preventing systemic symptoms. Carcinoid SYNDROME typically appears only once LIVER METASTASES release these mediators directly into the hepatic veins and systemic circulation, bypassing hepatic clearance—explaining why this patient symptomatic now has a nodular metastatic liver. Lung spread (A) is not the mechanism here, though a primary bronchial carcinoid can cause syndrome without liver mets because it drains systemically. Reduced renal clearance (C) is not the cause. The tumor secretes serotonin and other peptides, not catecholamines (D), which would suggest pheochromocytoma. The liver-bypass concept is the key teaching point.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The echocardiogram shows tricuspid regurgitation with thickened, retracted leaflets. What deficiency should also be screened for given chronic serotonin overproduction?
stages.explanation: Carcinoid tumors divert dietary tryptophan toward serotonin synthesis, depleting the tryptophan available for niacin (vitamin B3) production and potentially causing pellagra—the classic triad of dermatitis, diarrhea, and dementia. Screening and niacin repletion are warranted. The right-sided valve fibrosis (carcinoid heart disease) results from serotonin-induced fibrous plaque deposition on tricuspid and pulmonary valves, sparing left-sided valves because the lungs inactivate serotonin. Vitamin B12 deficiency (A) causes megaloblastic anemia and neuropathy but is not the tryptophan-related deficiency here. Vitamin K deficiency (C) causes coagulopathy, unrelated to serotonin overproduction. Folate deficiency (D) causes megaloblastic anemia but is not the carcinoid-specific concern. The tryptophan-to-niacin diversion is high-yield.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate therapy to control this patient's carcinoid syndrome symptoms?
options: A somatostatin analog (e.g., octreotide or lanreotide)
stages.explanation: Somatostatin analogs (octreotide, lanreotide) are first-line therapy for carcinoid syndrome; they inhibit secretion of serotonin and vasoactive peptides, controlling flushing and diarrhea, and also have antiproliferative effects that slow tumor growth. They are additionally used to prevent carcinoid crisis around procedures. High-dose corticosteroids (B) are not the primary treatment for the secretory symptoms of carcinoid syndrome. A beta-blocker (C) does not address mediator secretion and could be problematic; carcinoid-related flushing is not catecholamine-driven. Loperamide alone (D) only symptomatically reduces diarrhea and does not address flushing, wheezing, or the underlying mediator excess. Liver-directed therapy and surgical debulking are also important adjuncts for metastatic disease.

stages.label: PERI-PROCEDURE
stages.question: Before any surgery, anesthesia, or tumor manipulation in this patient, what life-threatening complication must be prevented?
options: Carcinoid crisis (massive mediator release causing profound hypotension and bronchospasm)
stages.explanation: Carcinoid crisis is a life-threatening surge of vasoactive mediators triggered by anesthesia, surgery, or tumor manipulation, causing severe flushing, profound hypotension (sometimes paradoxical hypertension), bronchospasm, and arrhythmias. It is prevented and treated with octreotide given peri-operatively. Thyroid storm (A) results from decompensated hyperthyroidism, not a neuroendocrine tumor. Addisonian crisis (C) is acute adrenal insufficiency, unrelated here. A catecholamine-driven hypertensive crisis (D) characterizes pheochromocytoma, a different neuroendocrine tumor; carcinoid crisis is driven by serotonin and other peptides, and hypotension is the classic feature. Prophylactic octreotide is the key preventive measure before procedures in carcinoid patients.

cases.diagnosis: Metastatic well-differentiated neuroendocrine (carcinoid) tumor of the ileum with carcinoid syndrome and carcinoid heart disease
pearls: Carcinoid syndrome (flushing, secretory diarrhea, wheezing, right-sided valvular disease) from midgut tumors usually appears only after LIVER metastases let serotonin bypass hepatic first-pass metabolism.
Confirm with 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA (serotonin metabolite); chromogranin A is a useful adjunct neuroendocrine marker.
Serotonin causes right-sided (tricuspid/pulmonary) valve fibrosis—the lungs inactivate serotonin, sparing left-sided valves; tryptophan diversion can cause niacin-deficiency pellagra.
Somatostatin analogs (octreotide) control symptoms and slow growth; give prophylactic octreotide to prevent life-threatening carcinoid crisis before procedures.

cases.title: Dark Stools and Fatigue in a Middle-Aged Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of black tarry stools and progressive fatigue.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man is admitted with three weeks of intermittent melena and worsening exertional fatigue. He denies hematemesis, abdominal pain, weight loss, or change in bowel caliber. He reports early satiety and mild epigastric fullness. He has no history of peptic ulcer disease, takes no NSAIDs, aspirin, or anticoagulants, and drinks alcohol only socially. Past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 20 mg daily. He has no prior endoscopy or known GI disease. He has no drug allergies. Social history: retired electrician, 20-pack-year smoking history (quit 10 years ago). Family history is notable for colon cancer in a paternal uncle at age 70; no known hereditary cancer syndromes. He appears pale and tired but hemodynamically stable on presentation. He has not had any blood transfusions previously. Review of systems is otherwise negative for fevers, night sweats, or dysphagia.
cases.exam: The patient is pale but alert and oriented. Conjunctivae are pale. Cardiovascular exam reveals a soft systolic flow murmur and tachycardia without gallop. Lungs are clear. Abdomen is soft with mild epigastric fullness and a vague, deep, non-tender fullness in the left upper quadrant; no rebound or guarding. There is no hepatosplenomegaly appreciated and no palpable superficial lymphadenopathy. Rectal exam reveals melenic stool that is guaiac-positive; no external lesions or masses. There are no stigmata of chronic liver disease, no spider angiomata, and no peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After establishing IV access and sending labs, what is the most appropriate next step in evaluating this patient's melena?
stages.explanation: Melena with epigastric symptoms and early satiety points to an upper GI source, so EGD is the first-line diagnostic and potentially therapeutic test. Exploratory laparotomy is premature without localizing the bleed. Colonoscopy evaluates a lower source and would be lower yield given melena and upper symptoms. Capsule endoscopy is reserved for obscure bleeding after negative EGD and colonoscopy because it offers no therapeutic capacity and is contraindicated if obstruction is possible. A tagged RBC scan localizes only brisk active bleeding and lacks diagnostic specificity; it is not the first step when EGD can directly visualize and treat the lesion.

stages.label: LABS & ENDOSCOPY
stages.content:
Hgb 8.6 g/dL
MCV 74 fL
Platelets 310 ×10³/µL
Ferritin 9 ng/mL
BUN 22 mg/dL, Cr 0.9 mg/dL
EGD: 4 cm smooth submucosal mass along the gastric body with central ulceration and overlying clot; mucosa otherwise normal
CT abdomen/pelvis: 5 cm exophytic gastric wall mass arising from the muscularis propria, well-circumscribed, no lymphadenopathy, no hepatic lesions

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Endoscopic biopsy of the submucosal mass is performed. Which immunohistochemical/molecular finding would most strongly confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Desmin and smooth muscle actin positivity with CD117 negativity
CD20 positivity with monoclonal kappa restriction
stages.explanation: A well-circumscribed submucosal gastric mass arising from the muscularis propria with bleeding is classic for a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST), which derives from interstitial cells of Cajal and is defined by CD117 (c-KIT) and DOG1 positivity (KIT or PDGFRA mutations drive most cases). Cytokeratin/EMA indicate carcinoma. S-100 suggests schwannoma or neural tumor. Desmin/SMA positivity with CD117 negativity defines leiomyoma/leiomyosarcoma, the historical mimic of GIST. CD20 with light-chain restriction indicates B-cell lymphoma (e.g., gastric MALT), which is mucosal rather than a muscularis-derived submucosal mass.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms a c-KIT-positive GIST. The tumor is localized, resectable, and 5 cm. What is the most appropriate definitive management for this patient?
options: Surgical resection with negative margins, without routine lymphadenectomy
Wide resection with extended regional lymphadenectomy
stages.explanation: For localized, resectable GIST, complete surgical resection with negative margins is the definitive treatment; because GISTs rarely spread to lymph nodes, routine lymphadenectomy is not performed (option B is wrong). GISTs are not responsive to conventional cytotoxic chemotherapy or radiation, so chemoradiation with FOLFOX is ineffective (C). Imatinib alone without resection is appropriate only for unresectable/metastatic disease or as neoadjuvant therapy to downstage; a resectable tumor should be excised (D). Endoscopic mucosal resection is inadequate for a 5 cm transmural mesenchymal tumor arising from muscularis propria and risks rupture and incomplete excision (E).

stages.label: ADJUVANT THERAPY
stages.question: Pathology shows a 6 cm gastric GIST with a mitotic rate of 8 per 50 high-power fields, placing it at high risk of recurrence. Which adjuvant therapy is most appropriate?
options: Adjuvant external beam radiation to the resection bed
Adjuvant imatinib for at least 3 years
stages.explanation: High-risk GIST (large size and high mitotic rate) warrants adjuvant imatinib for at least 3 years, which improves recurrence-free and overall survival by targeting residual KIT-driven disease. Conventional chemotherapy such as doxorubicin is ineffective in GIST (A). Radiation has no established adjuvant role for GIST (B). Observation alone undertreats a high-risk tumor that has a substantial recurrence rate (D). Sunitinib is a second-line tyrosine kinase inhibitor used when GIST is imatinib-resistant or intolerant, not as first-line adjuvant therapy (E).

cases.diagnosis: Gastric gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST), c-KIT positive
pearls: GISTs arise from the interstitial cells of Cajal and are defined by CD117 (c-KIT) and DOG1 positivity, usually driven by KIT or PDGFRA mutations.
They present as submucosal masses arising from the muscularis propria; GI bleeding from central ulceration is the most common presenting feature of gastric GIST.
Localized resectable GIST is treated with margin-negative surgery without lymphadenectomy, since nodal spread is rare.
Imatinib is used as neoadjuvant therapy, adjuvant therapy for high-risk tumors (≥3 years), and first-line treatment for metastatic disease; sunitinib is second-line.

cases.title: Persistent Knee Pain in a Teenage Athlete
cases.chiefComplaint: Two months of worsening pain and swelling around the right knee.
cases.history: A 15-year-old boy presents to clinic with two months of progressively worsening pain around his right knee, now waking him at night and unrelieved by ibuprofen. He plays competitive basketball and initially attributed the pain to overuse, but it has persisted despite rest. Over the past few weeks he has noticed a firm swelling just above the knee. He denies fever, weight loss, prior trauma other than minor sports bumps, and reports no other joint involvement. He has no significant past medical history, takes only ibuprofen 400 mg as needed, and has no allergies. He is up to date on immunizations. Social history: high school sophomore, no tobacco, alcohol, or drug use. Family history is unremarkable for cancer, though a maternal grandmother had breast cancer in her 60s. He has had a recent growth spurt over the past year. Review of systems is otherwise negative for cough, bone pain elsewhere, or systemic symptoms.
cases.exam: Well-appearing adolescent in no acute distress. There is a firm, tender, non-mobile swelling over the distal femur just proximal to the right knee, with overlying warmth but no erythema or skin breakdown. Knee range of motion is mildly limited by pain at terminal flexion; there is no effusion and ligaments are stable. Distal pulses are intact and neurovascular exam of the leg is normal. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy and no other palpable bony masses. The contralateral knee is normal. Spine and other joints are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a teenager with persistent night pain and a firm distal femoral mass, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Plain radiographs of the femur and knee
MRI of the entire femur immediately, skipping x-rays
Bone scan of the whole body
