Empiric course of antibiotics for presumed osteomyelitis
stages.explanation: Plain radiographs are the appropriate first imaging study for a suspected bone tumor; they are inexpensive, rapid, and often reveal characteristic patterns that guide further workup. MRI is essential later for local staging but should follow x-rays, which frame the differential (B). A whole-body bone scan assesses for distant disease but is not the first step before the primary lesion is characterized (C). Joint aspiration evaluates effusion/infection but this is a solid bony mass without effusion (D). Empiric antibiotics are inappropriate without evidence of infection and would delay diagnosis of a possible malignancy (E).

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Plain radiograph (distal femur): aggressive lytic and sclerotic (mixed) lesion in the metaphysis with cortical destruction
Elevation of the periosteum forming a Codman triangle
Spiculated 'sunburst' periosteal new bone perpendicular to the cortex
Soft-tissue mass extending beyond the cortex
Alkaline phosphatase 480 U/L (elevated)
LDH 310 U/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The radiograph shows a metaphyseal lesion with a Codman triangle and sunburst pattern. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Osteosarcoma is the most common primary bone malignancy in adolescents, typically arising in the metaphysis of long bones (especially distal femur/proximal tibia) during growth spurts, and classically produces a Codman triangle and sunburst periosteal reaction with elevated alkaline phosphatase. Ewing sarcoma favors the diaphysis and produces an onion-skin/laminated periosteal reaction (A). Osteomyelitis can mimic aggressive lesions but lacks a true sunburst pattern and usually has systemic signs (C). Giant cell tumor occurs in the epiphysis of skeletally mature patients (D). Osteochondroma is a benign cartilage-capped exostosis pointing away from the joint, not an aggressive destructive lesion (E).

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before definitive treatment, which step is most critical to confirm the diagnosis and guide management?
options: Excisional biopsy with immediate wide resection
Core/open biopsy performed at the treating sarcoma center, plus staging imaging including chest CT
Start chemotherapy empirically based on imaging alone
stages.explanation: A biopsy is required to confirm osteosarcoma histologically, and it should be done at the center that will perform definitive surgery, along a planned approach so the tract can be excised, plus staging with chest CT (lungs are the most common metastatic site). Excisional biopsy with immediate resection risks contaminating tissue planes before the diagnosis and plan are established (A). Empiric chemotherapy without tissue diagnosis is unacceptable (C). Bone marrow biopsy is part of Ewing sarcoma staging, not routine osteosarcoma workup (D). PET alone does not provide histology, and surgery should not precede tissue confirmation and staging (E).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms high-grade osteosarcoma localized to the distal femur with negative chest CT. What is the standard treatment approach?
options: Surgical resection alone (limb salvage or amputation)
Neoadjuvant chemotherapy, then limb-sparing surgical resection, then adjuvant chemotherapy
Radiation followed by chemotherapy without surgery
stages.explanation: Standard management of localized high-grade osteosarcoma is neoadjuvant multiagent chemotherapy (e.g., methotrexate, doxorubicin, cisplatin) followed by wide surgical resection with limb salvage when feasible, then adjuvant chemotherapy guided by tumor necrosis on the resection specimen. Surgery alone leaves patients at high risk of relapse from micrometastatic disease (A). Osteosarcoma is relatively radioresistant, so radiation is not definitive therapy (B, E). Imatinib targets GIST/CML, not osteosarcoma (D). The combined chemotherapy-plus-surgery approach dramatically improved survival over surgery alone.

cases.diagnosis: Osteosarcoma of the distal femur
pearls: Osteosarcoma is the most common primary bone malignancy in adolescents and arises in the metaphysis of long bones during growth spurts (distal femur, proximal tibia).
Classic radiographic signs are the Codman triangle (periosteal elevation) and sunburst spiculated new bone; alkaline phosphatase and LDH are often elevated.
The lungs are the most common site of metastasis, so chest CT is essential for staging.
Treatment is neoadjuvant chemotherapy, wide resection (limb salvage), and adjuvant chemotherapy; osteosarcoma is relatively radioresistant.

cases.title: Worsening Mid-Thigh Pain and Fever in a Young Teen
cases.chiefComplaint: Six weeks of deep mid-thigh bone pain now accompanied by low-grade fevers.
cases.history: A 13-year-old girl is admitted for evaluation of six weeks of progressively worsening deep pain in the mid-right thigh. The pain is constant, worse at night, and not clearly related to activity. Over the past two weeks she has had intermittent low-grade fevers and feels generally fatigued. There is no preceding trauma. She denies night sweats, weight loss, and pain elsewhere. Past medical history is unremarkable; she takes no regular medications and has used acetaminophen for the pain. She has no known drug allergies and is fully immunized. Social history: 7th grader, lives with both parents, no substance use. Family history is noncontributory for malignancy. On review of systems she notes a palpable tender swelling has developed over the mid-thigh in the last week. She has had no respiratory symptoms, dysuria, or rashes. She has not been ill or had recent infections, and there is no history of indwelling lines.
cases.exam: Adolescent appearing uncomfortable but non-toxic. There is a firm, tender, warm fusiform swelling over the mid-right femoral diaphysis without overlying erythema or fluctuance. The overlying skin is intact. Hip and knee range of motion are preserved but the patient guards due to thigh pain. Neurovascular exam of the lower extremity is intact with palpable distal pulses. There is no regional or generalized lymphadenopathy and no hepatosplenomegaly. The remainder of the musculoskeletal exam is normal, including the contralateral leg and the spine.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A teen with diaphyseal bone pain, fever, and a tender mass needs imaging. After plain films, what combination best distinguishes a primary bone tumor from osteomyelitis and characterizes the lesion?
options: MRI of the lesion with contrast plus laboratory inflammatory markers
stages.explanation: MRI with contrast best delineates marrow involvement, soft-tissue extension, and lesion boundaries, helping distinguish tumor from infection, and inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP, plus LDH) add diagnostic context; both inform the biopsy plan. Delaying with repeat films wastes time in an aggressive process (A). Ultrasound is insufficient for marrow and bony detail (C). Empiric antibiotics without tissue diagnosis risks missing malignancy when the presentation overlaps with osteomyelitis (D). DEXA measures bone density for osteoporosis and is irrelevant here (E).

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 11.5 ×10³/µL
ESR 68 mm/hr
CRP 6.2 mg/dL
LDH 520 U/L
Plain film (femur): permeative lytic lesion in the diaphysis with a laminated 'onion-skin' periosteal reaction
MRI: large marrow-replacing diaphyseal lesion with a circumferential soft-tissue mass
Biopsy: sheets of small round blue cells; CD99 (MIC2) membranous positivity
Cytogenetics: t(11;22)(q24;q12) EWSR1-FLI1 fusion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given small round blue cells with CD99 positivity and t(11;22), an onion-skin periosteal reaction, and a diaphyseal location, what is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Ewing sarcoma is a small round blue cell tumor of childhood/adolescence that favors the diaphysis of long bones and the pelvis, produces a laminated onion-skin periosteal reaction, and is defined by the t(11;22)(q24;q12) EWSR1-FLI1 fusion with CD99 membranous positivity. Osteosarcoma is metaphyseal with osteoid production and a sunburst/Codman pattern, not small round blue cells (A). Osteomyelitis can mimic it clinically but lacks the t(11;22) and small round blue cell histology (C). Lymphoma of bone is also small/round cell but is CD45/CD20 positive and CD99 negative without t(11;22) (D). Chondrosarcoma produces cartilage and occurs in older adults (E).

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: Which set of studies is most appropriate to complete staging for newly diagnosed Ewing sarcoma?
options: Chest CT, PET/CT or bone scan, and bilateral bone marrow biopsy
Echocardiogram and pulmonary function tests only
stages.explanation: Ewing sarcoma commonly metastasizes to lungs, bone, and bone marrow, so staging includes chest CT (lung mets), PET/CT or bone scan (skeletal mets), and bilateral bone marrow biopsies (marrow involvement). Chest CT alone misses skeletal and marrow disease (B). Brain MRI and LP are not routine because CNS spread is uncommon at diagnosis (C). Colonoscopy and abdominal ultrasound are unrelated to its metastatic pattern (D). Echocardiogram and PFTs are baseline assessments before cardiotoxic/anesthesia exposure but do not stage the cancer (E).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Staging shows localized disease. What is the cornerstone initial treatment for localized Ewing sarcoma?
options: Multiagent neoadjuvant chemotherapy (e.g., vincristine, doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide alternating with ifosfamide/etoposide) followed by local control with surgery and/or radiation
stages.explanation: Ewing sarcoma is treated with multiagent systemic chemotherapy (classically VDC alternating with IE) as the backbone, because micrometastatic disease is presumed even in localized cases, followed by local control with surgery and/or radiation. Unlike osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma is radiosensitive, so radiation is a valid local-control option, but neither surgery nor radiation alone addresses systemic disease (A, B). Imatinib is not active in Ewing sarcoma (D). Observation is inappropriate for an aggressive malignancy (E). Chemotherapy markedly improves survival and is essential.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Shortly after starting induction chemotherapy, the patient develops a rapidly rising potassium, phosphate, and uric acid with a falling calcium and rising creatinine. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Hold all IV fluids to reduce volume overload
Aggressive IV hydration, rasburicase, and management of electrolyte derangements
Administer calcium gluconate and discharge home
stages.explanation: This is tumor lysis syndrome (hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hyperuricemia, hypocalcemia, acute kidney injury) from rapid cell turnover after chemotherapy in a bulky, chemosensitive tumor. Management is aggressive IV hydration plus rasburicase (or allopurinol) to lower uric acid, with correction of electrolyte abnormalities and renal protection. Withholding fluids worsens uric acid/phosphate nephropathy (A). Calcium gluconate may transiently stabilize cardiac membranes in hyperkalemia, but discharge is dangerous and ignores the syndrome (C). Transfusion does not treat TLS (D). Antibiotics address infection, not metabolic derangements (E).

cases.diagnosis: Ewing sarcoma of the femoral diaphysis
pearls: Ewing sarcoma is a small round blue cell tumor that favors the diaphysis of long bones and the pelvis, with a laminated 'onion-skin' periosteal reaction.
It is defined by the t(11;22)(q24;q12) EWSR1-FLI1 fusion and membranous CD99 (MIC2) positivity.
It can mimic osteomyelitis (fever, elevated ESR/CRP); biopsy and cytogenetics are essential to distinguish them.
Treatment combines multiagent chemotherapy with local control by surgery and/or radiation; unlike osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma is radiosensitive.

cases.title: A White Glow in a Toddler's Eye
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents noticed a white reflection in the child's pupil in photographs.
cases.history: A 2-year-old girl is brought to clinic because her parents have repeatedly seen a white glow in her right pupil in flash photographs over the past two months, and they recently noticed her right eye seems to drift inward. The child is otherwise developing normally, eating well, and has no pain or eye rubbing. There is no history of trauma. Past medical history is unremarkable; she was born full-term without complications. She takes no medications and has no allergies. She is up to date on vaccinations. Social history: lives with parents and an older sibling who is healthy; attends daycare. Family history is significant: her father had an eye tumor removed in early childhood and has a prosthetic eye, though details are limited. Review of systems is otherwise negative for fever, weight loss, vomiting, or developmental regression. The parents deny any prior eye examinations beyond newborn screening.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, interactive toddler. Examination of the right eye reveals leukocoria (an absent/white red reflex) and an inward deviation of the eye (esotropia). The left eye also has an abnormal, diminished red reflex on careful examination. There is no proptosis, conjunctival injection, or periorbital swelling. Pupils are otherwise round; extraocular movements are limited by strabismus on the right. There is no palpable orbital mass, no lymphadenopathy, and no hepatosplenomegaly. The neurologic exam is otherwise age-appropriate. The remainder of the general exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A toddler presents with leukocoria and strabismus. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure the parents that strabismus is common and re-check in a year
Urgent referral to an ophthalmologist for a dilated fundoscopic exam under anesthesia
Order a CT scan of the orbit as the first and only test
stages.explanation: Leukocoria with strabismus in a young child is a red flag for retinoblastoma and mandates urgent referral for a dilated fundoscopic exam, often under anesthesia, by an ophthalmologist. Reassurance and delay risk progression of a potentially fatal but treatable cancer (A). Glasses do not address an intraocular tumor (C). Topical antibiotics treat infection, not leukocoria (D). While imaging (MRI preferred) supports the diagnosis, the fundoscopic exam is the key initial step; CT is generally avoided to limit radiation in children with a possible heritable cancer (E).

stages.label: EXAM & IMAGING
stages.content:
Dilated fundoscopic exam (under anesthesia): right eye with a large white, chalky, elevated retinal mass with intralesional calcification; left eye with two smaller similar retinal lesions
MRI orbits/brain: bilateral intraocular masses; no optic nerve extension; no pineal mass
Genetic testing: germline RB1 pathogenic variant identified

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Bilateral calcified intraocular retinal masses with a germline RB1 mutation are found. Which statement about this diagnosis is most accurate?
options: Bilateral disease indicates the hereditary form caused by a germline RB1 mutation, conferring risk of additional cancers
Calcification rules out malignancy and suggests a benign hamartoma
RB1 is an oncogene that has been activated
Bilateral disease is due to environmental toxin exposure
stages.explanation: Bilateral (or multifocal) retinoblastoma essentially always indicates the hereditary form, caused by a germline RB1 mutation; per Knudson's two-hit hypothesis, the first hit is inherited and a somatic second hit triggers tumors in multiple cells/both eyes. These patients carry increased lifetime risk of second cancers (notably osteosarcoma and pineoblastoma/'trilateral' retinoblastoma). Sporadic unilateral disease arises from two somatic hits (A). Intralesional calcification is characteristic of retinoblastoma, not a benign feature (C). RB1 is a tumor suppressor, not an activated oncogene (D). It is genetic, not toxin-induced (E).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this child with bilateral intraocular retinoblastoma without extraocular spread, which is a guiding management principle?
options: Immediate bilateral enucleation of both eyes
Eye- and vision-sparing therapy when feasible (e.g., systemic/intra-arterial chemotherapy with focal therapies), reserving enucleation for advanced eyes
Observation only, since the tumors are intraocular
External beam radiation as first-line for both eyes
stages.explanation: For bilateral intraocular retinoblastoma, the goal is to save life first, then preserve eyes and vision when possible using chemoreduction (systemic or intra-arterial chemotherapy) combined with focal therapies (laser, cryotherapy, plaque brachytherapy); enucleation is reserved for advanced eyes that cannot be salvaged. Routine bilateral enucleation needlessly sacrifices vision when salvage is possible (A). Observation alone allows tumor progression and metastasis (C). External beam radiation is avoided when feasible because it markedly increases second-malignancy risk in germline RB1 carriers (D). Topical drops cannot treat an intraocular tumor (E).

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: What is the most important long-term consideration for this child and her family?
options: No further follow-up is needed once the eyes are treated
Genetic counseling and lifelong surveillance for second malignancies, plus screening of at-risk relatives
Avoiding all forms of education due to vision loss
Annual chest x-rays to screen for lung cancer specifically
Discontinuing all eye exams after age 5
stages.explanation: Germline RB1 mutation carriers need genetic counseling and lifelong surveillance because of elevated risk of second cancers (especially osteosarcoma and other sarcomas, melanoma, and pineoblastoma), and at-risk relatives (including this child's affected father's other children) should be screened. Treated retinoblastoma still requires ongoing ophthalmologic and oncologic follow-up, so stopping is unsafe (A, E). Vision rehabilitation supports education rather than avoiding it (C). Surveillance is broad for sarcomas and other tumors, not narrowly chest x-rays for lung cancer (D).

cases.diagnosis: Bilateral hereditary retinoblastoma (germline RB1 mutation)
pearls: Leukocoria (absent/white red reflex) and strabismus in a young child are the hallmark presenting signs of retinoblastoma and warrant urgent ophthalmologic evaluation.
Bilateral/multifocal disease indicates the hereditary form from a germline RB1 (tumor suppressor) mutation; Knudson's two-hit hypothesis explains the early, multifocal tumors.
Germline RB1 carriers have increased risk of second malignancies (osteosarcoma, sarcomas) and trilateral retinoblastoma (pineoblastoma); external beam radiation is minimized.
Management prioritizes survival, then eye and vision preservation via chemoreduction plus focal therapy, with enucleation reserved for advanced eyes.

cases.title: A Painless Belly Lump in a Preschooler
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents felt a firm lump in the child's abdomen while bathing him.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is admitted after his parents felt a firm lump in his left abdomen during a bath three days ago. The child has been well, active, eating normally, with no pain, vomiting, fever, or change in bowel or bladder habits. There has been no hematuria noted and no recent illness or trauma. Past medical history is notable for being large for gestational age at birth and a history of an umbilical hernia that was repaired; the pediatrician once noted asymmetric limb size. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He is fully immunized and developmentally appropriate. Social history: lives with both parents; no exposures of concern. Family history is unremarkable for childhood cancer. On review of systems there is no weight loss, night sweats, bone pain, or eye findings. The parents report his appetite and energy remain good, and he has had no recent infections.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, playful toddler. Abdominal exam reveals a large, firm, smooth, non-tender flank mass on the left that does not cross the midline. There is mild hemihypertrophy of the right lower extremity compared with the left. There is no aniridia. There is no hepatomegaly, and no other masses are palpable. There is no abdominal tenderness, distension, or peritoneal signs. There is no lymphadenopathy and no lower extremity edema. Genitourinary exam is normal. Cardiopulmonary and neurologic examinations are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young child has a painless, smooth flank mass that does not cross midline. What is the most appropriate initial imaging and an important caution during the exam?
options: Vigorous deep palpation to define the mass, then CT
Abdominal ultrasound first, while minimizing aggressive palpation of the mass
Immediate biopsy of the mass in clinic
stages.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound is the appropriate first imaging study for a pediatric abdominal mass; it characterizes the mass and its origin without radiation. Clinicians should avoid vigorous palpation because excessive manipulation of a friable renal tumor risks capsule rupture and tumor spillage, which upstages disease (so option A is harmful). A plain film cannot adequately characterize a solid mass (C). Percutaneous biopsy in clinic is not the initial step and can risk spillage; tissue is typically obtained at surgery in many protocols (D). Barium enema evaluates the colon and is irrelevant to a renal mass (E).

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Ultrasound: large solid intrarenal mass arising from the left kidney with a 'claw sign' of renal parenchyma; mass does not cross midline
CT abdomen/pelvis: heterogeneous left renal mass, intact capsule, no renal vein/IVC thrombus, no nodal involvement
Chest CT: no pulmonary nodules
Urinalysis: trace blood
BP 112/74 mmHg (elevated for age)
Catecholamine metabolites (VMA/HVA): within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A solid intrarenal mass that does not cross midline with normal urine catecholamines, plus hemihypertrophy, is most consistent with which diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) is the most common renal malignancy of childhood (peak age 3-4), classically presenting as an asymptomatic, smooth, intrarenal flank mass that does NOT cross the midline, with normal urine catecholamines. Neuroblastoma typically crosses the midline, encases vessels, and elevates urine catecholamines (VMA/HVA) (A). Renal cell carcinoma is rare in young children (C). Congenital mesoblastic nephroma presents in neonates/infants, not at age 3 (D). Polycystic kidney disease produces bilateral cystic kidneys, not a solitary solid mass (E). Hemihypertrophy further supports a Wilms-associated overgrowth syndrome.

stages.label: GENETICS
stages.question: Given the hemihypertrophy, prior umbilical hernia, and large birth size, which associated condition and gene are most relevant to this child's tumor risk?
options: WAGR syndrome from a deletion of an oncogene
Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (overgrowth), with Wilms predisposition linked to the WT genes/11p region
Neurofibromatosis type 1 from an NF1 mutation
stages.explanation: This child's macrosomia, umbilical hernia/abdominal wall defect, and hemihypertrophy fit Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, an overgrowth disorder linked to the 11p15 region that predisposes to Wilms tumor (and hepatoblastoma), warranting tumor surveillance. WT1 is on chromosome 11p13; WAGR (Wilms, Aniridia, GU anomalies, Range of developmental delay) involves an 11p13 deletion including WT1, but WT1 is a tumor suppressor, not an oncogene, and this child has no aniridia (A). NF1, Down syndrome, and RB1 are not the classic Wilms-overgrowth associations described here (C, D, E).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging shows a unilateral tumor confined to the kidney with an intact capsule and no metastases. What is the standard management approach?
options: Radical nephrectomy with surgical staging, followed by chemotherapy (and radiation for higher stages)
stages.explanation: Standard management of unilateral Wilms tumor (per the North American/COG approach) is upfront radical nephrectomy with surgical staging and lymph node sampling, followed by stage- and histology-directed chemotherapy (commonly vincristine and dactinomycin, adding doxorubicin and/or radiation for higher stages or unfavorable histology). Radiation alone is inadequate as primary therapy (A). Observation allows progression of a malignancy (C). Bilateral nephrectomy is unnecessary for unilateral disease and causes renal failure (D). Imatinib is not used in Wilms tumor (E). Wilms tumor has excellent survival with this multimodal approach.

cases.diagnosis: Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma), left kidney
pearls: Wilms tumor is the most common pediatric renal malignancy (peak age 3-4) and presents as an asymptomatic, smooth flank mass that does NOT cross the midline.
Normal urine catecholamines and an intrarenal origin help distinguish Wilms tumor from neuroblastoma, which crosses midline and elevates VMA/HVA.
Associated syndromes include WAGR (WT1 deletion, with aniridia/GU anomalies) and Beckwith-Wiedemann (overgrowth, 11p15); these warrant tumor surveillance.
Avoid vigorous palpation to prevent capsule rupture/tumor spillage; treat with nephrectomy plus stage-directed chemotherapy (± radiation).

cases.title: A Crossing Belly Mass and Bruised Eyes in a Toddler
cases.chiefComplaint: Abdominal swelling with new bruising around both eyes and unusual jerky eye movements.
cases.history: A 2-year-old boy is admitted for evaluation of a firm abdominal mass noted over the past few weeks, along with new dark discoloration around both eyes that the parents initially attributed to bumping his face. Over the same period he has become more irritable, less active, and has been eating less. In the past week the parents noticed rapid, chaotic, dancing eye movements and unsteady, jerky limb motions. There is no fever or obvious trauma. Past medical history is unremarkable; he was a term infant. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He is fully immunized and was meeting milestones until recently. Social history: lives with parents, attends daycare. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems notes intermittent low-grade fevers, decreased appetite, and weight loss. There is no hematuria, jaundice, or rash. The parents deny any recent infections preceding these symptoms.
cases.exam: Irritable, pale toddler. There is bilateral periorbital ecchymosis ('raccoon eyes') with mild proptosis but no obvious orbital trauma. Neurologic exam reveals chaotic, multidirectional conjugate eye movements (opsoclonus) and intermittent myoclonic jerks of the limbs and trunk. Abdominal exam reveals a large, firm, irregular, fixed mass that crosses the midline. There is hepatomegaly. Several small, firm, blue-tinged subcutaneous nodules are present on the trunk. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Cardiopulmonary exam shows tachycardia without murmur. No focal weakness, but the child is ataxic when attempting to stand.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given an abdominal mass crossing the midline, raccoon eyes, and opsoclonus-myoclonus, which initial workup best targets the suspected diagnosis?
options: Urine catecholamine metabolites (VMA/HVA) plus abdominal imaging
Lumbar puncture for meningitis as the first step
stages.explanation: This constellation, a midline-crossing abdominal mass, raccoon eyes (orbital metastasis), opsoclonus-myoclonus (a paraneoplastic syndrome), and bluish skin nodules, strongly suggests neuroblastoma, so urine catecholamine metabolites (VMA and HVA) plus cross-sectional abdominal imaging are the targeted workup. Ceruloplasmin/liver biopsy evaluate Wilson disease, unrelated here (B). Sweat chloride tests for cystic fibrosis (C). While altered mental status can prompt an LP, opsoclonus-myoclonus here is paraneoplastic, not infectious, so LP is not the first step (D). An upper GI series evaluates the gut lumen, not a retroperitoneal mass (E).

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urine VMA and HVA: markedly elevated
CBC: Hgb 8.0 g/dL, platelets 90 ×10³/µL
CT abdomen: large, heterogeneous, calcified suprarenal mass encasing the aorta and crossing the midline; displaces but does not arise from the kidney
MIBG scan: avid uptake in the primary mass and multiple bony sites
Bone marrow biopsy: clusters of small round blue cells (Homer-Wright rosettes)
Tumor genetics: MYCN (N-myc) amplification present

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Elevated urinary VMA/HVA, a calcified midline-crossing suprarenal mass, MIBG avidity, and Homer-Wright rosettes confirm which diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Neuroblastoma arises from neural crest-derived sympathetic tissue (often the adrenal medulla), crosses the midline, encases vessels, contains calcifications, secretes catecholamines (elevated urine VMA/HVA), is MIBG-avid, and shows small round blue cells with Homer-Wright rosettes. Wilms tumor is intrarenal, does not cross midline, and has normal catecholamines (A). Pheochromocytoma also secretes catecholamines but presents with paroxysmal hypertension/sweating in older patients and lacks this pediatric paraneoplastic picture (C). Rhabdomyosarcoma is desmin/myogenin positive without catecholamine secretion (D). Hepatoblastoma arises in the liver and elevates AFP (E).

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: The tumor demonstrates MYCN (N-myc) amplification. What is the significance of this finding?
options: It indicates an excellent prognosis and favors observation
It is a marker of high-risk, aggressive disease that intensifies treatment
It indicates the tumor will spontaneously regress
It confirms the tumor is benign
stages.explanation: MYCN (N-myc) amplification is a well-established marker of high-risk, aggressive neuroblastoma associated with rapid progression and poorer outcomes, and it upstages risk classification toward intensive multimodal therapy. It does not portend an excellent prognosis or favor observation (A). It is highly prognostically relevant, not neutral (C). Spontaneous regression occurs in certain low-risk infants (e.g., stage 4S without MYCN amplification), not in MYCN-amplified tumors (D). MYCN amplification denotes malignant, aggressive behavior, not benignity (E).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given MYCN amplification with metastatic bone and marrow disease, the tumor is classified as high-risk. What is the appropriate treatment strategy?
options: Intensive multimodal therapy: induction chemotherapy, surgery, high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell rescue, radiation, and immunotherapy (anti-GD2) plus retinoic acid
stages.explanation: High-risk neuroblastoma requires intensive multimodal therapy: induction chemotherapy, surgical resection of the primary, consolidation with high-dose chemotherapy and autologous stem cell rescue, radiation to the primary site, and maintenance with anti-GD2 immunotherapy (dinutuximab) plus isotretinoin (differentiating agent). Surgery alone (A) or radiation alone (E) cannot control widespread metastatic disease. Observation is appropriate only for select low-risk infants, not MYCN-amplified metastatic disease (B). Single-agent home chemotherapy is grossly inadequate for high-risk disease (D).

stages.label: PEARL
stages.question: The parents ask why their son had the abnormal dancing eyes and jerky movements. Which explanation is most accurate?
options: Direct tumor invasion of the brainstem
Opsoclonus-myoclonus is a paraneoplastic, immune-mediated phenomenon associated with neuroblastoma that can persist even after tumor treatment
A side effect of the contrast dye used for imaging
stages.explanation: Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome ('dancing eyes, dancing feet') is a paraneoplastic, immune-mediated neurologic syndrome associated with neuroblastoma; cross-reacting antibodies are thought to target neural tissue, and symptoms can persist or relapse even after the tumor is treated, sometimes leaving neurodevelopmental sequelae. It is not from direct brainstem invasion (A) or contrast dye (C). While viral causes of ataxia exist, in this context with confirmed neuroblastoma it is paraneoplastic, not an unrelated encephalitis (D). It is not a medication overdose (E). Interestingly, neuroblastoma presenting with OMS often has comparatively favorable tumor biology.

cases.diagnosis: High-risk neuroblastoma (MYCN-amplified) with opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome
pearls: Neuroblastoma arises from neural crest sympathetic tissue (often adrenal medulla), crosses the midline, encases vessels, calcifies, and secretes catecholamines (elevated urine VMA/HVA).
Classic clues include raccoon eyes (orbital mets), bluish skin nodules, opsoclonus-myoclonus (paraneoplastic), Homer-Wright rosettes, and MIBG avidity.
MYCN (N-myc) amplification marks high-risk, aggressive disease and intensifies treatment; stage 4S without MYCN amplification can spontaneously regress.
High-risk disease is treated with intensive multimodal therapy including anti-GD2 immunotherapy (dinutuximab) and isotretinoin.

cases.title: A Toddler with a Skull Bump and Excessive Thirst
cases.chiefComplaint: A tender lump on the head and several weeks of excessive drinking and urination.
cases.history: A 3-year-old boy is brought to clinic for a tender soft lump on the right side of his head that his mother noticed three weeks ago, which has not resolved. Over the past month she has also noticed he is constantly thirsty, drinking large volumes of water, and soaking through diapers and waking at night to urinate. He has had a chronic scaly, greasy rash on the scalp and in the diaper area that was treated as seborrhea and candidiasis without resolution. There has been intermittent ear drainage as well. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He is fully immunized. Social history: lives with both parents, no concerning exposures. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems notes mild irritability and decreased appetite, but no fever, vomiting, or focal weakness. Growth has plateaued slightly over recent months. The child remains alert and interactive at the visit.
cases.exam: Alert toddler with a 2 cm soft, tender swelling over the right parietal skull with a palpable underlying bony defect. There is a greasy, scaly, petechial-appearing eruption on the scalp and in the diaper/intertriginous areas. The right external auditory canal has chronic-appearing drainage. There is no neck stiffness; cranial nerves are intact and there are no focal neurologic deficits. The abdomen is soft with a mildly enlarged liver edge. There is mild generalized lymphadenopathy. Mucous membranes appear slightly dry. The remainder of the exam, including the lungs, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A child has lytic-feeling skull lesions, polyuria/polydipsia, and a refractory seborrhea-like rash. What initial workup best clarifies the polyuria and the bone lesion?
options: Skull/skeletal imaging for lytic lesions, plus serum/urine osmolality and electrolytes to evaluate for diabetes insipidus
Trial of more potent antifungal cream only
stages.explanation: The combination of skull bony defects, chronic refractory rash, ear drainage, and new polyuria/polydipsia raises concern for Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) with central diabetes insipidus from hypothalamic-pituitary involvement. Appropriate workup includes imaging for lytic bone lesions and paired serum/urine osmolality with electrolytes to evaluate DI. A random glucose with reassurance misses both DI and the systemic disease (A). Allergy testing and stronger antifungals (C, D) chase a misdiagnosed rash. An echocardiogram does not address these findings (E).

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Serum Na 148 mEq/L
Serum osmolality 304 mOsm/kg with inappropriately dilute urine osmolality 110 mOsm/kg
Water deprivation test: urine fails to concentrate; concentrates after desmopressin (consistent with central DI)
Skull radiograph/CT: multiple sharply marginated 'punched-out' lytic lesions of the calvarium
MRI brain: thickened pituitary stalk; absent posterior pituitary bright spot
Skin biopsy: infiltrate of histiocytes; CD1a and CD207 (langerin) positive; S-100 positive; Birbeck granules on electron microscopy

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Biopsy shows CD1a- and langerin (CD207)-positive histiocytes with Birbeck granules. Which is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) is a clonal proliferation of Langerhans-type dendritic cells that are CD1a positive, CD207 (langerin) positive, S-100 positive, and contain tennis racket-shaped Birbeck granules on electron microscopy. The triad of lytic skull lesions, diabetes insipidus, and exophthalmos is classically described (Hand-Schuller-Christian). Juvenile xanthogranuloma is CD1a/langerin negative and CD68/factor XIIIa positive (A). ALL is a marrow lymphoblast malignancy, not langerin-positive dendritic cells (C). Atopic dermatitis lacks lytic bone lesions and DI (D). HLH features fever, cytopenias, and hemophagocytosis, with a different immunophenotype (E).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient has multisystem LCH (bone, skin, pituitary/CNS, liver involvement) with central diabetes insipidus. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Topical steroids to the rash alone
Systemic chemotherapy (e.g., vinblastine plus prednisone) for multisystem disease, plus desmopressin for the diabetes insipidus
Broad-spectrum antibiotics for the ear drainage only
stages.explanation: Multisystem LCH (especially with 'risk-organ'/CNS involvement) requires systemic therapy, classically vinblastine plus prednisone, and the central diabetes insipidus is managed with desmopressin (DDAVP) to replace ADH. Topical steroids treat only cutaneous disease and ignore systemic involvement (A). Although isolated single-system LCH (e.g., a solitary bone lesion) may regress or need only local therapy, multisystem disease does not reliably self-resolve and needs systemic treatment (C). The polyuria is from central DI (lack of ADH), not diabetes mellitus, so insulin is wrong (D). Antibiotics for ear drainage alone miss the underlying disease (E).

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Which long-term complication should be specifically monitored in this child after treatment?
options: Permanent diabetes insipidus and other anterior pituitary hormone deficiencies
Guaranteed complete cure with no sequelae
Inevitable progression to lung fibrosis regardless of treatment
stages.explanation: Central diabetes insipidus from LCH is often permanent, and pituitary stalk involvement can lead to additional anterior pituitary deficiencies (growth hormone, etc.), so endocrine surveillance is essential. LCH outcomes vary widely; multisystem CNS-risk disease can leave lasting sequelae, so a guaranteed sequela-free cure is incorrect (B). LCH does not cause hemophilia (C). Pulmonary LCH (more common in adults/smokers) does not inevitably progress to fibrosis in a treated child (D). Cataracts are not a characteristic LCH complication (E). Neurodegenerative CNS-LCH is another recognized late risk.

cases.diagnosis: Multisystem Langerhans cell histiocytosis with central diabetes insipidus
pearls: Langerhans cell histiocytosis is a clonal dendritic-cell disorder; cells are CD1a and CD207 (langerin) positive, S-100 positive, with Birbeck granules ('tennis rackets') on EM.
The classic triad is lytic skull lesions, central diabetes insipidus, and exophthalmos (Hand-Schuller-Christian); a refractory seborrhea-like rash and ear drainage are common clues.
Central DI from pituitary stalk involvement is often permanent and treated with desmopressin; monitor for additional pituitary hormone deficiencies.
Single-system disease may need only local therapy, but multisystem/risk-organ disease requires systemic chemotherapy (vinblastine + prednisone).

cases.title: Dreading the Next Infusion
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe nausea and vomiting after chemotherapy, and now nausea before even arriving for treatment.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman with newly diagnosed breast cancer presents to the oncology clinic before her second cycle of chemotherapy. After her first cycle of doxorubicin and cyclophosphamide (a highly emetogenic regimen), she experienced intense nausea and several episodes of vomiting beginning a few hours after the infusion and lasting two days. She received only ondansetron during that cycle. She now reports that she feels nauseated just thinking about the infusion and felt sick on the drive to clinic today. She has no abdominal pain, fever, diarrhea, or blood in vomit, and is able to keep down small sips of fluid. Past medical history includes a history of motion sickness and nausea during pregnancy. Medications: the prescribed chemotherapy and ondansetron as needed. She has no allergies. Social history: nondrinker, never smoker, works as a teacher. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is otherwise negative; she is mildly anxious and fatigued.
cases.exam: Anxious but comfortable woman in no acute distress. Mucous membranes are slightly dry but she is not orthostatic. The abdomen is soft, non-tender, and non-distended with normal bowel sounds and no organomegaly. There is no peritoneal sign. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. Her chemotherapy port site is clean, without erythema or tenderness. Neurologic exam is grossly normal. She appears mildly fatigued. There are no signs of significant dehydration such as poor skin turgor or tachycardia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: How should her nausea on the way to clinic, occurring before chemotherapy is given, be classified?
stages.explanation: Nausea triggered by cues (sights, smells, the trip to clinic) before any chemotherapy is administered is anticipatory nausea/vomiting, a learned/conditioned response that develops after poorly controlled emesis in prior cycles. Acute CINV occurs within ~24 hours after chemotherapy (A); delayed CINV occurs 24-120 hours afterward (B). Refractory CINV is emesis despite appropriate prophylaxis on prior cycles (D). Her benign exam and clear temporal link to chemotherapy cues make an acute surgical abdomen unlikely, so imaging is not indicated as the explanation (E). Recognizing the type guides prevention and treatment.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: For her next cycle of this highly emetogenic regimen, what is the most appropriate antiemetic prophylaxis?
options: A combination of a 5-HT3 antagonist, an NK1 receptor antagonist, dexamethasone, and consideration of olanzapine
No prophylaxis; treat symptoms only if they occur
stages.explanation: Highly emetogenic chemotherapy (e.g., anthracycline-cyclophosphamide, AC) requires multidrug prophylaxis: a 5-HT3 antagonist (ondansetron/palonosetron), an NK1 receptor antagonist (aprepitant/fosaprepitant), and dexamethasone, with olanzapine added (a 4-drug regimen) for further control. Her first cycle failed because she received only single-agent ondansetron, which is inadequate for highly emetogenic regimens (A). Metoclopramide or diphenhydramine alone are insufficient single agents for this risk level (C, E). Reactive-only treatment is wrong because preventing the first bad experience is key and reduces conditioned anticipatory symptoms (D).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Besides optimizing prophylactic antiemetics, what is the most appropriate additional intervention specifically for her anticipatory nausea?
options: Behavioral techniques (relaxation, systematic desensitization) and a short-acting benzodiazepine such as lorazepam before treatment
stages.explanation: Anticipatory nausea is a conditioned, partly psychological response and responds best to behavioral strategies (relaxation training, systematic desensitization, guided imagery) plus a benzodiazepine such as lorazepam given before treatment, along with excellent control of acute/delayed emesis to prevent reinforcement. Massively increasing ondansetron does not treat the conditioned anticipatory component and risks toxicity (A). Opioids cause nausea and are not indicated (C). Discontinuing potentially curative chemotherapy is inappropriate when symptoms are manageable (D). Surgery is irrelevant (E). The cornerstone is preventing poorly controlled emesis in the first place.

stages.label: EDUCATION
stages.question: Which statement about CINV prevention is most accurate to share with the patient?
options: Once anticipatory nausea develops, nothing can be done
Preventing nausea/vomiting from the very first cycle is the best way to avoid developing anticipatory symptoms
Antiemetics should only be taken after vomiting starts
Nausea from chemotherapy means the treatment is not working
All chemotherapy carries the same emetic risk
stages.explanation: The single most important principle is prevention: controlling nausea and vomiting from the first cycle with guideline-appropriate prophylaxis prevents the conditioned anticipatory response from forming and is easier than treating established symptoms. Anticipatory nausea can be managed (behavioral therapy, benzodiazepines), so nothing being possible is false (A). Prophylactic antiemetics are given before chemotherapy, not only after vomiting (C). Nausea severity does not indicate treatment efficacy (D). Emetic risk is stratified (high, moderate, low, minimal) and dictates the prophylaxis regimen, so not all chemo is equal (E).

cases.diagnosis: Chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (acute and anticipatory) on a highly emetogenic regimen
pearls: CINV is classified as acute (<24 h), delayed (24-120 h), anticipatory (conditioned, before chemo), breakthrough, and refractory.
Highly emetogenic regimens (e.g., anthracycline + cyclophosphamide) require multidrug prophylaxis: 5-HT3 antagonist + NK1 antagonist + dexamethasone ± olanzapine.
Anticipatory nausea is a learned response best prevented by controlling emesis from cycle 1; treat it with behavioral techniques and benzodiazepines (lorazepam).
Prevention beats rescue: give antiemetics prophylactically before chemotherapy rather than waiting for symptoms.

cases.title: New Breathlessness After Cancer Treatment
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive shortness of breath and leg swelling during follow-up after breast cancer chemotherapy.
cases.history: A 49-year-old woman returns to oncology clinic for surveillance after completing treatment for breast cancer. Over the past two months she has developed progressive exertional dyspnea, fatigue, mild orthopnea, and bilateral ankle swelling. She completed an anthracycline-based regimen (doxorubicin) several months ago; her cumulative doxorubicin dose was high. She also received trastuzumab for HER2-positive disease and underwent left chest wall radiation. She denies chest pain, fever, cough, or hemoptysis. Past medical history includes hypertension controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily; no prior heart disease. Other medications include the completed chemotherapy and a daily multivitamin. She has no allergies. Social history: never smoker, occasional wine, works in retail. Family history includes a father with coronary artery disease in his 70s. Review of systems is notable for reduced exercise tolerance and one episode of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. She has had no recent infections and her weight is up 3 kg from fluid.
cases.exam: Mildly dyspneic woman speaking in full sentences. JVP is elevated at approximately 10 cm. Cardiac exam reveals a displaced apical impulse, tachycardia, an S3 gallop, and a soft holosystolic murmur at the apex. Lung exam shows bibasilar inspiratory crackles. There is bilateral pitting ankle edema. The abdomen is soft without tenderness; there is no hepatomegaly appreciated but mild right upper quadrant fullness. Extremities are warm with intact pulses. There are no focal neurologic deficits. The surgical and radiation sites on the chest wall are well healed.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given new heart failure signs after anthracycline therapy, which initial tests best characterize cardiac function and the syndrome?
options: D-dimer and CT pulmonary angiography only
stages.explanation: New signs of heart failure (S3, elevated JVP, crackles, edema) after anthracyclines call for an echocardiogram to assess left ventricular ejection fraction and a natriuretic peptide (BNP/NT-proBNP), along with an ECG. This noninvasively confirms systolic dysfunction and quantifies the EF decline central to anthracycline cardiotoxicity. Immediate coronary angiography is invasive and not first-line without ischemic features (B). D-dimer/CTPA target PE, which is less consistent with this gradual HF picture (C). PFTs assess lungs, not the suspected cardiomyopathy (D). Reassurance and delay are unsafe given overt HF signs (E).

stages.label: CARDIAC WORKUP
stages.content:
NT-proBNP 2,800 pg/mL (markedly elevated)
ECG: sinus tachycardia, low QRS voltage, nonspecific T-wave changes
Echocardiogram: LVEF 35% (baseline pre-chemo LVEF was 60%); global hypokinesis; mild mitral regurgitation; no pericardial effusion
Troponin: mildly elevated
Chest x-ray: cardiomegaly with pulmonary vascular congestion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her LVEF fell from 60% to 35% with global hypokinesis and HF symptoms after high cumulative doxorubicin. What is the diagnosis?
stages.explanation: A substantial drop in LVEF (≥10 absolute points to below normal) with global hypokinesis and clinical heart failure after a high cumulative anthracycline dose defines anthracycline-induced cardiomyopathy (cardiotoxicity); concurrent trastuzumab can compound the risk. ACS would show ischemic chest pain and regional wall-motion abnormalities/ECG ischemia, not isolated global dysfunction (A). Constrictive pericarditis causes diastolic dysfunction with preserved EF and characteristic findings, not global systolic failure (C). PE causes acute dyspnea/right-heart strain, inconsistent with this gradual global LV dysfunction (D). Radiation pneumonitis is a pulmonary, not primarily cardiac, process (E).

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: The patient asks how this might have been detected or limited earlier. Which statement about anthracycline cardiotoxicity monitoring/risk is most accurate?
options: Risk is independent of total dose
Cardiotoxicity risk rises with cumulative dose, so dose limits and serial LVEF monitoring (echo/MUGA) are used; dexrazoxane can be cardioprotective in selected high-dose settings
Toxicity is always reversible once the drug is stopped
Anthracyclines never affect the heart if given with trastuzumab
stages.explanation: Anthracycline cardiotoxicity is dose-dependent (risk climbs notably with higher cumulative doxorubicin exposure), so oncologists track cumulative dose, set ceilings, and monitor LVEF serially (echocardiography or MUGA) before and during therapy; dexrazoxane (an iron chelator) can be cardioprotective in selected high-cumulative-dose patients. Risk is therefore NOT dose-independent (A). Chronic anthracycline cardiomyopathy is often only partially reversible, not always reversible (C). Trastuzumab adds to cardiac risk rather than abolishing it (D). Beta-blockers treat heart failure and do not cause this cardiomyopathy (E).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of her newly diagnosed reduced-EF heart failure?
options: Continue anthracycline and add a calcium channel blocker
Start guideline-directed heart failure therapy (ACE inhibitor/ARB or ARNI, beta-blocker, plus diuretic for congestion) and refer to cardio-oncology
Immediate heart transplant evaluation as first step
stages.explanation: Anthracycline cardiomyopathy with reduced EF is treated like other HFrEF: guideline-directed medical therapy with a neurohormonal blocker (ACE inhibitor/ARB or ARNI), a beta-blocker, an aldosterone antagonist as appropriate, and a loop diuretic for volume overload, with cardio-oncology co-management; early treatment can partially recover function. Continuing or resuming anthracyclines worsens cardiotoxicity (A, C). Calcium channel blockers (especially nondihydropyridines) are not first-line and can worsen systolic HF (A). Fluid restriction alone undertreats HFrEF (D). Transplant is reserved for refractory end-stage disease, not the first step (E).

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding her ongoing cancer therapy with trastuzumab, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Hold trastuzumab and re-evaluate cardiac function, coordinating between oncology and cardiology before any rechallenge
Double the trastuzumab dose to overcome resistance
stages.explanation: Trastuzumab causes a typically reversible decline in LVEF and is additive to anthracycline cardiotoxicity, so with a symptomatic LVEF of 35% it should be held while cardiac function is reassessed, with joint oncology-cardiology decision-making about optimization and potential later rechallenge once the EF recovers. Continuing it unchanged risks further decline (A). Stopping cancer surveillance is unrelated and unsafe (C). Increasing the trastuzumab dose has no rationale and increases cardiac risk (D). Switching to another anthracycline would worsen the cardiomyopathy (E).

cases.diagnosis: Anthracycline-induced cardiomyopathy (doxorubicin cardiotoxicity) with reduced ejection fraction
pearls: Anthracycline (doxorubicin) cardiotoxicity is dose-dependent and presents as a dilated cardiomyopathy with reduced LVEF and heart failure; trastuzumab adds to the risk.
Monitor cumulative dose and serial LVEF (echo or MUGA) before and during therapy; dexrazoxane can be cardioprotective in selected high-dose patients.
Chronic anthracycline cardiomyopathy is often only partially reversible, whereas trastuzumab-related dysfunction is usually reversible with drug interruption.
Treat with guideline-directed HFrEF therapy (ACEi/ARB or ARNI, beta-blocker, diuretic) and cardio-oncology co-management; hold cardiotoxic agents.

cases.title: Blue Despite a Normal Oxygen Reading Mismatch
cases.chiefComplaint: Bluish discoloration of the lips and fingers with shortness of breath.
cases.history: A 30-year-old man presents to the emergency department with bluish discoloration of his lips and fingertips and a feeling of breathlessness that began over the past several hours. He recently started dapsone for a skin condition and last night used a large amount of an over-the-counter benzocaine-containing topical anesthetic gel for a painful sore in his mouth. He denies chest pain, cough, fever, or known cardiac or pulmonary disease. He reports mild headache and lightheadedness. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: dapsone (recently started) and the benzocaine gel; no other regular medications. He has no known allergies. Social history: occasional alcohol, no smoking, works in construction. Family history is noncontributory; he is unaware of any blood disorders. Review of systems is notable for fatigue and the new cyanosis; there is no leg swelling, palpitations, or recent travel. He appears anxious about the color of his skin, which has not improved with rest.

vitals.SpO2: 85% on room air, unchanged on supplemental oxygen

cases.exam: Anxious young man with notable central cyanosis of the lips and peripheral cyanosis of the fingertips; the skin/blood has a brownish, slate-gray hue. He is mildly tachypneic. Lung auscultation is clear bilaterally with good air entry and no wheezes or crackles. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia with a regular rhythm and no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. There is no jugular venous distension and no peripheral edema. Capillary refill is normal and pulses are intact. Neurologic exam shows a mild headache but no focal deficits. Notably, the cyanosis does not improve despite high-flow supplemental oxygen.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A cyanotic patient has an SpO2 of 85% that does not improve with oxygen. What finding would most strongly point toward the underlying cause?
options: An elevated PaO2 on arterial blood gas with a 'saturation gap' (calculated SaO2 much higher than pulse oximeter SpO2)
A markedly low PaO2 on arterial blood gas
A normal carboxyhemoglobin and normal everything else
A widened A-a gradient from pneumonia on chest x-ray
stages.explanation: In methemoglobinemia, the arterial PaO2 (dissolved oxygen) is normal/high because the lungs oxygenate blood normally, but the pulse oximeter reads a falsely low SpO2 (trending toward ~85%) because methemoglobin absorbs light similarly at both wavelengths. The discrepancy between a high calculated SaO2 (from PaO2) and the low measured SpO2 is the classic 'saturation gap.' A markedly low PaO2 would indicate a true oxygenation/lung problem, not methemoglobinemia (B). Normal everything ignores the abnormality (C). A widened A-a gradient from pneumonia or PE points to pulmonary/embolic causes, inconsistent with clear lungs and oxygen-refractory cyanosis (D, E).

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
ABG (on supplemental O2): PaO2 240 mmHg, calculated SaO2 ~99%
Pulse oximeter SpO2: 85% (saturation gap present)
Blood appears chocolate-brown and does not turn red on exposure to air
Co-oximetry: methemoglobin level 28%
Carboxyhemoglobin: normal
Lactate: mildly elevated
CBC: hemoglobin 14 g/dL; no significant hemolysis at this time

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Chocolate-brown blood, a saturation gap, and a co-oximetry methemoglobin level of 28% after dapsone and benzocaine confirm which diagnosis?
stages.explanation: Methemoglobinemia is confirmed by an elevated methemoglobin level on co-oximetry, chocolate-brown blood, and a saturation gap, here acquired from oxidizing drugs (dapsone, topical benzocaine/local anesthetics) that convert hemoglobin iron from ferrous (Fe2+) to ferric (Fe3+), which cannot bind oxygen. Carbon monoxide poisoning elevates carboxyhemoglobin (normal here) and produces cherry-red, not brown, blood (A). PE and congenital cyanotic heart disease are excluded by clear lungs/normal PaO2 and absence of cardiac findings (C, D). Sulfhemoglobinemia is a separate oxidized-hemoglobin entity but is not detected by standard co-oximetry as methemoglobin and is not reversed by methylene blue (E).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient is symptomatic with a methemoglobin level of 28%. What is the first-line treatment?
stages.explanation: Symptomatic methemoglobinemia (typically methemoglobin >20-30% or with symptoms/end-organ effects) is treated with IV methylene blue, which is reduced by NADPH-methemoglobin reductase to leukomethylene blue, accelerating reduction of methemoglobin back to functional hemoglobin. Stop the offending agents and give supplemental oxygen, but oxygen alone does not reverse the oxidized hemoglobin (A). Exchange transfusion is reserved for severe/refractory cases or when methylene blue is contraindicated/ineffective (C). Hyperbaric oxygen is not first-line for methemoglobinemia (D). Sodium nitrite is an oxidizer used in cyanide poisoning and would worsen methemoglobinemia (E).

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before giving methylene blue, which underlying condition is most important to consider because it changes management?
options: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency, in which methylene blue can be ineffective and may precipitate hemolysis
stages.explanation: G6PD deficiency is critical to consider: methylene blue relies on NADPH generated by G6PD to be reduced to its active form, so in G6PD deficiency it is often ineffective AND can itself act as an oxidant precipitating hemolysis. In such patients, alternatives such as ascorbic acid, exchange transfusion, or supportive care are used. Hypertension, gout, migraine, and lactose intolerance (A, C, D, E) do not alter methemoglobinemia management. This is also why dapsone (an oxidant cleared in part via pathways affected by G6PD status) is a classic precipitant and why screening for G6PD deficiency matters.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After treatment, what is the most important counseling point to prevent recurrence?
options: He can safely resume benzocaine and dapsone immediately
Identify and avoid the offending oxidizing agents (e.g., benzocaine and high-dose dapsone), and review safer alternatives with his physicians
He should avoid all oxygen therapy in the future
stages.explanation: Prevention centers on identifying and avoiding the precipitating oxidizing agents, here topical benzocaine (a well-known cause) and dapsone, and coordinating with his physicians on safer alternatives and appropriate dapsone dosing/monitoring (including G6PD screening). Resuming the offending agents risks recurrence (A). Oxygen is not the problem and should not be avoided (C). Follow-up matters, especially to confirm resolution and address G6PD status (D). Nitrites are oxidizers and would induce methemoglobinemia, so prophylactic nitrites are exactly wrong (E).

cases.diagnosis: Acquired methemoglobinemia (dapsone/benzocaine-induced)
pearls: Methemoglobinemia causes oxygen-refractory cyanosis with chocolate-brown blood and a 'saturation gap' (normal/high PaO2 but low pulse-ox SpO2 ~85%); diagnose with co-oximetry.
Common precipitants are oxidizing drugs: dapsone, topical/local anesthetics (benzocaine, prilocaine), and nitrates/nitrites.
First-line treatment for symptomatic disease is IV methylene blue (reduced via NADPH to convert ferric Fe3+ back to ferrous Fe2+ hemoglobin); stop the offending agent.
In G6PD deficiency, methylene blue is often ineffective and can cause hemolysis; use ascorbic acid, exchange transfusion, or supportive care, and avoid nitrites.
